I quit this game after 6 hours because the death mechanic is extremely punishing. I've played many other difficult indie roguelikes such as Binding of Isaac, Cuphead, Crypt of the Necrodancer, Super Meat Boy, and more, but this was the only game where I actually quit out of frustration. I've also never been so frustrated to actually leave a negative review on a game.

Like many other negative reviews said, playing this game felt like a chore. I dreaded playing it instead of looking forward to it. Retracing your steps after dying felt like an extra level of punishment. If I already can't pass an area, why up the difficulty even more by making me retrieve the shade? Also the fact that benches are so far away from bosses makes it too frustrating. Bosses are supposed to be difficult, and I expect to die at least a few times before learning their attack patterns. The fact that I spawn 10 screens away from a boss makes me want to pull my hair out.

This was super unfortunate because overall I actually enjoyed the game (good gameplay, graphics, audio, etc). But I think the devs need to rethink their risk-reward mechanics to make exploration and death less punishing, or make the punishment more proportional to the reward.

What can I say?  The fundamental issue I had with the game was that for almost the entire time I spent with it, I was effectively forcing myself to play, with internal promises that it was supposed to get better eventually.

I was the fool for expecting something like Metroid or Castlevania.  This game is like those games, in a single sentence.  But the nitty gritty is what's missing.  Breakable walls?  This isn't the kind of game that rewards constantly looking for secrets, because the kinds of secrets it holds aren't the sort that you'll find tucked away randomly.  That's deflating.  The variety of the game's world is 99% visual.  Reduce the graphics just to barebones platforms and almost every location in every corner of the map is exactly the same.  The level design is barren.

I also understood that the music was supposed to be good.  When I think "good music in a Metroidvania game", I unavoidably start comparing what I'm hearing to the music in the Metroidvania games that came before.  This, evidently, is the wrong idea.  The music in this game is good compared to today's average, but where's the melodies?  It's largely just atmospheric constructs.  14 hours should be plenty of time to start hearing something that would make me interested in an OST.  Didn't happen.

I am turned off by two aspects of the design.  First, the hand-drawn quality.  You want it to be hand-drawn?  Fine.  That's not an excuse to cheap out on the frames-per-second of individual sprites.  The protagonist's "cloak" should animate at 60fps or, at the very least, some high standard, like 24 or 30.  Not single digits.

Second, the sound design.  This in particular brings this game down to iOS levels.  Almost every single sound in this game is short, lasting a fraction of a second.  Plinky-plonky.  Blips.  It gives everything a very cheap sound.

On a more personal preference level, I dislike the deliberately cartoony nature of everything.  This does not have to go hand-in-hand with the hand-drawn look.  I like my games more serious.  Less adolescent and goofy.  Even the sound is guilty of this: When a corpse poofs, it very often makes a short flatulent noise.  Somebody on the design team thought it would be cool, funny, whatever, if one of the sounds in the game was just him going "pptt!" into a mic.  I regret having to be the person who tells you, but it wasn't.

That the reviews for this game amount to universal acclaim is what really worries me.  Clearly, if I'm looking for a Metroidvania fix, I can't trust a review aggregate, because too many people have rose-tinted glasses on and are excessively forgiving of deep flaws.

Dec 2021 review.
So I replayed this recently with the latest updates on a modern gaming desktop/monitor and after about 30 hours in I can comfortably say I've had a remarkably different gaming experience.
For one, despite being a relatively simple looking game graphically compared to modern AAA games, this game will actually consume quite a bit of your PC's resources and your PC's RAM in particular. This is a data hungry game, even at low graphical settings and performance can occasionally tank to frustrating levels at crucial gameplay moments. There are certain parts of the game that really suffer from graphical or hit detection lag and disabled or delayed input issues; particularly combat scenarios involving many enemies and particle effects (usually during post hit or post respawn states). There are some ways around this, such as launching the game in certain graphical modes. But I suspect this is a programming issue at its core and it's still surprising to see a game with such derivative gameplay struggle with clunky control issues like this. I suspect that the Steam Windows Exe Port is probably not the best port available for this game and it really does feel like it was meant to be played on an old school 6 button gaming controller; which is great for the nostalgia, but frustrating for the average modern PC gamer.
This game still looks and sounds amazing, even by today's standards, and it's easily one of the better metroidvania style games of its era. Hollow Knight certainly has a charm and personality to it that feels totally original a deeply compelling at times.
Despite all the design flaws I've mentioned. I will 100% recommend sinking at least 10 hours into this game to get a taste for it. Once you get rolling, the game will grow on you quickly, and while it can be hella frustrating at times, the challenges will often only piss you off juuust enough to make you want to immediately try again.

Over the course of 10 hours, I went from loving it to hating it. Hollow Knight is like a mix between Dark Souls (setting, difficulty) and Ori - or, perhaps, other metroidvanias (but it really feels like Ori with that level and map design).

It's all great at first but at some point you realize the platforming sucks. The amount of times I've died to spikes and enemies I couldn't possibly see is absurd - and force of gravity in this game is much higher than in Ori, giving you very little time to react to danger.

Respawn spots are few and far in between. Spending 3-5 minutes running to where you died is very common. And you're forced to return where you died - not only to recover your money dark souls style, but also to restore some functionality to your character. Can't just say screw it and go to a different zone when you're tired of dying in one spot.

And when you do want to "quickly" go somewhere, you have to make your way to a fast travel point, which are very few as well.

It's easy to get lost in Hollow Knight if you don't have maps. To be able to use the map feature at all, you have to find a vendor in the given region and buy that region's map. Until then, you won't even be able to track what rooms you've been to. If you don't find a vendor quickly enough, you might end up running around for an hour trying to figure out where to go. Happened to me.

As if all that wasn't enough, I once had my shade (the thing that appears on your death spot for recovery) appear ahead of the thing that killed me, instead of before. So I died twice in a row and lost 3000 coins.

Great aesthetics though!
This is one of the most finely tuned games I've played in a while. It is one of those games with no major flaws. Probably the best game of last year. Pretty much everything is great in this game.
Bad game design

Played for 5ish hours and the game stalled out and made me close it from task manager 3 times during those hours. I'm running a high end Alienware that easily handles games like RDR2, GTA 5, BF 2042, Witcher 3 at max settings, so it's not a PC spec issue. Not sure what the deal is, but with a game that HEAVILY punishes you for having interrupted gameplay, I'm definitely tapping out and requesting a refund. First time it froze it forced me to spend 30 min backtracked. Second time froze in the middle of the Mantis lords fight and backtracking took almost an hour to get back to that spot. Third time it froze apparently one screen before the second bench in city of tears, before I was able to collect my shade with 6k geo and honestly.. not worth spending any more time on the game. 

Beyond the freezing issue, it's generally not that fun. I've loved metroid since Super, and enjoyed most of the darksouls games. Difficulty and metroidvania style games are what I gravitate to. But the save point (bench) placement in this game make it unendingly obnoxious. Of those 5 hours, 1.5 of them were spent backtracking from when my game froze, and over 2 of them were spent backtracking to bosses after deaths because the combat is punishing. Because for some reason, save points aren't situated anywhere near bosses? I'm very unclear on why they made that decision. I dont mind backtracking to progress exploration and backtracking to areas that now have more things accessible because of a new item, and backtracking in an attempt to find everything is super fun... But that's not whats happening here. Backtracking because you put a challenging fight right before a save bench is just bad design. Also the invisible input timers for stuff like jumping is just not okay. When I jump on a ledge and most other popular platformer games in history have conditioned me that I can jump again as soon as I touch the ground... but Hollow Knight says "nope you're going to walk off this ledge into spikes even though your feet have been planted for a full second"... and then sends you back like 5 platforms from where you were. No thank you game, hard pass.

Long story short, technical issues making already annoying issues absolutely intolerable. It's a shame. The atmosphere is great, the combat is fantastic, the platforming is generally okay, and everything I have seen from the game prior to buying it has made me super excited. There's a lot going for it and I'm kind of still excited for silksong in hopes they correct some of their design issues in that new game. But definitely can't recommend it to people after a very subpar experience.

While I really want to like the game, I can't stand the constant back tracking. Then staring at the map trying to figure out where you can actually go to advance the game, only to constantly run across the map to find it is a blocked area that you can't access yet. 90% of the game is trying to figure out how to get to the next zone. If this is something you don't mind then you should play it as everything else about the game is great.

Don't get the appeal of "Lol die a bunch until you make it" games like this, guess I never will.

I think this game has a lot of great aspects; there is some good story leading the direction of game play, the environments vary as well as the creatures in them, there are both more intricate enemies with a good backstory behind them, as well as those who are numerous and have less personality behind them, a world with plenty of mystery and exploration available, and ways to add runes, spells, and upgrades to your character to add and change the game play. This game has a lot to offer, without a doubt. However, the big thing for me is the 2D style of it. I loved games like Assassin's Creed, the Witcher 3, and Fallout 4; they had story, great character design and development, as well as great exploration within the world, and for me, the 3D style is a big deal. I like the design of the game, but with it being 2D I find myself wanting more in terms of how the world is presented. I am going to guess those who grew up and loved games like Sonic and Mario Bros will have a blast here; there is a great deal to like and have fun with. But for me, who loved and cherished games that simply had the different aspect of being 3D, I find this game not to my taste.

Your experience of playing Hollow Knight will likely depend on having completed many very similar games in the past. That's what the developers are are going for here: An old-school MV experience for arcade buffs, where you do exact moves with exact timing or die.

As someone who finished and enjoyed Ori and the Blind Forest, but doesn't normally go for arcade-style action, I couldn't get into Hollow Knight. It is a challenge for the challenge's sake. You start out knowing nothing about the world (no up-front exposition) and completing a challenge might reward you with a powerup or a storyline reveal, or it might not. This for me is the basic failing of the game: too often your "reward" is just moving on. Nope, I need more than that.

I'm not much of a reviewer, but I will say that this game is really great. It has really fun combat as you start collecting more abilities, allowing you to bounce around on top of enemies, juggling yourself, and it encourages you to play aggressively.

One example of the impressive design of the combat is that you have this meter that you can use to heal yourself, but it's mostly charged by attacking. That means that you can either stay back and dodge attacks, getting the occasional hit off, or you can go aggressive and allow yourself to heal much faster.

If you don't want to buy this game at full price, I would at least suggest picking it up on a sale or something. It's a really good game.

When I saw the "Overwhelmingly positive" this game was getting, I gave it a try because I thought there was no way it was going to disappoint. It was only when I started playing it that I just realized how hugely overrated it is, and the game itself is mediocre at best. Now that doesn't mean you won't like this game, it could be right up your alley, but I will tell you why I personally didn't like it and why you may want to avoid it. 

The game is, for a lack of a better word, boring. You spend most of your time walking around these deep cavern underground, and then occasionally you get these big exciting boss fights, and then it's back to walking around these boring tunnels, that to be honest, all look exactly the same. The pacing is terrible. And then when you're exploring and trying to find something to actually, you know, progress the story, you're just walking to the same place over and over again, back and forth, forth and back, and it's just monotonous. I know this is a key element to Metroidvanias, so you may think I just don't like Metroidvanias, but that isn't true. I enjoyed Ori and the Blind Forest, which is a Metroidvania, but it gets to its point, there's less backtracking and there's just more to keep me hooked, more variety in the gameplay too. 

You might be saying, "but you only have a little over 3 hours on this game, how can we believe your review?" But there's a reason I only played 3 hours. The game just had nothing to keep me coming back, I felt like I was forcing myself to play, waiting to start enjoying it. People will just say, "You have to wait for it to get good!" But I should not feel like I have to force myself to play a game I don't like to wait for when I enjoy it. I just see poor game design display here in a lot of ways. 

And there's just a few things here and there that just really prevent me from liking this game. The first one is how rarely you can save the game. You have to go to one of these benches, which are few and far between, and I know it's a game design choice, but oh my gosh it's some annoying game design. I feel way more compelled to a short play session in a game like Celeste, where you can save at every single screen. The other little thing that just annoyed me about this game is how you have to actually acquire a map, you don't just have one. This is not making the game better in any way, it's just making things more difficult for the player. I mean, this game has a massive map that is so easy to get lost in, you really need the map and I don't like that early game grind, but whatever. I mean there aren't the reasons I dislike the game, but I thought they should be mentioned. 

This game might be for you, but it's definitely not for me. I am still so confused how it's so unanimously liked.  This review is here to prevent people from spending their money on a game just because they think it'll be really good just because almost everyone likes it.

This is one of the most artistically creative games I've ever played, and it's a whole lot of fun to play.

At first.

It's also the most brutally difficult and unforgiving game I've ever played. It's not just "challenging", it's basically impossible after a certain point - maybe 1/4 of the way through? I'm guessing that less than 1% of the people who play this game will be able to beat it. The sole difficulty level of this game is what other games would call "nightmare mode".

It also has some very unfriendly game mechanics.
* Save spots are very few and far between, and none of them are near boss battle locations.
* There are no respawn spots (for death), so if you die, you wind up back at your last save spot.
* When you die, you lose all your money, and can only get it back by going back to the place where you died - the place that was so hard to get to that it killed you.
* Fast-travel spots are even more scarce than save spots, so you're going to be doing a TON of backtracking throughout the game.
* There are many places where you need pixel-perfect precision, timing, and reflexes to avoid insta-kill spikes.
* Boss battles are about 10x harder than the rest of the game.

I even used cheat mods so I could keep playing and seeing all the incredible areas of the game. Look for one called ModGod 1.3 once you can't take it anymore. First I gave myself infinite energy (so I could at least heal when I got a chance), then I had to increase the damage my weapon did (many times), but eventually the game got so hard that I had to use the clipping (move through walls) cheat to get past nigh-impossible jumping puzzles. I finally gave up after that.

There's also a mod that lets you teleport to any save spot or fast-travel location whenever you want, and I strongly recommend that you get that once you get tired of backtracking.

I REALLY wish I could recommend this game, because the artwork is incredible, the music is gorgeous, and it's fun to play until it becomes too difficult to play.

I play a lot of games. I grew up around a super nintendo, and though I loved that system a great deal, due to monetary limitations I was unable to play the classics until a good while later. One of the wonderful games I missed was the now legendary title "Super Metroid." Alongside the Castlevania series, these games created a genre unto themselves- the Metroidvania. Characterized by sprawling maps which are slowly rendered explorable by the ever expanding ablilities of your protagonist, I often find myself in awe of the worlds and the sense of exploration that these games bring to the table. Though I've played a number of these over the years, one has risen to the pinnacle of what this genre has to offer. Hollow Knight. 
   
     While veterans to the Metroidvania may not find a slew of new and inventive abilities in this title, what it DOES offer is an immersive, melancholic atmosphere, memorable characters, and a level of refinement that I've never seen in a Metroidvania. The opening few areas may be fairly linear, but you will quickly find that the map opens up, and few people will discover the same areas in exactly the same ways. Numerous entrances and exits to each new location lead to the feeling of a living, breathing world that is interconnected and never felt tedious to cross.
    
     The battle system is fairly simplistic in nature. You have a sword strike (referred to as a nail in this universe,) a healing ability, and several other abilities that drain from the same resource as your healing maneuver. Some of these are unlocked as the game progresses, but it leads to a risk versus reward scenario more often than not. Healing also takes a moment to start up, and if you are hit before completing your heal, it will be cancelled and you will take damage instead. Battles, especially those involving bosses, can be quite difficult, which leads to the expectation of mastering mechanics given to you, rather than overloading you with an exhausting amount of superflous hyper-specific attacks and manouvers that you need to remember. It can get frusterating for some, and I'll admit to getting frustrated with the difficulty from time to time myself- but never so much so that I didn't want to return. This game keeps me coming back for more.
     
     Team Cherry, the wonderful developers behind this game, have released four expansions for free to this game. These expansions include new charms (abilities that can be equipped), new bosses, and new amazing music. All of this has been seamlessly integrated into the base experience, and will not hinder any newcomers to the game. If anything, this is the best time to get into Hollow Knight than ever. If it isn't already obvious, I cannot reccomend this game highly enough. In my eyes, it is a masterpiece, and has become my favorite game of all time. For fifteen dollars? It's a steal. I had to buy it again on the Switch just to have it on more than one console. I also highly recommend the soundtrack. 
    
     To those reading, thank you for your time. To Team Cherry, thank you for crafting this exhilerating world. I look forward to the Hornet DLC  and any future games you have in store for us. To those on the fence- take the plunge. You won't regret it.

There is not much left to say that hasn't already been said, but I love absolutely everything about this game. It is one of the few that have left me hyped up for a sequel. When I bought this game I dove in with my expectations already very high, What I got out of it was so much more. 

It's not so easy to explain, but when games like these come out it's so inspirational to play through them and see what may have inspired the different aspects of these games. For instance, All of the enemies - including bosses - are completely unique and and fit with the themes well. The level design is perfect; extensive, very  detailed and interesting with memorable soundtracks to boot. 

There are secrets everywhere with hints of metroidvania exploration. However, the game retains it's own style. npcs and vendors have a plethora of personalities as well as enticing dialogue. It's always fun to talk to them and then come back after progressing to see what else they have to say. Play this game for 30 mins and see what you think. even for someone who isnt a fan of platformers the story itself is nothing to shy away from.

I went into this game completely blind. I heard the animations were hand drawn, and being an animation nerd I decided to buy it on that alone. I didn't regret it. 

The artstyle of this game isn't just amazing to look at, it helps tell the story. Rather than relying so on much on scattered notes and character dialogue, the story of Hollow Knight is told largely through the enviroment. And the enviroment is incredible and varied. You never know what's around the corner, and your curiousity to see everything there is is one of the many things that push you to keep playing. The juxtaposition of the extremly dark and lonely atmosphere (All helped along with a great soundtrack) with the cute bug designs and interesting NPCs you meet along the way made for a really unique enviroment to explore. 

I'm not much of a metroidvania player, and hard, punishing games aren't my thing. You will die in this game quiet a bit. It never feels like a cheap death though. The controls and movement feel fluid and natural. And when you're fighting one of the many, many bosses and mini-bosses, and finally win, it feels great. It feels even better when you find a new one and manage to beat them in one try. It's difficult, but satisfying. 

After having played it a while I can tell this is one of the most tightly designed games of all time. I can't even think of nitpicks. I still can't believe this game is under $20, it's better crafted and filled with more content and charm than most Triple-A games. There's a lot more I could praise but I'll keep it brief and spoiler-free. Just play it and experience it for yourself.
better than commiting suicide

I've heard amazing things about this game for a long time and I love metroidvanias so I was excited to see how the game played. I have to say I'm extremely disappointed with the game. There are multiple choices they made that baffle me. My first hour with the game was confusing because the map is very open with multiple exits in many rooms so I kept trying to check my map to get a feel of the area but you don't start out with one. That was a little annoying but really not that bad since I got the map soon after. However upon getting the map I was very confused. I saw multiple icons on the screen but nothing to tell me what they meant. I saw a skull that looked similar to my helmet so I assumed that was my player and tried navigating off of that for a few minutes but that only made me more confused because it wasn't moving. It turns out that for some reason in a game with a big of a map as this the developers decided not to include to ability to see where you are on it. I was so confused and the fact that my map wasn't updating as I explored only served to confuse my sense of where I needed to go more. I finally bought the map upgrades that automatically mark things like benches and stations on your map, which confused me because in every other game those are just base features not "upgrades". You even have to purchase markers so you can place some on the map yourself. I also got the upgrade that allows me to see where I am on the map and I was relieved that even though it had been frustrating before, that was behind me because I had that basic functionallity now. I was very upset when I realized its not even a permanent upgrade like the rest of the functionality on the map. You have to actually equip it, and it takes up a slot in your gear that's otherwise used for combat upgrades to make your player stronger. In the first place seeing your player on the map should just be part of the base functionality of the map considering how big this game is, but it also makes you chose between it and a combat upgrade. This really frustrated me. It felt like the game didn't have enough things for the player to buy so they stripped as much base functionality as they could and turned them in to purchesables or even worse: equpables. This killed my excitement over playing the game because I was so tired of struggling to get access to things this game desperately needs, but I had to work to get them or give something up to keep them. 

There are other mechanics that really frustrated me as well and just ruined my experience of playing the game which is a shame because its a beautiful looking game and one of my favorite genres. There were some times when I had fun but they were so far and few between the frustrating aspects of the game that I'm just uninstalling it because I feel like I'm just forcing myself to play it.

Some of the other things that made no sense to me were: 

1) Why when you get hit its so overdramatic. Sometimes I would accidently walk into an enemy that didn't even have attacks and my character would exploded into particle effects, the screen would go dark and shake like it was the second coming of Christ, and my controller would vibrate excessively. I already didn't want to get hit because healing is slow and hard to store up, so I was already annoyed when I would get hit but then on top of that I always thought I was dying because the effects were so extreme it felt like the game was shouting at me that I was in danger and needed to heal. Where in reality I had only lost the first of my hearts. I've never turned rumble off in a game before but I had to for hollow knight because I was getting so frustrated every time I got hit from the overload of negative feedback the game was giving me.

2) Why do the paths that you can't access because you don't have the right ability waste so much of your time? There were so many rooms that I would half explore or kill a couple of enemies and do some platforming before I was unable to progress due to not having the right ability yet. In the first area alone there are probably 6-8 paths like this that take 30-45 seconds to figure out you cant go that way. That's at the very least 3 minutes of thinking you found a new area and then being disappointed that you can't go that way and have to turn around. It also doesn't help that the map doesn't tell you anything about the rooms so it was hard to tell which areas I hadn't explored because I had gotten distracted or I just wasn't able to progress through yet. I wasted so much time revisiting rooms I had already been trying to find where to go that I actually had to look up a guide 3 times in the first area. Metroid does a much better job of communicating to the player where you can and can't go so its not nearly as hard to figure out where you need to be. First off most of metroids areas are locked behind doors with different colored blast shields that wont break when you shot them so you don't need to waste time exploring in that room if you couldn't progress through it anyway. Secondly, the map shows you icons for all the doors and the ones you don't have the ability to open are just marked as ??? so when you check the map you immediately know that you can't go there yet so you don't need to backtrack to it.

3) There were other things that got on my nerves but I don't want to spend more time on this review since this game wasted too much of mine already. I think I've only ever reviewed one game and it was because it was so phenomenal that I felt I owed it to the developers to write a good one. I'm writing this review because I don't see anyone else talking about how slow this game starts and how poorly designed some of its mechanics are so I feel like I have to. Maybe the game does get better but I don't want to waste more time forcing myself to play a game I don't enjoy and would rather go have fun in a game I know I'll enjoy from my list of games I have to play.

In the beginning, the bosses are fun, you take a few attempts, and you feel like you've gotten somewhere and enjoyed it. You might think I'm just bad at the game, but games that are intentionally suited for most audiences just trying to have fun shouldn't be that difficult. I understand maybe a rage game having difficult bosses is what a specific audience is looking for, but this game is supposed to be for many different types of people. I just can't get behind this one, incredibly overhyped.  Obviously the artists earned their pay here, but it's a really polarizing aesthetic, one that I just never enjoyed.  Music is atmospheric, but it's just that - I rarely actually pay attention to it or enjoy it.  Movement was a chore for a LONG time, way longer than it needed to be in this game.  There IS such a thing as too much backtracking - other games in the genre smartly push and pull you through areas and connect them to old ones in an organic way to open new pathways.  In this game it's just "Oh, you don't have that yet?  Tough luck.  Check back in a few hours."  What's worse is that the mechanism by which you're supposed to keep track of this is on the user end - the map definitely could have been more user-friendly here in telling you what kind of obstacle is the reason that you didn't unlock the most remote, desolate corner of the map.  It hugely inflates game time in a really bad way that's tedious and frustrating.  Also pretty much the entirety of the games dialogue is a huge waste of time - it developed the world in almost no useful way, almost purely ornamental.

The one redeeming quality this game has is boss fights.  When they're on, they nailed it.  Everything else is a trivial chore that I endured just to see another boss fight - it's literally the only thing getting me through this game.

This time of game might be for some folks, but it's definitely not for me.

I have found Hollow Knight to be an enjoyable game.  

The story starts with a text from a wannabe poet, then drops you into a strange world, a hellish world for those suffering from entomophobia. Leaving all jokes aside, the story starts out vague, becoming more clear as you explore the world.

The world is populated with insectoid creatures (nightmares for some). The overall design is cartoonlike, with each area of the game feeling distinct from one another, with some beautiful backgrounds, if you stop to look at them.

So what type of game is Hollow Knight? Well Hollow Knight is a Metroidvania, as such the gameplay is divided into three sections: exploration, platforming and combat. The areas, you explore, do not overstay their welcome. The platforming sections were good, I did not feel overwhelmed, the combat on the other hand was challenging. Some of the bosses will test your reflexes, but with the right charms (power ups) and knowing their moves you can succeed. I have no complaints with the controls, as the felt tight (I mainly played on a joystick).

I don't recommend it!

Then you go onto getting something called the Voidheart and face the true ending boss - which yet again is a mindfuck. 

Finish 1 ending get the "Hollow Knight" achievement and be done with it. I don't recommend this game to anyone if you have other games to play.

Hollow Knight is one of the best metroidvanias, but was killed by over-ambitioned devs.

[table][tr][th]

Difficulty   ★★★★★☆☆☆☆☆ (base story for casual gamers, rest for pure hardcore gamers)


Best things

* huge world with a lot of interesting places and content
* nice character ideas (for example Zote the Mighty)
* a lot of special things to discover (the financial lesson with the bank is a nice one)
* cool bossfights (25+ bosses) and a boss rush mode
* charm system is very vesatile to adapt to your playstile or to certain bossfights
* solid game for a kickstarter
* EDIT: my speedrun in under 120 minutes following a video with a lot of skipping was actually quite fun

Biggest flaws

* opening the inventory does not pause the game
* you loose your money permanently if you get killed before you can reobtain your dark soul
* the world is so huge that you get lost in the beginning without Cornifers maps
* checkpoints (benches) are often too far away from the bossfights (produces long travels between bossfight retries)
* the difficulty is swaying from casual directly to hardcore gamer, there is nothing in between
* the story telling is so chaotic and cut in too little pieces
* 2 out of 5 endings are not possible to reach for normal gamers

The two main flaws is the story telling and the difficulty balancing. Both is way off the course.  

In the first half I was not even realising why on earth my character is running around and doing stuff. There was neither a certain goal, nor a logical concept noticable. Probably I missed a lot, because of all the tiny story pieces you collect here and there. Don't ever expect the devs help you in putting them together. The basic game ending is very unspectacular, the other endings, besides extra special boss fights, are not much better, even if they try to give them a scent of epicness. The devs just failed, because they skipped a proper story telling totally.

As soon as you leave the trails of the story bosses, the game becomes really mean. The base difficulty is suitable for casual gamers. If things get too tough, collect better charms or more notches and come back later. Fair enough.
Some of the dream bosses (like Lost Kin), the NKG (Nightmare King Grimm) or Radiance was driving me crazy. Most bosses had a concept, if you get it and adapt to it you will win, but some just didn't. Too chaotic, no proper signs of which attack will be initiated next was rendering these fights very luck based if you have not very godlike reflexes. Even the jumping sequence in the White Palace with the asynchronous lance corridor (which was far away from any bench) was freaking me out.

Really, devs, I don't get it. Did you wanted to make a game for anybody or just for hardcore gamers? Make up your mind. There was nothing in between in your game. And for completionists like me it is a slap in the face if you cover up special endings behind impossible bossfights. And to point it out: it was just the "normal" mode I was playing atm! This is nothing anyone can enjoy and destroys any replayability. Even after I did it I felt no satisfaction at all, just bitterness. And hey, congrats devs, you are the first ones who actually managed to make me destroy one of my PS4 controller in anger. You owe me one, and this is absolutely no joke. 

This is why I cannot recommend this game to any person out there. 
Unless you are up to a lot of pain or want to watch half of the game on Youtube.

In 2017 a hollow knight came out. It was an incredibly well-done game, every smooth and perfect animation hand-drawn, the soundtrack was elegant, the gameplay was sharp and precise, and the price was perfect. Every character had things to say, there were layers upon layers to the subtle story, and new content was released every 2 months. The incredible thing about all this is that this game was made by 3 developers in Australia! The world within the game is gigantic, and the ability and talent of these three men is unprecedented. They kept close tabs with their community, and continue to be self-owned. 
	When the initial patreon for the game was filled, they had “stretch goals”, goals that essentially said “If you get x amount of extra money, we will add x”. The 2nd playable character stretch goal was reached, and they confirmed the character, Hornet, would be coming in a future update to the game. 
        They claimed that Hornet would be added as a playable character in the final DLC for the game. They also said they were working on another game, and that DLC would likely not come out for another 10 years.
	On valentine’s day, 2019, they announced their next game. A complete sequel to the first one, with Hornet as the protagonist. They had been working on this game in secret since the release of their first game, all the while releasing content for it! In an interview, they say they had started out with the first work on the Hornet DLC, and gotten very invested in it, and the project soon became too big to just be part of the initial game, so they made it a brand new one. 
	I still am staggered by this team. I don’t understand how these three 25-30 year old men can simultaneously add content to a masterpiece of a game, and at the same time be working on a side project that accidentally becomes too big for its boots! A side project that looks just as good, if not better than the original! As you can probably tell, I love these games, and am very hyped for this newest addition to their world. Would 100% recommend, this is my all time favorite 2-D game.

A good example is when trying to fight some of the flying enemies while over the acid pools in the second area. I felt like I was being punished for trying to defend myself with the knockback shoving me away from the ledge. At other times when I wanna abuse an enemies opening with my nail I felt like I should be able to mash the button and reward myself with a lot of hits rather than only being able to usually just get one in. I'd rather be able to risk it just to get a few more hits in and beat myself up because I got to greedy, rather than have to deal with the sluggish swings of the nail. 

My final gripe with the game is the health/power system I don't know exactly what you would call it. I am used to the idea of having a finite set of health regen, it being via an estus flask or simple reusable health potion. I really wish I could have enjoyed this game more, a lot of work went into it and it does show. But for me I just can't get over what I personally consider to be flaws with the game.

Hollow knight is a metroidvania game with the theme of insects being front and centre. It is probably the best modern example of the genre to date.

Before any of the review stuff, I need to say something. Originally, I was never going to play this game, I didn't like the art style and I thought it looked a bit too simple, then I bought it on sale a while back, deciding to give it a fair shot. Hollow Knight didn't just win me over, it has become my favourite metroidvania title of all time. I am addicted to the genre, and out of them all, none have challenged me and entertained me so well, to a point where I actively spent twelve hours without food or water playing.

Hollow knight's story is a sombre, easy to grasp tale. The insect kingdom of hollownest is dying, a plague has swept through the land, driving everyone who gets infected insane before taking control of them and slowly mutating them, filling them with an orange puss like substance. You are the knight, a small, voiceless warrior who arrives in hollownest and begins the long, slow process of releasing seals that keep the source of the plague locked away. The reasons for why you're doing this, what the source is and so on is all explained in small tid-bits, with a complete picture forming at the very end.

The gameplay is your standard metroidvania affair, slowly exploring a large, ever expanding map while obtaining new abilities to open up new routs, slowly getting stronger over time by getting colletables and gear. But, Hollow Knight controls are very tight, you always have full control of the knight and he will do every action you desire near instantly. Every major upgrade you get (aside from one) drastically opens up your moveset, even the classic ones like the dodge and the double jump feel so much more liberating in hollow knight than they do in other titles.

It also introduces badges, which you can equip a limited amount of, depending on how many notches you have. These range from the simple, like increased health, to the more game changing, like causing damage to enemies you dodge through or giving you massive amount of health, but preventing you from regenerating any of it outside of checkpoints. My personal favourite set up was to have increased damage, and all the minion spawning badges, so i could focus entirely on dodging and let them kill bosses for me.

The areas in Hollow Knight are all very distinct from each other, both visually and "gimmick-aly". I mentioned the art being very simple earlier, i was very wrong on my initial assumption. The backgrounds are lovingly detailed, a personal favourite being the capital city where it's always raining, but every building is designed after insects. The actual enemies, hazards and main character on the other hand are very simple by comparison, which might seem negative at first, but it means you'll never mistake yourself, or your enemies for the background, and even then, despite being simple on the surface, there are some wonderfully realized enemies that could only be created with this simple art style.

The soundtrack is full of sombre pieces that set the tone for whatever area they are used in, really painting a bleak picture to match the overall art style and vibe the game is going for. It's full of string instruments, and i can't really remember any tracks that used brass instruments at all, and only a few that featured piano.

Overall, Hollow Knight isn't just the best example of a metroidvania game, but it's probably the perfect one to begin with to. It has tight controls, an engaging world, a unique look and a smooth difficulty curve that can, and will ramp up to brutally hard if you ask it to. I love this game, and strongly recommend it to anyone with a passing interest.

Side note, when I say this game can be punishing, I spent 6 hours on a single boss, I lost track of how many times I died. But i came away satisfied and feeling like it was still a very well designed fight. nightmare king grimm is not for the faint of heart, but you'll feel like a god after beating him without taking a single hit.

This game is...something, for sure. It has good parts, but overall this game is genuinely unbalanced and unfairly difficult.

[h1] The Good Parts [/h1]
The art, the music, the story, the characters, absolutely phenomenal. This is what drew me in at first, because the game looks so pretty and enjoyable in trailers.

The platforming is AWESOME and i love the skills you can get to help with that and open up new areas. The platforming is easily my favorite part of this game.

Yeah that's about it, I really disliked every other aspect of this game.

[h1] The Bad Parts [/h1]
Holy MOLY this game is WAY too difficult. Every fight except against basic enemies expects way too much from the player. you get barely any health to do every boss fight and you CANNOT HEAL DURING THEM. Okay, well, you technically can, but none of the bosses i encountered let you even get close to being able to do it. There is a power you can get that puts up a shield around you while you're healing, and at first i was ecstatic to know i could actually do something about not being able to heal during fights....except it doesn't work. I dont know if it's just bugged or something, but the shield does literally nothing. Enemies can still pass through it and can still shoot projectiles at you, it's as if you don't have a shield at all. Not healing would be fine if it weren't for the fact that bosses completely overwhelm your senses and bombard the screen with bullet-hellish attacks at every moment. Not to mention your attacks are short, deal minimal damage, and are really clunky to use because of getting pushed back whenever you hit something. I mean, I had to try for a full 2 hours OVER AND OVER AGAIN at the first hornet fight, which happens very early in the game.

I could EVEN possibly look past the glaring flaws in combat if the exploration wasn't equally as frustrating. The game does not tell you where to go, how to get where you want to go, and very often blocks you entirely from going to most places. To even GET to these blocked off areas you need special abilities that you have NO IDEA how or where to get, or even that they exist at all. It's like playing an open-world game but you have partial blindness the entire time. In addition, you only get a save point every few hours, so if you die then you have to go ALL THE WAY BACK, likely to just perish again. It genuinely feels like the game is actively trying to prevent you from exploring.

[h1] Conclusion [/h1]
I really enjoyed this game at first. But the more I played, the more i dreaded opening up the game and trying just one more time. Which especially sucked because my sister was watching me play this and was really excited to see me complete it. Eventually though, I had to tell her, "I can't complete this game. I know you really like watching me play games and were especially excited for this one, but I have stopped enjoying this game and no longer feel any reason to continue playing it." Please do not buy this game unless you have an extraordinarily high tolerance level.

to reiterate, just buy Ori instead.

[h1] My honest review of Hollow Knight [/h1]

The [b]best[/b] metroidvania game I have played ever. The [b]fluidity[/b] of gameplay and the [b]stunning artwork[/b] really is what makes this game [b]remarkable[/b]. Honestly, I would have bought this game for a 60$ pricetag, because of how much content I received for my 10$ worth. The DLC is even better that is not only just an expansion but a way for you to sharpen your combat skills in the game. I have beaten the game 6 times and acquired all the endings except for the Mr.Shroom cameo ending. I [b]enjoyed every bit of the game[/b] and truly think it's [b]worth your money[/b]. Even if you have never played metroidvania games before this game is awesome. I tried Ori and the Blindforest and when the 2 games are put side by side I think that hollowknight is much better. Ori is better in terms of platforming, but Hollowknight's story, game mechanics, and visuals really captivated me and made me play for a long amount of time. I think I'll play this game for a long time. [b] [i]Thank you Team Cherry for creating a game like this[/i]. [/b]

[h1] [ UPDATE ] [/h1]

After [u]earning every single achievement[/u] in the game and [u]over 75+ hours in the game[/u], I can affirm that this was one of the [b]best purchases[/b] I have ever made on steam. The game was [b]inricately designed[/b] with a lot of [b]thought and detail[/b]. You know that the game is good when you as the player gets frustrated for dying, or failing an attempt on a challenge and the game still [b]makes you want to play more[/b]. I have not had this feeling for a while, and hollow knight gave me the [b]best experience in a 2d metroidvania platformer[/b] I could ever have. I enjoyed every bit of it, and will continue playing the game after exhausting it of all the challenges it throwed me. I am excited for the future of this game and for what else Team Cherry plans to release in the future.

This game hates it's players, and they hate themselves too. 

Everything it does gameplay-wise, is done against the player to make it more unleasant to play. Just some examples:
- Hitboxes are wonky as fuck, a lot of the times the visual sprites of attacks miss the player's character clearly, are counted as a hit. This is the exact opposite for enemies: many fringe cases when attack barely hits, and i mean that as the attack animation clips enemy sprite, the attack misses
- Movement, while most of the time is pleasant, fails to live up to the challenges that require *perfect* movement control. There is a delay before dashing, jumping and double jumping. I cannot count how many times i died while in animation for double jump was already playing (the wings effect) or after hearing the sound cue for hitting a pogo jump, but the game failed to account for it in time. Invisible dash signals being invulnerable to damage with making the character black, and *STILL* there are frames where judging by the sprite you should be immune to damage, but aren't (this is true for both entering and exiting the dash). Every time this happens, and it happens more the harder the challenges get, You feel like you've been cheated.
- Some RNG-dependent fights where there are possible patterns of attacks for enemies that make it simply impossible to dodge the attack, which makes it especially frustrating to fail when you couldn't even control this.
- To locate yourself on the map, you need to equip a charm that uses up one of the slots or 'notches' for upgrades that could be used to make character stronger. This means, that the game punishes inexperienced players for not knowing the map, which makes the beginning of the game harder for the first time players. This doesn't make sense, it's a total reverse of the priciple that you should make the game more challenging for more experienced players. By all means this should be a built-in functionality of the map, it's not rewarding higher skill- the 'fury of the fallen' charm rewards skill. This is just an unnecessary obstacle that makes it more frustrating to play.

This obviously doesn't mean it's impossible to master, obviously you can get skill to intitively feel where the actual hitboxes are and to feel out and anticipate the unfairness of the game- proof of that you can find on youtube where experienced players beat unimaginable challenges they set themselves, which only prooves my point that anyone who enjoys this game is a masochist (including me).

The fascinating lore, amazingly creative worlbuilding and the art is breathtaking. Every area has its own unique and beautiful vibe, enviromental storytelling is really genius and it's fulfilling to piece together the story from little scraps from each character. Which pains me even more, that there is not a journal for lore (there is one for enemies, a bestiary) which means you cannot find dialogues anywhere except for the one single time you hear them. Not to mention some parts of the lore that unbeknownst to the player, lock themselves out after some triggers.

This game could've been so much more enjoyable to discover and explore and learn about, and instead every piece of progress feels like crawling through a barbed wire naked. For this reason, this lost potential, i can't leave a postitive review, but for anyone who hate themselves enough to still find enjoyment in it's beauty despite the contempt it has for the player, highly recommend.

Hollow knight achieved a feat of which few games manage to. Its combat may not be the best one in gaming and its music may not be the best of all time, but it manages to hit such a magnificent balance of quality across all of its fields. I think the weakest part of it is its start. Before obtaining the dash the game can feel even a little too easy and slow at times, but it easily manages to redeem itself after the player encounters the mantis lords, and from then on the game never stops to lose the players interest. Its fun combat, fantastic music and charming characters all assist in the solemn yet elegant adventure that the knight is about to embark on, exploring its intricate world and intriguing, dark story line. Hollow Knight is an absolutely gorgeous game, with incredible design, animation, storytelling, and more. It's an experience just to wander around this beautiful, destroyed world, and learn what led to its fall.

It is also one of the most unnecessarily frustrating gameplay experiences I've ever been subjected to.

I'm going to be 100% honest: I think good game design concepts are wasted on Soulslike games, because the people who can appreciate them can't deal with the difficulty, and the people who can deal with the difficulty don't appreciate the design.

But even if we take the difficulty out of the equation, there's parts that are just plain...bad. Gameplay/control-design wise.

The amount of backtracking one is expected to do, not just to get back to bosses after a loss (travel in this game is an absolute nightmare), but to get back to areas you might not have been able to previously reach without upgrades as well. There is also very little in the way of explaining what you might need to pass by a certain obstacle - I am not saying the game should specifically say "you need X item found in Y location before you can pass this" but maybe even a breadcrumb in the right direction would be helpful. Something like "your wings are not strong enough to pass this place" or "you must strengthen your bond with the Void first", things that don't take away from the atmosphere but would still be a step in the right direction. 

The controls themselves end up being VERY finicky (was this designed with controllers in mind, just like Dark Souls? I have a very strong suspicion it was) to the point that, a move you pulled off not moments ago is for some reason not registering the second time, and thus, you take damage as you mess up the pinpoint perfect platforming demands. 

The most galling thing by far is the fact that nail-bouncing movement is an essential part of exploration and continuation in this game (Fungal Wastes in particular comes to mind and me spending well over an hour or more trying to figure out how to get to the next platform with the purple bouncing mushrooms), yet there is absolutely NO TUTORIAL on how to do so in the entirety of the game unless one stumbles blindly into it or looks it up somewhere else. I should not have to go looking online in third-party walkthroughs for base gameplay mechanics, and a mechanic that does not even work consistently 95% of the time, at that.

And this is just on the base difficulty. I have no idea how people manage the patience to try and play this on Steel Soul, or even just to speedrun this. It's so. Tedious. It's pretty, but it's tedious.

For what it's worth, too: I don't hate all soulslike Metroidvanias. I played and 100%-ed Blasphemous, which is similar to Hollow Knight in gameplay mechanics. Was it difficult? Yes. Especially beating Esdras of the Anointed Legion with no Bile Flasks. My FUCKING god that bossfight is a nightmare. And yet I had a lot of fun with Blasphemous and would absolutely play it again, even with the nightmare of Esdras, because somehow, Blasphemous managed to get its game design right and not make it needlessly frustrating.

I would love to see Hollow Knight adapted into a larger franchise, ala an animated series or something. You could not pay me to willingly play this game again, however, at least not until Team Cherry takes some notes on how to improve their base mechanics and gameplay.

It's a bit hard to fully get into this game, but once you do, I guarantee you will get lost in its world. Hollow Knight is absolutly gorgeous. The kingdom of Hallownest is huge and there are calm and soothing areas as well as uneasy and creepy ones. The music accentuates the game's visual style perfectly. 
The gameplay is precise and fleshed out and just at the right level of difficulty. Yes, it can be very challenging but it's always fair and the difficulty makes beating a boss so much more satisfying. The only thing that can be a bit frustrating is the backtracking but it didn't bother me too much as you're seeing more of the game's beautiful world.

It starts slow and boring. So much that when I bought it, years ago, I've dropped it in twenty minutes.

Attacks are basic, extremely short range, uninteresting.
You acquire powers : "charms". They feel underwhelming and it's my understanding they can break and you need to repair them.

I've fought a few bosses and it's only a matter of patience and dodging. It bores me.  I know the game difficulty raises a lot later but I don't want "difficult", I want "interesting".

But in the end it's not even the boredom that makes me want to stop : it's the lack of respect of my time.
I've found a "parkour" where I have to guess the position of monster I need to jump on out of the screen. I've failed several times because I can't figure the timing. The issue is that when I die I have to do a trip of several minutes to try again.

The fact I speak of respect of my time is a good indication on how much I despise moving around on that world. Moving around, exploration, is usually my favorite part in a game.
Not here. What I've seen of the world is bleak, contrast is poor, I often can't distinguish background and ground. Sometimes I don't even see an enemy before it walks into me.

This is it, I'm calling it. Time of death : 8 hours and 30 minutes.

Hollow Knight is probably the most perfect game that you possibly shouldn't play. It is a search adventure Metroidvania game that might eclipse the greats in the genre simply because it really plays up those aspects--searching and adventure--more than any other game like it. However, it is purposefully designed to be a Soulsborne level of intense pain for newcomers. It took me 36 hours to get a 106% completion good-ending. The first 15 hours were absolutely terrible, and made me put down the game for days at a time, to the point that I was going to request a refund at hour 2 and only just missed the chance by a few minutes.

This is my favorite genre of game and I love a challenge, but so many minor design choices added up to a frustrating disrespect for the player's time, like:

* Wonky platforming thanks to the recoil on hitting an enemy.
* Currency that explodes out of enemies and tends to get lost so that you're not rewarded for your kill.
* A corpse-hunt mechanic so that you are usually obligated to retread dangerous territory even if you would rather backtrack and explore that alternate route you saw before.
* A real lack of consistent design choices on how to know when you're about to walk into a deadly boss battle.
* Traversal that never feels feels fast or convenient, and it is certainly never safe, despite all the interconnected-ness.

You are going to retread areas over and over when you die or get lost, and you're never going to feel rewarded for the time you spent. And I'm referring to the first 10% of the game before you get the ability to climb walls, which is the actual point when the whole game map is supposed to open up to you. My first 10 hours were that 10% of the game. That is fundamentally screwed up. Half of the mechanics I complain about above are known issues, because the designers put in a charm system that lets you cancel the effect of recoil and scattered money. But if you play with those quality of life charms turned on, you have to give up other abilities instead.

You can read other reviews to see that this game is gorgeous visually and aurally; that it has a rich lore, great combat, and heaps and heaps of content. However, even though I will go back and play it more, I want people who have sat on the fence for three years to really think about those two words I used: frustration and disrespect. I have become accustomed to the mechanics enough that they don't kill me anymore, and I know now that the sense of being lost is a side-effect of the  structure that Metroidvania's use and that this game has made more non-linear than ever before.

Now if you decide to play the game, you can't say you weren't warned. I simply needed to vent because the first half of my playthrough was so very not fun. If you do power through, you'll have a game experience that is entirely your own, and that is exciting. The order that you find new areas, new items, story elements, etc. is all very open-ended. That is my favorite thing about this game, but it may come at the cost of your sanity.

Tight controls, good boss fights, varied enemies, and above all else, a fantastic environment are what set [I]Hollow Knight[/I] apart and make it so worthy of praise.

[I]Hollow Knight[/I] tells the story of a wandering knight, drawn into a city that had already fallen, not to war or political strife, but to infection. At first, the knight has no goal but to explore yet as they do they run into mysteries, gods, and signs that they themselves aren't what they seem.

There's a great amount of secrets in [I]Hollow Knight[/I], and while it's a long game, about 30 hours, by the end it still feels like there's more fun to be had. It doesn't get boring, basically. The environmental and sound design is incredible and plays a big part in keeping the game fresh. The soundtrack fits the environments well and all songs are quite varied from one another. In general, the somber tracks are best - songs like 'Sealed Vessel' or 'Kingdom's Edge' really capture an ethereal vibe and do wonders at contributing to the story lines their associated with. Other aspects of the sound are great, most notably the gibberish that NPCs and enemies use. 'Bapanada' and 'AHHHHHHHH HONDAAAA' are... strangely endearing?

The general gameplay is your relatively standard fare with options between either melee or magic, however the degree of maneuverability and responsiveness of the controls makes for some often-stylish combat. Many times it's required, like the 'Sisters of Battle' fight later in one of the DLCs. It's a combination of attacks having self-knockback, a double jump with startup frames, an infinite wall jump, and a strong dash mechanic that makes this possible. It's not rare to jump down on an enemy, bounce off of them, attack another once then dash through them and cling to a wall to reset your jump counter to do it all over again. The enemy spawns and projectile spacing is handled with care to make this possible. Surprisingly, the game never feels spammy even with multiple hazards moving around you.

Overall, I think [I]Hollow Knight[/I] has a perfect blend of gameplay, production, and story that serve to make it a classic that will be remembered for years to come.

Quite frankly one of the finest platforming games I have ever played. If you like action games, adventure games, or the oft labeled "Metroidvania", you should definitely check this one out.

Long story short: A masterfully crafted Metroidvania.

Hollow Knight is a side-scrolling platformer. You hack & slash enemies and explore a sprawling underground city while finding iconic metroidvania upgrades like double-jump and dash. Metroidvanias have existed for a while now, so expect the classic tropes from the genre: blocked passages, secrets, secrets inside secrets, puzzles and of course, some good old platforming jumps.

The gameplay isn't exactly new yet Hollow Knight does it in an elegant way. Everything you see in this game is hand-drawn so playing this game is like watching a moving painting. The game's intro is bit bland on the color palette side, however the deeper you go, the more colorful it gets.

The music complements the game very well. It's so good, I bought the OST. In these tracks, I hear the likeness of the Squaresoft dream team. It gives off a certain nostalgic feel that will remind you of Final Fantasy, Chrono Trigger, Vagrant Story and Castlevania: SOTN, just to name a few.

Combined together, both the art and music creates an atmospheric density I've never seen before. I think it's impressive that a game that looks cartoony on the outside can still manage to take itself seriously and deliver the most refreshing metroidvania experience in the longest time. Don't let the childish-looking nature of the game fool you, HK has many challenges waiting ahead that will give veterans of the genre a run for their money.

My only gripe with HK is its difficulty spike at certain areas, however, most of these areas are optional. Looking at Coliseum of Fools and White Palace (Super Hollow Boy aw yeah).

Most definitely recommended. I rate this game a solid 9/10, loose 10/10. A masterpiece for an old genre that needed that kind of fresh air.

now as a preface, i own this game on both switch and pc, my pc is just a potato when it tries to run it so i have almost 20 hours clocked in on the switch version, and also as there is no maybe rating just a yes or no reccomedation i decided to do yes as my personal score for the game was a 7/10 due to some irritating issues i encountered.
so here's my scores;

music- 10/10
this game takes a lot of inspiration from jrpg's past and features an excellent score that perfectly fits the levels you traverse

map design- 8/10
the map itself is wonderfully designed 2d platforming, however, theres a number of issues such as safe surfices being just close enough to hazards to damage you, it's only really noticable on 2 of the maps in my experence though but when you notice it, boy is it frustrating.

mechanics- 2/10
unfortunately this was the biggest let down in the game, while changeable, the game unfortunately has a habit of binding everything to a few keys which makes some moves difficult to use, or down right useless in every situation due to the akward binding. Another major issue with the game is imunity frames sadly this game tries to be both dark souls, and a classic platformer akin to castlevainia. what it fails to understand however from these two games is that when you're in total hitstun and in your immunity frame wall spikes shouldnt register till you've had a moment to try and reorient your positoning. Because of this, a lot of your deaths later on feel alot less legitimate, more feeling like the game was just screwing you instead of you actually failing to avoid an obstical.  Lastly the game has some issues in terms of collison detection for its wall jumping mechanic, ie you'll do the animation for a wall jump on non clingable surfaces causing you to fall.
now with that you're probably wondering what do they do right in mechanics? well the game features an interesting death mechanic akin to Nier automata/demon souls, where you lose your SOUL gauge and currency on death if it is not banked, making an interesting risk v reward scenario. another interesting (And somewhat well made) mechanic is the SOUL gauge itself acting as healing item, and special move bar. Lastly the powers, barring one or two (looking at you nail arts) the powers are absolutely fantastic in this game and actually feel like powerups and not just useless trinkets you;ll use in the respective level and never touch again. Lastly, one thing it draws right from prior metroidvania type games is the exploration, for a 2d game this game is huge, and will take some time to 100% even with all power ups and charms. 

replayability- 8/10 the game is fairly replayable after you've 100% it, i started a second run through trying to beat it without upgrading my blade once myself, but i cant say anymore on this aspect without spoiling the game

story- 10/10 The game follows that wonderful halflife/metroid style of story telling where you only get the story if you are looking for it, but if you do, it's quite satisfying, but that's all i can say on it without spoiling it.

so as a tldr; Hollow knight is a wonderful game, but it does require you to be a fan of the metroidvania-darksouls esq titles inorder to really enjoy it, and if you're easily frustrated by sometimes wonky and unbalanced mechanics this may not be the game for you. If not however, definately think of picking up a copy if for nothing else then the story and some expertly crafted music.

Hollow Knight is a triumph of the genre.  Having never been a fan of the metroidvania type games this one stood out to me. To begin, the artistic style is absolutely gripping, from the satisfying nature of the animations to the atmospheric backgrounds, the visualization of this fantasy world is anything but disappointing. 

The beginning of the game highly recommends a controller, but for those keyboard gamers I completed the game with a mouse and keyboard without running into issues. 

The sound is beautiful: intense during boss battles, or ambience for exploration, each zone is accompanied by a score that does nothing but add to the experience. The sound never once got in the way of the game nor was it ever distracting.

There is no shortage of a map to explore; initially the size is overwhelming but once you learn the routes and collect the map then it will become more familiar to the player. Backtracking feels very natural due to the immersive nature of the game.  There is no shortage of collectibles, either. I have yet to collect every item in the game, but I will proceed to the task with joy. 

The difficulty of the game scales in a relatively small sense. The player will gain new powers and abilities, but in no particular order are they necessary. The game I found was most enjoyable at the midway to late game point where the player has access to all of the movement upgrades and can ninja around the levels with ease. The sense of difficulty is unflinching, however with the starting area actually housing significantly more difficult enemies near the halfway mark, but not with simply bigger health bars, I mean the enemies become physically different and the area has some elements changed. 

The combat is unflinching and at times impossible, but this is an aspect of the game I cherish. The combat becomes mildly easier with upgrades in game, but the effective implementation of them and the skill cap is still high. The player  could complete the game without any upgrade usage beyond the key upgrades if their skill was high enough. 

The story and NPCS are all memorable. The story is told with the show don't tell methodology and is one of my favorite parts of the game along with the unforgettable NPCS, it's very easy to lose oneself in the long dead Hollownest. 

The only gripes I had with this game were easily enough pushed out by my praise of the good elements that I won't even bring them up here. I thoroughly enjoyed every facet of this game, and it's an experience that I won't soon forget and I highly reccomend.

Near-perfect metroidvania with a great world to explore and simple yet deep combat mechanics. Crafted by a surprisingly small team given its size and consistent delivery of polished, engaging levels and bosses. Adding to that the immersive world and intricate lore, it becomes a special and well-rounded journey that anyone even remotely interested in this type of games should experience. Thank you Team Cherry!

Some highlights:
+ Some of the best 2D boss-fights ever designed, with top-notch art, music and flow. These reach their peak in the late game, also becoming quite challenging.
+ Vast world with memorable places, a thick atmosphere, and a unique art direction.
+ Late game platforming segments that offer a spectacular challenge connected to the narrative.
+ Engaging exploration mechanics lost with most modern games (i.e. incomplete maps and lack of a player tracker, only in the early game).

To keep in mind, all of the above points can double as a downside for many players (experiences such as dying repeatedly or occasionally getting lost should be expected).
