[h1]The Metroidvania we've all been waiting for[/h1]

Hollow Knight is the truest, most natural progression of the Metroidvania genre to date, the logical endzone every indie game strives to reach, and the crossroads of many gameplay philosophies first charted and set into motion by games such as Dark Souls, Super Metroid, Ori and the Blind Forest, and many, many more. If that sounds like high praise, it is, and deservedly so. Hollow Knight packs in more creativity, discovery, gameplay refinements, challenge, and value than most AAA $60 dollar games, and as far as I can tell by a team of only [b]four indie developers[/b]. Not only that, but the game has received a steady stream of excellent, free DLC additions with still more on the way to keep players coming back. To top it off, at only [i]fifteen dollars[/i] and [b][i]ten dollars on sale[/i][/b] this game is an absolute thievery and it is a [b]crime[/b] for you not to experience the crafted experience developer Team Cherry has laid out for you. At 34 hours myself and nearing 100% completion on my first save I feel like I can safely review this game, but also like the game still has a few more lessons to impart to me, a couple more well earned deaths in store, and at least one or two secrets still hiding behind a cracked wall or breakaway floor for me to unveil. I could (and did) write for hours about all the genius, deliberate, and nuanced achievements Hollow Knight manages to cram into its little indie package, but at currently over 22 thousand reviews, I will do this game a severe injustice and just sum it up briefly for you.

[h1]Pros[/h1]

-The gameplay is simple but subtlely complex. Your limited combat options expand inwardly to give you many, many options to approach any fight. Factor in different builds with the unlockable charm upgrades and you've got a game that truly lets you play it how you want.

-The animation and visual design is top shelf. You've never seen a world like Hollow Knight's. The denizens of this place are all insects or mushrooms or generally things you would not expect yourself to become as endeared to as you no doubt will. The different areas are dead or disgusting or unnverving or all three, and that's okay. Not every game has to be rainbows and sunshine, and Hollow Knight will make you revel in its creepy crawly world. 

-The soundtrack lends itself well to the game world, story, and gameplay. The soundtrack never takes center stage, but it compliments the gameplay and atmosphere which is all it needs to do. There's nothing upbeat or catchy here, and it would be misplaced if that was so. The game is desolate, grim, and unforgiving, and it has a soundtrack to match. 

-There is such a wealth of content in the game it's hard to believe that you paid only $15 (most likely $10) for it. The world expands as you explore, and you will be continually shocked at how far it continues to unravel, and these are big, fully fleshed out zones with tons of new enemy types, bosses, and secrets. All in all I think there are 28 unique bosses, and extra hard variants of those bosses for those who are gluttons for punishment. The amount of value you get for such a seemingly small little indie game is astounding.

-The challenge is real. For those who welcome a game that pushes back, and are willing to be patient and learn from their mistakes, Hollow Knight is what you've been waiting for. For those who rush into battle half prepared and will hurl themselves at an obstacle repeatedly while ignoring what the game is trying to communicate to you, Hollow Knight will sort you out until you yield to its gameplay vision.

[h1]Cons[/h1]

-The tradional staples of Metroidvania games are all present, with all the trappings you may love or hate. Within the first few hours after getting a few necessary items and upgrades, the game essentially opens up in all directions which will inevitably lead you to many areas you just can't access. For some this is a draw of Metroidvanias, but for all the game philosophy progressions and logical improvements to the genre that it incorporates from many other well thought out games, I am surprised it sort of just left the idea of the map at the pass. You purchase icons for your map at the store which will mark things of interest automatically, but it would have been helpful if I could manually mark things myself that the game doesn't provide icons for like in many dungeon crawling games such as Legend of Grimrock. Marking save points and vendors is helpful, but was I really expected to remember that one ledge I couldn't reach 15 hours ago? Some would argue this is part of the fun of Metroidvanias, the retreading of old areas and rediscovery of areas you thought impassable previously, but I would have liked Hollow Knight to apply its forward thinking to the map in more than just purchasable icons at least as an option for players.

-The story. This is going to be where those who have played the game either vehemently disregard this review, or quietly agree with me. The story takes not a page but a whole chapter from Dark Souls' book in its subtle storytelling. Yes, there is a story that will be told to you (whether or not you understand or enjoy it will vary), but there is a much larger story hidden vaguely throughout the game world. I feel that when I replay it I will have a firmer grasp on what things said earlier meant now with context, but piecing together vague quotes from stone slabs and contextless jabber from random insects is difficult for me to get behind. As a concept, subtle background storytelling is something I love, but there has to be a middle ground. Many players, after finishing the game with little to no understanding of what was achieved, had to resort to online discussions to get some sort of closure on the game they just completed. This is another wholly subjective point as many players enjoy that sort of "community deduction" aspect of the story and lore. It really is up to you whether or not this is something you are going to find enjoyable. If it sounds like an interesting way to convey the game world and story to you, then you are probably safe. If you are more the type who would like to play a game by themselves and get a full grasp on the plot and lore without any need for discussion or scrounging the game world for answers, do not expect the story of Hollow Knight to do much for you. 

[h1]Conclusion[/h1]

Hollow Knight is probably one of those games that you can watch a trailer and tell, quite honestly, whether or not you will enjoy or despise it. Hollow Knight is not a game to play if you want to relax. Those types of gamers are completely justified in wanting that experience, but this is not it. Hollow Knight is not a game that is going to give itself over to you easily. You are going to have to work for it. Gamers who want a game to have fun and breeze through are also justified in desiring that experience, but this is not it. Hollow Knight is an exacting, challenging game full of unforgettable areas, visuals, characters, and enemies. If you push, it will push back, but it is not insurmountable, and for those willing to commit to it you will find an amazing experience absolutely deserving to be ranked among the very best games on Steam, and in general. I'll quite frankly say that it is personally up there with Super Metroid and Castlevania: SotN. If you have any interest whatsoever in any aspect of this game and think you would enjoy it, please, do yourself a favor and try this game. If 4 guys can make a game like this, it deserves to be played by everyone. Don't miss out.

I'm sure many people will disagree with me for my take. I don't think Hollow Knight is a bad game. I enjoy Metroidvanias, and Hollow Knight has one problem that ruined my enjoyment of the game. New areas aren't locked behind progression, and difficulty doesn't scale consistently since progression is non-linear. This is all fine on its own, but it meshes terribly with the Shade recovery mechanic. Normally in these kinds of games if you stumble into a difficult area and die, you can just leave that zone for now and try a different way. However, instead now I have to try and get back to where I was, or lose progress, neither of which are fun options. The Shade recovery just clashes so harshly with their open non-linear approach. I got so frustrated just with exploring because the game was punishing me for it, but then punishing me again if I wanted to try a new approach on respawn. I don't understand how this feature improves a non-linear game, it certainly didn't improve the experience for me, and in fact made the game too frustrating for me to enjoy. Other than that, the game is beautiful, the ambience and atmosphere is great, the controls are good. I just am hung up on the Shade thing and can't get over it. I wish I liked it, I really do.

TLDR
If you're looking for a metroidvania get this game, i imagine its one of the best.

But if you're looking for a game with souls like mechanics, steer clear of hollow knight and go for a different game. 2d Recommendations : salt & sanctuary or grime. 3d Recommendations: Remnant: From the ashes, Hellpoint or the surge 1/2.

Hollow Knight is a Metroidvania, that differentiates itself from other Metroidvania not only through its unique artstyle and it's gloomy world, but most importantly thorugh the fact that instead of the main character being an epic hero-type character à la Samus Aran, you play as the Hollow Knight (makes sense, given the title of this game) or simply „the Knight“, who is a vessel filled with void like many others in this world.

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=1504191477

Gameplay-wise, this game is one of the best of its kind, being very responsive, smooth and rewarding. As already mentioned in the beginning, you play as a tiny Hollow Knight, journeying through seemingly endless worlds, fighting gruesome enemies and bosses and collecting upgrades that help you progress through blocked areas, where, you guessed it, more enemies wait for you. It's the classic Metroidvania formula, but it's executed brilliantly and makes for tons of hours of fun. While there is a ceratin difficulty to this game, it's always to a point where it doesn't become too ridiculous to handle and most importantly it always feels fair.

The storytelling of this game is, unlike the gameplay, very unconventional. Instead of trying to explain the plot to you by showing you flashy cutscenes or having characters talk about exactly who they are and what their motives and plans are, the game lets you find small pieces of lore hidden in dialogue or in the environment and encourages you to put the pieces together for yourself. However, while to some extent you actually can get a better grasp of the world around you, you'll probably have difficulties putting all the pieces together by yourself, which is why, if you want to go deeper down the rabbithole, I'd recommend checking out a YouTuber called „mossbag“, who makes great lore videos (obvious Spoiler warning). While I personally really like this way of telling a story, I can see that many people would probably dislike this kind of storytelling, so story-wise, I can only recommend this game to people who enjoy this type of searching for clues, because if you do manage to piece the lore together, you are rewarded with a great-written lore that keeps on expanding the deeper you go. The writing also does a great job at giving many clues that help you with your task of exploring the lore, as well as creating many charming characters, that you'll get attached to just to crush your spirit when you find out about their fates. The game is an emotional rollercoaster and it's great.

The soundtrack of Hollow Knight is amazing, it's constantly giving you chills, either because of the dark and gloomy atmosphere it helps to create most of the time or because of the amount of intensity it creates during the boss fights. The tracks are really beautiful and I can't recall a single one I didn't like.

The same can be said about the artstyle of the game, which with it's strong colours and it's thick lines captures the atmosphere of the areas you're visiting and makes them feel more lively which is being supported greatly by the soundtrack. The different colour schemes used for each area also makes no two areas feel the same but each one unique and amazing in their own way. The game looks beautiful, the regions you travel to are all very unique and have their own flair to them, the enemy design is neatly done, giving all enemies at little personality and the backgrounds always look stunning.

This game is absolutely phenomenal. I highly recommend it for anyone who is a fan of 2D action platformers and/or Metroidvania style games.  
While it starts slow as you learn the basics, the combat in HK eventually becomes fast paced and acrobatic, as you dodge and weave around enemies and projectiles while getting in sword slashes of your own.  
The world is fantastic too, even in just scope, as it continues to expand long after you think you've reached the end. The size of it is mind-boggling and every area has its own style and enemies.  
There is a ton of collection to do as you progress through the game, with most likely a lot to finish once you beat the final boss, which adds a lot of fun to the exploration.  
  
This is easily a $30-40 game, so it is a complete steal at $15, especially with all of the free DLC.

The game is seriously overestimated. 

The plot is barely understandable from amount of time you spend fighting and exploring. Also it feels like that bunch of side characters and bosses with their own "deep" stories wasn't initially planned and that pile was added in the last minute before the release just to increase the game time. DLCs aren't connected to the main plot or lore in general, they bring literally nothing new to the game. 

The OST is purely meh, very low effort and not consitent with the visual.

In general fight system is pretty unbalanced regarding the fact how much gameplay time is required to get some major improvements for defense or damage. Most charmes are useless. 

The gameplay is like 90% of wandering around sadly looking locations and slicing basic mobs. Literally the whole gameplay is based on repetition. The game forces you to visit the same location for like 20 times. In general this plays not in hands of devs and simply after 2-3 hours the game becomes just boring. 

Looks like developers had some cool plot inside of their minds, but actually had zero idea how to make it into solid game. Really waste of time.

If you like masochistic, frame-perfect, have to be a speedrunner type games that punish you in completely unnecessary ways, this is the game for you.

Did you die?  Have fun repeatedly starting at one of the very few checkpoints that's several minutes away.  Oh, and you're going to die because the enemy have every advantage and yet you can play for hours without a single upgrade unless you know exactly where to go and those upgrades never seem to amount to much, at least the ones I managed to get didn't.  And you won't know where to go, because the map system is awful.  

The game is beautiful, and it's kind of fun when it's not expecting you to be literally perfect under penalty of starting all the way over on some insanely hard platforming section or completely absurd boss fight.  The thing is, it's not interesting enough or fun enough when it's not being complete BS to warrant continuing on.  

There's almost no substance to any kind of story or the characters, it's one of those games where the story is half ass implied, rather than told or shown in any way.  So it's basically a bare bones metroidvania with that ever-present "gotta be pointlessly frustrating like Dark Souls" level of difficulty.

I never once knew where I was supposed to be going, and every way I went was either blocked off with requirements for abilities I didn't have, or required perfection to make it through an area.  I barely opened up a fraction of the game in 9 hours because I spent most of that time frustrated, trying to figure out where I was going with no map, no abilities, no upgrades, pissed off at having to redo the same run over and over and over to try and get past one ridiculously difficult section or another.

If you have inhuman reaction time or hate yourself, this is probably a great game.  I've spent 9 hours mad, so I can't recommend it at all.

This game gets a very tentative recommendation. It's definitely not my favourite platformer, but I really liked exploring the game's environment and I can see the appeal. It's a beautiful game, runs well and the level design all makes sense, but I'm always a little annoyed when playing it. 

The art style is unique and cute, but the visual design is incredibly cluttered. A lot of times I couldn't tell if a feature was in the foreground or background.

Platforming was a pain because the physics doesn't feel right. Coming from Ori and the Blind Forest, I found the knight handles like a brick tied to an anchor that's also floaty somehow. Attacking has recoil, getting hit generates varying amounts of knockback, and bounce platforms are exactly as round and slippery as they look. Jumping really handles more like a jetpack than a traditional jump with good acceleration, and holding left or right while in the air just adds movement linearly. A running start doesn't get you nearly as much distance as you'd expect, and I just couldn't get used to it. Even wall jumping is weirdly unresponsive and throws you just far enough off the wall that you think about going for the adjacent wall, but half the time one button press or the other won't register.

I also had input issues. I would guess that the key input for a directional attack was put in a FixedUpdate(), so you have to press a direction well in advance in order for a directional attack to register. More than half the time the directional attack didn't work for me. The default button mapping doesn't really make sense, and there are some quality of life things like using cursor input in the inventory that should have been implemented. 

Overall I feel like the strange physics and input issues make this game a lot less fun than it looks.

I couldn't make myself play even through the first area. This game is just not for me. First, it's not a platformer at all, even though it seemed so at first glance. It's a game about madly swinging a weapon at enemies for several minutes and then doing it all over again when you inevitably die. Again. It got repetitive and frustrating very quickly. After playing it for a couple of hours, I felt like shit. Physically and mentally drained. Maybe, that was the intention of the game all along. And, admittedly, I'm just plain bad at playing it, but mastering it isn't very appealing to me. The feeling of inability to do anything is hugely enhanced by stunningly grey-black world and the otherwise beautiful music that only raises the not-very-pleasant feeling of being stuck. So, yeah, I haven't played the game long enough to draw any real conclusions, but I didn't like the experience at all.

If you're reading this you are probably as skeptical of the Hollow Knight positivity cult as I was.

Don't get me wrong, there is a lot to like in Hollow Knight but there are a lot of game play choices that were made that feel needlessly frustrating, especially at the beginning of the game.

There is a reason games have moved away from the map and save systems found in this game; it wastes the players time.

I get it, I am not very good at the boss fights, but punish me by making me learn the fight. Don't make me pound my head against the wall while walking the same corridor back to my ethereal corpse for the 100th time to reattempt the boss. 

Spawning me outside the various boss rooms feels like it would have made a 25 hour play through about 20 hours.

And the map system as it is, what does it add to the game? I still don't have an answer. It doesn't make me feel like an explorer it just weighs me down with a group of "exploring charms" and "fighting charms (once I know the way by memory anyway)".

I wholeheartedly recommend you download a Hollow Knight complete map/guide picture, it will save you time and needless frustration.

And then there is the perennial crutch of save points. These are, for the most part, haphazardly placed and a needless inclusion. Again, it just felt like a way to pad out the run time of a game that already had 15 hours of VERY solid game play. They weren't needed and detracted rather than added to my experience.

There is an assumption that anyone who is critical of this game just didn't 'git gud'. I am not sure what else I need to prove that I got as good, if not better, than most that ever play this game. I finished the Path of Pain, seriously, what more do you want from me?

The truth is the game is good, but it doesn't deserve the wall-to-wall positive reviews it has received. Ultimately that hype tarnished my view, I went into this game expecting a flawless masterpiece. That just wasn't the case and people need to be more honest; there are inclusions in this diamond.

It is a solid 8/10, not a 10/10 or 11/10 like people keep saying it is.

[h1] 👎 DON'T BUY  [/h1] 
I deleted the game before.
After re-install and re-play the game, I quickly realized why I deleted it in the first place.
👎 Does not explain its game mechanics well / at all.
👎 Plenty of blocked paths / tedious exploration
[table] [tr] [td]
[b]External guide / walkthrough required to just play the game.[/b]
Many game mechanics are not explained in game.
Without some of those mechanics, player can be "soft-blocked" from progressing through the story.
It also causes player to waste a lot of time just roaming around aimlessly, or has to back tracking through long path just to get back to the right track.
[/td] [/tr] [tr] [td]
[b]Map is locked (including the area you had visited), until certain conditions are met.[/b]
This discouraging player from exploring the game.
Player has to back track a long way if player had gone the wrong way.
[/td] [/tr] [tr] [td]
[b]No clear clue where to go[/b] 
There are plenty of rooms with what seem like impossible path.
By the time I found the potential skill / mechanics to unlock those path, I realized I have to back track the whole map.
The work to too tedious, and un-rewarding.
It makes the game tedious to explore through.
[/td] [/tr] [tr] [td]
[b]Tedious way of viewing the surrounding[/b]
To look up / down, player needs to hold R-Stick up / down.
But, whenever player moves, the camera pans back (even if player hold down R-Stick).
Thus, discouraging player from look around the surrounding.
Furthermore, a lot of the time when player descends downwards to lower platforms, those platforms are outside of the screen, invisible to player.
Many time, I dropped down into pits and spikes.
Some of the time, I was damaged by enemies, and was forced over the edges, and there is no way to check the lower area, to stay away from pits / spikes.
[/td] [/tr] [tr] [td]
[b]Annoying enemy designs.[/b]
♦ Flying enemies which keep themselves airborne, attacking player from unreachable height. Flying away when player jump towards them.
♦ Mantis Warrior, short-range charging enemies. 
Player cannot block charge attack, so the only option is jump attack.
But if player's jump attack miss, very likely players will touch the enemy, causing player to get "touch" damage, and left player opened to be attacked by another charge attack. 
♦ Sporg, Long range missile silo. 
Usually placed on unreachable area, where they keep firing long-range seeking projectiles at player, which also deal wide-area explosive damage.
Another unexplained mechanics : This enemy's missile can damage itself, but all other enemies projectiles do not damage any of their allies. Found this fact by chance. 
[/td] [/tr] [/table]

While I absolutely love the art style and also the audio of this game, this game somehow made me feel annoyed throughout the entire time I played it.
- Waypoints feel completely misplaced as one always needs to travel for several minutes to get anywhere. 
- Enemies and attacks tend to spawn inside the player, making it actually impossible to dodge them.
- There are about 40 charms to equip in this game, but one only has enough slot to actually equip 3-4 of them, rendering the weaker (but more interesting) ones completely useless.
- Some of the Jump'n'Run parts feel less like a challenge and more like work.
- The ingame currency Geo is almost completely useless as one quickly has purchased everything that can be purchased (except for three endgame charm upgrades which, on the other hand, are completely overtuned.
- There were quite some situations where I didn't know what I had to do. So I needed to check the entire map again. This, however, felt like a chore because of the poorly placed waypoints.

Despite all these points that seriously bothered me, I would love to like the game, since it's truly beautiful, and one can feel how much love was poured into it. If it were some unknown hidden gem, I'd probably write a positive review. But since it's insanely popular now, I recommend to try it for 2 h and then decide whether to refund it. My impression didn't change over the course of 30 h playtime.

Behind all the music and the art-style, this game just feels flat gameplay-wise. My biggest gripe with the game is the game-play and boss design. Almost all the bosses felt like I was fighting AI. At no point did I ever feel threatened because the bosses just had recognizable attack patterns. Any deviation in the attack patterns just wasn't enough to make the game ever feel like I was fighting an actual boss. They just felt like harder versions of regular NPC enemies. Instead of going into a boss with different types of approaches, the game wants you to learn the boss like a book. For some that may be fun, but I honestly felt like it was practicing an instrument, where I'm just trying to play all the right notes in the piece, which honestly gets boring. The game does handle it better later on in the game where you have access to more charms, and different play-styles. You end up using the same combination of charms that you normally use though, so the other charms just feel useless. Would've been cool if they put a mechanic in where you can sell unwanted charms for charm notches, geo, or dream essence

Also why is it that this game gets to re-skin all the bosses and gets a pass. Granted, they're slightly harder versions but honestly I don't think they add any value to the game. I know they're not necessary to complete the main game, but its still a part of the game to get to the true final boss. They're tedious obstacles to get to Absolute Radiance who I wanted to fight. 4 Pantheons for one boss though, WTH! As somebody who wanted to fight all the unique bosses, I just can't imagine wasting so much time for one boss. Really dumb decision; anybody who defends this is just an elitist who think you should waste an extra 40 hrs of your time to get a shot at the final boss. Makes no sense

Great music though, Dirtmouth theme is one of the best themes I've ever heard from a game.

TL;DR Play this game for great music and art-style; don't play it for game-play and boss design though, as they leave a lot to be desired

This is, without a doubt, the best purchase I have made in over five years. The music and artstyle are beautiful, the gameplay is fun, engaging, and challenging, and the controls are perfectly responsive. If you're the kind of person who really enjoys watching the lore of a world unfold, this game also tackles that aspect incredibly well (cutscenes are minimal; most of the lore is just tidbits you pick up throughout the course of the game). The pacing is great, too; a Metroidvania veteran will have no trouble finding "sequence breaks" to explore any part of the map at (almost) any point in the game, while someone who has less experience will still be allowed plenty of freedom to complete sections and bosses in whatever order they happen to find them. The game is designed so that there are only two or three obstacles that must be overcome in a set order; for instance the (intended) first boss must be defeated in order to progress beyond the first four or five areas. No game is without flaws, but I'm having a hard time thinking of any that had a significant impact on my experience.

This is one of my favorite games in all of history. Out of every game I have ever played (and I have played a lot of games), this game is easily in my top 10, even my top 5.

I could talk about the beautiful artstyle; I could praise this game's musical score, a masterwork by Christopher Larkin; I could talk about the tight controls, or the amazing boss battles, or the bittersweet emotions that permeate it. What I would instead describe to you is the sensation of Hollow Knight. Hollow Knight is a game that is complete, in a sense that few games are. It has no wasted space, no half-thoughts; every place within it is fully realized, and its characters are fleshed out and rife with hidden dialogue and reactions.

But more than that, Hollow Knight is a game about challenge and discovery. It will challenge you, no matter what style of player you are. If you are casual, it will challenge you early, if you are hardcore, it will challenge you later on, but it will challenge you, if you dare to seek those challenges out. It is fair not brutal, punishing but not unkind. Every time you think you have peaked, Hollow Knight will present yet another challenge to overcome, yet another mountain to climb. Most tantalizingly of all, Hollow Knight will make you aware of the fact that you can climb every mountain it presents, all borne on the wings of your own skill.

This game is a masterpiece, and every game collection is hollow without it.

FINICKY CONTROLS

Overall, Hollow Knight is a great game, but it has too many design and technical issues. I rage quit.

The graphics are very dark and sometimes you can't see things on the edge of the screen. This is made worse when you get hit and the world seems to end for a moment, meaning you can't see yourself or enemies around you for a second, which is an eternity in fast-paced combat scenarios, of which there are many. Additionally, when you get down to 1 life left, the screen gets even darker, making it even harder to see things. On top of that, some enemies blend into the dark-colored background and you can get attacked from seemingly out of nowhere.

When you die, you leave behind a shade that has all your money. You have to kill it to get your money back. This is a very punishing design and discourages players from exploring too far when they have a lot of money. It's additionally frustrating when you're trying to do a difficult platforming section and/or boss fight while carrying little to no money and you have to kill your shade. Not because you want to get your money back, but just because he's in your way and if you don't kill him, he's going to make your platforming challenge and/or boss fight near impossible to complete. Sometimes he spawns right in a boss room and you can't kill him before the boss attacks you. This mechanic could work, but the way it's implemented in Hollow Knight is just way too punishing.

This game very clearly does not know the difference between punishing and difficult. It will even put in artificial difficulty and call it "challenge" and just expect its players to "git gud". It's frankly on par with Getting Over It in terms of how bad it controls sometimes.

Ok maybe that is unfair. Most of the platforming is tight and is responsive, even if it does take a little getting used to. But what is not forgivable is the wall jump. I forget what they call it in-game, but it's wall jump. The actual hitbox of the wall is a little inside the sprite, so sometimes it looks like you're on the wall when you really aren't. And then when you try to jump off the wall, you just fall straight to the floor. It is never really clear whether you're on the wall or not and you just have to pray that you are. On top of that, some "walls" aren't actually walls and you can't actually climb up them. How do you know which walls you can climb on? You have to test it. There's literally no way to tell whether a wall is climbable or not without testing it. That's not to mention that some background objects look like solid terrain from a distance, but when you get closer (i.e. to land on them), it's revealed that it's just a background object! It gets better: a lot of platforms blend in with the background (especially in Green Path), so it's really a chore to figure out where you can go from where you are.

It also really likes to be unresponsive. Every chance it gets it disables your input. Sitting on a bench? Unresponsive. Teleporting to a ledge after hitting a thorn? Unresponsive. The worst is sometimes you can move around but not use jump and/or abilities. Sure, if you wait a couple seconds it will do want you want after you let up on the key you've been holding down for 5+ seconds and then press it again, but it's not enough.

The NPCs are fun and quite unique. They're a lot of fun to interact with and even have different dialogue to react to how far along in the game you are. I often went back to revisit NPCs just to see what they would say. The save points and the transportation system was really well done in that they both fit the game world really well. Plus, both added more NPCs to talk to.

In terms of Metroidvania, Hollow Knight is even better than Ori and the Blind Forest. It has a huge sprawling map and several avenues of exploration. Its unique map mechanics, although sometimes frustrating, really make you feel like you're exploring uncharted territory and they provide a sense of adventure. At any point in the game, there is always several possible routes to go down next, and although you do need certain abilities to progress in the game, it never feels like you're being railroaded along a certain path. However, that's about the only thing it does better than Ori and the Blind Forest.

You can see how great Hollow Knight is in the design of Ori and the WIll of the Wisps. Several design decisions from Hollow Knight found their way into Ori's sequel. Hollow Knight has some good ideas but ultimately it has a lot of flaws too. As a platformer, Ori 2 is so much better than Hollow Knight, even though Hollow Knight has better pure Metroidvania elements. Ori has a tendency to railroad the player quite a bit, but doesn't have any major playability issues.

Ok now I've cooled down a bit from my rage quit anger. But I'm still not going to reinstall this game. One thing that Ori got right is the core mechanics. Everytime I die in Ori, I feel like it's my fault and I can do better. In Hollow Knight, it often feels like the character controller is intentionally screwing me over and calling me a noob at the same time. IDK, if they fix the wall jump issue I think I'd probably pick it up again.

I can handle difficult but I will not tolerate finicky.

TL;DR:
Hollow Knight is overall great game, but due to several issues with it, I will not be playing it anymore.

Super Meat Boy does this well with instant respawns, but Hollow Knight requires you to trudge through tedious transits before re-attempting it's twitch challenges. No game has toyed with my emotions just like this one. The game held me like a puppet on strings throughout the entire journey. When the game wanted me to feel happiness I felt it. When the game wanted me to feel sadness I felt it. When the game wanted me to feel fear I felt it. When the game wanted me to feel determination...I felt it.

There's one point in the game where you go into this deep area that I'm not going to spoil. There's no music, just an ambient track that seems like it's getting louder and louder. No game has ever given me a feeling like that before. A feeling of "I'm not supposed to be here".

Hollow Knight is a masterpiece. The story doesn't revolve around your character, but instead the world around them. The story is scattered around the world and you have to piece it together yourself. I have spent many hours just staring at screenshots I've taken (I have over 3000) and just trying to piece together the story and understand what it all means. This is game that is best experienced completely blind. The story is so good that at one point I actively looked up a spoiler because I the mystery and tension was so great and I just had to know what was going on. The spoiler didn't really affect my expierience because it was only one mystery and the wonderful world of Hallownest is full of them. And that's my favourite part of Hollow Knight, the world. There's an entire society made up of bugs and it's perfect. There's a mine where bugs mine for crystals, there's a city where it always rains, there's a lake above it where that water comes from, there's stations where bugs ride on stag beatles! And these are only a fraction of all the beautiful locations of the large world of Hallownest.

Now let's move onto the gameplay. Hollow Knight revolves around exploring new areas, platforming, looking for collectables and fighting enemies and bosses. Each area introduces new mechanics and enemies. When you die you loose all your money and respawn at your last checkpoint. Then if you want your money back you have to go back to where you died and fight your ghost. Once you defeat them you absorb them and get your money back. The enemy and boss design is perfect. One of my favourites is a dung beetle that throws balls of dung at you. There's tons of charms to collect that give you special perks and you must pick which ones you want to use since you can't fit them all on you. I had lots of fun creating loadouts.

Most of the population of Hallownest are husks of former bugs possessed by a strange force. But not everyone is a soulless husk. There are tons of likeable characters that you meet during your travels.

The art direction and sound design are on point.

There's also tons of little details that really add to the whole experience such as when you hit a wall it gives a little recoil despite being pointless since you're not going to be constantly hitting walls during fights, this isn't Dark Souls. It just really goes to show how much care Team Cherry put into this game.

There's tons of optional challenges for only the toughest players. There's this super hard platforming section near the end called the Path of Pain that feels straight out of Super Meat Boy. Even though it is entirely optional I still feel obliged to beat it. After many hours I still haven't beaten it but I won't give up. (Edit: I beat it!) There's also 4 free DLC packs that add even more to the content of this game. For a game that's only £11 I feel like I've robbed the develepers. I'm still waiting for a super expensive collectors edition to come out so I can begin repaying Team Cherry for the many hours of joy they've given me.

Hollow Knight is one of the few games that has made me depressed after finishing it because it's over. I'll never be able to discover the mysteries of Hallownest again. But I'm still glad I got to experience this masterpiece.

Gameplay: 10/10
Narrative: 10/10
Graphics: 10/10
Sound: 10/10

It brings me great joy to award Hollow Knight with my first ever 100/100

This game is so annoying to traverse. While exploring you are constantly running into dead ends that becomes accessible later on in the game when you unlock more abilities. Which would be fine by itself, but it becomes really irritating because the game does not have convenient fast travel system. Most of the time you just end up running back and forth through the same areas you already explored. And you can't really speed run (at least I couldn't) them due to platforming elements, enemies that explode in your face, and traps (spikes, acid pools etc).
When entering a new area you don't unlock a map until you find a map merchant. However, if you take a wrong turn, you can explore pretty deeply without ever encountering said merchant. Combination of the lack of map with a maze-like structure of areas (every new screen can have up to ~4 exits) makes it super easy to get lost. Good luck retrieving the money from the body once you die deep in the dungeon without a map.
After collecting a significant amount of money while exploring, I always found myself in a position with an  uncomfortable choice between going back (and losing 15 minutes), and continuing my journey forth (risking the loss of money). This, and lack of proper fast travel hindered exploration immensely.

This game is listed and talked about as a very hard platformer, however, the platformer part of this game isn't very difficult at all. In fact - it's as simple as your average Mario or Kirby game, if not simpler.

...At least those two games have the platformer aspect pinned down as their main focus and as such, include a lot of great movement mechanics.

But Hollow Knight does not fit this description. The "platformer" part of Hollow Knight is simply moving around the world towards the next boss fight (Which are not platforming related either), with maybe a few enemies sprinkled through the pathway to the boss, with no real difficulty, which is a shame because I was really looking forward to a challenging platformer.

Hollow Knight is a phenomenally open metroidvania. It’s astonishing where you can get to after acquiring a few basic moves. With so many nooks to explore, I found myself writing down notes on goals and curious sections worth reinvestigating in the future.

Hollownest is full of many small tales told by the detailed environments, cryptic messages and interactions with the memorable NPCs. There are so many side stories to encounter and the [spoiler]dream nail[/spoiler] can give even more chilling insight into a corpse or statue. There’s more to find in the corners of Hallownest than just loot.

Early on, bosses can be tougher since you have a smaller arsenal and are still learning the basics. Hollow Knight gives you the space to look elsewhere until you’ve improved in technique and stats. Consequently, you have the option to blaze a forwards if you’ve got the skill. For the most part, rpg numbers aren’t very important and are kept low, keeping things more precise while making small improvements more significant.

Optional dream bosses, extreme platforming segments, combat trials, and a permadeath difficult offer stupendous challenges for the iron willed. All the overworld bosses are quite doable and the majority of lore and upgrade related tasks aren’t unreasonable. With a little elbow grease, I was able to complete the vast majority of what I wanted to see. 

I adored the way badges work. They’re substantial rewards for side quests or optional routes and have a ‘collect ‘em all’ quality. The more badges you have, the more playstyle diversity you have available. I found myself constantly switching badges around for difficult bosses or challenges. Dying is an opportunity to reconsider how else you could tackle the situation. I also love that the compass takes up a slot. As an option to apply you map knowledge and orienteering, you can have space for other abilities by choosing to not equip it.

Christopher Larkin’s stunning soundtrack gives each environment a distinctly memorable atmosphere. With my emotional attachment to cello, many of the mournful, reoccurring themes had me transfixed. Harmonious tracks for exploring make a powerful contrast to the fast-paced anthems that suit the personality or nature of bosses. It’s been a long time since I’ve played the cello or piano. If I ever decide to revisit those instruments, I’d be motivated to find transpositions of a Hollow Knight’s music. I was surprised to learn that Larkin also scored Expand, which has beautiful music that tied a game about geometry together skillfully.

There’s not much original I can say about Hollow Knight’s visuals and [url= https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2IfLmc9ZHt8]animation[/url]. Cute and creepy, the hand drawn sprites and move at a lower framerate. This touch is the ground zero for Hollow Knight’s gamefeel whereupon the particle effects smooth everything out. Touches like falling leaves, fog and rain as well as the lighting in backgrounds facilitate a spectrum of tranquility and decay in every location in the fallen kingdom. It’s pretty inspiring. The unique yet straightforward design of enemies has been a safe source of templates for my recent attempts to practice sketching.

Hollow Knight is a game to get lost in.

This innocent game quickly turns into a serious platformer, pattern learning boss fighter, and action timing challenger. Normally, not my type of game. But much like Ori and the Blind Forest (though this is more hardcore), the atmosphere and exploration elements are fantastic. Even if I don't finish this game, I consider myself a happy buyer.

Hollow Knight is an awesome game. I would describe it as being the true heir to the first Dark Souls game, more so than much more blatant knockoffs or even the game's own sequels. It the same punishing-but-fair difficulty, the same sense of exploration and discovery, the same deep lore the devs have no fear of letting you miss, and the same empowering feeling of moving a weighty, uncaring world with nothing more than skill and will. There are definitely some major differences, but I feel like they all work in a way that a lot of games that just blindly ape Dark Souls don't. It's a metroidvania platformer, and compared to dark souls the combat demands a lot more speed and reflexes compared to the slow, methodical combat based around timing in Dark Souls, and where Dark Souls keeps its hidden surprises a bit sparse, Hollow Knight encourages exploration by putting items and surprises in basically every nook and cranny.
Hollow Knight's metroidvania side also stands on its own quite well, with a variety of powerups that open up more of the map and allow for more variety in the platforming challenges. All but one of them also meaningfully affect how you can fight, so they aren't just keys for funny-shaped locks either.

This game just keeps on giving. While I was a bit bored for the first few hours, after coming back to it and accessing some of the later areas I discovered I was having a lot of fun exploring. Lots of the areas are unique, and define themselves as more than the tropes they're based on, like the "grassy starting area," usually do. The art in general is just very pretty, even for some of the less visually-interesting areas. 
The bosses are great, too, for the most part--losing to one and having to trek back to the boss arena (dying makes you respawn at the last bench you rested at) was annoying, especially for a select few bosses, but most of them were enjoyable to learn and beat. Moreover, coming back to the bosses you struggled with on another playthrough and seeing how much you've improved is really satisfying! Except for the two or three RNG-based bosses out of the fifteen total bosses or so, all of the bosses are fair. Quick warning: for the first time around, there are some very hard bosses, with a few taking 1-2 hours to beat. [spoiler]Nightmare King Grimm took 3-4 hours, but he would've been quicker if I'd taken a break in the middle.[/spoiler]
This might be an odd thing to mention, but the writing is really nice. The tone is consistent throughout the game, and lots of the item and location names are almost poetic. A charm that lets you do more damage when you have one hit point left is called Fury of the Fallen, for example, and instead of exploring an area with the name "Sorta Sewer System" later on, you explore an area named the "Royal Waterways." 10/10 naming conventions and tone.
Finally, it's a great deal for its price. Definitely worth more than the $15 it's priced at (or around $10 during sale times) for all the time you can spend on it. My first playthrough took around 25 hours while I was intermittently using a guide, but getting 100% for the first time took around 40 hours for me.

I'm no expert on the Metroidvania genre, but I can certainly say this game is worth your time, especially if you're like me and love bugs! (The insects and spiders kind, that is) If you like pretty games, difficult games, or just good games in general, you should really just go ahead and buy this game.

I'm gonna start with, Hollow Knight is a good game, it really is, but all the hype it gets feels a little bit undeserved. I'm gonna point out some flaws in no particular order, since there are more than enough positive reviews. There will be some minor spoilers gameplay-wise.
- The double jump is the worst in the history of double jumps, maybe ever. The way it works, it elevates you with wings, so it has a little buildup (delay), which is very annoying, especially in platforming areas. You can literally die at a spike pressing jump, having the wings animated already, but not elevating. Happened to me multiple times in the jumping puzzle area (path of pain)
- There is no late-game tool to help you find secrets after you finish the game, some of them are so random there is no way you would find that by yourself ever, like walls that you can destroy but there is nothing that would suggest that. What you have to do is, open a guide or wiki, which is flat out bad game design. It breaks immersion.
- You can slash downward and upward, except the game forgets to tell you that, and it doesn't work as it should. If you press down and attack at the same time, it will attack downward yes? No.... it will attack forward. The way you have to do it, keep pressing down, THEN attack. It's clunky and weird. It's especially a problem because the jumping puzzle area relies heavily on attacking downwards.
- Using fast travel plays an unnecessary 5-10 sec animation every damn time you use it, like why?
- The map doesn't get updated as you are exploring it, you have to sit on checkpoints for it to update, makes no sense to me, especially when you have to buy the map beforehand.
I enjoyed Dead Cells and Ori more, better controls and you don't have to have the Wiki open while playing the game. Hollow Knight is good, but not excellent to me.

My experience with Metroidvania games has been light, but this one has blown the god damn water out of the others I've played.  When you start Hollow Knight, you are thrown into the kingdom of Hallownest with no objective stated to you or a reason for what is going on.  The longer you play though, the more information you will find that opens up the lore of the game.  The game starts off as rather innocent but eventually dives into madness once you start putting the pieces together in terms of the lore and making sense of what initially are vague references to why things are set in place as they appear.  Unconventional for telling a story normally, but here after many hours when you hit that moment of zen and everything comes together it's just insane.

Regarding how the game plays, the controls are extremely tight.  For example, the MOMENT you let go of jump your character cuts upward momentum entirely and will start descending.  It feels a little weird at first but the game offers absolute control at any given point to a tee to what buttons you are currently holding or pressing, which is something a lot of other games lack in favor of something semi-realistic for feedback.  The immediate feedback feels right for the game when you are in tight combat situations later on and nothing feels particularly unfair with some practice.

Hollow Knight is a surprisingly long game for a first time playthrough, I'd say 100% completion the first time would clock in about 30 hours.  Even after the first playthrough though, the game is also great for speed running and the game pushes for this in the form of achievements tied to finishing the game quickly from start to finish.  There is something here both for casual players and hardcore players wanting to push towards a bigger challenge.  Anyone remotely interested in Metroidvania style games would be doing themselves a great disservice not getting this game.

With the wave of new AAA games having been announced at this year’s E3 by major companies such as Nintendo, Bethesda, Naughty Dogs, and so on, it is bound to happen that some of the lesser known titles, such as Hollow Knight, go unnoticed by most people as they wait in anticipation for these new releases. Hollow Knight is a game that offer the player so much more than the low pricing would first seem with it’s simple yet addictive gameplay, unique art style, and creative world building and narration. But despite its relative success and positive feedback the game has received since its initial release on PC, Hollow Knight still remains as somewhat obscure in the face of other mainstream video games, although its new release on the eShop for the Nintendo Switch could remedy this by helping it raise its popularity as more people start playing it due to new accessibility.

Hollow Knight is developed and published by the Australian indie games company Team Cherry. The game was initially revealed on Kickstarter on November 18, 2014 with a funding goal of 35,000 Australian dollars. Hollow Knight officially reached its initial goal on December 15 of the same year and successfully raised AU$ 57,138 with 2,158 backers at the end of its thirty days Kickstarter campaign. On February 14, 2017, Hollow Knight was officially released on Steam for Windows at the price of 14.99 USD. Since its release, Team Cherry has put out 2 of the 3 free content packs for Hollow Knight, each of which add additional areas, items, and bosses to the game, and has confirmed that a planned DLC with a second playable character will be coming some time in the future. 

Although Hollow Knight was released as a standalone title, it is heavily inspired by games of the Metroidvania genre, such as the Metroid and Castlevania series, all of which focuses heavily on exploration, combat, and boss fights. Unlike games that are linearly divided up into different levels, Hollow Knight has one giant world map with multiple sections of the game all linked together with order in which to explore these areas being left up to the player’s discretion. However, that does not mean that the player has complete freedom when exploring due to some parts of the game being blocked off from the player until they unlock certain items or abilities needed to progress due to it being important to the game’s narration. As a result, this can require the player to do a lot of backtracking to progress but there are some ways to fast travel in the game which help ease this problem.

Most of Hollow Knight’s gameplay is comprised of combat, 2D platforming, or a combination of the two. In addition to having to defeat enemies in order to continue to progress through the game, players are also expected to be able to control their jumps and movements in order to safely land on platforms and avoid environmental obstacles as they explore through the game’s labyrinth-like map. In regards to combat, the player can either use their melee attacks or spells to damage enemies, both of which can be upgraded at certain parts of the game.  Like most video games, players should be aware of their ingame health to avoid dying and having to restart from the last checkpoint. Hollow Knight also include a “charms” system, which allow the player to choose different power-ups that modify certain attributes of their character. While the game does not bring anything revolutionary to the table in terms of gameplay or game mechanics, Hollow Knight’s simple, tight controls and charms system help to keep the game’s combat to remain fresh and non tedious even after multiple playthroughs.

That does not mean that Hollow Knight is free of flaws though. One of the issues with Hollow Knight is the lack of a difficulty setting. While this isn’t a huge problem when exploring the different areas of the game, it can be for trying to beat certain boss fights that are need to progress through the story. This can end up being discouraging to some player who like playing the game but end up being unable to continue. However, there are things you can do to make these parts easier such as upgrading your melee weapon to deal more damage, switch the charms that you are using, or trying out different strategies against the bosses. Despite this, I still found these parts of the games to be enjoyable due to the challenge and how satisfying it is to finally be able to beat a boss that I was stuck on for hours. The amount of awe and excitement that Hollow Knight has given me on my first playthrough has lead me to consider it to be one of the best games that I have ever played.

One of the easiest examples for grindy, and the main one, is that to get to the final stages of the game you have to get 1800 of a certain resource. You get this by fighting spirits to challenge to fights or by finding nodes for it. After I hit every node and fought every spirit I found in ~20 hours I had 800. Spent 3 more hours going around and got 1.2k, and keep in mind that when you go around the map it automatially places markers for graves you were even near, along with having to explore nearly the whole thing to even get to this point.

All in all its a good game, has some flaws like everything else. But i'd only reccomend it if you really like the category.

Overall the game has been a mixed experience, and it has given me both satisfaction and frustration, the latter being the most common case. I understand that it is a game that skill is required, and most certainly the majority of people will advise me to 'git gud'. Well, I may suck at gaming but I play games to enjoy myself, and not to get frustrated. 
If the excitement and frustration were properly balanced I would have given the game a positive review. Sadly this is not the case.

Edit: I just beat the Trial of the Fool. Still my feelings are mixed.

Man, it's been a minute since I played something soo good as this. I'm talking Castlevania SOTN good and I'm extremely hesitant to compare anything to that masterpiece, but this is that good. When the story, music and gameplay come together to form such an amazing game you just have to take a step back and appreciate what a group of people are able to achieve. To top it off, the price of the game is an enigma. I got this on sale and yet even the base price is such a downplay of its worth. As a person who deeply respects quality, well-made games regardless of genre, Hollow Knight shines among its peers. If you do not try this game out, I truly feel you're missing out on something very special in the gaming community. A special thanks to Team Cherry, this game reminded me of how players can still experience joy in gaming.

Hollow Knight is one of the best games that I have ever played. Confusing at first, this metroidvania gets elaborate fast. Considering that the game is 15$ you would expect it to have maybe 20 hours worth of content at most. However this game has about 70 hours of gameplay with the current free dlc (with a whole new paid story to come at the time I am writing this). You can quickly find yourself falling in love with the amazingly designed npcs and the lore of the game which is rich and is absolutely packed from every corner of this game's large map. The enemies (about 110) are plentiful, especially the bosses (about 40). The game has so much content including numerous abilities you gain, charms to aid your adventure, a great soundtrack, many areas, and so much more to find (oh yeah, a ton of secrets too). You could play this game multiple times to unlock new endings and you would probably find something you hadn't before. Spending 15 dollars on this game is almost a rip off and you should totally take it.

TL;DR - Wikis should not be mandatory.


A wonderful game, with amazing combat, excellent upgrade systems, and a beautifully grim setting. And all the other reviews are so positive! Why the negative review?

Because after going 20 hours in you're going to have explored much of the game, but you'll be looking for new powerups to help you travel, and then looking for places to use those powerups. Backtracking. Standard metroidvania stuff.

However, this map is designed to waste your time. It will not mark down some obvious exits you can go through, and WILL mark down one way passages you will never go through. Some of these one way doors you'll spend 15 minutes getting to, realize you'll never go this way, mark it as such on your map, and then spend another 15 minutes trudging back to a travel hub. Those last 15 minutes are the worst, because it's time spent where you know you've wasted your time, and you know you're just getting back to square one.

There's insufficient signposting, which you may appreciate, a map that actively misdirects you to explore and backtrack to the wrong places, which you may appreciate, and there's too much opportunity to miss vital stuff. Mushrooms are clearly a way past this obsticle? Well, I'll come back when I have an item that lets me use mushrooms. SURPRISE, THERE IS NO ITEM. A down slash on the mushroom will bounce you. There's no NPC to tell you, hints, no enemies that demonstrate it's possible, no pit you get trapped in until you demonstrate you can bounce your way out, to force you to try new things until you solve it. No, you just leave, and your progress is basically blocked and you don't know why. Have fun spending 10 hours pulling your hair out until you look at the wiki!

And god help you if you do miss a powerup. I played for 30 hours, missed the Crystal Heart, and then spent 20 hours exploring, finding lots of little sub bosses and trinkets and hidden prisoners to free, but never making any actual progress. Without the Crystal Heart, you can't get several items you need to beat the game. And you'd think that finding the item I had missed would open the game up for me, but you'd be wrong. I had the item, and I spent two days, about 10 hours total, trying to find all the places I could use it. And again, I missed the one place you need to use it to get an item that allows you to get another item that allows you to get through a hidden passage that eventually gets you to another whole area where you find one of the three main bosses.

God help you if you miss any one link in the chain, because the map is VAST, and thick with secrets, dead ends, jump puzzles, enemies, and mazes. If you miss something, it might be hours until you find it even if someone says, "Oh yeah, you need to find the hidden passage in an acid pit in Greenpath."

The game goes from being a fun exploration game with tight combat to a slog where the wiki is practically mandatory., and it doesn't have to. If the map wasn't "earned" through exploration, OR if the map marked dead ends, one way passages, and available exits more consistently, OR if the game gave you the option to mark your map immediately at the start of the game when you first see these places, the problems would be this bad. But as it stands, there's a very likely chance this game will eat up too much of your time frustrating you without giving you a way to win but to bash your head against it until you find a hidden passage you didn't see in a room you last entered 30 hours ago, with a powerup you got 12 hours ago.

I'm going to beat this game, but only out of spite. I'm not going to let it win. I may not be enjoying myself, but I will not let anyone say I wasn't able to beat it. I've had to use the wiki twice, and I anticipate having to use it at least once more, to find the double jump. Overall, I am left with one takeaway lesson: Wikis should not be mandatory.

I want to start this by saying metroidvania is my abasolute favorite style of game. But after 3 hours of this game, I have found almost nothing I like about it. This game makes a very long series of design decisions that I find questionable, and see no purpose behind said decisions.

I readily recognize that the visual style is one of the most immediately striking selling points of this game. I will admit that I was never a fan from the outset. The extremely simplistic characters and enemies on highly complex backgrounds always looked like a jarring and unpleasant mixture. Combine that with the character designs themselves being unsettlingly deformed led to something I was not into. If you like the art that's fine, this is just my opinion. 

However, this artistic decision also hinders the game itself. Going through 5 or 6 areas of the game, each one was very unique from the other. But the dedication to monochromatic backgrounds makes it very dull to move through the overly large areas, and confusing to discern one room from the next. What at first just turned me off visually, turned into a clearly poorly made design decision that hindered gameplay.

Another aspect that quickly irritated me was that your character gets knocked back every time you attack an enemy. I have never seen anything like this in any game ever. There are plenty of games that knock back your character when you get hit, but NEVER when YOU do the hitting. It makes basic combat with the hordes of enemies a merciless chore. I can find no reason for this decision, and it does nothing but make basic combat an unreasonable annoyance.

The next aspect of combat that irritates me to no end is the obscene particle spam. Every time you hit enemies, get hit by enemies, and especially when you kill enemies creates such an enormous cloud of particles that it greatly obscures the visual space, often to such a degree that you can't see the next enemy coming to attack you. There are no options to reduce these particle effects, and there is no reason to have such effects that continually interfere with gameplay.

The last straw for me came when I died several times in a row and found that all of my money was taken from me as punishment. There. Is. No. Reason. To. Punish. The. Player. Like. This. Once again, this decision adds absolutely NOTHING to the game. The only result is to drag out the game by forcing the player to farm the same enemies over and over to recollect lost funds. There is apparently a bank in the game to deposit your money and avoid this fate, However I did not come across the first bank until after I had lost all my money. And the bank, like save points, and fast travel points, are FAR too few and far between, forcing unreasonable trekking through the same areas just to try and mitigate awful game design decisions.

I do not believe I will be putting any more time into this game. I am disappointed, and absolutely mystified as to why so many people enjoy this mess.

I need to find the next ability and there is absolutely no way of knowing where that ability is.

I currently have 18 hours in this game, that was 3 hours getting to this point then 15 more just wondering around the map over and over, thinking I'm heading toward the next area only to find a wall of black smoke blocking it off, and realising I still have no idea what I'm meant to do.

The other two dreamers are beyond passageways I can't yet access. Every passageway I can find on my map is blocked by an obstacle I can't yet surpass. There is no obvious indicator of where I'm meant to go, and if there were any hints earlier on it's been 15 mind-numbing hours since then and I've completely forgotten them.

Just now I had a massive wave of euphoria, I actually found a new area! The Hive! However after walking 20 feet in I reached a jump I can't make and was back to square 1.

I even looked online to see where to go next, and someone said the Elderbug in Dirtmouth gives hints when you're stuck. The only hint he seems to want to give me is that while the bench next to him is in fact made of iron, it's surprisingly comfortable.

I really, really, really want to like this game. I think if I were to just play the boss battles I'd love it to bits. But I've reached a point where I have no idea where to go and honestly have had all motivation to keep exploring completely drained.

Metroid + Dark Souls + Ori and the Blind Forest = 1 extremely satisfying adventure.

This game satisfies your urge to explore by offering you an expansive world that teases you with new mysteries and secrets around every bend. The crisp movement and just-as-deep-as-it-needs-to-be combat make getting to those secrets fun every time, and every new discovery is gorgeously designed and dripping with atmosphere. If you want to discover everything and try to dive deep into the story, you can, but you can also blaze forward (and backward and forward again) across the map, making this game as long as you want it to be. And with free DLC (and paid DLC later) still coming out for the game that gives you new reasons to explore, it feels like the developers are attentive to their players even after launch.

It's pretty rare that you get a game that seems to accomodate so many different types of players successfully while still delivering a satisfactory story experience, so I'm extremely thankful that I believed the hype and picked up this game. Despite the name, Hollow Knight is clearly a full game - full of polish, full of surprises, and full of ideas. It is one of the best single-player experiences I have had in years.

I almost never write reviews for a game, let alone a bad review. But this game frustrated me so much that I feel like I have to write one.
          First, the game has a beautiful art style, and it's probably the best thing about the game.
The rest, however, is debatable.
The game play is smooth, but it really gets overshadowed by the amount of time wasted from exploring the areas.
         The exploration part of the game is what I have the most problems with.
You are pretty much forced to explore a huge empty area with little to no guide on where to go next, some see this as a plus side, but I simply don't have that much time to waste. You also don't even get access to map half of the time, which makes exploration feel more like going through a maze rather than making progress.
          The story is also non-existent, at least in the early game. You get bits and pieces of what's going on, but honestly, it doesn't seem that interesting. Deadcells does a similar thing with its story but I was actually intrigued with it and actively invested in it, this game not so much. The story is actually one of the biggest components that drives me to play or continue in a game, I just got non of that from this game. The story of this game might actually be incredible, but if I can't get invested in it after 5 hours, there's a problem with the game.
          The entirety of the 5 hours I've played has been boring, and simply not enjoyable. I know a lot of people had enjoyed this game, but it is just not for me. If you don't want to keep getting lost, just want to enjoy good game play, or want to quicky invest in a good story, don't playt this game.

[h1] Hollow Knight [/h1]
[i] "For so long i've felt drawn here. So many tales of wonders and horrors. No longer could I resist. I just had to see it for myself." [/i]

[b] 30 Second Review: [/b]
Hollow Knight is a fantastic 2D rogue-like/metroidvania style platformer with tight gameplay, fun and charming characters, intriguing story that is still being added to, and a fantastic soundtrack that everyone should play at least once 10/10.

[b] 30 Minute Review: [/b]
Hollow Knight was a game that I had bought during one of Steam's many sales as I had heard that it was a fun platformer with dark souls esque characteristics, and coming off of playing dark souls 1 and 2 I was interested to see what it was like. Flash forward to the end of my spring semester at college and my roommate at the time played through Hollow Knight on his computer and constantly praised it. After hearing his high marks for the game I knew I needed to play it right away. Upon a full playthrough with 103-104% completion I absolutely adored every second of it, there was rarely a time where  I did not enjoy playing the game. I was almost immediately in love with the art style of the game from the start, and as I progressed through the game more and more I fell in love with it through its varying environments and areas. The gameplay of Hollow Knight was incredibly responsive and had a metroidvania style to it where you start out with very little in the way of items and as you progress you begin to discover new powerups/items that grant you unique abilities making the game both easier all while adding a layer of depth to it. Instead of levelling up or having experience, there are items called charms that each grant a unique ability or effect that can help aide yo udepending upon the situation. There are also numerous different methods to help increase your health, and damage output leading to interesting combinations and strategies to help overcome the obstacles within this game. As for the platforming there were very few segments, mainly only one (white palace) that gave me stressful challenges and reminded me a little too much of super meat boy in terms of difficulty.  The story for the game is not in your face and left up to the player to piece things together based on clues left all throughout the game and environments, similar to other titles such as dark souls. One of the many things that I greatly enjoyed throughout this game was the wide cast of characters you meet throughout your journey. Many of them are full of charm and won me over so easily that I wanted nothing more than to see them happy. The bossfights in this game are truly a splendor, with difficulty ranging from easy to incredibly hard. Each bossfight is unique with none coming to mind of being copied versions of prior fights (excluding multiple encounters of the same boss). To top off this proverbial fantastic sundae of a game is the cherry on top, the wonderful soundtrack. This games music has been fantastically created by Christopher Larkin, and he did a phenominal job of making a memorable and enjoyable set of music throughout the game. Each piece accurately and effectively conveys the emotion of the scene, whether it be exploring an overgrown area of the map with calm lush sounds, or frantic fast paced music for a particular encounter with a strong enemy or group of bosses. The current DLC in this game are all free (and will continue to be) each add their own unique change to the base game. With lifeblood being a variety of quality of life changes to help improve the overall playthrough experience, hidden dreams adds two new boss fights while also giving new aspects to the dream nail. Finally the grimm troupe adds a whole new cast of characters, bossfights, and mechanics to help give even more variety to an already incredible varied game. To add on to the mounds of praise I have already given to this game, its still receiving new DLC and has more in the works to add even more additional content to this already fantastic game. Becuase of this, while I normally delete a game from my library/catalogue it as finished upon its completion I plan on leaving it in my library until all of the DLC are finished for it and at that point I will edit my review to reflect the state of the game with all of the included DLC's. With that being said, I give Hollow Knight my highest marks as it is a game that i have enjoyed from start to finish. Sometimes with longer games I tend to get fatigued and want to just make it to the end to finish, but with this game I was left wanting more, which in this case is exactly what we will be getting down the road. With everything involving my gushing ramblings of this game, I give it a 10/10 and would recommend it to anyone who is remotely a fan of these types of games and even to those who arent as it has something that everyone will love.

Thank you Team Cherry for making this masterpiece of a game, I look forward to seeing what the future holds for you.
~The Fool

I personally bounced off of the game. I found Ori and the Blind Forest or Dust: An Elysian Tail more enjoyable to play. My biggest issue is the choice of using Dark Souls or Shovel Knight-type death punishment, where you have to go back to where you died while debuffed in order to get your money/exp back. That combined with the few bench save spots as well as having enemies respawn constantly means that if I want to go exploring in a place where I don't have a map yet, I have to remember exactly my route, kill everything again, and also kill the respawn spirit in order to even start thinking about if I want to keep exploring that area, unless I want to be out all of my cash.

I get that this is a souls-like, but I really don't want to keep fighting the same ten enemies at the beginning of the game with only one attack and a fireball. I also had basically no idea where to go aside from the rarely updated and vague info from the elder on the surface. Four hours in and I had no idea what my character was, who anyone else was, or what I was trying to do in the story.  It's hard to keep motivated while backtracking for the fifth time aimlessly when I don't even know what I am looking for.

I'm a huge fan of metroidvania, but this game is both unnecessarily hard and opaque. Save points are too far apart, the minimap is intentionally frustrating (doesn't update in real time, costs resources to even see where you are), it takes forever to navigate even small areas, and while the controls are reasonably intuitive and fights against trash mobs are satisfying, the tougher enemies are way too hard and take multiple tries to beat - which means you're doing a ton of backtracking from the last save point in a weakened state. There's no difficulty slider either, so if you're not a master of platformer close combat you're basically screwed.

Do not play this game if you do not enjoy extremely punishing platforming and combat with sometimes muddy visual cues. Emphasis on *enjoy.* If you find yourself repeatedly throwing yourself against challenging fights or platforming sessions out of stubbornness or some sort of compulsion to complete the game, rather than to have fun, do yourself a favor and avoid this.
