Hollow knight is a pretty overrated game, beautiful looking game, good controls, but a level design makes the game sometimes fairly challenging or unnecessarily hard, I would constantly find myself trying to speedrun my way to a boss' room that was 5 minutes away from the nearest save room with a lot of enemies in the way, or I would not find the nearest save room and have to walk 10 minutes to get to the boss. I would lose my patience trying to beat an optional boss that I didn't know was optional, only to realizes that there was a hidden shortcut to the boss that would have saved me literally an hour had I known it existed before. I would find myself with no money because my soul was lost somewhere I couldn't find without a map, and I would try to buy one only to realize that I couldn't because I had no money, and I wanted to save, but I couldn't either because someone thought it was a good idea to make the player pay money to save in one random area in a game where you can lose all your money at any time. Unfair, that's what the game is when you don't go to the left and get destroyed by an army of enemies or one-shoted by a boss because you went to the right, because you just happened to go the opposite way to the save room or healing place. Unfair when you want to go save and find a boss in the save room. The difficulty varies too much depending on where you go and when you go and without a reliable fast travel system, you just have to bite the bullet and keep struggling forward. This is not good level design.

Worst respawn mechanic ever. Die twice and lose all your geo you were saving for expensive items. Don't want to lose it, try to get it from the same place you died, where the chances are you will die again. Want to save your money? Walk across the whole map to reach the bank then back. Incredibly frustrating.

Not sure how this game has gotten such good reviews, I am suspecting they are paid for as playing it for several hours now I cannot understand how anyone can think this is worth playing.

1. The game is extremely unforgiving, to the point where it is unplayable (you restart from last save instead of anywhere near the place you died, you lose all your coins if you die before getting back and killing your soul etc.). How is a new player suppose to know these things? 

2. The game tells you nothing a long the way on how to learn the mechanics of the the game, meaning that you do not learn the skills that prepare you for different part later in the game. You are just expected to know them out of nowhere. It is so ridiculous that it is hard to make sense of it.

3. Basic things that would allow you to learn the game as you play, you have to purchase (with the in game coins). Why is this good, how can this make any sense? As if the developers were so un-creative in making the game interesting that they just put random things like this in the game.

I know some might think "hey it is hard and that is not a bad thing" well my answer is "only because it is hard does not make it a good game either". There has to be a natural flow in the game where you learn how to play your character and everything that does not align with that is just bad game design (besides being plain stupid as well).

Another thing I have heard people write is that you can use Youtube and things. Every game where you have to use external sources to make sense of what is happening in a game and what you are expected to learn is a bad game, there are no exceptions to that! The game, through different ways, and they do not even have to be obvious or linear, should provide this information, either directly or subtle. On several videos I have heard people, semi-pro's  on top of that, saying that "ow and this I learn from the comments section in a previous video". What, you did not learn this by playing the game despite all the hours you played? Even you, a semi-pro player have to watch others to learn basic mechanics about the game? The stupid game did not provide you with this understanding after all those hours of game play? Are you frikking kidding me???

How is a new player suppose to learn how to play by actually playing the game (or even enjoy it for that matter) when even semi-pro players have to figure out things from external sources to figure out what the hell is happening? How is this suppose to be fun in any way?

Hollow knight is an incredible game. Not only is it beutiful visually speaking, but it has a great sound track and most importantly; excellent gameplay.

STORY:
While the story takes a bit of a backseat for the first half to two-thirds of the game, favoring exploration and environmental storytelling over an explicit narrative. This is very well executed relying on the player's sence of curiosity about the world to push them to explore and as you progress you really begin to wonder who you are and what your motivation for exploring the world of Hallownest. There is also a lot of interesting background and story lore found in enemy descriptions and character dialogue. The story does becaome more prevalent in the second half of the game though.

The game also has three seperate main endings to the main story, two are neutral endings and the third is a good ending, though the first two are very similar to each other. There are also 2, or more, seceret endings depending on your in game progression.
(7.5/10)

GAMEPLAY:
As a Metroidvania style game you will be doing a great deal of exploring and backtraking to find new abilities, upgrades, secrets, collectibles, idden bosses, etc. So if you players who enjoy exploring and finding these hidden details will really like this aspect of the game.

The controls are tight, easpeacially the aireal controls, which make navigating the world smooth and fun. Though there are some platforming segments near the mid-late game that are very difficult if have not mastered the controls, though they are still possible to complete even using a keyboard rather than the recomended controller. (I would however recomend remapping the controls so that they feel more comfortable.)

Combat is also very tight and fluid. Your main weapon is a nail (sword) that has a short reach, but can be swung to either side as well as up and below you when in the air, and has a minor knock back effect, on both enemies and you. Aside from attacking enemies the nail can also be used for platforming and breaking damaged walls an ceilings. You also have a resource called Soul which can be used for either healing yourself or casting damage dealing spells. However the only way to regain lost soul is by attacking enemies with your nail. This creats a risk reward system when fighting enemies and easpeacially during boss fights.

Speaking of Bosses there a ton of different ones with unique abilties whose patterns you will need to learn and memorise in order to defeat. Some are easyer than others and they will almost always reward you in some way, either with a new ability, upgrade, or just a ton of cash. There are also refights against hared versions of the same boss as well as a combat gauntlet and a boss rush, both of which need to be discovered through exploration.

The mapping system is very similar to oldr style metroid games. First you must aquier the map of a given area by purchasing it from an NPC named Cornifer who can be found throughout the world. the player must then explore to add rooms to the map. However you will only be able to update the map when stopping at a bench and only if you have purchased the quill pen from Cornifer's wife Iselda. Stopping at benches will also save the game and cause defeated enemies to respawn.

As you explore you will find charms which are Hollow Knight's method of character customization. Charms all have unique effects that may change the way that you play the game. Some give greater attack power, gainig more soul when hitting enemies, an increased range for your nail, faster movement speed and a whole host of others. It's even possible to combine different charms to increase one's effects, meaning that the player needs to experiment with different charm sets to find the one for their playstyle or in game situation. While at the begining of the game you can only equip a small number of charms it is possible to increase this through exploration and other means. It is also possible to equip more charms than you have slots, however you must beware that when overcharmed you will take double damage when being hit.

There's only one real negative for me in the gameplay section and that there are two endgame areas, The Path of Pain and The Eternal Ordeal, which can only be accessed by destroying a breakable wall. Normally this would not be a problem, however there is no indication what so ever that these areas even exist at all unless you know about them and this is something I really dislike.
(9/10)

DLC
Hollow Knight has three Content packs on top of the base game; Hidden Dreams, The Grim Troup & Godseeker. The kicker? Each of these content pack are completely free and come pre-installed with the base game. They are also so seamlessly integrated that you almost don't notice that they aren't actually part of the main game unless you know otherwise. Each DLC pack also adds multiple hours of playtime to the overall experiance including; sidequests, boss fights, and even secret endings.
(10/10)

CONCLUSION
Hollow knight is an incredible with fluid exploration and combat, a light story but deep lore, and a ton of character customization. The world of Hallownest is beutiful and charming and while some enemies are very similar to each other there is a large enough variety to keep the combat interesting. It's also one of the very few games that brings out the completionist in me, I felt compelled to find every secret, beat every boss and get as many acheivements as I possibly could, and I still haven't got them all. So if you're a fan of Metroidvania or Soulslike games, or even if you're looking to try something new, I would definitly recomend this game.
(9/10)

[h2]I've always loathed this game[/h2]
In a nutshell, the game has always been one that I would put down for a day or two, and then I would completely forget where I was and where I needed to go next. In most cases, I would have to start the game again from the beginning in order to get my bearings. Despite the fact that I finished some bosses up until the spider queen and completed the arena, I was never able to get any further than that. The game has no sense of direction, and you will wind up spending hours or looking up guides on how to proceed.

[h2]Conclusion[/h2]
Undoubtedly, it is a fun game. But there are some things that I wish were a little bit more streamlined for a smoother experience with navigation, such as a synopsis or something similar. Playing for extended periods of time and writing down the things you have completed yourself or constantly reminding yourself is the only way that this would allow you to figure out what to do next without the need for an excessive amount of backtracking.

After spending 100 hours fighting my way through almost every challange this game offers, looking back, it's the enviromental storytelling, the immediate, almost physical reality of this world is what I love most. In any other game this would amount to "audio and visual effects". Here these details give life to this gothic kingdom as the Knight makes its way, stomping, bouncing, slashing, cutting and tracing a path - making sense of its history and its fate. There is a classic tragedy unfolding through this journey - hubris, defeat, sacrifice and redemption. A lot of games strive to make a pass at these themes but rarely succeed. Here, the cheap, pretentious or condescendingly simplified storytelling traditions so widespread in videogames give way to careful and tasteful poetry - less so with words and more in the way only games are able, by actively engaging the player to explore, understand and overcome.

[2/10] OVERALL
I really wanted to like Hollow Knight but I can't. The entire game is just really bad compared to any other options. Invest your time in other Metroidvanias that reward good combat and punish players fairly rather than the RNG punishing the player.   

[4/10] AUDIO & GRAPHICS
The music and graphics are fun and new at first, both pleasing and enjoyable. But as you progress there are the same shades of blue, green, and black throughout making it less enjoyable and absolutely depressing. All of the music is exciting at first but quickly becomes obnoxious as it loops fairly frequently. The default is for the sounds and music to be at the same level as well... and EVERYTHING makes sounds which quickly makes any sense of a masterpiece sounds like noise.

[0/10] STORY & IMMERSION
There is no story or lore. The characters are one-dimensional and add nothing to the plot. 

[1/10]
The combat is horrible and lacks any thought. Every enemy seems to have a RNG associated with it's attacks and movement. Bosses will increase or decrease their range at will; seemingly target random areas; or will use completely different attack patterns. The combat is also very clunky, slow, and unreliable. Every hit you make on an enemy or boss feels more like a task than being worth it. There's no payoff after killing most bosses either.

This could be the best bad game I’ve played. It’s so good at masking how bad it is, it took me almost 60 hours to realize that. I’m taking a break from it just because of how annoying everything is. Especially traveling around. I mean, I could beat Symphony of the Night with a 100% completion in about the same time it takes to travel to the vendor to repair a broken charm.

At first, there were some annoyances like bad game mechanics which you have to negate with charms, or bad level design like these huge areas with randomly scattered platforms. But the more you play, the more you realize how much time is spent on absolutely nothing. 

The way the story is presented doesn’t work. All these little bits and hints, the hidden NPCs that exist just to tell you a few lines. Storytelling like that works in games like Demon’s Souls and Dark Souls but there’s a limit to how much atmosphere and immersion you can get from a 2D game. Just give me a bad guy to kill and I’m good to go.

Hollow Knight has some good parts like enemy variety, boss battles, platforming segments, some standard metroidvania elements, but the game would need to be dismantled, rearranged, and rebuilt to make something that can be enjoyed. It’s like they had the puzzle pieces but combined them in the worst possible way.

It's actually surprised me to see that many positive reviews on this game, so I decided to take a look. I've played many 2D & metroidvania games. This game has unique atmosphere, beautiful graphics, interesting lore and story. These are excellent features, yes. But, these features are far from making up for its poor & repetitive combat system and boring grind style. To unlock new skills and abilities your only way is to grind in the underground, get back to the merchant in town and buy stuff from that merchant. Each time you die, you lose all your loot (coins) and keep grinding the same places again and again because dying is super easy and the game is ridiculously unforgiving in that aspect. I say ridiculously, because it really is ridiculous. Most of the time you die to spikes, simple enemies and monotonous traps due to lack of mobility. I can say that it has more of a souls-like concept. Not that I don't like grinding, because I do. But, it shouldn't be this way in my opinion. Grinding in this game just feels dull, boring and unrewarding. Anyone who's interested in 2D & Metroidvania should give it a try. But, overall, it is not my cup of tea.

A great Metroidvania game, maybe the best I've ever played. The thing that surprised me the most: The game world is incredibly huge! There is so much to explore, so much to find, so much to simply [b]see[/b], just for the fun of it, because the art design is amazing, and so is the music! The fact that the developer team added four free (!) expansions to the already gigantic base game shows that this must have been a true labour of love for them. 

I also liked the elements from "Dark Souls" that were added to the classic formula. Apart from the more obvious ones (the game is comparatively hard and you have to return to your ghost after you die or lose all your 'money'), there's also the organic way in which the entire world is interconnected through countless unlockable shortcuts, and most of all the deep lore which seeps through every little detail you encounter on your journey. 

All in all, this game is a work of art and can hold its own against the best of the genre - and that at the low price of 15€. Nine out of ten, easily.

I never wrote a review before, but I think that this game truly deserve this effort.
In one word, this game is AMAZING ! 
From the unique hand-drawn graphisms to the near perfect music (i'm gonna speak about it later), you can only feel attracted to this twisted but surprising world, which is extremely well crafted, by a clever way. It may seems weird to say that a game is clever in its building, but it is, and as a result it's incredibly fun to play as a gamer. You have a lot of thing to find by yourself, and the game rewards you for your patience and the efforts you put in your exploration, always. And you also have a lot of ways to go from a place A to B, which means that everyone will have his own experience with the game, in the way of discovering it. The player experience is in consequence unique to every gamer, which is a very strong feeling, you feel as you playthought is unique ! In the end you lose yourself exploring this world and finding every hidden secret without paying attention to the real time anymore, cause it's immersive and fun. To add to this, platforming section are pretty cool, classic but working pretty well with a perfect responsivity from the Knight, your ingame character.
To speak about the combat system, it is a pure success : challeging, sometimes (very) hard but fair, thight and intense. Simplicity and timing are here the keywords, and it works pretty well ! Sharing the souls bar with healing and offensive abilities is a very good idea, and having a kind of casting time on the heal gives a strategic approach to every encounter that I appreciate very well. Futhermore every area of the game as plenty of ennemies to defeat. Really the bestiary is very large ! And bossfight are really cool as well ! 
The story and lore are sometimes hard to get, but if you feel involved in this game, you'll understand all you need to know (and trust me it's a good plot), and youtube is full of videos if you still have any questions after your playthrought or if you wanna theorize :) 
If I speak about the graphisms, I will describe them in one word : charming. It is really eye-friendly and rich, with a lot of details, expecially in the various environments. Each zone has a unique atmosphere, both visually and melodically.
Which is a perfect transition for the audio of the game. Sound design is excellent, and the soundtrack is simply perfect. Some of the themes in the game are just among my favorite of all time. Hornet and (a zone I won't spoil you the name) theme are masterpiece composed by a genius !
With that much effort put in from the developers to give you such a quality game, my only regret is to have paid only 10 € for this gem, and i'm gonna buy a copy of the game for some of my friends to share with them one of the best game I have ever played !
Team Cherry, I wish you the best and sincerely hope I would have the opportunity to play with another of your game in the future !

I think I've played through quite a lot of the game now, at about 106% completion, with only the trial of the fool and the pantheon stuff left to do. But I've now come to realise that through all the frustration and repetition of learning boss sequences, I'm truly not enjoying myself one bit! Putting myself through it has been akin to self harm and like anyone with an addictive personality, I couldn't stop when I was playing it in lock down. Now that I'm not stuck at home I'm back to casual gaming and the thought of putting myself through this horror show with my little free time fills me with dread. So I'm going to leave it there as far as end game content goes.
I've also come to realise the game offers absolutely no reward for your sweat and frustration, only the ability to feel superior to other nerds on the interweb that are having a hard time with a boss you've managed to get through. Never in all of the hours I played did my character suddenly feel strong, or a challenge give a reward that made a big difference. By this point in any other game, you'd probably be able to one-shot most of the common enemies and your movement would be advanced and fast, making travel easy and less annoying. But here the knight still feels like a slug that can die to a common enemy just as easily as the start of the game. 
The charms are mostly junk and unrewarding, and you have to use up most of your charm slots for basic things that should be given to you as progress rewards. Things that MINIMALLY improve your speed, agility, attack speed and nail length should not stop you from equipping the more fun and interesting charms! These things should be given to you permanently after enduring a wanker of a boss that's made you want to punch your TV for the last hour, at least then you might feel some sense of achievement! The fact you have to use a slot just to see where you are on the map is some serious developer trolling.
The sequence of the game for me has generally been to equip all the charms that make movement and attack speed slightly more bearable, mindlessly wander the same areas for the billionth time looking for the next thing to do, with no clue as to what's going on or what the story is even about. Then when I stumble into a boss area, get pummelled because I don't yet know his attack pattern, go and change all my charms to something that might help, before learning the pixel perfect attack sequence required to beat him. Then, after gaining my reward (of fack all) and changing back to all my original charms I continue the hopeless hunt for more nothingness.

Overall, I would say this game is for those guys that like to have their balls booted by a woman in stilettos.

This game is a Metroidvania-style platformer with a world that tells you its story not only as you explore, but as you progress. Wonderful upgrade mechanics as well as customization for passive effects that change the way you play.

WELL worth $15. I happen to get it on the Steam Summer Sale, but if you're thinking about buying it, $15 is 100% worth it. It took me about 24 hours to beat the game, but I'm only at 77% completion. Apparently it actually goes up to 106% - not entirely sure what the 6% is. 
 I still have not only completionist things to do, like getting all of the charms or finding all the grubworms, but also actual content to pursue - they've updated it with 2 major patches at no cost to the players, AND a 3rd one is supposed to come out some time in the next few months - again to no cost. After that, they're going to do a paid DLC that adds a second playable character - one who you already encounter in the game. We don't know of any plans after that; it could be a new game entirely, or more updates to this one.

What I'm trying to say is, this 2-man indie developer duo did an AMAZING job and clearly love the world they created, and the players that support them. They deserve far more support than any AAA company out there right now, and I look forward to their future endeavors. This game also just came out on Switch a month ago, so if you're interested you can check it out there.
Okay, I underestimated this game at first but this game is crazy good. Hollow Knight is very different from Dark Souls, yet similar in a lot of aspects. Just as good, if not better.

Only play this if you are already a seasoned gamer. It is 90% combat and has sooo many boss fights that are ridiculously hard. Be prepared to die at least 10 times for some of the medium level bosses and probably more times later in the game. The other annoying thing is that when you die, there's only specific save spots you go back to so sometimes you have to work your way through a dangerous area just to get back to where you were and then fight a ghost of yourself AND the boss which you have to start from scratch with. 
I will say the world building was very interesting and fun to explore but the fact that you had to die over and over again to unlock the majority of the maps made it too difficult to be fun anymore. 
So, if you don't mind repetitively dying and possibly losing all your money and sanity, then you might like this game. But for someone like me who needs more wins to feel encouraged to continue - then this probably isn't for you.

Hollow Knight is a pretty traditional metroidvania but that doesn't stop it from being a very special game that I would hold up to the classics of the genre.

The world of Hallownest is the main draw of the game, it's ancient and fallen kingdom is full of strange areas to explore and lore that will make you want to uncover it's secrets. The unique map mechanic of the game lets you explore naturally, using landmarks and routes to navigate instead of staring at a blocky map screen like many games in this genre. 

The controls are super responsive and fluid, which is great to use against the game's huge variety of enemies and bosses (both story and optional side bosses). The amount of content in this game for 15 dollars is insane. One of the easiest recommendations I could make.

Everything about this game is beautiful. From the art, OST, smooth and fluid movment and gameplay. Everything about this game is to be admired.

I am a huge fam of Dark Souls, so seeing how many elements from HK are shared with it is really cool to see. 

Only being a short while into this game, I ca safly say and with confidence that this is a $15 game with $60+ worth of content. I love this game and everything about it. If you love beautifully hand draw 2D side scrollers with excelent controls and an emersive world, then Hollow Knight is for you.

I feel like this game is polarizing. I want to like this. I'm not its intended audience though, I can see that much. I liked the Soulsborne games, Surge and the like. The objectives feel better communicated (granted they are pretty poorly conveyed in Souls games, but at least you just kind of 'follow the path ahead' and something destroys you. 

With this, I'm utterly lost. It ticks all the right boxes. It's pretty, innovative graphics, and good audio. I can't for the life of me figure out what I'm supposed to do, and to be honest at this point (Crystalised Mount and Howling Cliffs), I kind of don't care. 

Don't get me wrong, for the right person, I think this would be awesome. There are some cool gimmicks in this, and there are lots of different ways to 'the objective' at the same time, it isn't clear what your objective is. 

For instance, I tried to get to City of Tears...why I don't know, that was what the guide told me. For some reason, my character didn't have the City Pass, despite beating the knight that should have dropped it. I went through the trouble of getting the glowworm, the tram pass and going the back end through the Royal Waterways and coming up to the City of Tears. Kudos for the game for having the option. At the same time, finally getting there, I'm like 'why did I even do this'. I don't like it in games when I'm basically feeling, 'I did it because the guide told me so'. 

This is how the game feels to me. 

For some context, I'm heavily time poor. I like to play games to unwind, not because I'm trying to piece together a puzzle. This game's not hard, its just byzantine and confusing. There are probably ten year old kids that can speed record beat this blind folded in 20mins...great for them. I don't have the time to invest in dissecting this. I prefer my games a little more straight forward. 

If I were to score this for its target audience (kids who like complicated puzzles), I would say, as a game its 8/10...it presents well, and if its 'for you' you'd love it. 

At this point though, I wouldn't recommend this. I don't think this is fun. This is misdirected, confusing, and there are no markers to really guide you. Sure you can speak with cornifex, and get maps and markers and all that sort of jazz. That said, there's no apparent point to fighting bosses. You can do anything in any order, bosses don't really mean anything to anyone. They are just there. They aren't gatekeepers that are holding the area to ransom, and there is no celebration once done. There's no real life or celebration in there. For me, with how its put together, this feels like a 6/10 personally. 

Hopefully that makes sense. Stuff you love is stuff 'you love'. This is a game that isn't for everyone. I'd watch some gameplay vids before getting this. Just see if it gels with you...it definitely didn't for me, and I'm halfway through (and failing to see what we are trying to accomplish).

This game is "not for everyone", in my opinion this game is not for most people. This game is for a very specific kind of person: somebody who is patient, insightful, thorough, perseverant.
Just like sodomy, a minority of people seem to be really, really into it. If you are really, really sure that you are one of those people, then by all means...

The gameplay, like sodomy, is rough at first. This is part on purpose, because this game makes you work for everything: checkpoints, abilities, gear... everything. At the beginning, all you have is a laughably short attack and a ridiculously bad jump and... not even dash, man, not even dash.

Such diabolical and tortuous level design is being cleverly camouflaged behind beautiful hand-drawn art and music. In the aforementioned metaphor of being pounded in the rear, consider music and graphics as the proverbial vaseline that allows the player to push through unnecessarily long and difficult challenges.

The gameplay improves as one advances. The levels get better, the graphics and bosses get better, the music gets better.
It is safe to say that the best content of this game, by far, is in the second half. It is also safe to say, by looking at the achievements, that the vast majority of players will never get to that content. The music for Gods and Nightmares is amazing, a jump in quality even by this games' standards. It is also playing in missions hidden under achievements that 10%-15% of players are getting. My recommendation: just get the damn OST and be done with it.

This is what surprises me about Hollow Knight: people like [b]the idea of Hollow Knight[/b], not so much playing the game, it seems. People like to talk about Hollow Knight. Write about Hollow Knight. People like maybe [b]watching somebody else play[/b] Hollow Knight.

Who knows, with enough lubricant, you too might get to play the game and like it. But don't say I didn't warn you.

Even within the "metroidvania" genre, I think there are more enjoyable games out there. Here are some ideas:

- Checkpoints should be next to the bosses' spawning points. Many games already do this. In Hollow Knight, there are lots of bosses that don't have a checkpoint anywhere near. Cool that the boss is a skill challenge, but getting to the boss from the checkpoint is just tedium, it doesn't really add to the game.

- Hollow Knight demands incredible reaction times, but can offer poor performance on very crowded scenarios. The developers went too over the top, and Unity shows its limitations as an engine, even on rather powerful hardware. Other Unity games get around performance issues by having pixelated graphics.

- Improve the contrast in the color palette: those small enemies that are the exact same color as the background can go f- themselves. Those small enemies that have almost the same appearance as the protagonist can go f- themselves.

- Game saves lost after switching to another PC. And "Steam cloud sync" decided to delete my saves in my main PC. Unbelievable gymkana to figure out where the game save files actually are. This cannot happen in such a long and difficult game.

It's just another disappointment. The game looks really cool, the art style is good, but the game just sucks.

[h1]
Overall Score: 9/10
Worth buying? Yes
Recommended Input: Controller
[/h1]

[h1] Abstract [/h1]

For a while now, I've been a fan of platformers, both 2D & 3D: from the SNES' Super Metroid, to the PS2's Sly Cooper trilogy, to Celeste & Dead Cells on PC. They always tickled some sense of enjoyment, a thrill of exploration. However, there was always something that was left unsaid, some... itch that needed scratching.

Hollow Knight was the treatment to my ailment. The scratch for my itch, as it were. (Sorry, it sounded better in my head)

Hollow Knight brought to me some sort of unfounded magic that I never thought existed. From beginning to end, Hollow Knight was a gripping adventure, sucking me in like a black hole. With a dedicated team of developers & the DLC they release, I'm always crawling back to the game for fear that I missed something new & exciting.

[h1] Pros [/h1]

[u]Art:[/u] All of the art for this game was hand-drawn, much like Jotun, Cuphead, & Darkest Dungeon. The stunning settings of each region in Hallownest were nothing short of breathtaking; There was a solid level of detail for platforms & backgrounds, things like grass & dust being kicked up by walking & dashing were a nice touch, & hanging vines rustling when walking past them gives the player that sense of tangibility. The characters of Hallownest are cute, yet elaborate & dynamic. For example:
[list]
[*]The Elderbug is the oldest resident of Dirtmouth & a friendly face for new travelers seeking the Kingdom. He's wistful of how silent & empty Dirtmouth has become, what with the citizens all exploring the Kingdom.
[*]Quirrel is a strange lad with a strong sense of adventure, yet troubled by a lack of remembrance of who he really is. He's always happy to see the Knight on his adventures through Hallownest.
[*]Cloth is a timid warrior with locked-away courage begging to be teased out; as you go out of your way to cross paths with her, she awakens that bravery from how much you inspire her... and she's so stinkin' cute the whole time, too.
[*] Iselda & Cornifer are the helpful, doting couple in Hallownest, supplying the Knight with invaluable maps, map pins, & Charms (I'll explain those soon) to aid him on his journey.
[*]Zote is the personification of everything you want to punch in the face. He's pigheaded, annoying, boorish, & condescending.
[/list]

The designs of Hollow Knight's monsters are also a treat, ranging from "Aww, it's adorable! It couldn't hurt a soul!" to [i][b]"DEAR LORD IT'S DISGUSTING KILL IT KILL IT KILL THE FUCKING THING"[/b][/i]. This mix-n-match of design, along with the fact that there are well over a hundred different monsters across Hallownest, keeps the player on their toes; it makes them think a little in combat rather than blindly mashing the attack button. Nice to see Team Cherry say 'No' to the tedious monotony of "Hooray, there's only 7 different types of enemies...."

[u]Music:[/u] Christopher Larkin's composition of Hollow Knight's soundtrack (Which you can find [url=https://christopherlarkin.bandcamp.com/album/hollow-knight]right here[/url]) is beyond diction. If it was anyone else, I don't think this game would have the same enjoyment factor. One thing for sure is that the game's original soundtrack was capable of bringing the player that sense of danger, excitement, or fear that should ensue when entering a new area or fighting a new boss. Lemme explain:
[list]
[*]The Forgotten Crossroads forges determination in an ominous, abandoned land, with the husks of Hallownest's bugs resurrected in a fit of bloodlust. It incites you to see your mission through, even if you didn't quite know what it is at the moment.
[*]Greenpath generates that feeling of exploration & wonder, fueled only by curiosity, as the flora & fauna skitter about in lush greenery. The monsters don't pose much a threat, yet the walls of thorns & pools of acid will test your platforming.
[*]Deepnest drills fear & paranoia into the player, as all around you are the scattered carcasses of Hallownest's denizens, with predatory spiders & pools of parasites itching to make you into their next meal.
[*]The City of Tears crafts an eerily beautiful, sorrowful setting as you wander the remnants of a bustling town reduced to a hotbed of reanimated husks, all caught in an endless downpour.
[*]The Abyss is pure, chronic, & sustained dread. The very air around you wails "GET OUT", & grows ever powerful as you descend; when you finally hit the bottom, the sense that you don't belong could crush diamonds to dust.
[/list]

There are many more examples of these moments, but then we wouldn't get anywhere with this review if I listed them.

[u]Gameplay:[/u] Now, I know this is going to make me sound like a game journalist, but the game can be boiled down to "Dark Souls: A Bug's Life Edition," While it's an accurate trivialization of the game, subjecting it to such abstraction just puts the game in a terrible light. Team Cherry does do the Soulslike label justice, with the mechanic of "in Normal Mode, you lose your stuff if you die, so respawn & get it." You can regain it all if you defeat the ghost of your past life; but if you die again, a new ghost forms, & the old one with your stuff is gone forever. In Steel Soul Mode, though, it's just permadeath. That alone will makes your gameplay & decision-making safer than the gold in Fort Knox.

Platforming in this game makes you come up with ingenious ways to get to different spots. As your progress through the game, you can dash in mid-air, double-jump, & even do nigh-endless charged dashes to unexplored regions, which, in a platformer that falls under the "Metroidvania" label, you're gonna be doing a lot of, but will be rewarded for.

The combat in Hollow Knight is fair. I say fair because the Knight suffers from short range & knockback from landing successful hits on enemies. The SOUL meter mechanic makes you have to choose between casting spells at the cost of healing, or healing at the cost of dealing spell damage. Boss fights don't pull punches either, forcing you to think on your feet or get bopped real quick.

Charms give the Knight combat buffs, movement buffs, or just quality-of-life buffs. You have a specific amount of Charm Notches on you that you can raise throughout the game. Each Charm you get has a special trait, whether it be temporary HP, dealing extra damage, increasing your running speed, or make reading the map less of a headache. However, Charms cost a certain number of Notches. But, by trying 5 times to equip a charm, you'll equip it & become Overcharmed, meaning that you'll take double damage from everything; only do this with a high-cost Charm & if you can read bosses harder than a college textbook.

[h1] Cons [/h1]

[u]Pacing:[/u] Hollow Knight's lore is actually up for the players' interpretation. Thus, there's no proper lore, only hints given through various sources. No assumptions can be made, & in what fashion these hints are pieced together in is totally for the player to decide. While I appreciate that creative approach to storytelling... Hollow Knight's pacing is slower than I would like. The game only really picks up after 90 minutes to 2 hours of play, which I honestly am not comfortable with; & your main objective is given to you after... what, ~7 hours? For players new to the genre, let alone the game, 7 hours before you figure out what the hell you're really supposed to do besides explore & bash some heads in can seem unappealing to most players, discouraging them from continuing.

[h1] Conclusion [/h1]

All in all, I give Hollow Knight a well-deserved 9/10. Solid gameplay, gorgeous soundtrack, enthralling art & characters, & diverse monster pool; however, the pacing of the story is much slower than what I'd like.

Nevertheless: Thank you, Team Cherry. This beautiful game is a testament to your hard work & dedication. I look forward to further work from you.

I picked up this game a while ago, and thought nothing much of it at the time. I played through the first bit of it, and had a good time doing so. It rubed off as a many a metroid game before, so when it sayed it needed to update I left it in the queue and played other things for a while. When I finaly decided to update what I thought was just way to large of a file. I found that the developers just dumped a huge free DLC in the game, and there seems to be one more with a new playable character in the near future. This respectable behavior made me curious as to what all lies within in the deapths of Hollownest. Thus I found that the game, while nothing more than a metroid vania at first glace, has much more to offer. I stoped playing just before you meet the first nugget of story, and one of the best boss fight I fought in a long time. And the game procedes to please long after that.

The gameplay consists of swinging you sword(which is called a nail, cause they're bugs. Cute, no?). And collecting a myriad of helpful items. You can upgrade your sword a few times if you find certain hunks of ore, and there are a variety of spells that can be enhaced once for each, in differant locations. There's a few health and soul(basicly mana) upgrades that work like pieces of heart from zelda. And charms, of which there are a whole lot, and their effects ranging from making you crap smaller versions of yourself to kamakase enemies, to being able to turn into a speedy ass snail when healing. It's exploration and combat focused, and provides places with some tough chalange. I can say that the platforming, while smooth, is often painful. There's actualy a long and dificult optional area called the path of pain. They ain't kidden around with this stuff. Spikes and your own slow reflexes are your worst enemy.

Story wise, there's a suprisingly intricate and engaging story behind the arthropods and fungi you encounter. Something about bug gods, and an infection, you'll get the idea once you're about half way through. it's done in a nice way, making you curious about things, and letting you discover at your own pace. There's oftain a boss in front of anything story relaited, but there are very few bosses that I dislike fighting in here. Although, as a guy who tend's to cheese his way through things, I can't say there's a lot of room for that. It allows me to "git gud" so to speak, so I'm not complaining.

Oh, did I mention the graphics? The whole game is  nothing but those small gross looking things that crawl all over the place and get in your food. But they look so damn cute in this! It's truly adorable. part from character design, the backgrounds are stunning, and the way things are animated and colored allows you to pick out what you can interact with, what you'll pass though, and what you'll die from. It allows for some very clear, yet very detailed levels. Making it possable to do something perfectly the first time you try it. Only to fail the next five times, but hey! It's only my lack of shkilz that's getting me, not the way a platform looks just like the background.

So my rating would be:
Gameplay: 10/10
Story:         8/10
Graphics:   10/10
Music:        8/10
Bosses:      8/10

Overall: 9/10

Starting to get kinda meh...

The game starts off promising enough... appearing to be a metroidvania-style game with some Dark Souls elements sprinkled in.  

For about the first third of the game or so, I was pretty captivated and enjoyed my experience... but things slowly started to go sour as i moved forward.

One of the things that let me down the most would have to be the extremely shallow/niche remaining upgrades.  
The crystal dash, which I had long since anticipated after having seen the game tease you with the large gaps of spikes... ended up being such a disappointing power-up... that is so rarely used outside of "hey you gotta cross this giant gap of spiked floor/ceiling" that it makes me wonder why it was even included in the game at all.

The monarch wings (double jump) was locked behind the crystal dash, but i felt as if it was something that should have been given to the player much earlier, seeing as how the crystal dash serves next to no purpose.

The acid tear? (i forget what its called but the item that allows you to swim in acid water)  was another huge disappointment... The upgrade had been teased  in a few locations with passages filled with acid water... but thats it... you can swim through the acid... there was no additional layer of gameplay added.

Shade cloak was a bit better... and while there are some passages blocked to the player until they get this, it also acts as a dodge... which again is something that should have been in the game much much earlier, imho.

About half of the upgrades ended up being very disappointing additions, or should have been there from the beginning (I'm looking at you wall-jump and shade cloak) or at very least given to the player much sooner.
It felt like they wen't belly up on the upgrades, and that's why they waited to give you some of them for so long.

That being said, lets move on and talk about the bosses...

The very first moment I started disliking the game was when I stumbled upon the Flukemarm hidden boss fight.  Very lazy boss design, I thought.  The boss literally has one move, and that is to spawn two of the (up until that point) most annoying mobs (that are more like homing projectiles), and it just spams the "move" repeatedly.  ...but okay one boss out of the lot missing the mark isn't so bad I thought... 
but flukemarm was just the tip of the iceberg...

It seemed that in the developer's attempt to make bosses challenging, they were having considerable difficulty... particularly with boss fights that can be revisited (such as dream variations).   Their solution?  just add an endless spawn of random cancer in the boss room.  Take The False Knight dream fight for example... there is randomly spawned shit falling from the ceiling endlessly... The collector?  I don't remember him actually using any attacks other than summoning trash mobs 2-3 at a time.    Broken vessel?  yep... an add spawns every second.   Soul Tyrant?  Not really too bad until you get to phase two... then what the actual fuck?  The projectile spam is back in full force.  (Sometimes the projectiles spawn in behind you in a split second... or directly behind the boss where you can't possibly see them before they hit you)  

Most of these encounters are so bad that its not even possible to heal mid-fight unless you get lucky with rng.

So I didn't like the direction the game was going with the boss encounters... but I pushed on... and that's when I met Grimm's Troupe and started doing the little flames/charm quest.  

I fought Grimm and really thought the fight was one of the better-designed... but was just astonished that they included a literal bullet hell attack... like wtf?  Is this game trying to be cuphead or something?

...and that's when I realized that the game was indeed trying to be cuphead... the same way it was trying to be castlevania... and dark souls... and later still I discovered it trying to be Super Meat Boy... and overall the game just has a massive identity crisis... 

Instead of trying to wear seven different hats... they should have just stuck with one and done it well...
Instead they went half-assed on several different game genres and the game ended up feeling very mediocre.

I don't like cuphead... and I don't like Meatboy... and I'm honestly regretting getting the game at this point.
I'll continue on... but it seems like every new direction this game takes a turn in is one for the worse.

I sincerely hope that's it as far as curve balls in design are concerned... but I'll update if I find anything else I don't like.

I'm so disappointed.
I had heard so much about this game, so I'll admit I might have had my hopes a bit high.  I've only played an hour, but that hour was one of the worst of my life.  To me, the game feels like a poor platformer with a combat system hastily thrown in.  First, I'll explain why I don't like the platforming aspect of this game.

I'll admit, the controls do feel somewhat smooth.  That's about the only compliment I can give.  It's all ridiculously easy, so the developers decided to ramp up the "difficulty" with the occasional blind fall that, should you fail to land on the extremely small platform, you will land on an enemy or some liquid that deals you damage.  This game has a vast world that you can explore, but every place feels the exact same.

The combat is also extremely lackluster.  Honestly, there's no reason not to just spam your basic sword attack while exploring, as enemies will sometimes appear from the ground directly in front of you, dealing damage to you because you don't have time to react.  Constantly spamming the sword helps you deal with this, as when you hit an enemy, it will push you and them away from each other.  The ranged attacks are also extremely powerful, but are balanced with a charge system.  The few bosses I have fought have been extremely easy and have all had pretty much the same gimmicks.  Overall, you shouldn't be taking damage from enemies at all in this game.  The combat is that easy and straightforward.

Everything said, I might continue for a bit longer.  I do understand that the game might pick up after a bit, but I can at least say without a shadow of doubt that the beginning of this game might be the worst game I have ever played.

Hollow Knight is a metroidvania, a 2d platformer with beautiful graphics and amazing story. It's not easy to describe just how perfect this game has been, and will be, in every aspect.

[h1]Story[/h1]
[b]10/10:[/b] The story goes much deeper than surface level, and the more you learn is the more you decide to play in-game. The sense of progression in the story is driven by the progression made in the game. Even I, despite having 72 hours in the game (at the time of writing the review), haven't grasped the full extent to the lore of the universe yet.

[h1]Art[/h1]
[b]10/10:[/b] The assets used in the game are incomprehensibly beautiful, and through building rooms, increases the feeling of depth in the platformer, either through background items that are interactive, particle effects, and detailed walls and backgrounds. The walls are decorated with small details, as with all the other sprites in the game. Particle effects that fill the room create an atmosphere that can make an area feel calm and serene or dangerous and dark.

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]
[b]10/10:[/b] Double jumps, dashes, dives, and other gameplay mechanics make this game a joy to control. Despite this, it is said that using a controller provides a much better experience. I used a keyboard to complete the game, and even though I did, it didn't feel any worse to control. This feels how you want all platformers to feel, no floaty feeling at all.

[h1]Bonus Point[/h1]
[b]1/1:[/b] The combination of the beautiful graphics, responsive controls, and mystical story work together amazingly.

[h1]Final Score[/h1]
[b]31/31[/b]

[h1]Do you recommend this game?[/h1]
Yes, for only $15, this game is insanely cheap for the amount of content it has provided and continues to provide, with 3 free DLCs already released, this is a game that keeps on giving. This is a must have game, and no doubt any amount of years from now will not change the amazement of playing the game.

(DLC not considered)


Platforming & Combat -


Hollow Knight is pretty challenging, not just in the combat but in terms of 'platform' gameplay as well. 

I personally found that some of the platforming elements needed a lot of concentration/ precision; although, when you do complete those segments there's a real pay off (in terms of in-game reward and receiving a hit of dopamine). Not much variation in terms of obstacles though but I wouldn't say that's a negative. However, if you're into 'octane' platforming with plenty of surprises that is something to consider. 

The combat isn't necessarily something to write home about. It's simple with a few varying abilities you can pick up along the way. Again, Hollow Knight can be quite challenging; requiring high levels of focus here and there. I certainly enjoyed boss battles (when I was winning) but they could become a little dull. In fairness, there is still a very fulfilling feeling you get when you succeed in the combat of Hollow Knight; from the grunts to the bosses, there is always a feeling of - "yay, I did it". 



Art farty stuff - 

The art work and atmosphere are brilliant; if you're into the arts I highly recommend it. The scenery, the landscape and the music make Hollow Knight a real pleasure to sit and explore. Honestly, the whole theme sets the game aside from other 'Metroidvania' games. There's a lot to see in terms of imagery, truly. 

It's important to realise the game isn't quick and jovial, it's slow and somber. The art style is representative of the overall story. Despite a cute appearance. 


Story, possible *SPOILER*, not really - 


I won't say much about the story, aside from there's a very 'sacrificial lamb' kinda vibe. I wouldn't say it put me off but it did wear thin after awhile. If you like that kind of thing or you can push past the repitition you may really enjoy the story. Characters are a little lacking, or at least that's what I found. 



If you have a bad temperment - 

If you get frustrated easily I would say this isn't the game for you. There are points (specifically in the combat) that can become agitating. So if; "uhhh, no way did that hit me" is your mantra, don't buy Hollow Knight. It's definitley the kind of game you just gotta say: "yep, it got me" and try again.



What's the verdict - 

In my humble opinion it's a good game. I recommend it; overall the content is enjoyable.



P.S -

Pros: sound and animation are great

Cons: Here's the paragraph. The .... story isn't sure if it wants to show itself? You're a guy killing things in a ruin. Something about seals we should care about. 
It might get better but the painful grind of backtracking to where you die just killed it for me. 
There's a difference between 'challenging' and 'punishing' and this definitely falls into punishing.
Often when you are trying to learn patterns and die you'll spend waaaayyyy more time just getting back to the spot you died at than the actual attempt. 
A lot of the game time is padded out this way. I've had to spend more 3x more time just killing things for money or backtracking than exploring new content.
It is definitely not clear where you need to go next a lot of the time. Are you supposed to do a weird jump, down attack glide to get across (where you can try and die a dozen times) or do you have to wait for some boss encounter on the other side of the map to get a mcguffin?

Another niggle which now seems small in comparison is you lose all your cash if you die twice in a row. People go 'oh don't worry, later you'll be swimming in it'. Which is fine except money is kinda XP for upgrades so you're essentially losing XP that you'll have to grind again. And there's a couple big ticket items you HAVE to save for so you'll be real annoyed when you pop like a balloon.

Last year was a great year for games. Cuphead, Sonic Mania, Prey, Nier: Automata, Super Mario Odyssey, OneShot (I know it's a 2016 game, but it was a pretty late release)... and that's just looking at some of the stuff I bought! Still, Hollow Knight stood out for me.

I have to admit it's partly because it appeals to my tastes. I have a thing for platformers, bleak environments, high-skill combat and cute things. This game has all that, plus on astounding amount of love which you can feel in every little detail. Every nook and cranny seems crafted with the utmost care, nothing feels out of place. The only other games that have ever given me this impression was Derek Yu's Aquaria in 2007, and Pixel's Cave Story in 2004.

In the end, this is such a tight package it's hard to pick faults... I would guess its only noticeable problem is the slow pacing in the beginning. It quickly picks up after [spoiler]you get the dash upgrade[/spoiler], though, so enduring that slow start is absolutely worth it.

Some other games this reminds me of you should take a look at if this interests you:
[*] Dark Souls series (similar vibes with a focus on timing and reaction in combat)
[*] Momodora: Reverie under the Moonlight (very similar game, smaller scope)
[*] Aquaria
[*] Cave Story

Only recommend this game if you are a masochist who enjoys wasting their time. Pretty stylized graphics with great music and good combat that is ultimately ruined by frustrating mechanics that unnecessarily punish players. Namely, arbitrary save locations that aren't anywhere near challenging sections and permanent gold loss if you die again trying to retrieve your loot.

Die to a boss? Enjoy making your way through the entire area all over again because there's no save points near the fight. Better hope you don't die a second time on your way there, or the gold you were carrying disappears forever.

Attempt a challenge and die partway? Same thing. Make your way back there from the "closest" save point and hope you don't die attempting to retrieve your loot. You now have no choice but to complete the challenge or give up on your loot.

Sure you can store your gold in a bank, but there's only one location that I've found, and I don't want to have to back track every time I want to attempt anything interesting.

It took me a while to think of what I wanted to say, and how to properly articulate it. But I think I know what I want to say now. I'd like to start this review by something something completely insane: I hate this game. I actually, truly, from the bottom of my heart, really hate this game. And that's crazy to me, because for all intents and purposes, I should have loved it. I love Metroidvania games, I love platformers, I love Dark Souls games (or used to, but that's another can of worms I'm not going to open). Everything about this game screamed "This is the game for you, it's perfect for you!" Hell, I even had friends tell me this game was right up my alley.

Hollow Knight is a hauntingly beautiful game, from its visuals, to its soundtrack, to its world building and character design. Everything in this game makes you want to get lost in it and explore, see what's around the next corner, and this is further enhanced by the incredible map making system included in the game. At first your map starts out completely empty, then looks like a kindergardner drew it, and you can't see yourself on the map. Navigation by the map, for the most part for early sections of the game, was navigation by memory on what you've seen, and by what things look familiar on your map. It was incredibly immersive, and dare I say addicting. You could get more tools back at the game's central hub town to improve your map making skills, and make navigation a hell of a lot easier, and watching your map grow in size always felt cathartic.

Which is why it's all the more heartbreaking that I didn't get any enjoyment out of this game. Maybe I'm just a filthy casual. Maybe I just couldn't appreciate the game for what it was. Maybe I was blinded by something else and couldn't truly look past what I perceived as faults. Maybe I started playing the game with the wrong impression of what exactly Hollow Knight was trying to be. To me, the game played "too perfectly". And what I mean by that, is that almost every platformer I have ever played has had some slight "clunk" to it. Movement would take just a fraction of a second to actually happen, jumping would be just VEEEEEEEEERY slightly imperfect. Jumping would only really have, 4-5 heights to it at most depending on how hard you pressed the jump button and for how long. Yet in Hollow Knight, it felt like jumping had infinite heights. It was so extremely precise, that it actually threw me off at how perfect the moving and jumping in this game was. I would constantly run into enemies, constantly make extremely novice mistakes I shouldn't be making, and constantly made the game harder for myself because of my lack of understanding of the underlying mechanics as a whole.

Hollow Knight does an excellent job at teaching the player how to play the game. While the game isn't ball crushingly hard, it still has some large degrees of difficulty to it. I feel like this is just a minor reason as to why my interest in playing this game was so minimal. I didn't want to return to the game and continue playing. I beat the first boss, and in my arrogance, wanted to explore as much as I could because in my mind, this was a Metroidvania game. However, this also plays like a Dark Souls game in terms of enemy variety and complexity, and you also have next to no health in the early game. So little health that most enemies will kill you in 3-5 hits depending on what they are. Luckily you have a way to heal yourself quite often, so that more than likely is 10 hits based on a technicality, but the fact remains that you are incredibly frail and will die quite easily if you find yourself just turning your brain off and trying to play it like a Metroid game. And this is the trap I fell into. I certainly believe without a doubt I had the wrong idea and impression of what Hollow Knight exactly was. The level design and the way progression works is almost identical to a Metroidvania, so it was easy for me to be fooled. But the enemy design and how they interact in combat, despite being a 2D platforming plane, is almost identical to how enemies interact in Dark Souls. What this essentially means is that exploration in Hollow Knight cannot be tackled like you would in a traditional Metroidvania. The two styles of play are counterintuitive towards each other and don't mix. If you try to run around and do whatever you please in Hollow Knight like in a Metroidvania, you will die. And you will die a lot. You have to take your time, examine your surroundings, and approach it like a Souls game.

And I think this is why I ended up not liking this beautiful, wonderful game. I approached it with the wrong mind-set, with the wrong way of thinking. And I ended up getting punished for it. I wanted to give this game a chance, to convince myself that I would love it. But the game never clicked with me, and in the end, I suffered as a result over my purchase and regretted getting the game, and was deeply saddened that I wanted to refund the game because I saw how much love, care, and time went into crafting this gem.

I suppose if anyone read this far, just know this: Hollow Knight is not a bad game. In fact, it's an amazing game. That's why I wholeheartedly recommend Hollow Knight. But it's an amazing game that just wasn't for me. 

Maybe you learned something by reading this, and this will help you decide to finalize your purchase, or prevent it from happening in the first place. I didn't really want to make such a long drawn out review like this, but I had so much on my mind and so much I wanted to say, that I felt like I needed to say it and put it somewhere.

I thought for sure I would love this game. I mean look at the trailers, the positive reviews, etc.
As someone who loved SotN, Ori, and other platforming/metroidvania games, Hollow Knight just let me down.
The graphics, soundtracks, combats, and especially the boss fights: these are all superb. Now with that out of the way, here are my main issues with the game and why I probably won't come back to it.

1 - Too much backtracking and wandering. Waste your time by going through the same places, killing the same enemies, again and again and again. Boredom and frustration guaranteed. There's no pretty art or music that can alleviate that, leading to issue number 2.

2 - No sense of progression while travelling. Killing the same monsters won't make you stronger, won't drop cool random items, they only give coins. And some of these maps with puzzles and monsters can be a challenge to go through, so it gives a feeling of all that work for nothing. 

3 - Too few save points and teleporting places. This can be an acceptable design choice when done right, but unfortunately it exacerbates issues 1 and 2. Or could be that BECAUSE of this, issues 1 and 2 exists. This is especially frustrating when you die in a boss fight. The bosses in this game are designed to be difficult and challenging, you have to go through numerous attempts to finally learn their pattern and beat them. So you will obviously die in the first few attempts, and you want to challenge it again right away, right? Too bad, the closest save point is almost 5 min away. Slog through the same map repeatedly just to try to beat the boss again. Is not like your time is precious right? I mean, allowing the player to restart the boss fight right away or placing a save point near him is too much handholding, I suppose. 

The game is good, but it is just not for me. The first few hours were a delight, because I haven't come across those issues yet. But then, it reared its ugly head. I feel like the game punishes the player in the wrong way.
Anyway, even though I didn't like it, I'll definitely see if the next game (Silksong) improves in these aspects.

For a game with so much depth to it, it feels pretty shallow. It fails horribly on ensuring you can navigate without cracking open a guide. This game also manages to make upgrades feel pretty weak and like crutches rather than boosts to your abilities.

The moves are simple and not complex to pull off but the usage is particular to bosses for the most part, some other moves work specifically for platforming but not much else.

Upgrades as I described above overall don't really make me feel stronger at all. Sure I'm doing more damage or have more health but it's not very engaging and at best only makes me feel VERY slightly better. I go from poking a boss like 50 times to maybe 40 or 30 times. I take an extra hit at best, maybe have some long range, faster healing, etc. But it's all boring and adds very little other than a requirement to make things less tedious to deal with.

I'm not excited for Hollow Knight: Silksong anymore, the amount of time waiting around gave me a chance to look for something better in the mean time and I have found it.

I came fairly late to the party, but I'll still leave a review. 

If you've haven't played this, find $15 and get this now.

I still haven't fully beaten the game, I'm going for the 106% completion, and I have enjoyed nearly every second of this game. For the content you get, it's not worth the cost, in my opinion, its worth far more. 

Pros:
+Stunning Visuals: An coloring style that bases itself around cool colors and the contrasts of orange (warm colors) makes this visually pleasing as well as the art style that has a "drawn" feeling, which is up for debate whether that's good or not, but I personally like it.

+Music: I make music if I'm not playing games, and the music in this game is enough to make me want to purchase the soundtrack. Seriously, if you haven't heard any of these tracks, I recommend listening to them before, during, and after playing this game.

+Story/Lore: Depending on how you want to look at the game, there isn't much in terms of story that's blatantly handed toward you, but rather subtle nods to what happened and why, as well as nearly a lifetimes worth of speculation and lorecrafting on the internet's and reddit's part. I would highly recommend experiencing the game before you delve into that territory though.

+Combat/Movement: My most played game on steam and in general is Warframe, a game with a pretty stellar movement and combat system as well as my favorite progression system in a game. While it isn't as fluid as Warframe, in my opinion, I would still regard it as a satisfying combat system, where you can make your combos and find different ways to take down enemies, it's very non-linear and I like that in a game. The movement is basically the same as combat, it leaves a lot for improvement and experiment. I've seen countless videos exploiting certain mechanics, like nailbouncing, to get to areas you normally would need a certain upgrade for (in my case it was getting to Hallownest's Crown with only a dash).

+Travel System: This is a fickle beast to take down since it either makes the game a loading screen simulator, like endgame Skyrim for many people, or it makes it too tedious to travel. This game did a really good job with it though, by making a checkpoint system for "Stag Stations" as well as a Tram System in mid to late game, which balances the playtime with the travel time. If you ever want to travel far, you have to atleast travel by foot for a little bit, although I find myself travelling by foot most of the time because that's just how I like to do it. 

+Devs: If there ever existed an opposite of EA, Team Cherry easily would be it. Not to bash on them, but they aren't as popular as EA is, or rather infamous, they don't share the greed of EA, as well as a lot of other top companies and corporations in the video game industry, and they had fairly humble beginnings on Kickstarter. This really was more of their passion project, and props to them for making this masterpiece, for only $15. 

+Free DLCs: I suppose I could've put this up on the Devs part, but I felt it needed a seperate section. At the time of posting this (8/3/2018), there are four confirmed fully free DLCs, which are Hidden Dreams, Grimm Troupe, Lifeblood, and Gods and Glory. While Gods and Glory hasn't released as of yet, I expect it to atleast yield similar results to Grimm Troupe, which is my favorite DLC. But knowing the devs, I wouldn't be surprised in the slightest if it blew my standards away.

Cons:
-Difficult: If you rage easily, this probably isn't the best game to play if you're in a bad mood. Otherwise just be patient and have some persistence and you can get through every part in the game, trust me its what I did. 

-Story Progression: This one isn't that bad, but it is kind of confusing if you don't know where to go on how to progress the story. I tried to blind run it, but ended up caving in to a couple of guides. My best advice is to just explore everywhere and fill out every map.

-Path of Pain: For you select few who know, you know. 

All in all, I would buy this game for double its price and still not feel like I was wasting any money on it. If you know someone who might enjoy this game, definitely recommend it or atleast bring it up. If you're willing to put some time in, it makes you develop your tactics and abilities naturally, instead of just teaching you linearly on how the game works.

If there's anything I left out of the pros and cons that you want to add, feel free to comment below what it is, and maybe I'll edit it in.

Hollow Knight is a 2D side-scrolling platformer metroidvania, though I don't think that was ever a question from any of the videos or screenshots.

However, what the videos and such might not convey as well, is just the sheer awesome that this game exudes. I am normally not a person who is into dark, edgy and emo stuff (not saying that this game is, but it could maybe be mistaken for that if one just looked at screenshots and video clips), and I would have to say that the aesthetic is quite nice.

You play as this little cute ... uh, not sure what he is. Is he a bug person like everybody else I've seen in the game thusfar? Maybe. Either way, he's short, has a huge horned skull (?) for a head and he uses the weirdest looking "sword" (called a "nail") I've ever seen.

All of the NPCs I've met thusfar are simply adorable in a weird way. They are all bug people, and usually bugs cause a "ick" reaction, but not these bugs. They were given semi-voice lines (they mumble things but don't actually talk) that is done in a very nice manner, and I find myself loving each NPC I come across, even a couple that turned out to be enemies, partly because of the awesome voice blurbles that they mumble.

So, with that said....

Graphics: 9/10 : They used this... hand-drawn art style. It's not pixel art like a lot of games, this is... different. They abuse the ever-living crap out of lighting and bloom effects and the resulting aesthetic is just pure awesome. There's a ton of atmosphere here, and graphics are half of the reason why. It's a beautiful game, and the art style is very unique.

Sound: 9/10 : .....and here's the other half of why the aesthetic is awesome. The wind sounds, the dripping of water, the echo in some of the caves... the game is chock full of ambience and atmosphere, even with the little sound details such as the pitter-patter of tiny little feet of your character, many enemies make subtle little sounds. This is a game you simply MUST play with a nice pair of headphones. Don't be afraid to turn the volume up. The background music I've found really fits the themes and the areas too.

Controls: 9/10 : Your character moves very fluidly. He does exactly what you tell him to 90% of the time. The remaining 10% of the time, I've found that occasionally (especially with wall-jumping) I thought that I Should have pulled a jump off, but didn't for whatever odd reason. But otherwise, I felt the controls to be very nice. There is a technical gripe I have, but I'll cover that later.

Challenge: 8/10: This is not a "1 is easy, 10 is super-hard" meter; I'm saying 8/10 as in, I feel the challenge is just right. Boss enemies are hard at first, but after a try or two, you should be able to learn their patterns and defeat them. I knocked 1 off here, because once you get the wall-jump, there are a few rooms that can be mildly frustrating becuase of small margin of error sending you back several jumps. It's a minor thing, and I've still enjoyed the game otherwise, outside of a few mumbled curses at a couple spike-heavy walljumping hallways. I think it wouldn't be as bad if the game treated spikes a little diffrently (more on that in the tech section).

Technical: 7/10: I knocked 2 points off on this, each for their own reason (and a third thrown in):

First, controller support is somewhat spotty. I have a DirectInput controller, and the game didn't see it with the beta controller detection turned off, or on. Then I tried x360ce, which captures DirectInput and turns it into XInput so that most programs that support XI instead of DI should see it, and indeed it works on most games. I turned controller detection on, restarted the game, and it saw it, though for some odd reason, it was not allowing me to rebind certain keys. I shut the game down to fiddle with x360ce to see if I could change the profile in there.... booted the game up... no controller detected, even with detection turned on. So, I just said "screw it", turned it off, and used Joy2Key instead which works fine.

Secondly, the way the game handles spikes is a bit wonky. In most games where spikes/bottomless pits are not instant-death, if you fall into one, you are usually teleported back to the last solid ground you stood on. Not so in Hollow Knight. You are teleported back to a *recent* solid object, not the last one. This means that sometimes you can make 2, 3 difficult jumps, touch spikes and get sent back to before those 2-3 difficult jumps to have to do them all over again. It can get mildly frustrating in a few rooms, especially those right after you get the wall jump ability.

And last, but certainly not least (this is my biggest gripe about this game), is the focus cast functionality. You have a SOUL meter. You use this SOUL energy to heal yourself (I suppose you get other spells that use this mechanic too? I haven't gotten that far yet). To do this, you press and hold the cast button. You will begin to drain SOUL until you release the button, hopefully after casting the spell, because if not... you lose the energy. Completely. I cannot count the number of times I went to heal myself, and either let go of the button half a second too early and lost an entire heal's worth of SOUL energy for nothing, or, held the button too long, healed myself and then spent just a smidge of SOUL energy afterwards.

It seems like such a clunky mechanic, I don't know if that's a bug or feature, but it seems to me, if you cancel the casting, you should either get refunded the SOUL energy, or, the SOUL energy should only be spent once the spell is actually cast. It's not a huge deal-breaker, but it does cause annoyances far more often than I would like.

OVERALL: 8/10

I heartly recommend it, particularly to people who enjoy Souls games, like Metroidvanias and are not discouraged by challenging platforming.

There isn't really anything that hasn't been said about this game already. Seriously, it's an instant classic metroidvania, just a quarter-inch below Super Metroid and Symphony of the Night on the grand scale. I'd go so far as to say I like it more than Axiom Verge, mainly because of the world.

The world-building is what sets this game apart from its peers. You are an insect in a world filled with husks, other insects and beautiful gothic architecture, at some points deep underground in what looks like a post-apocalyptic, subterranean wasteland. At no point do you feel like "the one", overpowered insect. This game can be tough. Be prepared to learn enemy hit patterns, you canNOT just swing wildly - and this serves the same purpose of a Dark Souls: it gives the combat meaning, and it leaves you satisfied and happy with your progress. The bosses and NPCs are memorable. Plenty of worthy extra unlockables to extend game time. And on the subject of game time, there's plenty in the main quest to last you at least 8-10 hours, if not more if you'd like to explore extra areas and gather collectibles/power-ups.

It's been said before, but I'll add my voice to the chorus: if you're a platforming/metroidvania/side-scrolling exploration fan in any capacity, get this game.
