i dont want to give this game a thumbs down. i actually quite like it a lot of the time. my complaint is that is can be absolutely rage inducingly difficult at times (frequently), as well as punishingly unfair.  i get it, its supposed to be a "hard game," but this is stupid. games should have multiple difficulty settings so as to allow a wider range of players to be able to enjoy said game, as well as to give the devs more money so as to keep making cool games for people to play. but this game only has one difficulty setting and while most of the time is pretty manageable (with patience and practice), there are many times when it is infuriatingly difficult, and there is nothing you can do about it, but to try again or give up. let the players who masochistically enjoying pain in video games (or who want the bragging rites) play on the hardest difficulty, but let the rest of us, who just want to enjoy an otherwise BEAUTIFUL game actually have fun while playing it.

the first complaint is that there are not enough "save" benches. often you have to travers large, extremely dangerous areas to get to where you are going (such as to retrieve your shade/geo (corpse/money)), often making you either die twice and lose all your hard saved cash, or have to double back to the bench over and over again to recharge and try the run back to your shade again.  but the worst is that while often your shade will hang out in a relatively safe area for retrieval, there are many times when this is pointedly NOT the case (such as when it only stays in a locked room miniboss battle), which usually ends with you either dying twice and losing your shit, or killing your shade, but then immediately dying to the intentionally hardest situation they put it in, which then forces you to run the gauntlet all over again, and again, and again, and again, and....

but really the biggest annoyance are the boss fights. im ok with difficult bosses (especially final bosses), but basically all of them in this game are INSANELY difficult. they have multiple move sets, and very quick reaction times to avoid them. they need to be hit so many times (i have never actually counted, but its a LOT) and you can only survive a few hits back. often i feel like i have been wailing on them for a long time on a good attempt and im like "how the hell are you still alive?!? and then i die again. they usually go through multiple phases of different attack patterns. and yes, to an extent you can "learn their moves," but they almost always also have a few radically different moves for each "windup," and even a few "fake out" moves, where they look like they are about to perform an attack and then at the last moment switch it up to an attack specifically designed to hit you if you attempted to avoid the attack it was looking like (damned if you do damned if you dont. wait to see if its a fake out and you will never have time to dodge, but react to the move you think it is, and its a fake out). the bosses are so much harder than the rest of the game i have often spent literal hours trying over and over to beat them (one early boss literally took me 3 play sessions to beat). and im not a terrible player either, i consider myself to be a pretty average gamer (in that i beat pretty much all of the games i try to beat, unless i get bored and dont bother to finish). add to this there are never saves close to said bosses, so even if you unlock shortcuts to make it easier to get there, you always have to run a gauntlet of dangerous enemies and areas just to get back to the boss to retrieve your shade to try to fight the boss again (and probably lose). you cant just get into the flow of a boss fight, because your attempts are always broken up fighting you way back to try again (and as often as not you start the fight slightly wounded).

when it comes down to it, my complaint is about time more so than pure difficulty. it takes so much time just doing the same things over and over and over again to recover from your failures that i feel like %70 of the time spent playing this game is frustratingly trying to get back my stuff just to die again and repeat. i feel like only a very small amount of the time playing is actually making any progress at all. i dont mind a difficult boss fight, even one that takes 3-10 tries (especially on a major boss), but this game pushes it too far, and far too often, where even minibosses can take dozens of attempts with far too much time between tries as you struggle just to get to die again.

and its a shame because in every other way the game is AMAZING! the art and music are incredible, the "voice acting" is adorable and awesome, the controls are pretty solid, the skills and charms, and other play mechanics are great, and the story is interesting enough that i give a shit enough to really pay attention to try to figure it out and see where its going!

give us at least two difficulty settings (the one it has now, and another less punishing one) and this will be one of the best platformer metroidvania games of all time. but as it is.... unless you are a huge fan of the genre, and either love frustration or are VERY good at these kinds of games, you should probably just go play dead cells instead. you'll die just at much, but at least thats basically the whole point of that game, and death really just an excuse to roll the rng rogue-like dice again and try out a different build.

TLDR: believe the hype. this game is stupidly difficult! to the point of detracting from an otherwise amazing game. if you like being frustrated and spending %70 percent of your time recovering from mistakes, rather than actually playing the game and making progress, then give it a shot. otherwise just go play dead cells instead.

EDIT: it does get a little easier once you have slogged away for a long time and unlocked several charms and skills and whatnot (especially the double jump, and shadow dash moves! wholey carp they makes a big difference!). but that said, it can still be a right bastard to you frequently. the levels of epic frustration continue long into the game, and there are just so many times when you are beating your head against a boss, or particularly infuriation bit of platforming for a loooong time... i dont think it will ever get "easy."

First impressions shout 'soul!', gameplay quickly stifles that excitement sadly.

I really wanted to like this game, it looks and sound really nice and is quite impressive for (presumably) a rather small studio.

Unfortunately, imo, the game mechanics aren't really fun.
You spend a lot of time fighting, but the fighting is really simple, while in the soulsgames you have heavy and light attacks and limited stamina, here there is only one kind of attack, unlimitted stamina, and most opponents die after 2 or three hits. And since you have more or less unlimited healing, the fights become pretty easy. I read a lot of times that it's supposed to be a hard game, but to me it seemed pretty easy, and I would'nt call mysyelf a good gamer. There are probably bosses in the souls-games against which I died more often than in this game alltogether. I even beat most bosses on the first try, including the last one. 

Another thing that really bothered me is the amount of backtracking you have to do. It's just so annoying! Since there are so few waypoint that you can use to travel to and from, everytime you get a new skill, you need a lot of time to get back to the new areas that you weren't able to access before. Also, because on the map it doesn't show you which skills you need, to go on, a  lot of times I wasn't sure where to go next, so I just wandered around, trying all the places I wasn't able to go to before to see if I could continue there now with the new skill.
And, like I said, I didn't die too often, but if I did, I often needed a few minutes to get back to the place where I died. And while in the souls-games there is always the thrill of dying on the way back to claim your lost souls and loosing them, since this game is a lot easier, it's pretty unlikely to die again, and even if you do and loose your souls (geo), it doesn't really matter, since you can only use geo to buy some stuff, most of which isn't really necessary or helpful. So while in the souls-games, dying and retrieving your lost souls is part of the fun, here it's just irritating.
With a few more waypoints, they could have reduced the amount of backtracking by a lot, and the playing time by a few hours, making it a lot more enjoyable.

Another point where imo they wasted potential is the character development (or the lack of it). If you find them, you can learn 3 special attacks (nail arts), but I didn't use any of them not even once, they seemed completely useless.
Same goes for the spells, I barely every used them, since they didn't seem very helpful, and also I needed my soul for healing. Of course you can't expect character development like in the souls games, but a few more helpful spells or special attacks could have made the fights a lot more exciting.

So, all in all it's not a bad game and it has a lot of potential, but because of the reasons stated above i didn's really enjoy it.
I can't understand the amount of positive reviews, I would maybe give it a 7,5/10 rating.

I understand that hollow knight has a very opinionated fan base – come at me fanbois and grrls!

Visually and musically, Hollow knight is a real treat. With a beautiful hand-drawn art style that feels like Tim Burton covering the Dark Crystal by way of Nausica and the Valley of the Wind, Hollow Knight is easily one of the best-looking games I’ve ever played.  Likewise, the fantastic use of minimalist music is sublime and incredibly atmospheric.  It really is a joy to exist in Hollow Knight’s bizzarro Bugmare before Christmas world, and I would have loved to discover more of it.

The problem is that I found the gameplay both boring and frustrating.  Firstly, controls are floaty, blocky, and lack the kind of precision you really need in a good metrodvania.  Playing Hollow knight is like trying to use a touch screen with Ski mits on; you can kinda get it to work but never in the way you want it to.

Secondly, and most egregious in my opinion (and yes before the fan bois and grrls wet themselves I am allowed to have my own opinion and no you don’t have to agree) is the flat and uninspired combat.  You spend the whole game with your one attack, which doesn’t even combo, a few charge attacks and your one spell.  After about 8 hours it felt old and after about 13 I gave up from sheer boredom. No new weapons. No ranged combat. No combos. No familiars.  No new spells. No items. You might as well make me a camomile tea and sing me a lullaby.  And to think that this game is like 40 hours long…

Worse still is just how boring the enemies are to fight.  Most of them are about as challenging as a bowl of lukewarm oatmeal and could be felled with a well-aimed fart – they just barrel slowly towards you and wait for you to slap them.  Or the ones like the beardy shield nights, the teleporting magicians or the flying lancers which you can only hit after they attack you, I swear I feel like I spend about 30 secondly between hits just waiting for them to strike.  And even the ones that seem challenging, like the mantis people, are no match for the epic ninja art of jumping on top of them and downwards slashing. Most of the enemies are just reskins anyway.  And while the boss sections were good fun, I just couldn’t push myself through more of this very, very pretty game with bland, boring gameplay.  

By way of comparison, I’ve recently played Blasphemous, Cave Story and Bloodstained: Ritual of the Night, and all three, in my opinion, absolutely trounced Hollow Night.  Blasphemous KOed it in round one with similarly high-level graphics and sound but far deeper combat, slick controls and light years ahead in enemy variety.  Bloodstained is a total slough in the graphics department but with so many weapons, armour, spells, familiars and builds (not to mention freelc with new characters) it forced Hollow Night to throw in the towel before the half way point. Cave Story, likewise, can’t compete with this in terms of graphics but blew it away with gameplay and the audacity to have more then one weapon.

I do concede that there are a lot of people who love Hollow Knight, and I very much envy them.  I would have loved to enjoy this game and explore more of its world, see more of its NPCs, and maybe finally figure out what the hell is going on.  But it was not to be.  I do also understand that this was the work of a very small team and perhaps as such my review is a tad harsh.  However, when the quality of the visuals and sound is of such an astronomically high standard, I would have expected the gameplay to also be...

It is common to say that this game is a perfect metroidvania, that rewards your skill. I will say - it does reward not your skill, but your zealotry. 

I did really try to love this game, for more than 40 hours already, but for every moment I felt something good about it, and there were plenty of these moments here, it gave me ten more reasons to feel frustrated, annoyed and, at times, at a point of a nervous breakdown.

Unfortunately, after finally beating a difficult battle, a difficult platforming segment, I felt not a joy of achievement, but being able to narrowly avoid another cheat that developers had thrown at me. There are a lot of metroidvanias, that, while still being difficult, had a good aftertaste about them, and so I could feel compelled to return to these games in future - I do not  feel the same here, I'll never want to repeat any of these experiences. Which is really a shame - a great potential lost by the so called 'purism' of the developers, that I myself see as an example of lazy game design.

The game has many good aspects - it's a Metroidvania, you get a LOT for your money because the game is just huge. The art style is a nice mix between cute and melancholic and there's a lot to discover and unlock.

Unfortunately, this game takes it a bit too far for my taste. The map for an area isn't available until you found the guy who sells those. Which means in an unmapped area, you have no idea where you are, where you were and where to go,

Even if you do have the map, it's sometimes of limited usefulness, as it's a rather rough and somewhat minimalistic map, but at the same time confusing at times, as it's not always obvious if you can reach spot A from spot B. You have a ton of markers (imo too many) and a legend, also you can place markers yourself, even of different types. Nice, but took me a bit to figure that out. Not only that, you may wanna write down somewhere which marker you use for what. A locked door? A place you can't reach yet? A wall you can't demolish yet? A place where you're lacking a key, or Geo (the money)?

Travelling from A to B is a pain. You do have a quick travel system via the bug you can ride, and also the trams. But these obviously just connect certain parts of the map and aren't exactly close to each other. So you will have to navigate this absolute maze over and over and over and over again in order to find things.

Last but not least, if you run out of health, you start over at the last save point. That may be more or less far away and requires you to go back to where you died in order to pick up your cash again.

Oh, better don't dare to not play for a week or two, as there's a good chance you forgot what you needed to do / where you needed to go.

- Combat doesn't have many options. Your enjoyment of this game may hinge on wether you get tired of wacking insects with the same sword for dozens of hours.
- Anyone proficient in platforming or Metroidvanias may find 90% of the game not very challenging
- The game hiccups sometimes, specially when you get hit, leading to getting hit further. YMMV as it's still getting patches in anticipation to the Switch version

I've spoken with a few people who did not like this game like myself, so with a 96% positive rating on Steam I thought I'd share my opinion on why I did not enjoy this game in the short time I played it:

-The gameplay is on a lower tier in terms of movement mechanics and level design than many other platformers I have played in recent memory. Off the top of my head Celeste and Ori and the Will of the Wisps come to mind as having better gameplay mechanics
-As someone who absolutely adores the Dark Souls series, the lack of direction in this game is seriously frustrating. I regularly found myself going through the same areas over, and over, and over again before finally figuring out where to go next. It didn't feel like I was exploring, it just felt like I was lost. 
-Maybe it is just me, but while I found the environments and score to be positives in the game, I just didn't feel they were anything as incredible as many other reviews are saying, it just seemed above average.
-Combat did not seem to have a lot of depth. Most encounters just felt like swing, back up, and repeat over and over again. 

Perhaps on a rainy day I'll give this game another try and see if I'm just missing something, but as it stands I failed to see what people felt was so special about this game

To be fair, it's a lot of content for its price, but I must give this a thumbs down because of Hollow Knight's horrible mapping system (1), weird save point (bench) placement/ can only change perks (charms) at save points for some reason (2), and various pointless mechanics/ game design put in just to waste your time or annoy you (3). I would recommend this to a niche group of people that might enjoy it. I wouldn't recommend this to anyone else.

(1) Conifer's a whore, paying to explore makes the game an absolute chore: 
Why the hell do I have to pay some random guy so I can get an incomplete map of the area? And why doesn't my map update automatically like every other metroidvania to exist? Personally, I couldn't start semi-enjoying the game until I bought all the maps over twenty hours in.

(2) Benches are Bitches:
Save points are usually in the middle of nowhere. Because of that, if you die you're usually thrown into the middle of nowhere (Yeah I know. Floor is made of floor, whatever). This becomes even worse with bosses because, since you can't change your perk build on the fly, your two choices are to quit and start the game again to be thrown back to the last save point you visited and edit your perks there or to YOLO the boss and probably die and then be thrown back to the last save point you were at. Whichever way you chose doesn't matter because either is inconvenient and a waste of time. The only exception to this is the final boss, any other boss asks you to retrace your steps should you fail. The worst offender IMO is Hornet's second fight where the game must make you go through a long pointless hallway to get back to her. They could've put the bench right next to her given how pointless it is, but no. Also, all the important NPCs are nowhere near fast travel stations. NPCS like Salubra (Which give half the charm notches in the entire game) is nowhere near anything, same goes for the sword forger who upgrades your sword's damage, same for some relic guy that helped me make a lot of money, and for Godseeker in the Junk Pits (which you'll use your only dream gate on since it's so out of the way).
A Quick Note: I partially understand why the developers didn't allow for changing perks on the fly. If you could, you could change your perks to something like quick focus to allow for faster healing in a pinch. Not to mention, some badges are designed around benches like the lifeblood charms and the baldur shell. However, this would only be helpful under the circumstance where accessing your inventory pauses the game. In Hollow Knight, accessing your inventory does not pause the game, so, if you needed to quickly heal, going into your inventory to switch out for the quick focus perk and healing might take far longer than the default heal and puts you at risk for longer. For the perks previously mentioned (baldur shell and life blood) you can have it, so they don't work unless they are equipped at a save point or until you sit at a save point.

(3) Just some things that annoyed me: 
-default horizontal push-back from melee attacks
-money (geo) falls like loose change. It can fall into acid or spike pit because lol
-the world map, by default, opens to a general overview of the map instead of the more comprehensive sub-map that gives information about fast travel, shops, platforming, etc. Why not just make the comprehensive map the default map or the only map?
-Having to ring a bell to wait for fast travel to be ready and be forced to watch a cutscene every time (i know you can mash A to quicken it but come on you always see it)
-double jump has output delay. By this, I mean that when you press jump in the air there is an intentional delay before the actual double jump initiates. This delay is where the monarch wings (what the double jump is called btw) raise up to flap. Once they flap, the actual double jump takes place.
-the shadow dash is annoyingly implemented. This works as an invincible dodge, and the shadow dash upgrades the basic dash you get in the game. However, your shadow dash isn't available for every dash. After shadow dashing, there is a cooldown before you can shadow dash again. Any dash made during this cooldown will be a regular dash where you can take damage. The problem arises when you use your shadow dash to avoid a boss's or enemy's attack like you would using a regular dash, so you lose your helpful shadow dash. Then you try to dash through something only for the shadow dash to not be ready by 0.1 seconds and you dash straight into an enemy feeling like an idiot. If there was another button that just did the shadow dash exclusively instead of doing both the regular dash and shadow dash that'd be helpful, but there is no way to do that. If there is, it's because of a mod.
-Bosses don't have health bars. This is a nitpick, but in a game that prides itself on its boss fights it would be a nice addition to have because it communicates to the player how well they're doing on a particular fight. Darksouls has this, soulslikes have this, metroidvania games have this to differing extents, and cuphead (a game all about boss fights) even has a post-death screen that illustrates how close you were to beating the boss. I just don't understand what ignoring that achieves.
-Having unskippable boss intros is annoying, especially in pantheon. Had to practice Perfect Vessel for Pantheon 4 and was just annoyed by his 10 second intro EVERYTIME you start a fight with him. Same goes for Radiance, infected Hollow Knight, and much more. 
-Having a monster encyclopedia where you must kill the same monster over and over to complete their info is annoying. I know you don't have to complete this to get 100% or even 112%, but, for someone like me who wants to get as many achievements as I can, I did it anyway and it was a mistake on my part.

In the end, I am someone who had high hopes for this game. I've been on this metroidvania craze for a few years now and I haven't regretted it. This genre has opened my eyes to a type of video game that I like instead of playing your basic fps or rpgs that get shoved out to the market every year. Playing those stupid games past midnight not because I liked them but because I had nothing else to do. I love this genre, I love its backtracking, so if you think I'm being too critical that's probably true. However, when I went into this game seeing 10/10, 5 star, "overwhelmingly positive reviews" I thought I was going to have the best gaming experience I would ever have in a genre I love. Tragically though, I didn't. I had my fair share of complaints because, while I understand no one person, developer, game, etc is perfect, I thought I would experience the best metroidvania ever made, and I was wrong.

I like this game very much. 
Its morbid decaying world is fascinating and inspires the same atmosphere that made the world of Dark Souls such a treat to go through. It features amazing artwork, each biome being different but still connected with each other in a sensible way. Unlike other metroidvanias, background and foreground melt together (WITHOUT making platforms hard to recognize), forming the illusion of a breathing (I mean crumbling) world you just happen to traverse in 2D instead of 3D. The controls are crisp and responsive (and they need to be!) and fighting the bosses is a source of great suspense and frustration alike. 
The reason I only like, but not love the game (although the lines are blurred) is the fact that it took one page too many from Dark Souls, especially regarding map progression. DS employs a shortcut system, so does Hollow Knight, but HK only has 2 dimensions to realize that system and herein lies one of its problems.
Being a Metroidvania, backtracking is sadly mandatory, but here it occurs way too often. 
Resting places are safe zones, but cannot be teleported to (like in DS), for transportation the so called stagways (Kind of a taxi between different stations), a real railway (far less useful, only connecting a few points and kind of end game content, but serves as save point also) and a late-game one-way teleportation (between everywhere to a point you set) are available.
That sounds like much, but due to the game's giant map the ways of getting around proofed insufficient to avoid constant backtracking and annoyance.
This is however a point I could oversee because of the massive amount of quality content otherwise present in the game.

Would give it a 9/10, although I am tempted to subtract another point for that outraging nuisance that is the Path of Pain (The White Palace can suck it, it was way too hard compared to the rest of the game)

[b]Hollow Knight is a game making masterclass.[/b]
It features everything i love about solo video games.
[list]
    [*]Immersive, mysterious world. Deep story and backstory unveiled through discovering the game and open to interpretation and research.

    [*]Exemplary character design. Every NPC has his own strong personnality, backstory and character developement. All are very likeable and have a great and fitting visual appearance.

    [*]Creative, original & stunningly beautiful art. Each area of the colourful world of Hallownest has its own visual personnality. Hollow Knight's 2d graphisms feel both traditionnal and refreshingly modern. 

    [*]Flawless controls and huge skillcap. Highly satisfying & rewarding gameplay.

    [*]Perpetual character customization, adding a entire tactical aspect to the skill-based gameplay and allowing each player to approach the game with it's own manner.

    [*]Incredibly well designed metroidvania world. There are countless possible routes depending on which upgrade you get first, which area you decide to explore and where and when you decide to start backtracking.

    [*]Very extensible late game. Completing 100% of the game is a very interesting challenge and allows a better understanding of the story. The differents DLCs also add a lot to this.

    [*]Great replay value. Starting a new game from scratch is highly enjoyable. If you already played a metroidvania before, you know what i'm talking about. Also you will notice lore elements you probably ignored on your first run.

    [*]I almost forgot music. Very diverse and memorable musical ambiances for each area of the game. Epic boss themes. Moving cinematic themes during the scenaristic scenes. Overall refreshing, fitting and enjoyable music.

    [*]Special mention for the sound design. Characters and enemies voices and noises are surprising.
[/list]
Every aspect of the game has an incredible depth to it. Team Cherry's attention for the smallest details is remarquable and unique. If you are a demanding and experienced player, this game is made for you. [b]It is perfect. 10/10.[/b]

Playing this game helped me to realize how much hand-holding can ruin gameplay. Almost the entirety of my experience playing Hollow Knight consisted of me discovering my own way through the game. While exploring the HUGE map of Hallownest, in a way that I thought was unorthodox, I was repeatedly surprised to find a major boss, item, or plot line that were placed in the smallest nooks and crannies of the game. It was probably after the third time this happened that I realized the developers PLANNED on the players ability to blaze their own trail following their curiousity. This game blew my freaking mind time and time again. Overall, this game is amazing. The atmosphere is thick and immersive without ever being too overwhelming. The deliberate use of color and shading to establish a unique tone for each area is fine tuned to perfection. The difficulty level had me laughing out loud at times as I realized I had a sizable obstacle to overcome: the perfect level of difficulty to keep players invested. The movement and combat are both extremely smooth and compliment eachother nicely; I never got bored moving or fighting throughout. The use of simple yet lovable characters as encouraging guides serves a brilliant contrast to the game’s tone overall. The music. The freaking music... it’s so simple, yet so deliberate. Most of the melodies do not stick to a minor/major scale, but instead use more chromatic intervals as to subconsciously keep the player aware of the mysterious themes of light vs. dark. Overall, the game is a very complex and deep experience with a simple yet inviting skin on it. One of the most immersive games I’ve ever played. Please buy it.

Dear future game devs: just because your game is difficult does not mean your game is fun or well-designed. This is no Dead Cells, the difficulty in this game is not rewarding at all, and most of the time leaves you feeling worn out and regretting ever picking the game up. You don't feel triumphant at  beating a hard boss or section, you just feel tired because you know the next section is going to be even more tedious and unfun. The story has been the only thing convincing me to continue playing, and even that is starting to matter less and less with every drawn-out, pointlessly long encounter, and with every death requiring a 150-mile pilgrimage from the last bench. 

Wasting the player's time is honestly the biggest sin of the game. So much land and yet it feels so empty and barren. Some would say this is for the plot, I say it's because they didn't know how to design an interesting environment. Not only that, but basic timesavers are extremely few and far between, almost treated like privileges; the distances between each beetle station and each bench is ludicrous and results in spending far more time than necessary just walking back and forth.

An excellent example of this is the Soul Master boss fight. While by itself the fight is mildly annoying and just kind of not a fun experience, consider that the nearest bench to you is well over a 2 minute trek away [i]even not counting the annoying as piss enemies between the bench and the boss[/i]. This leads to a boss fight where not only is the boss itself pointlessly frustrating and borderline impossible to telegraph his attacks (at least until his second phase, where the developers seem to have pulled their heads out of their asses and figured out how to make a fight challenging but fun for all of one minute), but you just got done wasting your time fighting some unnecessarily difficult other enemies just trying to get to him again. If it wasn't for this, the boss probably would've taken me maybe 10-15 minutes to beat. With them, it took me well over 2 hours. For one boss. That's basically how the rest of the game goes, too. Is it difficult? No, just time consuming. Does it add to the game? No, it takes away what little fun was there. Is there [i]any[/i] good reason it's there? Hell. No. It serves no purpose past wasting the player's time.

I don't even know where to go to progress through the game anymore, and I'm to a point where I just don't care anymore. Why SHOULD I continue playing? The game's not fun. The game's not rewarding. It's not a good metroidvania, it's not a good soulslike, and it's just not a good game. Got time? Like a challenge? This games is for you
Likes
- Exceptional art design
- Great soundtrack 
- Fantastic atmosphere
> Rain, wind, the echoing sewers filled with gurgling of monsters, the pulsating infection and the spine chilling scuttles of creatures in The Nest. For the most part sound design is perfect.
- Gameplay has tight controls and tactics aplenty
- Skill progression and difficulty
> Unlocking new abilities and becoming better at the game create satisfying periods of efficiency as you cut through enemies without taking a hit. Beating bosses feels like an accomplishment due to the rather unforgiving difficulty of the game.
- Save points are frequent
> I have died a lot but the developers have placed enough points in convenient enough locations that coming back for my stuff was rarely an issue.

Dislikes
Pacing
> For me Hollow Knight drew me in slowly and then burst into times of great discovery and times filled with lots of backtracking. As the game opens up you're expected to stumble on things in the correct order for the story to progress. More often than not I was finding places I couldn't go than places I could due to not completing things in the correct order. Travelling around the tunnels can become very tedious if you're not in the right places at the right time and going through the same areas time and time again trying to see if you've missed anything ruins the wonder you feel as you see the same enemies in the same positions in the same locations time and time again. Moreover, the same issue with the dark souls series, you cannot play this game sporadically. You have to remember so much exploration (map layout, enemy placement, types/attack patterns, tactics) that playing this game with breaks large enough to forget things would be an easy way to never finish it. Remedies obviously include guides (the same as dark souls), but at the same time this game would be best recommended to someone who can drop a good amount of time on it each gaming session without long breaks (don't take a week off) until they finish it. 
Another issue with pacing is mood. The game has light hearted/nice moments but for the most part it is very dreary. You're travelling through the ruins of a dead civilisation where everything wants you dead except for a few small encounters with other adventurers, vendors and some cheery grubs you rescue every so often.
Due to the player choice being key in which order things will be experienced, all these things can be better or worse depending on your playthrough. It is however an exceptional and lengthy title for the price however, and well worth the effort for some of the beautiful and terrifying moments it provides.

Hollow Knight can be loosely described as a Metroidvania game, though I think it is more precise to describe it as a cross between Super Metroid and Dark Souls.

The visuals, while technically simple, look wonderful, the sound effects are solid and the music is excellent. The gameplay is very good for the most part: controls are tight, combat is simple but fun and the platform sections are well put together.

There are some wrinkles: There is a lot of backtracking and there are various instances where the game design could have been better, though these don't detract from the overall experience too much.

Minor frustrations aside, Hollow Knight is a very good (and lovingly crafted) game that is rewarding to play. It is also outstanding value for money and I would have no problem recommending it at a higher price point.

Metroidvania games have never been very appealing to me.  The idea of constant back tracking through areas you've already been in sounded tedious and unfun to me, so I never bothered. I bought Hollow Knight on a whim and if all Metroidvania games are as good as Hollow Knight, then it's safe to say I'm already in love with the genre. 

The maps are truly gorgeous and full of atmosphere, which makes it rewarding in its own right to backtrack through already explored areas to see what more they have to offer. I adore the imaginative environments,  it's so easy to see how much love and care was put into this game through the backgrounds alone.

The game was not necessarily easy. Some boss fights could be rolled through with a creative use of charms, but there are some (especially early on) that force you to read the enemy's movements and think carefully about how you want to approach the fight. "It's difficult but not impossible," was a mantra I came to repeat over and over to myself. While being difficult, I never thought that a fight was unfair. I could clearly see where I made my mistakes and slowly learned from them. If it felt impossible, then it seemed clear that I just wasn't ready for that fight, which encouraged me to leave and go explore until I was better equipped.

The story of Hollow Knight is completely fascinating. Rather than dumping exposition onto the player and forcing them to read paragraphs at a time, the player instead has to go and seek out the lore of the game. NPCS, signs, and item descriptions dole out bits of lore at a time, letting the player piece things together and come to an understanding of the situation on their own. 

All in all, Hollow Knight is a fantastic game that I would recommend to anyone.

So far as Steam metroidvanias go... Ori is a game you can play for a week and it be one of the best gaming experiences of your life. Hollow Knight is a game you could play for months out of obsession while everything you worked so hard for in the real world fell to ruin.

Some bosses can be so hard it becomes unenjoyable at times, but you can always go explore a different part of the map, get a hair stronger, then return and beat that boss with 1 hit point to spare.

It would be really annoying having to do so much walking just to get a little stronger, just to beat some overpowered boss, but Hollow Knight's amazing level design makes backtracking an enjoyable chore. Each area is beautiful, different, and full of attractive secrets that are only accessible after you gain a certain ability or item.

To me, it's not perfect. It's heavily inspired by soulsborne games, so you end up with a bunch of really interesting-looking NPCs that just end up rambling jibberish and only a few that are actually interesting. I also wish weapon upgrades were a bit faster to make some of the middle/late-game hiking feel smoother. But overall it's obvious Team Cherry has worked really, really, really, hard jamming this game with tons of well-thought-out content.

At one point I gave up thinking I would ever 100%+ complete the game* and just wanted to finish it, but during my time with it, there were so many impressive moments—from finding HUGE optional areas by mindlessly hitting an innocuous tile, to how perfectly paced abilities are, to how every area in this 2D game makes you feel like it could be real—that I had to actually write a review just to thank the team for all their extra efforts.

If you're a fan of 2D platformers or metroidvanias, definitely give this game a chance.

* I ended up using the Wiki to get 100%+ and feel much better now.

[h1] [u]The Short of it[/u] [/h1]

[u]Pros:[/u]
+Incredibly tight controls with difficult combat
+The level of interaction you have with the environment in this game is amazing
+An indirect narrative that’s rooted into the very atmosphere of the game
+Well done sound design and music
+Beautiful 2D animation
+DLC is free, a practice I wish was more common

[u]Cons:[/u]
-The world is a bit too open ended, personally speaking
-Not every boss is great, in fact there are some that are just bad...
-The means to reach the latest ending is rather ridiculous

[h1] [u]More In-depth[/u] [/h1]

+[u]Challenge:[/u]
Let’s start this off with the bread and butter of what Hollow Knight is all about. The game is acclaimed for its difficulty and I’ve gotta say that the press is right. The combat is both challenging but fair. Hollow Knight features some of the tightest controls I’ve ever experienced, ultimately making it so that any small misstep isn’t anyone’s fault but your. This is especially true with the platforming challenges in the game. There are locations (though I have one in particular in mind) where not your ability to kill, but your skill at maneuvering around in dangerous terrain is tested. As far as metroidvanias go, Hollow Knight sits at the summit when it comes to either fighting or platforming.

+[u]Interaction:[/u]
On the note of Hollow Knight’s tight controls, an incredibly important factor that needs to be mentioned is the level of interactivity you have with the environment. Your basic slash attack can be used for so much more than just hacking at an enemy in this game. The primary example I wish to give attention to is how you interact with spikes in the game. Normally in metroidvania, if you fall into a pit of spikes you’re left at the mercy of death. However that’s not the case here, performing a downward slash will actually cause you to bounce off the spikes. There’s a fair amount of times when this comes in handy, especially when you’re missing certain abilities, like the double jump. The same applies to parrying enemy slash attacks, it’s possible to simply stand your ground and not take damage if you have the appropriate timing.

+[u]Story:[/u]
Contrary to most games we’re probably used to, Hollow Knight does very little in telling you what is exactly happening in the world. Many of these details are told through dialogues with npcs or given as hints in the many areas of Hallownest. The lore of Hollow Knights world runs deep and it’s evident in everything you see in the game. Indirect though it may be, Hollow Knight gets the job done spectacularly.

+[u]Sound:[/u]
Every jump, landing, attack, death, etc. are done with great precision in terms of the sound design. In the simplest of terms I’d have to say that the sounds, no every impact I make in this game, sounds incredibly satisfying. The music is no slouch either, the darker atmosphere mixed with some of the more serene soundtracks is both an unnerving, yet serene feeling.

+[u]Graphics:[/u]
Let’s skip the formality on this, the game just looks straight up amazing. I love my 2D games, and Hollow Knight just has many details in how everything looks, from the environments to your attack animations to the multiple enemies and npcs. It’s to the point I can’t even try to say anything bad about it. How the game looks is an important factor with any video game, and seeing the art style of Hollow Knight, I immediately had to give it a try, one of the best decisions I’ve had the pleasure of making.

+[u]Free DLC:[/u]
Again, need I say more? We’re living in an age where games aren’t really “complete” unless you have the “game of the year” edition. Most DLC is purchased, or can be obtained cheaper collectively with a season pass, all on top of the purchase of the base game. But Hollow Knight? Yeah it has DLC, but each one is basically a huge patch since it’s immediately installed/updated to the game with no purchase whatsoever. Team Cherry has no real reason to be giving good content like this for free when no one else does it, and for that, their generosity alone deserves recognition.

-[u]Lost:[/u]
The world of Hollow Knight is not only detailed, but it is vast. So vast in fact, that I personally felt completely and utterly lost. I, as a player, prefer to have at least some level of direction, and Hollow Knight doesn’t really provide any of that. I’m sure that this is a point many people will feel differently on, but the feeling of not knowing where to go is a far worse feeling than any boss destroying me. I’ve only managed to get as far as I have with the occasional use of guides giving me general directions, once I have an idea I went on my merry way to discovering and fighting whatever the area had to hold. Hollow Knight truly is a wonderful experience, but it’s one I wouldn’t have enjoyed nearly as much if I didn’t have some guidance.

-[u]Some bosses:[/u]
Not every boss you fight is great, in fact… some are plain terrible. I will reference one particular fight as vague as possible to avoid spoilers, but any who have met this boss will immediately know to what I am referring to. One fight, that’s required story wise, has almost nothing to do with your ability to play the game. Essentially the boss receives 0 damage until an NPC performs an action that will allow you to hurt the boss for a brief amount of time. This will have to occur several times (maybe more depending on how much damage you when you reach it), and there’s no way to trigger this action yourself. It’s essentially a waiting game, and in a game that tests your reflexes… yeah, it’s a big no no from me (I HATE THIS FIGHT). The fight has been revamped in the DLC, but I believe the story fight hasn’t changed. If it has then this point will be subject to change.

-[u]Godmaster DLC:[/u]
Some spoilers ahead! Okay, okay, let me explain. “How can you say that the DLC is negative?” I hear you ask, and it’s not. However, it’s the reward, the dlc ending, and how you must acquire it that I have a problem with. Godmaster introduced the ability to refight bosses in a boss rush mode. As someone who has defeated the 4 main pantheons, I can tell you how much I loved it (except the fourth, I got beaten down in that one pretty hard…). The thing is that there’s a final fifth to access, and you must beat that one to unlock the ending. Problem? It’s ridiculous, you must face EVERY boss in the game with a few breaks here and there, the bosses themselves are stronger than normal as well. But that’s not even my biggest gripe, truth be told, I could probably do it provided I put in the time and practice, however, a large majority of people cannot. According to the global achievements less than 1% of players have defeated the fourth pantheon. Many players can’t attempt it, and even the ones who can will suffer. The reason I put such a large precedence on this ending is because it is meant to be a foreshadowing of things to come in the next update allowing you to play as another character. I welcome the challenge of the fifth pantheon but to lock the ending, not behind a single difficult boss but a plethora of powered up bosses (with the former included at the end), in a free content update everyone has access to seems a bit over the top. At the very least I’d imagine they’d try to make this ending a bit more accessible for it’s players.

[h1] [u]Conclusion[/u] [/h1]

All in all, Hollow Knight is a near masterpiece in the metroidvania genre, but it isn’t without it’s blemishes. While some of these so called blemishes are a bit more based on personal taste, I’d say everyone’s got a gripe or two to admit with the game. That said, these blemishes make up a very small percentage of the game (ignoring the fact I felt utterly lost), and on the whole is 100% worth getting.

It's a shame that Steam has such a clunky review system because this game gets an EXTREMELY qualified positive review.  It's got a lot of good parts, and the challenge is fun.  But the designers are weirdly attached to some of the worst aspects of old-school gaming, like backtracking and confusing mazes.  You will waste a LOT of time between the good parts.  It's a game that frequently confuses tedious for challenging.

I'm not recommending this game, not because you shouldn't buy the game, but because I personally did not enjoy it. The game is hard, and if you're not good enough at games like this, the game is unrewarding and it becomes frustrating.

Let me explain: When a monster touches you, you lose health. You only have a very limited amount of health. One wrong movement will cost you a lot. So I ended up dying repeatedly while trying to kill a boss, or even when fighting small guys.. But the thing is, I didn't even know if I had to kill that boss. I didn't know where to go, I didn't even know where I was and why. The game just decided not to tell me (which is fine, it's the puzzle/exploration part of the game, but it made it a lot less rewarding, because I never felt like I achieved anything meaningful).

This all isn't a big deal if you're good at these kinds of games, so it's definitely worth trying the game. I just feel like the game is massively overrated and not fun if you're not a good gamer.

Something that could be done to make the game more accessible would be to add an easier difficulty and/or give the player more hints about what to do or how to fight certain enemies. (I know, it's part of the challenge, but if you repeatedly die for 30 minutes straight it's not fun, so being able to opt in for help would be nice).

Ok so I am not a big fan of platformers but this game really got me. It's an interesting experience for everyone in my opinion and here's why.

The gameplay is rich in it's own unique way. You fight your enemies with a nail and some spells that you get through out the game. In addition you gather seals that you can equip to your character that give you extra passive abilities as long as you have them equiped. It's interesting and offers players diffrent ways to progress through the game. The boss fights are fun once you get to recognize their pattern and learn how to dodge their attacks. It can get a little frustrating till you beat them but if you stick to it you'll make it. The game also borrows the dying logic from dark souls. When you die you lose all your Geo and have to retrieve them.

The graphics are cool! The artstyle of the game is astonishing and mainly atmospheric. It's beatiful to look at every locations you visit. 

The sound design and the soundtracks are also atmospheric and go well with the theme of every location in the game. They really done a good job at that. I also like how the characters kind of mumble in diffrent ways when they talk and how this still gives them so much character.

The story is interesting so far and the NPC's are fun and each has something interesting or important to tell you. They bring this forgoten world of Hallownest to life.

So to sum up:
+great gameplay with lots of variety
+cool boss fights
+great soundtracks
+engaging plot and story
+atmospheric graphics
-a bit grindy sometimes

It's a solid 9/10 if you ask me. If you like platformers or fantasy in general take it. It will be a pleasant experience.

This game quickly surpassed Breath of the Wild as my favorite game of all time, and I really can't talk about it enough.
It is a beautiful, tragic, wondrous experience filled with intrigue and mystery in a world that is so flawlessly crafted that it feels real. Everything is seamless, from the world design to the inhabitants of Hallownest. The art in the game lends itself so well to the image and aesthetic of the world, and it really works. This, combined with the absolutely enchanting score (I could go on about the score for hours) by Christopher Larkin, provide a truly breathtaking experience through the realm of Hallownest. The game feels whole, rounded, and complete, never lacking in any aspect.

Gameplay is fun, and most importantly, difficult but fair. The bosses are all within the skill of a player; it's all about learning their patterns which can lead to conquering bosses that seem almost impossible. The platforming is fun, the movement options are great, combat is fluid and extremely well integrated into the world. Like the original Legend of Zelda, or Castlevania, combat is simply a part of exploration, and provides no disconnect from the environment: you must use the platforms, the holes, the divots in the walls to shield you and aid you. Most of all, it's fun. Fighting is never a chore. Bosses feel GOOD to beat and fun to fight. It's a blast of skill and accomplishment in each one.

I could truly go on and on about the majesty of this game. It's totally worth your time. It's a work of art, a collection of inspired genius, and one of the best games that I've ever played, and for a fraction of what the game is truly worth.
I cannot recommend it enough.

At first, some of the difficulties you might encounter are:

- Slow movement (around the map)-   This might get annoying, but you will get the abilities that boost your speed soon enough.

- You might feel that you progress slowly-   Please don't rush this game, you will miss a lot of secrets if you don't look carefully. You will often encounter parts of the map (you will need to buy the map tho) that are inaccessible to you, mark them on your map and return once you have the abilities that are required. Once you get those abilities exploring will feel very rewarding and the sense of progress will be satisfied.

- Feeling lost-   There will be times where you don't know where to go, in such cases I recommend talking to Elderbug (the first NPC you encounter by the first bench). He will usually give you hints where to go next.

When you die you are sent back to the last bench you sat on. The thing is, these benches are sometimes badly positioned. Because if you die to a boss you will get teleported back to the bench, which means you will need to platform your way back to the boss to challenge him again (which might take a literal minute or more each time). Now first 6 times might not be a big deal, but after dying for the 37th time to the same boss it gets a little annoying. This is the main problem with the game.

Also there is a charm system which lets you customize your stats (nail range, movement speed...) which feels kinda underwhelming at first as you have very little space for your charms in the beggining. But as you expand your slots you unlock the true power of charms and their combinations. I still feel that this can be expanded upon though.

[h1]The Pros[/h1]
[list]
[*] This game is [i]beautiful[/i], seriously. as an artist myself the game art is something to die for and they have a wonderful grasp on when to put detail and when not to that makes sure you can see exactly what you're doing, as long as you're paying attention
[*] The sound track is amazing and with all the characters voiced by little grunts and huffs from the developers you definetely get a feel for the quality of the game
[*] World construction is a meaty thing, you will get lost and you will find yourself in a place you swear was on another part of the map
[*] There is a huge range of upgrades, skills, items etc for those that like to customise their gameplay, want to quickly dash into a slug? Got it. Want a cute demon child? There we go. Weird exploding tiny fly yous? Sure they got that too. They also have multiple different attacks that you can learn which makes for interesting gameplay.
[*] The enemies are vast, there are well over a hundred different types of enemies in this game, this ain't no mario game.
[/list]
[h1]The meh/cons[/h1]
[list]
[*] [i]This game is hard.[/i] and you will have to put it down at least twice. You have to remember enemies' patterns, you have to prepare yourself for each boss once you understand them, there [i] is no rushing[/i] do not play this game if you expect a walk in the park like you're playing some cheaper second rate sega sonic game. Prepare to die. Besides that though the game is incredibly pleasing when you get past that hurdle and the challenge is fun and exhilarating, when you win [i] you win[/i] and when you lose [i]you lose[/i].
[*] Whenever I start this game up I have to reset my controlls, for some reason the game completely resets all my saved controls when I shut it down. Not too bad but irritating as fuck.
The game will bullshit you. Like all games it never always runs smoothly. glitches will happen and you can die from them. For example, Hornet, spawning out of thin fucking air. Twice. Because one of her animations didn't start.
[*] The delicate flower quest can suck my -insert developer name here-. seriously I hate that quest.
[*] There is always that one boss you don't click with, for example My fight with the Soul master was probably my easiest fights, my fight with Hornet however? Well, let's not talk about that shall we. Please I still have flashbacks.
[*] Deepnest.
[/list]

This is the best game I have played in my life.  The atmosphere, music, bosses, and design make it a truly otherworldly experience.  Seeing as video games are- at their base value- an escape from reality, this is the highest accolade a game can recieve from a reviewer.  That said, there are some aspects of the game that bug me even now(overused pun fully intended).

The most outstanding of these to me, the final challenge in the game, the Pantheon of Hallownest is a 40-minute deathrun of every boss in the game.  When a player dies to one of the final 3 bosses in the pantheon, they shouldn't be forced to slog through the 42 bosses to reach their death point again, this is just annoying.  My only other complaint is how hidden some parts of the game are.  I would never have known the secret of the Lifeblood Core or Mr. Mushroom without information from outside the game.  I feel there should be at least some reference to these in the base game so that some players aren't entirely excluded from phenomenal content.  

With my (minor) complaints out of the way, I can get to my praise of the game.  As a 15 dollar game, the content provided in Hollow Knight is the best bargain I've seen since I bought my old GPU for 20 bucks at a yard sale.  As I noted above, the game is unbelievable atmospheric and is essentially a work of art.  I could talk about what I love in Hollow Knight for pages but I'll keep it brief.  It's a beautiful timepiece and a love letter to the metroidvania genre, it has a lengthy campaign and legitimate, important choices such that the player's actions matter, and the game systems (charms, spells, nail-pogo) allow the players to be creative and thoughtful.  Players familiar with the systems can easily skip multiple bosses and access regions far before they ordinarily would in a normal playthrough.  Although the vast majority of players of Hollow Knight will never attempt this, the inclusion in the game means that a second or third playthrough remains interesting despite the lack of novelty in the main story.  Novelty can still be found though in the secrets, false walls, and lore scattered throughout Hallownest.  These small details are what makes the game such a flawless experience, with the player fully immersed and checking every surface for a hidden room.     

Finally, I want to address some of the other reviews here that complain about the difficulty of the game.  This is a not an easy game- there will be bosses you feel incapable of beating if you aren't a superhuman.  Don't get frustrated, every boss in the game other than the first two (Hornet  1 and False Knight) can be easily skipped and fought later with no consequences, and even these can be skipped although it requires some experience.  If you feel absolutely outclassed by a boss, please skip it or take some time to cool off before writing a scathing review of the game's unfair difficulty. 

 TL;DR:  Sorry for the wall of text, this is a fantastic game although challenging and not without some slight flaws(to me).  Still, I recommend it more than any other game I've played.

This game is one of my personal favorites. The art style is beautiful, one I have come to love. All the areas, enemies and NPC's are unique in their own ways—it makes for a memorable journey. I enjoyed every moment of my 30 hours of gameplay.  The challenge was so rewarded, the balance is immaculate in this regard.  Difficult bosses, and later on combined with difficult platforming, this game has done the best at both.  The art is beautiful, with background visuals for every room.  The controls are fine-tuned, didn't have any problems.  Progression was steady and satisfying.  The music is extremely memorable, the soundtrack is able to hit on dozens of emotions fit for the moment.  The combat is very fun and challenging, there are no difficulty levels, everything is based upon your progress of the game.  I found some of the bosses easy, most pretty difficult, and some extremely difficult.  But every defeat made victory that much greater.  Beating the [spoiler] Radiance [/spoiler] was one of the greatest feelings in recent video game achievement for me.

With all of these traits, you have a platformer that would score perhaps low 90s, but throw in the story, and I believe this game is a 100.  I can't really describe it, it takes a long time to understand everything that has happened and is going on.   Perhaps it's the way the game gives you little nibbles everywhere.  You absolutely have to piece together information, and in this way the game plays out like a mystery.  Perhaps that's the most gripping part of this game, the constant yearn to learn more and to leave no questions unanswered.  There is also a delicate balance of myth and fact that leaves you fulfilled in your quest for knowledge but leaves some topics to the imagination.

This game is a perfect archetype of the Metroidvania genre.  Exemplary in every way in that regard.  But also, I believe, perfect in regards to any game of any genre.  It executes on the formula that produces masterpieces that hardly any games these days do.

My second ever negative review (I have 50+). I want to say, this is very subjective, and considering the overall reviews are very positive, you will most likely like this game; despite that, even tho I very much tried to, I hated my time with this game.

First thing first, this game is gorgeous, the style is unique and lovely, the environmetal design is so well made and the soundtrack is great.

Second thing, the difficulty is nice. I liked the challenge that this game provided and a lot of bosses felt very much tight. However, if you do not like hard games, this might not do it for you.

However, I simply disliked the gameplay. I personally like my 2d platformeer to have very fast horizontal speed, like metroid dread, celeste, sonic etc. This is just so slow. In comparison, the jump is so high, he jump like thrice his own height, I would've prefer a shorter jump. I also like to have a lot more movement ability, give me double dash, double jump, wall climp, wall jump (edit: I had receive a wall jump, just forgot about it, again, I don't like the wall jump in this game), more, tho, maybe you unlock most of these further down the line. Again, this is just a personal preference; I just didn't like the movement of the game.

Edit: I hate the controls, even if all the movement ability I mentionned were to be perfectly implemented into the game, I still wouldn't like it. This game, to me, just feels wrong. Have you ever played a shooter with 400 ping on 10 fps. This game is kinda like that to me, even at prime performance. I can't put my finger on it, it's just not for me.

The game is punishing for no reason. It doesn't make it harder, it makes it painfull and time-consumming. Let's compare it to Elden Ring, a reasonably difficult game. In that game, you can spend 20-30 attempts on the harder bosses if you aren't incredibly well prepared, yet every time you die, you can go back at it: you go, you die, you respawn next to the boss arena, you go again, qquick and easy. Meanwhile in Hollow Knight, you go in, you die, you respawn 15 min away, you die going back, you lose your souls (I don't care what they are actually called, they are basically souls), then you respawn, go back and retry again. It doesn't make the fight itself harder or more challenging it makes the process of going to the boss more time consumming, and this is my other big complaint with the game; it is time consumming, every small thing takes thrice the time it could take if every elements was efficiently put together. I personnaly prefer a very dense 5 hours game then a slow 15 hour games, but again this comes down to personal preference.

In the end, as there are so many review explaining why you should buy this game, I made this review explaining why I hated my time with it, even tho this game could definetly be your future preferate game, as it has such amazing qualities. Thus if the complaints I pointed out don't botter you, definetly buy this game.

Please, don't just comment: "bro this game is amazing you don't know what you're talking about" it is amazing to you, tastes are relative, find a single game on steam with over 10000 review, and 100% positive review, I'll wait. or "it gets good after x hours, just keep playing." This game was the worst gaming experience of my life, I wouldn't finish the game for 100$. Thank you for your comprehension. It's almost sad that I have to say this, but I guess some people really like to shame others for their different opinions; again, if you liked the game, good for you.

This is a very well put together game, but it feels like enjoying the game / having fun wasn't the top design goal.

As a fan of Dark Souls, the comparison is clear but subtle, traversing the map in this game often felt like a chore. There is a fast travel system but the checkpoints are far between and somewhat distant from POI's like merchants. You slowly open new shortcuts which make these trips shorter, but still a chore to visit a store for 5 seconds. This same thing applies to some bosses, spend 2 minutes getting to boss, die in 30 seconds, repeat 20 times... It's a bit of salt in the wound that at some point certain paths close and make some of these "common" trips even more tedious.

I came back to this game and gave it a second honest shot, and after 20 hours into this second playthrough I just don't want to keep back tracking and running through areas I've already cleared to check if I have whatever key I need to open up the unexplored dead end on my map. 

Once you get what seems like the majority of the abilities and spells it feels good and controls well, but it just became to tedious to wander into where I needed to go, then even looking up hints of where to go it was still just too much going through the same boring halls to check dead ends until you find the one that actually leads to the next crucial upgrade.

I'm sure this is an amazing game to some, but it got to grindy in the wrong ways for my taste. I hate to leave a not recommended, but I think it will grab more attention from others with similar taste. Again, very well done game, but I think there are some lessons to be learnt from the design of this game.

I'm sad to say I do NOT recommend this game unless you are a fan of the genre. Full disclosure, I dislike roguelikes and metroidvanias, I only purchased this game (and Hades) because of the rave reviews and very positive reception they had gotten. Hades was excellent. This game...wasn't.

The game's initial difficulty curve is very wonky. The platforming isn't bad, but barely 20 minutes into the game, you have to fight these dudes waving sticks around with alarming alacrity, and you have to kite them with the skill of a Diamond LoL player.

It gets worse, you meet multiple showstopper enemies (not even bosses) early on that can only be reliably defeated with aggressive use of magic. But what happens if you don't have spells saved up, or you can't save what you need because you died and have a mana pool penalty? Well...pray, I guess.

Speaking of dying, when that happens, the game has you respawn at a checkpoint with a penalized mana pool, and redo the platforming segments while you hunt down your soul and fight it to restore your mana pool and currency.

After a few hours I didn't feel like hunting it down anymore.

[h1]Do you like a good story?[/h1]
[b] Do you like to spend your entire weekend failing miserably to defeat a boss? 
Do you love good character design and subtle but gorgeous animation?
Good! then you'll love this game![/b]
[h1]TL;DR:[/h1]
[b]If you like hard, but fair, games
And games that have a really interesing story and an amazing soundtrack, You'll like hollow knight.
It's an amazing metroidvania, specially if it's your first one, and it's also very cheap.[/b]

[h1] Good Aspects: [/h1]
[b]It's cheap ( VERY underpriced )
The dlc's are cheap. ( Free, duh )
The story is surprisingly good and very interesting
It's hard but not impossible. ( You'll get better with the more you play )
It's BEAUTIFUL, like, really. The game is gorgeous.
It's soundtrack is amazing.
The controls are smooth and easy ( Specially if you're using a controller )
The characters are amazing, every single one of them has a little quirk and a backstory.
The bosses, just that. The bosses, none of them are unfair, most of them are hard, but doable.[/b]

[h1] Bad Aspects: [/h1]
[b]Near the end game, your nail gets too strong for some bosses and too weak for others, making the game
a little unbalanced-ish.
Too many good ideas, a lot of them done well, but some are still pretty ''blank'', specially the dream world, there's
a lot of potential unused there.
Too many areas. I mean, they're all really, really, really, good, but after a while you realize they're pretty, same-ish.
Some enemies are pretty similar too, they're variations of one enemy but from different areas.
Some bosses, even though they're EXTREMELY fun to battle against, are just, too annoying and hard, that's not a bad thing for the game, because they're all balanced, I just see it as a tiring thing ( mostly because I'm stupid ), specially with the new DLC ( Gods and Glory/Godmaster ).
The story is too much hidden. There are stupidly hard challenges you need to do just to see THREE SECONDS of lore
( Path of pain. ) Even though it's great, it's like Team Cherry is scared of showing the players the lore, because every update they make it harder to see the new lore.[/b]

[h1] So, is it worth it? 
Yes, totally. [/h1]
[b] Hollow knight is an extremly interesting game and it has a TON of content for it's price ( And there's still more coming! For free! ) Team Cherry did an amazing work with it, and even though it has a thousand of flaws, they are NOT all major ones, and they're not all BAD ones. There are some pretty bad flaws when it comes to game design, specially, as said before, game balancing and game design, but they're mostly because of the scale Hollow knight is. Team Cherry did a very wise decision of making the game pretty and feeling diverse even when it's not. They could've made the game way smaller but with more diversified areas and enemies, but they chose to make the game big and still managed to make it fresh in most new areas. There were already some problems that got fixed with new dlc and I'm pretty sure they'll take care of whatever else they feel it's wrong with game. In general hollow knight it's an amazing, cheap, beautiful, and intriguing game and you should totally buy it. [/b]

Hollow Knight falls in the "not for me" category. I recognize the excellent production value, the high quality visuals, and beautifully somber tone but for a first foray into the Metroidvania genre, maybe it was a poor choice on my part.

My 11ish hours with the game span a handful of frustrating attempts to get into it. I think every attempt was thwarted by the infamous tedium of redoing a boss run. This is something I knew of going in but was not discouraged due to my experience with Souls game, Dark Souls 1 being one of my favorite games of all-time. It's hard to put my finger on, but everything in Hollow Knight feels unforgiving to the point of not being fun anymore. Platforming feels as if every platform is a couple pixels smaller than it looks visually. Your hitbox feels a couple pixels bigger than you look. Enemies feel tough to hit as if there is too small a difference between your sword's hitbox and your own. I say all this in criticism but am fully aware of my own inexperience in the Metroidvania and platforming genres. Hollow Knight just rubbed me the wrong way. Coming off playing an excessive amount of Spelunky 2, where I fell in love with platforming, Hollow Knight proceeded to push me back into the dumpster and tell me I'm nothing special.

I sense that someday I might install Hollow Knight again and it could just click, but I'm done trying for now.

Symphony of the Night and Super Metroid were references back in the day, and as such, they still live on as classic games that defined a genre. Hollow Knight sits besides those other 2 titles, it's a staple to the new generation of Metroidvanias, because it's that good. The gameplay is so fine-tuned, and the story, while in the beginning you can't see much, you start to learn from the game how wonderful and sad of a place Hallownest is. I played some quite recent titles, like Valdis Story, Dust, and a few other Metroidvanias, but NONE was as well made, as satisfying, as HK is.

Pros: The art and music is good. The bugs are cute.

Cons: The rest of the entire game. There is nothing unique or interesting about Hollow Knight, which is a weird thing to say about a modern 2D platformer. Picture a 2D platformer. Yup, that's Hollow Knight.

Some of the boss fights are interesting at first, but once you learn the appropriate response to both of their moves, it's then just a matter of repeating that same thing for 5 minutes until the boss dies. The rest of the game is just exploring, finding dead-ends, and occasionally getting killed in some BS way (rocks fell, you died!) and having to go collect your souls ("Geo") from where you died. Why? Because they want to be "Nintendo Hard"? Because Dark Souls did it? Except in Dark Souls, when you die, you feel like you got beat. In Hollow Knight, when you die, you feel like the game just chose to kill you.

The only remarkable thing about Hollow Knight is how frustrating it is. You'll have a whole screen where you drop down, down, down onto successive platforms-NOPE! There were spikes there that you couldn't see until you fell on them. You're deftly jumping over platforms-NOPE! A spike fell on you and knocked you into the water and the water hurt you twice as much that time for no reason, so you're out 3 hearts now! They love enemies that are hidden (and invulnerable) until you're near, or that explode when they die, or that can hurt you but can't be hurt by you. The entire first area is basically "oh look, an interesting area to explore-NOPE! You're not good enough to go here yet! Haha, you can't jump high enough! Lol, nope, gate is closed! Haha no you can't hurt this enemy yet; come back later! Nope, too dark for you, neener-neener! Guess you'll have to come back later LOL."  That's literally the first 30 to 60 minutes of the game -- just finding lots and lots of places you can't go yet.

Sometimes frustration can be good in small doses, especially when there's a good payoff. But there's no payoff in Hollow Knight, because there's nothing worth earning, buying, or achieving. The game starts off with "you're a bug with a sword and there's a big dungeon" and stays that way for as long as I was willing to give it the benefit of the doubt. I earned lots of Geo and spent it all on a key that looked like it would unlock a cool new area, and I went in and just found an NPC that said "You don't need to use me yet." Oh, cool -- glad I spent an hour's worth of Geo on that. Compare to Dark Souls where when you buy a random key-type item, it unlocks a whole new area to explore.

It's just not worth it. Play any other 2D platformer instead.

Edit: I so wish I could downvote this game twice. I keep getting lulled into thinking I must be missing something and it's actually good. Nope. There's an entire zone that is nothing but dead-ends! Neener-neener, you can't go this way yet, haha! And the controls for PC are absolutely awful. I don't understand why anyone ever upvoted this game.

To do anything other than reccommend this game boggles my mind. Since I'm lazy, I'll only really go over the things I don't like(everything else is positive for me). The beginning is difficult, and quite a chore for someone new to the game. This is why many of the negative reviews have less than one hour. You die quite easily, it is very easy to get lost without a map, and dying twice in a row makes you lose all your geo(money), which you need for a map, compass, and other essentials. But once you push through the first couple areas, and learn as fast as the difficulty curve, I (almost) guarantee that you will love Hollow Knight. It was arguably game of the year when it came out, and after 100%, I only feel happy that I immersed myself in this beautiful, deep, and rich world.

This game is hot garbage.

I really wanted to like Hollow Knight.  It was so close to being a timeless classic.  However, instead of focusing on standard tenets of good game design, the developers at Team Cherry embraced a recent toxic trend which dictates that everything should be as frustrating as "Dark Souls".

Instead of letting users select the difficulty level, Hollow Knight is permanently set to "Hard" mode.  The controls feel stiff and unnatural.  There's no way to attack diagonally, which makes positioning awkward, especially against flying enemies.  Platforming challenges always lean more towards trial and error than creativity and fun.  Bosses move and attack so quickly there's never any time to react.  If this game were 10% slower it might actually be playable.  Think retro like the SNES, but in a bad way.

The map has no prompts (like special markers for doors that can only be opened with specific items, like the entrance to the Abyss).  Nor does it show the player where they are, unless the compass is not only purchased, but equipped in an otherwise valuable item slot.  (Thankfully this is not the case for other basic tools).  NPCs never offer any guidance, such as "You can bounce higher by attacking purple mushrooms from above", and many breakable walls needed to progress are either too hard to see or very poorly positioned.  There are no tools near the end to help find missed power-ups and passages either.  Consequently, much of the game is spent feeling lost and stuck while dying repeatedly.

Hollow Knight seems to compound this lack polish by deliberately steering towards cheap tricks.  After one particularly difficult area, there's a save-point that can't be used until the boss sitting on it is defeated.  Conversely, the player is often suddenly tricked into making bad decisions (sometimes plot related) that are permanently saved both automatically and immediately.  Exploring is a big part of the fun here, but it often leads to the player losing all of their hard-earned money.  There are items that can be used to recover it when back-tracking is too difficult, but instead of starting with 3 of these for free, they all have to be found or bought, by which point they are mostly useless.  These moments are particularly disappointing.

At times, Hollow Knight can be fun, addictive, challenging, and beautiful.  But it is clumsy at the beginning, tedious at the end, and frustrating throughout.  Hardcore gamers will have a lot of fun here, but only if they use a strategy guide.  (The game is not finished enough to not need one).  Everyone else should steer clear, and I can't help but think that's what the developers intended.  The fact that a 50-hour game has a special mode that ends if the player dies just once says it all.  Hollow Knight is more about bragging rights than accessibility or fun.  Don't waste your energy, or your time.
