I can't believe I have put almost 100 hours into the game and not written a review yet, so here we go.

This game is simply amazing. That's all I should really have to say. The world is beautifully designed so you can explore to your hearts content and always find something new. There have been times where I booted up the game just so I could walk around and take everything in (which is why it took me around 40 hours to get the fastest ending) and find every little secret Team Cherry put into it. The games art style as a whole is very cohesive, and nothing seems out of place.

The combat for the game is fluent; every scenario is like a dance between you and the enemy, reading and understanding their attack patterns, listening for audio cues, and striking at the perfect opportunity. While fighting certain enemies can get incredibly frustrating, every mistake made is your own and can be learned from. And when that frustrating enemy is finally vanquished, and the wave of joy has settled slightly, you'll think: "Alright, who's next?"

This game's soundtrack is probably one of its best features. When starting up the game for the first time and entering the "tutotial" area there is no soundtrack, since you have not truly begun your journey yet. One you enter Dirtmout (The Fading Town), the soundtrack begins with a lonely and slightly sombre tone to it, since the once busy place you have just entered is now abandoned and "fading." Every other song ties in to their respective area or event. For example, places of rest are calm and peaceful, while bossfights are loud and fast paced. 

That's all I had to say. This game has very quickly become my favourite game, and I cannot wait see what Team Cherry makes next!

I initially was drawn away by how the character looks. Not that it looked bad, on the contrary, it looked very well made, but the trailers made it look too "cute". "Oh, is this one of those cutesy-wutesy semi-dark funny adventures?", I thought, not expecting much of the world. I couldn't have been more wrong.

As soon as I had started the game, I was drawn in to its world. Despite his looks, the player character felt badass (and gameplay-wise he controlled well). The story was deep and mysterious. There were a lot of parallels to Dark Souls, [b]BUT[/b] it did not feel like a copycat. It was its own world, despite some similiar tones, worthy of discovering yourself. And if you though DS plot was too hard to follow, don't worry, this one is, despite its subtlety, easier to read.

So the short of it all is, don't judge it by its covers like I did. The game deserves to be bought even on full price, and anyone interested in metroidvania, beautiful art and mysterious stories should not miss it if it goes on a sale.

Amazing game with breath taking visuals and diverse characters, along side an amazing soundtrack created by the dear Christopher Larkin.

The story takes heavy inspiration from dark souls, such as (the dreamers being similar to the lords of cinder, and the hollow knight being what is basically Gwyn) and many different endings. Although there are alot of characters that you can sympathise with and talk to, allowing for a broader scope of the world and it's inhabitants, this is also contrasted with the emotional score that plays in the background of the game, and really makes you feel as if you are part of the world and creates an immersion never felt before. There are also sound effects that you can hear throughout your journey in each area (e.g. the cartographer humming, grubs whimpering, and the singing/humming of Myla), that help signify the location of them without necessarily giving it out openly.

The characters are diverse, each with their own stories and accents that are surprisingly funny to listen to, my favourite character so far is definitely Quirrel, as i relate to him alot throughout the journey and as you find him in multiple locations through out hollownest

The art directory is superb, the cartoony, dark, art style meshes so well with the darkish colour palette and the orange that go's so well with the other colours.

The combat is VERY smooth, there isn't much diversity in attacks (apart from new attacks you learn later on) aside from the usual slice up, down, left and right. However, the controls are very responsive and feels as if there is no input lag what so ever. 

All in all, Hollow Knght is a deeply immersive MetroidVania style game that sets a new standard for the genre, with spectacular art, sound, and characters that help you enjoy and immerse you into the kingdom of Hollownest. 

9/10 - If only the story was more unique, then this game would 100% deserve that 10/10.

That being said Hollow Knight is by far THE BEST money I have ever spend on any video game EVER. 
-The art and animations for every character and biome is so simple but yet overwhelmingly beautiful at the same time.
-The controls are the smoothest, tightest 2D controls i've ever felt for a platformer (although if you don't have a controller it will be difficult.)
-It has an immersive tutorial, that doesn't hold your hand at all. (It constantly teaches you new things as you go to improve you gameplay)
-The boss fights are all unique and fun to learn patterns.
-Finally the platforming puzzles are to DIE for ;)

So in conclusion if you enjoy any kind of metroidvania platformers... you should definitely look into this game... also did I mention it was 15 dollars and they have released multiple free dlcs with the Gods and Glory dlc still coming soon... and perhaps an alternate character campaign.

Honestly, this game does not live up to the hype. There are certainly enjoyable aspects to it - I like the macabre elements, visuals, the size of the world.  I enjoy metroidvania type games in general.  But a lot of the lore is non-sensical and incoherent.   The map and travel systems are frustrating.  There is not enough exploration guidance provided; I feel like I have to constantly revisit areas and commit them to memory in order to make progress. I keep acquiring new skills/spells that don't seem to further my progress or ability to explore further in the game.  This game is evidence that it is possible for a game to be "too" open-world.  Not enough (barely ANY) clues to provide direction on what your capabilities are or where to go next.  

The game mechanics generally do not feel well-balanced - like it is a relic of a bygone era of inefficient gaming, but it was released in 2017.

Hollow Knight is just beautiful; the artwork is great, the music is great, the sounds are great, everything's just really great so far as assets go. There was one semi-annoying boss where two different offscreen enemies could make the exact same warning noise, so some kind of improved surround sound might have been useful for identifying which one’s getting ready to attack, but that complaint is a real reach; all of the assets in this game are great and that’s that. 

The game uses difficulty very wisely as well. Instead of letting you twiddle your thumbs then tap one button for a quick dodge, Hollow Knight does the reverse; if you see an attack coming, you need to get moving, and if you spend so much as a split-second moving in the wrong direction, you aren't going to have time to escape. Boss fights aren't just quick short-term decisions or slow long-term ones; they're quick long-term ones, and that’s the kind of difficulty that makes you feel like a dope when you die. That’s the kind of difficulty that can make a new game feel like it’s the first one you’ve ever played.

For the most part, the abilities you can gain play into the game’s difficulty. If you need certain powerups to reach a boss fight, chances are the fight itself requires you to use them. Almost every new option you get turns into an additional way for the developers to squeeze in more difficulty; it’s very much a “with great power comes great responsibility” thing. There are some exceptions though. The first example is the super-leap, which has pretty much no combat use. You can anchor yourself to walls with it by looping the startup via button mashing, but that was clunky and likely an oversight. When used as intended, it was basically just a travel/key item to help connect the map together. Another example would be the upgraded version of the dodge. It grants brief invulnerability, but comes off as awkward to use. This is because despite being mapped to the same button as the regular dodge, it has a separate refresh rate, which means that if you just want to spend regular dodge while both dodges are cooled down, you have to spend the upgraded one as well, when you might have wanted to save it for later. On top of that, I’d just really liked how Hollow Knight had initially avoided i-frame dodges, so adding one at the end felt like more of a letdown than a reward. The long-but-awkward cooldown usually does force you to save it for the attacks which absolutely require it though, so it still plays into that spiderman motto. The bigger issue is just that both dodges use the same button, which is fair but still a bit strange.

On that note, the control customization was lacking. It was at least [i]there[/i], which is more than I can say for most games, but it was sub nintendo-tier, and that’s a low standard. The right stick just lets you move your camera a quarter-screen up/down if (and only if) you're standing still, and the D pad goes unused. It would have been a lot better to just have the camera stuff on the pad and let the right stick work as an alternate input method for directional attacks. Even just a dedicated "attack downward" button would have made a lot of the pogo-hopping parts of the platforming sections a lot smoother with a controller honestly. Of course, anyone who just plays with a KB+M shouldn’t care at all about this though, and even on a controller this is just a small shortcoming. I’m mentioning it not because it’s too important, but because it’s really obvious. Games like smash bros let you do this and it would take nearly no effort to put in the game.

In terms of equipment balance, the only thing which strikes me as obviously overpowered is the Thorns of Agony charm. This is my major complaint. By the end of the game, you get 10 notches to equip charms in, or 11 if you’ve got the DLC; Thorns of Agony only takes up one of these, and in exchange for that, will make an AoE field around you each time you get hit which deals twice the damage of your regular attack. That’s no joke if you’ve got a lot of health; even the final boss of the DLC, who deals two hitpoints per attack, still inevitably loses 25% of its health just trying to kill an AFK thorn-tank, and pretty much any other boss will lose 60% of its health or more against a build with max hp and thorns equipped, and that's all without taking healing into account. To compare thorns damage to regular charms, the strength charm costs three times as many notches in exchange for a more modest 50% damage to your normal attacks, which shaves off only 33% of each boss fight assuming normal attacks are all you ever use.

Even if you aren’t using health charms, just getting all of the health upgrades and equipping three to four copies of thorns would theoretically be enough to kill almost every boss in the game by just walking into it over and over again. You can’t actually get more than one copy, but it goes to show how stupidly efficient thorns is since that’s only around a third of your total notch space. Thorns is at least over three times better than it needs to be; it should take up two slots instead of one and only deal identical damage to a regular strike rather than double. Maybe then you could let it get a damage boost from the strength charm, so that it didn’t just favor high health babymode builds. Health and damage builds are otherwise about equally strong and interesting though. Healing builds admittedly make the game a little easy, but I think it’s okay to have some training wheels in the game. Grubsong might be a little overpowered in that respect, since much like Thorns it rewards you for taking damage, but Thorns is just busted.

So long as you avoid that one charm, the gameplay is all great. The graphics and sound are superb as well, which you can verify on your own just by watching one of the trailers. I’dve liked to have replaced the super-leap button with a separate button for the second type of dodge you can get, and also to have had a smash-bros style C stick, but the only real flaw Hollow Knight has is the Thorns of Agony issue I mentioned. Everything else about this game is great though, go buy it. Another free patch is coming out soon even. You’re getting great gameplay and 20+ different bosses, plus some fun NPCs, a nice plot to unravel, some real meaning to it instead of just pure vagueness, and a huge map to explore if you really like the metroidvania genre. Go buy it nerd, it’s is one of the best games you’ll play in your life.

Boring, simplistic combat (like a worse version of Zelda 2). Slow movement. No sense of direction or progression in terms of player power and barely any so far in terms of gaining more exploration abilities. Already tons of backtracking and obtuse mechanics where you will only figure out how they work by brute force rather than by any sort of intuitiveness. I like breaking away from explaining everything like so many modern games do but there is truly no sense of direction or purpose in anything. After you rescue 3 or so worms at dead ends for no discernible reason the whole thing starts to seem pointless. I decided to play this game because it is revered as the ultimate Metroidvania but it seems to have none of the things I like about those--fast pace, a steady flow of interesting items and upgrades that make you want to return to places you visited, flashy bosses and music, etc. Clearly I did not play this game for long, but knowing that this is a 20+ hour experience and knowing how little enjoyment I was getting coupled with the few complaints about this game people have being the constant backtracking and hardly ever getting things that speed up combat with the trivial enemies makes me realize that the game simply does not respect the players' time and instead is trying to artificially inflate the playtime a la FFVII Remake. The effect when you take damage is so jarring and annoying and unpleasant that it really makes it hard to play, I legitimately thought it was a glitch at first with the audio cutting out but it's just a disturbingly annoying design choice. I had this game in my library for years and was really looking forward to it, so I really am very disappointed. I am sure some will think my playtime is not sufficient to have an opinion but consider some of the classics of the genre like Super Metroid, Metroid Fusion, or Symphony of the Night and realize how many upgrades and cool areas and bosses you would have fought in the first hour and a half alone. Meanwhile in here I have been in a depressing tunnel with lowkey music and fought two trivially easy but largely uninteresting bosses and my upgrades are some charms with effects that should come without taking the equivalent of an equipment slot and the ability to fire like 3 blasts before running out of 'Soul'. The game simply does not respect the players' time and will only be fun if you're in it for for the extremely long-haul and don't mind repetition. In a single player game, I see wasting the players' time as one of the worst things you can do.

Hollow Knight has a motto "git gud or get lost" slapped all over it. And while certain type of gamers would be overjoyed from such a prospect, there are people who don't find the tryhard mentality particularly attractive.

This downvote is not because the game is "bad" (it is not), this downvote is a warning for the more casual gamers to avoid the Hollow Knight unless you want to submerge into the sea of suffering and frustration. 

If you want a great metroidvania with the "just right" difficulty - I will strongly recommend Ori 1 and 2. Very beautiful, sometimes challenging, but never cranked up to such a degree that you want to smash your gamepad in a fit of rage.

Hollow Knight is a different beast. It is certainly a masterpiece, but if you lean more to the casual side of the gamer spectrum you better avoid it.

I myself did managed to beat it (but not the Path of Pain or most of the radiant fights) out of stubborness but i can say for sure that i will not buy the Silksong no matter how much praise the internet will give it. Im quite sure that the devs will crank up the frustration meter to 11 in the sequel and I've had my fill of it already.

So, in short:
Skilled or tryhard gamer, ready for a challenge? - Get the Hollow Knight, it slaps.
Casual looking for a fun evening? - Better try some other game.

This game is really special. I struggled to get into it at first and needed some helpful prods in the right direction at certain points when I got confused, but the exploration of and progression through the world is fantastic nonetheless. The combat is satisfying once learned, and the abilities and upgrades shake up the gameplay in a nice way that makes backtracking and discovering previously unreachable areas extremely rewarding.

If I had to make one criticism, there’s a particular late game boss that is challenging in the wrong ways. Unlike most of the other main bosses which involve learning opponent move sets and performing the appropriate dodges, this one felt extremely difficult to learn and I was forced to go away and grind to the extent that it made the remaining few things I had to do in the story trivial. Instead of reacting to the dangers of the world around me, I just walked through, swung my weapon a few times and then everything dropped dead. So this boss almost made me quit the game, because up until that moment in the story the game hadn’t forced me to grind for anything at all. I wasn’t accustomed to it and didn’t believe it was necessary to overcome challenges. I always expected that simply ‘getting good’ myself would be enough.

But I don’t want to let that sour my impression of this game. It’s wonderful, give it a chance and it will surely grow on you. The developers are still supporting the game, and there’s plenty of replay value and stuff post-game that I haven’t yet done but am eager to accomplish. It’s well worth the price and much more!

I don't normally write reviews, nor do I play a lot of games from Steam/PC but I can tell you that if you are into platformers this game is for you. Rarely am I sucked into a game where I forget about real-time. By this I mean I hopped on and played 2 hours of it and didn't even realize it. I was so emersed into this weird world. If you played Ori and the Blind forest and enjoyed it this game brings back the feeling. It has a slight Dark Souls tone to it as well, where you feel almost insignificant in a gloomy world stripped of hope. But at the same time, you know your character was destined to do something to change this world... It's a good feeling. Instead of bonfires you have benches. 
One of the greatest aspects of this game BESIDES THE ART STYLE (Its incredible. Not as good as Ori, but definitely keeps you staring at everything), are the characters you meet on the side. These characters have me exhausting every character I meets dialogue until they repeat themselves. Some characters you keep running into, that are on a journey themselves and continue to tell you a story, give you some lore on where you are, and often times have you stop and listen to the odd sounds and stare at the back drop of the area... I love it. 
I see a lot of people complaining about going through the same areas, I never experienced this. I mean, I would head in a direction, until I could proceed no further, then go back and find another way. If you are good at platformers/RPG's it follows the same type of principles which keeps the game play familiar and cozy. You know when you need to stop proceeding, find a key, defeat a boss, or unlock an ability to access. But if you aren't familiar with platformers this may be hard and I could see how you could find yourself wandering the same area, figuring out what to do. JUST MAKE SURE TO EXPLORE EVERY ROUTE. 
BUY THE COMPASS... BUY IT. ONCE YOU GET IT THE GAME BECOMES MUCH EASIER. I farmed the first mini boss, (Before False Knight) over and over once I got the first projectile spell, until I could buy the rest of the stuff that the Map makers wife could offer me. I probably had to kill it 9 times. (He isnt hard after the first time) This made the game much easier for me to proceed into the next stage. I recommend keeping all of your currency. As you will need it a lot.

8/10 Great game with a beautiful macabre atmosphere and a very smooth difficulty curve.

Platforming is occasionally frustrating as walljumping will fail on smaller surfaces and the player might not start wallsliding when coming into contact with a wall either. Most flying enemies are annoying as well, with Primal Aspids being the premier shitstain of an enemy as they require the most precise manuvering out of all enemies to finish the fight unscathed (god help if you fight multiples of them at once).

[b] Hollow Knight is an action-platformer metroidvania. With beautiful graphics, a brilliant soundtrack and tight controls, you'll find yourself lost in it's world. [/b]

[b] Hollow Knight [/b] is an [b] action-platformer [/b], and arguably controls are what makes or breaks a game of this genre; luckily for us, [b] Team Cherry nailed the controls [/b]. The controls are basic - yes - but with easy-to-understand controls comes masterful gameplay orchestrated by the player themself! It's honestly such a pleasure to control your character in this game.

The [b] art style is beautful [/b], I can't count how many times I've just let go of my controller to look at how pretty this game is - and it's [b] the same with it's soundtrack [/b]. I've spent about 50 hours on my main save file, and about 2 of those were spent not playing, just sat in-game at a bench in the first town of [i] Dirtmouth [/i] and that's not counting the hours I spent sat at other benches!

Whilst the world of Hollow Knight has a lot of lore, and it's very complex, the game in design is actually rather minimalist. You begin the game by falling down a deep hole, the controls are not told to you, and you are not told where to go or what to do. This might scare some players, might even turn some away - but for someone like me I was forced into a state of intrigue. What might I find in this world? What could any of this mean? I can only describe my experience as pure beauty. I truly wish I could replay this game without any of my current memories, just so I could experience that feeling again.

[b] In short, this game is f*ckin' good bro. [/b]

[h1]The Review[/h1]
I can't recommend this game enough. Yes, this game is not easy. Yes, this game is non-linear with a huge branching world. Yes, you have to put in a little effort in order to discover the true story of what's happening. Yes, some (if not, all) of the bosses can be a bit unforgiving.

These are the biggest complaints I've heard and read through negative reviews of this game and I still don't understand how these are bad things. Let's break them down one by one.

[i][b]"This game is not easy."[/b][/i]
So, this game isn't going to hold your hand with anything. Combat is very straightforward as to what's possible and what's not and you will learn through playing what is the right move and what is the wrong move to make. Only by playing will you actually *get better* at it though, so don't let it discourage you. Very much like the SoulsBorne games, you will get better at the games core combat mechanics the more play it and by the end of the game, if you ever start a new game it's going to seem like a cake walk because of how good you've gotten.

[i][b]"This game is non-linear with a huge, branching world."[/b][/i]
This one is the most confusing complaint to me. I, for one, absolutely love games that allow me to go practically anywhere I want whenever I want in order to discover where to go next. The early game is a *bit* linear in that there is a designated path for you to take, but that doesn't stop the game from letting you go in the opposite direction first to see if there's anything else to discover. [spoiler]In fact as soon as you get past the tutorial and make your way in to the main world, there's even a choice between two bosses you can fight first depending on which direction you take.[/spoiler]

But the complaint stems from "not knowing where to go next", which is a bit more of a defendable argument if the game itself isn't built around the fact it [b]wants[/b] you to explore, discover, and put the pieces together on your own as a player. The whole map mechanic literally backs this claim up as first off, you dont get a map unlocked for an area [i]until[/i] you explore it enough to find the cartographer NPC. Then, it doesn't reveal any rooms until you explore them first, and even then it won't [i]update[/i] the map until you've made your way back to a resting point. They [b]want[/b] you to tread in to unknown territory and take the environment in without a map as it puts more faith in the player to discover things for themselves rather be handed the information on a silver platter.

[i][b]"You have to put in effort to discover the story."[/b][/i]
I think this is the most genius part of the entire game. I hear a lot of people not liking the fact of how "vague" the story is or that there's no story involved at all. [i]This can't be farther than the truth.[/i] The story of Hollow Knight is actually very rich and full of detail as far as what's happening, you just need to read the dialogue and pay attention to the environment. 

Ori and the Blind Forest had the narrator telling the story as you played which was their way of presenting the story, which is also fine as it fit the aesthetic of that game very well. This game however, from its core puts you in the shoes of your character where you have no idea why you're there doing what you're doing (as it's implied the main character doesn't either) but through playing and just by paying attention to everything, the story becomes more and more clear the more you play (and pay attention), until that (true) ending comes and it leaves you thinking about it for days, even weeks afterwards trying to find the hidden meaning in everything.

[i][b]"Some (if not all) of the bosses can be a bit unforgiving."[/b][/i]
This is, by far, the biggest complaint I've seen about this game. It sort of goes hand in hand with my first point in that the game itself isn't easy but you will get better at it the more you play to the point where what wasn't easy before suddenly becomes easy. This is very true of the bosses in this game as well, but the biggest stigma with this is if you die, you need to run back to the boss room to try it again, possibly through waves of enemies in the process.

While I get that this may be an [i]annoying[/i] thing to have to overcome, I can't see how it's considered to be bad game design. It's a deliberate punishment for dying, not just to the boss, but at all. They're consistent in how it punishes you by sending you back to the last place you rested, but as you get farther and farther in to the game, the distance in which bosses are placed relative to these resting points get farther and farther away, [i]increasing[/i] that punishment. Luckily, however, they've since added a mechanic to the game that can actually circumvent this punishment [spoiler]by allowing you to travel back to the boss instantly through the dreamgate, a fast travel spot you can set almost anywhere in the map at any time,[/spoiler] but that is something you still need to [i]earn[/i] by playing. If anything, this is [i]more[/i] forgiving than just dying randomly as you'll still need to travel back to where you died to reclaim your lost geo/soul meter.

[h1]In Conclusion/TL;DR[/h1]
Hollow Knight is genuinely a masterpiece from not only its beautiful artstyle and visuals, but its fun and highly rewarding gameplay.

However, it does not cater to a casual market and will ask a lot of the player's will to figure out what to do/where to go next and how to tackle the combat. It will punish you for mistakes, but will also be highly rewarding to overcome. It also allows a lot of freedom to the player to explore and discover the world and its multiple paths to get to each area in different ways. The story is also very rich, but you will need to pay close attention to the dialogue and environment to truly understand it.

If these are things that you're not okay with as a player, then maybe this game isn't for you, but if you're any sort of fan of Metroidvanias and/or the SoulsBorne series, this game is amazing in both aesthetic and gameplay.

This game is very imaginative and inventive, visually superb and a good strategy + platformer game. The gameplay elements are all there and it doesn't buy its own hype too much. It takes skill to overcome the challenges and a willingness to fail a LOT. And that's not a bad thing! Overall, a very solid game.

The biggest downside for me is that the reward system is nonexistent. After clearing 2+ bosses (including some mini bosses) and investigating the overwhelming majority (if not all) of the intro portion of the map, you are rewarded with exactly nothing.

I'm not kidding, it's the weirdest thing!

It's like your DM says: "ok, you enter an old, dusty shop, what do you do?"
Me: "I ask the shopkeeper what upgrades and items he has, I have 40,000 rubies."
DM: "The shopkeeper answers: 'hello there! I only have random pieces of artifacts available and none of them combine or do anything significant."
DM: "They're all giving off a mysterious aura!"
Me: "Um, ok, but do you have like ANY weapons?"
DM: "No, just mystical aura items! Spend your money, damn you!"
Me: "I CAN'T. YOU ONLY SELL CRAP."

That's what it feels like to buy items in the early game.

The game insists that you play it a certain way (aka: Invest in 1/4 useless item so that at least you don't blow all your money or never get hit ever and pray you run across a shopkeeper with items of actual value), so much so that it's distracting and near stressful and not fun stressful, waste of time, stressful.

They may as well have put falling damage in their 2D platformer. *JonTron quits*


This review isn't a "boo hoo it's too hard" review, because the challenge is excellent and keeps it fresh, especially since it's a mystery at the same time; you're trying to figure out what the heck is going on. It's interesting. However, you have to hang onto your money and play super safely, because meaningful upgrades aren't available to you at a stage where most other games make them available and in hollow knight you're required to move onto the next series of challenges without upgrades and while saving your money that you could drop at any second. No, it's not like Sonic. The coins in Sonic just mean "alive", this is much much more than that.

The nonexistent early game upgrade cache makes this game frustrating to play to say the least. Games should be rewarding you for achieving goals, but this one actively chooses not to for some reason. I guess it expects you to have so much fun playing that you just "have to see what's next!"? (buying its own hype) Idk. That didn't pan out that way for me. You need the upgrades to move forward, but without them you're essentially gambling your savings, as you need to save those for upgrades, which is...irritating to say the least. Die twice and you lose all your money, therefore making the nonexistent upgrade system even harder to circumvent.

It's not a bad game necessarily, but the lack of rewards in the early game design just forces me to rate this a thumbs down as I believe that this is an intrinsic flaw in the game's design.

I bought it because I kept hearing that this game is like Dark Souls. Even though some gameplay mechanics are the same, the gameplay did not feel like DS to me for a second. Where are all the stats and where is the leveling system? Where is all the mysterious loot with vague item descriptions? Where are all the different weapons? I realize that you might obtain some later, but in DS you have a huge variety of weapons within 20 minutes of gameplay. Where are the different attack types and the one-handed/two-handed system? Where are the damage types like magic, fire etc.? I could go on for quite some time, but in short, this game is nothing like DS besides bonfires and corpse runs.

So I came looking for a game like DS and didn't get it. But I gotta say, even besides it not being like DS, the game just isn't fun. The whole game feels like a chore and the combat is boring and weightless. I should mention that I am also a little bit biased because I can't seem to enjoy 2D games. I've tried so many times, but they just never provided an enjoyable experience for me. 

If you are like me, and you are looking for a game like Dark Souls, then you should know that I was pretty disappointed by this one. But maybe you are different. The game itself is crafted very passionately and it features a lot of cool ideas. Sadly, those ideas just weren't fun to play for me.

If I would've known that this was "Dark Souls, the Platform game", then I wouldn't have wasted my time buying and playing this.

The game is so insanely oppressive and grindy. I thought I would be able to play and chill, but at some points I just find myself close to bashing the keyboard due to frustration. There is no natural progression in the game. Go around, explore, and hope you hit the areas that are relevant to your current power level. Died along the way? Tough luck, lose your coins and go pick up your ghost. What? You didn't find the map for that specific area and now you have no idea where your ghost is? Shucks, you just lost all your coins.

And why on God's green earth do I need to actually BUY maps and a compass just to see where I am? And why do I "accidentally" step into boss room's without some kind of indication? I have limited hours a day to play a game. Why would I want to spend it walking around between character deaths? Awful game design which just promotes frustration and grind. I don't mind losing due to difficulty, but I DON'T WANT TO SPEND 2-5mins between retries.

I have completely lost all faith in Steam reviews where games are stated as MASTERPIECES and then I find myself refunding them (wish I could in this case).

TL;DR - If you don't mind dying a lot and spending most of your time walking between retries, go ahead. Spend your money. If you want a game where there is natural progression, a nice story and is relatively chill - get "Ori and the Blind Forest" instead.

Hollow Knight is one of the best metroidvania games I've ever played, and close to the best game I've ever played. What came from a small indie developer who got most of their funding from kickstarter, comes masterfully crafted and whimsical entertainment that never bores for a second.

[h1] Combat and Exploration: [/h1]

The main focus of the game is combat and exploration, and both of these go hand in hand when it comes to this game. When you first start, you can only heal, jump, and attack, which gets expanded upon later in the game. There are fifteen different types of areas, each with their own theme, enemies, and music. But as you explore, you see paths that seem to be blocked off, or hard to reach. In reality though, those paths are accessible, but only until you gain a certain ability. 

While other games seem to introduce abilities or items to only be used to unlock certain areas, getting abilities in Hollow Knight greatly expands how you fight and kill enemies, while at the same time opening up many different paths. Each time you gain a new ability, you seem to remember every area that had a path now accessible with this ability, and you also discover how it can be used in combat effectively. 

Most abilities require you to use soul, a white substance you get when killing enemies and hitting soul totems, which is stored in a soul vessel, which can be expanded along with your health. This allows for being rewarded for killing enemies, and encouragers the player to explore more to earn more health and vessels. Combat is even more rewarding when you seek out charms, items that give you special abilities like gaining more soul from enemies or turning you into a slug while healing, allowing you to move and dodge (yes, that's a real charm). 

Players have a huge amount of option, and each person could have an entirely different experience based on what path they choose. I found myself being completely lost half of the time, which for me was fine since I was distracted by what I could find in the area I’ve just discovered and the secrets it has. 

The best parts are when you have no idea where your going, and hope you stumble upon a map for the area or a way out, only to end up in a completely new place that is brought to life by new art style, new enemies, new music, abilities, lore, items, charms, and more, which is why the pathfinding is always rewarding for me. Something is always around the corner if you look hard enough, and it’s always a pleasure finding it.


[h1] Story and Characters: [/h1]

It begins as your character is called back to the insect kingdom of Hollownest, a once glorious and massive populous deserted and forgotten, only leaving a few individuals and creatures left. As you began your journey at the town of Dirtmouth, your curiosity and exploration is rewarded, as a dark and mysterious past of the kingdom is slowly revealed bit by bit, until your objective becomes clear...You need to save this kingdom.

Characters bring a lot of personality and charm towards the dark world of Hollownest. Brave warriors like Hornet, a female vessel like you, chooses to defend the kingdom of hollownest from invaders, while the map selling Conifer, delighted by the wonders of the kingdom, chooses to explore every chasam you might find yourself in. Even minor characters that have very little impact on the story, add a sense of realism to the world, with charming little quirks or interesting dialogue to them. Without the characters, Hollownest would be lifeless.

While the main story and your goal is easily presented, specific stuff about the timeline of Hollownest and special parts of the lore require exploration throughout the kingdom. The good part about it is that it gives you just enough information throughout the game to get the main story, but also leaves you at a point where you really want to know more, and rewards you for exploring enough to get the ‘true ending’ of Hollow Knight.

There are 3 different endings in the game. While I won’t spoil anything about them, I will say that if you take the time to do the exploration required to get the true ending, you will feel much more rewarded than the other endings in the game. It does require a lot of work, but the motivation throughout the entire time is wanting to know the end of the story, and boy is it something.


[h1] Art + Sound Design and Music [/h1]

Hollow Knights blend of music and art design is honestly too perfect. I can't think of a better game that pairs these two together so well. What stands out is the variety, and how you usually never see the same enemy in a different kingdom, except for certain challenges and mini stages. I believe there are over 100 different enemies, 20 of them being unique bosses, never repeated in another area. Backgrounds bring everything in front of you alive, as the enemies blend in with the coloring and style, as well as their moves and attacks matching up as well. 

But what brings this game to life is the music, which would make the game seem lifeless without it. Every single track matches perfectly with what's happening on screen, making each area even more recognizable as soon as it's theme kicks in. Jumpy, rhythmic tunes dance throughout in kingdoms like Greenpath full of color, while slow, melodic piano drifts through the wind in an area like Dirtmouth, abandoned and left to rot. 

Dark, dreadful, and spooky areas full of centipedes and creatures attacking you at every corner replace music with sharp and spine-tingling sound effects creeping throughout the area, while areas completely devoid of life at the bottom of the map don't even have music, implying the area has something hidden or shrouded in darkness. 

Bosses are brought to life, as loud, bombastic, and fast melodies play over the intensity of the fight, and while it all goes on, the music keeps on climbing and climbing, introducing more and more melodies until it erupts in a perfect symphony of insanity and power, perfectly simulating exactly how you feel until you finally finish it, eventually returning to a slower and slower tempo until it all goes quiet.

[h1] Boss Fights and Difficulty: [/h1]

While I hate to be every video game critic ever when met with a challenging game, this game is a bit like Dark Souls with it's difficulty. Dying in the game leaves a ghost behind with all your geo (currency) and a third of your vessel. Dying without getting back your ghost means you lose all of your currency, so it adds a layer of difficulty, making players have to go through the same challenges again that led to their death. 

Most of your dying is done through doing boss fights, which can sometimes be a bit of a pain since your save points can be far away from these fights, and you have to make the same path to the boss room again each time. To be fair, it adds a level of realism to the game, since save points being right next to a boss room would be very unnatural in a kingdom like Hollownest. 

Back to the boss fights, almost all of it is done through learning all of the moves and being able to counter them and find the right opening to attack. It's all done so beautifully though, with each fight being extremely consistent and full of patterns. The small battling space allows for almost no errors to exist from the boss, but they will completely destroy you if you slip up two or three times. 

Some people seem to be critical of the boss fights for being too hard or frustrating, but I always seem to find my way through a bosses patterns if I keep going at it, and the only times I find myself being frustrated is when I make a mistake, never the boss. I think the most time I've spent on a boss was a total of 2 1/2 hours, and it was the final boss of the new content update 'The Grimm Troupe'.

I love metroidvanias... but this one. No. Period, just no.
I spend SO MUCH TIME wandering around NOT having fun, NOT exploring, just bashing my hollow skull into walls until I could find the next place to go. The worst part about that is the massive scale of the map and the horrible "fast travel" system in place. Even when you DO get more of them, they're so spaced out it should be called "half travel".
Then there's the combat. I have NEVER had such horrendous combat before in just about any game I've played. They're *THIS CLOSE* to making it very tight and controlled and responsive but it's just a bit off, which makes the nail-biting fights just frustrating. Couple that with the fact that every boss, after the halfway mark, seems to be TEAMING with HP and it just becomes this marathon of perfection for each fight. Also, check points are no where near the bosses, they're just the last "bench" (save point) you took, which is almost always designed to be nowhere near a boss. So if you're having trouble, you'll be trekking back to the boss which means any new load out could make things hard and you'll still need to do these frustrating returns just to try again.
Also, the load outs. I thought, at first, this was a cool concept, but as you go on you realize what they "change" for you isn't drastic and can even have such a minimal impact that you're scratching your head wondering what something even does or why did they both making multiple. It would have been much better to condense them into ones that had a major change and limit how many you could have instead of being able to equip more who barely change anything.
So even if you enjoy exploring these long, winding, repetitive, on-going hallways, you'd better be ready for some long, repetitive, damage sponges of boss fights. This game was good for a while, not great but tolerable. But after the twenty fourth rage quit from a boss who I knew better than any guide's insight, that was the last straw for me. If the "difficulty" was toned down just a bit, this game would at least be finish'able.

This is the second time I have written this review, because while I don’t recommend this game, I do like it and want to play it, but the issues I have with it lead me to hate playing it, hence why I have uninstalled this product from my account with no refund. My initial review was more vitriolic than I would like it to have been, and I want my criticism to be constructive.

Spoiler free TLDR: The game has great visuals and soundtrack, but is let down by an unoriginal story, and rubbish mechanics concerning healing and boss health bars.

////////////////////Spoilers Below///////////////////////////

My main criticisms with this game are as follows:
-	The story is just that of Dark Souls, and many elements are just taken straight from the Souls games and pasted here such as an infection turning the citizens of the kingdom into mindless drones that attack you (Hollows caused by the undead curse), the king trying to save his kingdom by containing the infection in the Hollow Knight but this ends up dooming the kingdom to decay (Gwyn linking the first flame), said king hiding away and being built up only to find that he is already dead (Vendrick), and all of the endings basically boiling down to taking the place of the Hollow Knight and containing the curse for now or ending the infection at its source (Link the flame or end the age of fire). There are many more elements and story beats I can draw comparison to but I think this makes my point, and while this may be a case of parallel thinking, the sheer amount of copied ideas in the story leads me to believe that Dark Souls must have been a large inspiration to a fault. It was very disappointing to me that I was playing this game just to find out I had played it 3 times already, but for those who aren’t huge Souls fans this probably wont be an issue to you.

-	The healing system is implemented in such a baffling way that I can only see the developers either wanting to infuriate the player or disincentivize healing. Typically healing works in one of two ways in a game; either you heal for a short period to heal a small amount of hit points (think of running over a med pack or a mushroom from Mario that heals you 1 hp essentially) or  you have a longer heal time for a potentially greater amount of health, but this leaves you open to attacks (The estus flask from Dark Souls). Hollow Knight healing periods are long and leave you open to attack and heal a measly 1 HP each time. If you try to do this in a fight there is a high chance that you get hit and lose the HP you just gained, essentially invalidating what you just did, but now you have less soul. This is especially egregious in boss fights but that is a whole point on its own and I will address the healing there as well.


-	You are given an illusion of many branching paths but the early to mid game exploration is very restricted. At the beginning of the game you have 2 areas: The Forgotten Crossroads and Greenpath. After that is the Fungal Wastes and then when you get to the City of Tears you can diverge your path of play. I’m not a fan of restricting the players choices by locking them behind bosses, perhaps I have been spoiled by games like BOTW where you can go anywhere, or DS1 where you have 5 or 6 paths to go down from the start, but this early game funnel annoys me. I would prefer to have all areas open but have the enemies in some be more difficult so that if I want to explore other areas I can but it will be challenging. Hollow Knight feels like DS2 in which you can see many diverging paths, but they are arbitrarily blocked off by locked doors so you really have 1 or 2 choices of direction. Hell, even with the 2 zones you get in Hollow Knight you cant do Greenpath immediately and need to beat False Knight so you really have 1 path until City of Tears.

-	All of my previous criticisms have been subjective, but this is the only objective issue I have with the game: No boss health bars. I suspect this was done so as to not have a health bar clutter up the screen and detract from the visuals, but it is a relatively minor exclusion that mixes in with some of the other issues I have which makes boss fights completely unenjoyable. Health bars relay information to the player on how well they are doing in the fight, and the player can use them to adjust their strategy. No health bars mean the player gets no feedback on how close they were to killing a boss, its like taking an exam, and each time you fail you just get 0/100 and get no useful feedback to improve upon, other than “Get better at it”. Yes, the bosses play animations a you do certain amounts of damage to them, as in they pause and allow you to get in a free hit or two, but with no health bar I don’t know the context of the animation. If the boss doing a special animation or pausing for a second because it’s got 66% health left? 50%? 5%? I don’t know. This is where the discencentivised healing comes in. Because healing is frankly rubbish in a boss fight, I’m incentivised to try and take as little damage as possible, meaning I’m not in the enemy’s face. If I could see the health bar of the boss, I could make a judgement on if I can rush it down and survive or not, but I only have half the picture, thus I keep having to play boss battles safe due to a lack of feedback.

Hollow Knight comes close to being a good game, and I think changing the elements I have talked about above would make it a game I could recommend. But in its current state I cannot recommend anyone buy or play it. If you do buy it I hope you can see past the problems that I have, and I hope that Team Cherry goes on to produce better games in the future.

This game totally deserves to cost much much more. Has more content than some of the AAA games. And it really is one of the best Metroidvania games I have ever seen.

---
Pros
---

+If you like exploration and some action that's not that easy (excluding Godhome and NKG), and not on impossible levels then this game is for you.
+Gives you at least 25hrs of gameplay. Still depends on your game style. I reached the final boss on 27-28 hour mark. Killed it on 35. Reason is:
+Multiple endings. Some are good, some are bad, some are -Godhome spoilers on here. Not gonna tell anything xd- They are mostly easy to achieve if you can beat White Palace. (First time Radiance is hard though.)
+Beautiful soundtrack that sucks you up to the game's atmosphere. An abandoned kingdom, with remaining bugs' depression all over the place. Soundtrack on the boss fights are pretty good too. (I'm looking at you NKG, it was really good.)
+Hidden lore that can be learned through out the game. Which goes just fine with this game's exploration element. As you feel you progress in the story, too.
+Sense of achievement. With all those explorations etc. you feel you are accomplishing things in the game and you want to see what's coming up next. Like on the next sections, new bosses, new upgrades etc.
+Hand drawn graphics. They are well drawn and they give the game some kind of special aesthetic.
+Game maybe giving you 25 hrs of gameplay though, you can shorten or lenghten it on your wish. (3 hrs on a normal speedrun that everyone can do, trying to get all achievements will result in a longer play time just like mine.)
+3 Free DLC's! These DLCs are : Hidden Dreams, The Grimm Troupe and Godmaster. Hidden Dreams adds more functionality to your Dream Nail, extra 2 bosses etc. Grimm Troupe adds -as it is in it's name- Grimm Troupe event to the game, which you'll collect the flames for Grimmchild, then fight Grimm. (Which second phase is really challenging and really fun.) Last is Godhome, simply a boss rush mode for Hollow Knight. -If you are a diehard fan, this section will give you 10+ hrs of gameplay.- These are all for free. And there's Lifeblood update, which is a general overhaul for the game.
+New upcoming Hornet DLC, this one will be pay to play this time, though the story will continue after all these hours of content. Wow.

---
Cons
---

-Most of the bosses lack interesting features that makes them fun to play. While progressing in the story, you may not realize this as you want to progress the game even further while defeating the boss quickly. But on Godhome, they pretty much get boring fast. REALLY FAST.
-Game is pretty challenging, but sometimes it just goes beyond that and becomes cancerous. -Until you learn to adapt to it but yeah. Having a platformer section on a game that doesn't have a camera system that suits it is bad. I'm looking at you White Palace. ESPECIALLY you Path of Pain. (Though Path of Pain is optional.)-

So this is an odd one for me. Hollow Knight's art direction and sound are fantastic, it really feels like a labor of love, and it’s also exactly what it advertises: a tough, exploration-focused platformer devoid of any handholding, and for me at least it was absolutely punishing. This is presumably great news for fans of the genre, but I wound up having zero fun with it. I don’t seem to have the patience for all the backtracking and required (but largely undirected) exploration, so I quit after less than an hour and moved on.

But I’m still giving HK a thumbs-up. 

It was a very bad fit for me, but that’s not the same thing as it *being* a bad game. On the contrary, it felt like an authentic and well-executed Metroidvania-style game, which is (unfortunately) a kind of game that I’ll never enjoy, regardless of its quality. Team Cherry deserves props for their effort here, even if I’m incapable of properly appreciating it.

Oh, and the soundtrack itself is hauntingly beautiful, so I feel like the cost of the bundle was worth that alone.

If you like platformers, challenges, or high quality products, buy this game.  It's 'just' a platformer/Castlevania without a lot of movement or attack methods that haven't been done before in other games.  But those moves and attacks are put to good use in an enormous, well-designed world.  When playing for the first time, you'll spend a lot of time exploring to reveal the world and find everything.  There is a lot of 'everything'.  Lots of locations, 40+ 'charms' you can equip and mix and match, 150ish unique creatures including several dozen bosses, each with their own moves, strengths and weaknesses.  Lots of variety in scenery, well thought out lore that is slowly revealed as you uncover the world but rarely spoon fed to you, and beautiful music that really adds to the atmosphere.  Also there are 4 expansions which all come free after the initial purchase.  This game is an amazing tour de force within its genre, at least as good as the best Mario games, and an incredible value at the $15 list price.

Many of the movement abilities and attack abilities you'll eventually get, you don't begin the game with.  You have to find them, and many are defended by challenging bosses, or difficult platforming sections.  Unless you have extremely gud reflexes and observational skills, prepare to have your a$$ handed to you often when initially encountering many bosses, they are a lot tougher and stronger than you!   You may not enjoy that part of this game, if you get frustrated by setbacks. 

But the boss fights are fair in that every boss has a finite number of moves, and tells which tip off which one is coming next, if you know what to look for.  There is some randomness in their move sequence, locations of flying debris, etc, but a lot of it comes down to your ability to learn their moves, choose charm combos that work best versus particular opponents, and some degree of dexterity moving around.  At times it's exasperating if it takes you many tries to get through the most punishing encounters, but there's satisfaction in finally prevailing, and getting better and better at playing the game.  I don't have the greatest 'fast twitch' gaming skills, but I've been able to finish all of the core game, and > 100% 'completion' the way they measure it, you probably can too if you stick with it.  If you play long enough and keep learning, you'll find that many encounters that seemed difficult the first time become a lot easier once you become fully experienced.

There many not be huge replay value after you have seen everything once, but there are achievements to chase, for completing a run without dying (Steel Soul), speed runs, and completing some optional gauntlets of boss fights  that are provided in the last expansion.  Just doing everything once for the first time can keep you happily occupied for dozens or even 100+ hours depending on your pace playing these kinds of games, and how many tries you need to complete the challenging bits.

Just buy this game, enjoy, and marvel at how well everything in it is done.

The time it takes to beat the endgame content is enough time to complete a completely different game, which is what you should do. "Practicing" the same boss over and over isn't actually fun. You're being tricked into thinking it's fun when it's actually a waste of time. Yes, most video games are a waste of time but playing a game that doesn't spark joy for hours at a time is basically the definition of a bad game. Maybe the base game is worthwhile and doesn't force you to waste your time, but the DLC is actually not very interesting or fun. The satisfaction of beating a boss is not nearly equal to the disappointment of dying to it over and over. I wouldn't recommend this to my friends because I don't want them to feel frustrated or sad. "Git gud" sure. I'll probably finish multiple single player games before beating Pantheon 5, and I'm not afraid to admit I'm not patient enough to waste any more time attempting it.

You arrive at a ruined town, there's a plague, it turns its citizens mad, and then some fans of the game toss the word "vagueness" around to excuse shoddy story telling. 

But screw the story. Honestly, you shouldn't be buying this game for the story, and I don't know if you should be buying it for the boss fights either. They're fun, but because there are so many different ways to progress, boss fights can often feel unfair or trivial -- depending on how much health or what charms you have. 

The best part about this game is exploring zones or re-exploring zones and finding breakable walls/new pathways that you might've missed the first time. If the two back-to-back illusionary walls in Dark Souls that hid an entire area and faction was your bee's knees, I think you'd like Hollow Knights.

I wanted this game to be good. I had heard such great things about it and, after finishing Dark Souls 2, I decided to give this game a try. I went in knowing this was going to be a different game than Dark Souls, and to be perfectly honest, I really do like the idea of this game. A Souls-esc platformer? That should be fun! But boy was I wrong. 
I'll first start with the good stuff. First off, the art direction in this game is simply beautiful. Every area has its own distinct feel, and really immerses you in its environment and story. The music is decent (if a bit repetitive) too. 
Now for the bad. I'll try not to go off on a rant, but man, this game frustrated me like no Souls game ever has. First off, platforming. By itself, the platforming wasn't too bad. There were a few areas that I found more frustrating than fun, but overall it wasn't terrible by itself. However, combined with the rest of the games bullshittery, the platforming quickly becomes a haste. To start with, the directional attacks (up for and upward attack, down for a downward one) felt very unreliable, causing huge headaches in certain areas/situations. Don't get me wrong, when those worked, it was fun, but that wasn't often the case. Whats worse (in my opinion) is the knock-back you receive not just from being hit by enemies, but also you hitting enemies. There are many cases in this game where you are forced to balance on these precariously small platforms, usually with spikes or steaming water underneath them. Factor in the ludicrous knock-back, and you have a recipe ripe for disaster. Be hit by an enemy will often result in you falling into the environmental hazard, causing you to lose 2 HP (not sure what its called) in just one hit. Not only that, but you also receive knock-back by hitting an enemy yourself, which can often result in the same scenario as before. Another problem I have with this game is the amount of backtracking I found myself doing. It was very annoying to have to travel back and forth across the map multiple times to find a certain exit or item. A simple bench travel mechanic would've been greatly appreciated. Along with this, I would've really appreciated maps just being a given, rather than having to find a specific NPC located within the area (although, to be fair, he was usually near an entrance). Many of the rooms in a certain area look very similar, and I got lost very often without a map.
Now, maybe everything I have brought up is just a skill issue. And if it is, I fully except that. I'm not that good at platforming, so maybe I was just really out of my element. But, if I'm honest, this game was often just more frustrating than fun. Maybe in a few years I'll give this another try. But for now, this'll have to stay in my backlog. I really wanted to like this game, but, maybe it just wasn't for me.

This is hands down one of the best games I have ever played PERIOD. The artstyle of the game is beautiful, the soundtrack is absolutely breathtaking, and every mechanic for combat is near seamless. Some of the NPCs that you can also meet are charming, unique, and also helpful (at times). At the same time, throughout my playthrough with the game I never encountered any gamebreaking bugs or glitches. There's even some very well hidden lore that you can find for yourself (I wouldn't want to spoil it for you). 

However, this game also extremely difficult, and I don't recommend this game for people who are new to 2d platformers, as some of the sections of the game can leave you in frustration, whether it be excruciatingly dificult platforming or some of the boss fights that you have in the game. 

Most of the game mechanics are the key aspects to a metroidvania, and I can respect that. There are also a few new twists to your playthroughs of the game, such as adding Charms, which can completely change how you play the game. Along with adding charms, there are also many sidequests that reward you with items to keep you going.

Then we get to the DLCs. Did I forget to mention that the DLCs are FREE? Sure, most of them are pretty short, but each one adds more secrets for you to discover, as well as adding awesome boss fights that will be sure to keep you on your toes. Some of the DLCs also add more sidequests for you to do, which adds to the experience and rewards you earn.

There are also multiple endings that you can get throughout your experience of Hollow Knight, some taking more skill than others. To top it all off, there are also multiple gamemodes in which you can unlock the more you play the game; BUT BE WARNED, the gamemodes that you unlock truly put your skills to the test.


Overall, I believe that Hollow Knight is one of the best games that any experienced player will ever play. If you know what you're getting yourself into, BUY THIS GAME. I highly reccomend it to anyone.

This time, I got to 87% completion before I hit that wall (ie, MUCH further than my first attempt), before I had to just call my time investment a complete loss. Aside from that the devs are very lazy and just splatter respawn points about the map haphazardly with no logic or reason behind their positioning with the only caveat that it will be miles from any boss. Hollow Knight is a tough Metroidvania title with a good amount of action.
The action is simple, with no combos or anything like that.

However that is the best part of the combat in my opinion.
Simple yes but shockingly fun, fluid, and difficult.

There are also Charms, which will effect your character in a number of ways, such as increasing damage when low on HP, or allowing you to run faster.
With the power of Charms, you can change your playstyle to your liking, but it will always remain simple.

That doesn't mean it is easy though.
Be warned that this game is much tougher then your ordinary modern game.
And there is no option to lower the difficulty setting.
There are ways to make the game more difficult, like by Oversouling your body for example (which makes you take 2x damage), but there isn't any real way to reduce the difficulty at all.
Levels don't exist so you can't just grind your way out of a tough boss fight and there is no such thing as armor.
So keep this in mind before jumping in, if you don't like difficult games then I'd advise you to stay away.


Now the Metroidvania aspect of this game is great also.
The world is incredibly vast, and to my surprise, even though I thought I explored through 100% of the map, I still missed 9 bosses which were in hidden areas in the map.

The game rewards exploration a lot and it's fantastic.
And in true Metroidvania fashion, there are multiple sequence breaks available in the game, allowing you to reach areas you aren't supposed to be in early on if you are skilled enough.
This makes speedrunning the game really fun.


The Story is almost non-existent but the lore is rich and runs very deep.
The world is absolutely beautiful and is dripping with care and attention.
Think of it like Dark Souls.

The visuals are absolutely stunning.
Great 2D artwork with a wonderful art style that will never show signs of age...

Visuals are great, but they are at their best when paired with an appropriate set of music tracks, and luckily Hollow Knight's OST is fantastic.
Music is one of the most subjective aspects of a game, but I highly doubt anyone could be disappointed with such a masterpiece.

And finally, I have to say that the amount of content you get in this game is staggering for how much the developers are asking for.
I almost feel like I cheated them out of money!
$15 is nothing.
Most games of this kind are around the same price and only 5-8 hours at most.

I am 32 Hours in at the moment, and I still have around 10-15 more hours to go for completion.
And that is only a single playthrough.
There is also a brand new game mode upon finishing the game which is basically a Perma-Death run.

And there are a ton of awesome achievments that will surely take some time to get, as well as push you to your limit.

---
All in all, this is the best Metroidvania game in all of existence in my opinion.
It's also one of the best action games in all of existence in my opinion.

Everything about Hollow Knight is just pure perfection.
And now is the perfect time to jump in.

There has been multiple updates that added in plenty of new boss fights, charms, and sidequest.
And the game recieved a update that significantly enhanced game performance.
So even my little baby laptop can run this game with zero slowdown no problem.

This is a must for action game fans and for Metroidvania fans.
It's 100% worth your time.

This game is also out for the Nintendo Switch and is also coming to the PS4/Xbox One this coming up spring.
Physical Editions included.

First and foremost, this is all based on how [b]I[/b] felt while playing the game, and probably won't (and I hope it doesn't) reflect other people's experience with the game.
I went into the game completely blind, with my only reference being that a friend said "it's really worth it" when I asked him about it, since it was on sale and he had mentioned the game before. 

[h1] What I liked about the game[/h1]
The game is possibly the best looking 2D platformer I have ever played. Just saying "the art is beautiful" doesn't do justice to how good it is. The lightning is absolutely gorgeous and the atmosphere is really embracing, each area feels unique, and [b]everything[/b] is amazingly well detailed, from the backgrounds to the small details on the ground, from the enemy animations to the cutscenes, I never felt like it was cheapen out at any moment. It is truly example of top notch craftsmanship and scenario building. 
And it also runs pretty well (I'm using Manjaro Linux with 8GB DDR3 RAM, a 2GB AMD video card and a 3.4Ghz i7 processor), never having any stutters, slow-downs, or any kind of problem with performance. 
The way it present it's mechanics is also a textbook example of how it should be done, presenting them on a safe environment where you can get familiar with it, then introducing increasingly harder obstacles.

[h2]What I did not like about the game[/h2]
Most of the charms felt useless to me, or at least not worth using most of the time. I probably used the same charm combo throughout most of the game, only changing the compass for something more useful during boss battles.

Something that angered me a lot was taking damage while wallsliding [b]from spikes there were not even on the wall I was sliding[/b]. By that I mean sliding on a wall that has spikes on its top or bottom extending to the sides. That happened mostly on Greenpass.

The shade mechanic is completely nullified by simply quitting to the main menu and loading a save file again when you're at 1 mask left. You'll return to the same spot that you'd return if you died, but without the hassle of having 1/3 of the soul gauge missing and not having any money until you defeat your shade. At this point, why not just add a shortcut to return to the latest bench you visited?

Speaking of soul, spells felt very ineffective to me, as in [i]why waste a potential heal using this spell if I can use this charm to heal myself [b]and[/b] spread this damaging cloud for a few seconds and still kill this enemy with the same amount of nail hits?[/i]. I tried using that charm that reduces the cost of using spells, but didn't really find it that worth using two notches instead of the one for the damaging cloud one.

The position of the Stag Stations were also something that bothered me while playing the game, as in they're just [i]inconvenient[/i], not in a sense of getting to them, but their position in the map, mostly at an edge of an area. This dissonates quite a bit if you take in consideration the world the game is set, as in [i]how can they build those tremendously huge structures underground but can't properly place a Stag Station in a better area of interest?[/i]

While I didn't feel lost in the game, at times I couldn't tell if an area was essential or optional for the completion of the game. Sometimes I'd waste a lot of time on one of those parkour sections just to get a Hallownest Seal or some useless charm for doing it.

Something that made me frustrated (although this is mostly my fault, as i'ts pretty much standard platformer mechanic) was getting damaged by simply touching bosses' legs when I just want to get to the other side of the screen, without them even attacking, or having some sort of weapon/spike I'd run into if I walked in their direction.

And the final boss was a bit of a disappointment, as in it was a lot easier than other bosses in the game.
[spoiler]C'mon, how the hell does the boss called [b]The Hollow Knight[/b] in the game Hollow Knight can be so easy to defeat?[/spoiler]

[h3]Wraping it up[/h3]
Hollow Knight is a masterfully crafted game and deserves all the praise it gets (judging by the Extremely Positive reviews) and I hope the best for Team Cherry and everyone involved, but unfortunately this game didn't go well with me. I might come back later and try it again, and if I do I'll change my review accordingly.

Post Scriptum/Afterword:

This game is cheap, and I don't mean in production. You will die in the most frustrating manner, again and again, and this is by design. The creators must have felt abused by the super difficult NES games of the past, and decided to make this sadistic game.

I wanted to wait until I had progressed enough before reviewing this to make sure I had at least gotten quite a few upgrades and whatnot just to make sure it wasn't a learning curve problem. 

Lets start with the map. It's almost useless. You can't zoom in or out (unless there's some charm or upgrade, which even if there is that is ridiculous that I haven't found it 30 hours in), and the character and marker icons are so big that when you get close to something they just overlap and it's often difficult to figure out which way to go to get to them so you just have to find your way by trial and error. There are also places you MUST pass through completely without a map, multiple times. There are two sections I still don't have a map for and it's impossible to obtain the map until you beat bosses or acquire other items. You also can't see where you are on the map unless you use the compass charm, which takes up a charm slot. I can't think of another game off the top of my head that does this. The standard in nearly every game is when you open the map you can see where your character is on it. I could maybe understand making it an early upgrade but making it something that takes up a valuable slot is just absurd.

Enemies. There is a thing called a crystal hunter you encounter early on that is just ridiculously OP. It takes 5 hits to kill this thing. There's no telegraphed indication of when they're about to fire. The projectile turns into a crystal puff ball wherever it lands and that also hurts you if you touch it. They are found in an area with narrow platforms and when you hit them or they hit you in both cases your character moves and you will fall and take more damage. Deflecting the projectile back at it doesn't damage it. Depending on the angle of attack, the projectile will pass right through your nail swing attack. This is just a normal enemy in an area you must pass through, not guarding anything special. Gigantic PITA that takes you right out of the game because you're going to die 20 times trying to get past them. This isn't a 'difficult' enemy, this is a rage inducing overpowered enemy that should not exist. I love difficult games. I hate cheap enemies that are designed to frustrate you and kill you over and over. It's not about skill, it's about luck. This is the type of design I would expect in an arcade cabinet game designed to get all your quarters.

Other enemies include ones that pop up from underneath you with zero warning. This is just horrific design. It's an unpredictable thing that takes life away. Cheap cheap cheap.

Bosses. You frequently walk into boss areas without any indication and then you are stuck and must fight them. No way to back out. It would be fine if boss areas were clearly indicated and then you couldn't back out, or if they are not clearly indicated then you COULD back out, but having them not clearly indicated and being stuck in the battle really, really sucks. And to top it off, when you die you have to go all the way back to the last bench, AND you have to find your little shade guy (your soul or whatever that floats in the area you died). And guess what? Yep, half the time you have to go so far into the boss room to get your shade that you're now trapped in the boss fight again. AND you have to hit the shade 2 or 3 times to reabsorb him, and he tries to attack you. If you die before you absorb him, you lose all your geo (currency). Cheap. Absolutely Cheap. There was even a time my shade guy was stuck in a wall or something. I could see him on the map (kinda, remember big icons), and went all around and he never came to me. Bad programming. Another thing with bosses is they usually have a primary and secondary attack, and a background attack like where something rains down on you. This makes it almost impossible to replenish your soul, as the animation takes a few seconds. Even with the charm that speeds it up, more than half the time you go to recharge soul you end up taking a hit negating the entire process.

Your spell is mapped to back button (B). You'll frequently hit this and waste soul when all you're trying to do is back out of a conversation. Why couldn't they map dream nail to B and spell to Y? It just does not make any sense. 

Not once did I encounter any of these things in Metroid Dread. That's how you balance difficulty and fairness. That's how you design a game where you die multiple times in hard spots but learn more of the patterns and get better each time, and don't just depend on luck to get you through.

I think the rabid fans of this game give these things a pass because of the cutesy design. And honestly it's not a terrible game, the controls are tight, the overall look and feel of the game is pretty good, but there's enough drawbacks I just don't think it's worth $15. I'm going to try and beat the game but I know I have a lot left to discover and I've read there are even more frustrating enemies so I may get to a point where I just stop. Hopefully they improve and balance the gameplay better for the sequel.
