This would be a good game if it didn't try so hard to imitate the piece of raw sewage that is Dark Souls. The checkpoint starvation and loss of geo upon death are designed to make the game as frustrating as possible.

I cannot recommend Hollow Knight enough. It is hands down my favorite of the metroidvania genre and joins my list of all time favorite games period. Its also become one of the few games I can tolerate for long enough to acheivement hunt. Team Cherry has created a remarkable game, released multiple free DLCs for it, and yet the price is still only $15, which is a great deal.

First off, the beautifully hand drawn graphics and fitting atmosphere is impressive by itself. It has such a rich environment with many memorable and impressive areas that each have their own distinct themes. The story is immersive and fullfilling, walking you through the games history without being pushy with cutscenes or dialogue. The combination of open exploration, story, and side quests give a large sense of options while still giving you the feeling of progression throughout the whole game, and I found it very hard to stop playing once I picked it up. Even on replays, I'm still enjoying the story, exploration, and ambiance, which very few games actually do sucessfully. 

As far as gameplay goes, the movement and combat are very satisfying, and the charm system allows you to explore many combat options without commiting to any specific build. After gaining a new ability, it wasn't even remotely a chore to revisit an area, as some other games in the genre make it. The boss fights are also a marvel, and a few have become some of my favorite bosses in any game.  They are greatly themed, skillful, and with the exception of 1 or 2, very fun to complete.

There are maybe a few hiccups in some areas, like some annoying RNG in a few boss fights, or some tedious traveling to retry bosses, but these issues are extremely minor in comparrison to the vast benefits this game has. Multiple fast travel systems and practicing bosses almost fix these issues anyway.

This game is so good that there are petitions from customers asking the developers to increase the price because they felt the game was too good to be worth 15 dollars. If that doesn't tell you how great this game is, I don't know what will.

All in all, you should definitely try this game out, it is well worth the price, and... uhhhh... in conclusion... gud game.

When I first found out about Hollow Knight, it quickly found its way onto my list of games to play. Metroidvania? Good difficulty? Comparisons to the Soulsborne games? My kind of territory. However, any enjoyment I could have gained from this game quickly fell when I realized more and more that it has a gorgeous exterior, and an incredibly ugly interior.


So, to start out, let's first see what the game does well.

Hollow Knight looks and sounds excellent. The art and the world makes the player want to explore and find out more about the world, which works excellently for a Metroidvania. Each zone is memorable and new locations push the player to become engaged in the fascinating world. The audio in Hollow Knight is also incredible, and I'm not just talking about the music. From the swoosh of the sword, to the various cries of bugs throughout the game, every sound fits perfectly.

The soundtrack is also very fitting for every area. It even changes dynamically based on how the zone would change. You can hear a mellowed-down version of the melody playing indoors, and then when you step outside, the music will open up, as if the whole world is opening up to you. Truly incredible. The audio as a whole is, by far, the best part of Hollow Knight.



However, music and visual appearances can go for any form of entertainment, and Hollow Knight is a game, after all. So, let's look at the gameplay.

Hollow Knight fits under the categories of 2D Action Platformer, and Metroidvania.

Let's start with the Metroidvania:

Map design is what makes or breaks a Metroidvania game. You explore the world, you find abilities, to backtrack, you enter new areas with the abilities you find. You can also find various secrets throughout the world as you acquire new abilities. Sounds simple enough, right? Not for Hollow Knight.

You see, the game takes a "show, don't tell" approach instead of having an actual story, so that the story itself comes from the vast world-building. However, this doesn't end up working, because the game seldom points you to the direction you actually need to go. And no, I'm not saying that the game needs to hold your hand the entire way, telling every location you need to go to. It just needs small landmarks, little nudges, the tiniest of pushes just to keep you going in the right direction. Hollow Knight has none of this. Thus, It's really easy to get lost and not have any clue as to where you are going, or what you are supposed to do. There was one point in the game I had an item that was supposedly a key(it doesn't actually say it is a key), which opened up the next zone I needed to go to to progress in the game. However, I didn't even know that the specific location where the "gate" was, was where I actually needed to go to. Go figure.

This is the key point of what makes up the biggest detriment of this game.

THIS GAME LOVES TO WASTE YOUR TIME.

Yup, every chance it can get, it will gladly use to WASTE YOUR TIME. Running around aimlessly? Check. Getting a new ability but not opening up any new areas with the ability and instead a few smalls secrets instead? Check. Shortcuts with really inconvenient locations? Check. Shopkeepers located in far-off areas with no way to send them to the main hub, or shortcuts to easily access them? Check.

There's even a charm that you can equip that makes literally one shopkeeper in the game turn you away, but none of the others. Good consistency, right? Good thing the game doesn't tell you that it will do that, or insinuate in any way, shape or form, that it would happen. Oh, and you need to go to a bench (check point) to change charms which means, more TIME WASTING.

The map is terrible too. First, you need to actually buy a map for every single zone, while in the zone, from an NPC which you need to specifically find. Even then, you only get a rough map which isn't complete that you need to pay the game's currency for. The map doesn't even update unless you reach a bench (check point), making you wander aimlessly in a zone, never knowing where you are at unless you want to backtrack (WASTE TIME) to updated it. You also need a charm (equipment slot) to actually see where your character is on the map. How stupid is that. Furthermore, the map doesn't tell you what secrets you have already found in a zone, so if you want to 100% the game even WITH a guide/walkthrough, I wish you my condolences.

I mentioned the game's currency, so let's talk about that too. The currency, (geo) is not too easy to come by, is hard to farm, and literally everything needs geo. You want to open up a shortcut? Geo. Got through a long segment and you see a bench (checkpoint)? Oh, you need geo to actually make it work. Charms, equipment, and even a major key item requires large amounts of geo, and the game never really let's you decide what is and isn't worth it to spend geo on. This is because you are too busy using it to open up shortcuts, buy maps, and other nonsensical stuff that should be a given to begin with. Oh, and if you want to ever upgrade your main melee weapon, bring lots of geo along, because the NPC won't actually tell you how much you need until you get there with the key item needed as well. Sounds like TIME WASTING.

And when you think it can't get any worse, if you die in the game, you lose all your geo on hand, but it creates a shade at the location you died which you can collect your lost geo from. But, if you die before getting your geo back, it is lost forever-a needlessly punishing mechanic that serves no purpose in this game except to WASTE TIME. This mechanic works in other titles if the currency is easily acquirable and isn't needed in large amounts for everything. This is not the case for Hollow Knight.


So, through various horrible game and map design decisions, we know that the game LOVES to WASTE TIME. However, this game is a 2D Action Platformer, so let's look at the platforming and the combat.

For platforming, none of the segments in the game are particularly hard. You get various ability which enhance mobility and are smooth to use. I wouldn't say the platforming in this game is either good or bad. It works, and that's just that.

Combat, however... You see, the main weapon you have is a generic melee slash. This is your bread and butter. You also get spells and abilities that you can find by exploring, but these all serve as pointless WASTES OF TIME because they serve no practicality. The abilities have a wind-up charge and just about as short of a range as your normal swing. With how fast-paced the game is, you will never reasonably have a situation to use them.

For spells, you can cast them by using soul energy which you acquire by using your melee attack on enemies. Spells for the most point are pointless, because the only way to heal besides the game's benches (check points) is by channeling some of your soul to heal a hitpoint. This creates a situation where you soul energy is 9 times out of 10 going to be reserved for healing purposes instead of combat. The game mechanics alone invalidate any reason to use spells, which are also the only ranged attacks you get in the game. This means that the main way of fighting anything is through a basic melee swing. Yipee.

Furthermore, most enemies will react to damage by attacking in your direction with a melee attack of their own. This includes bosses. That means, the combat in the game revolves around a hit-and-run playstyle for just about nearly every encounter in the game. Truly engaging. Bosses were also a pushover. Most fights were simple by genre standards, both in design and difficulty. Few fights were even memorable at all.


At the end of the day, Hollow Knight is a mediocre title. It WASTES so much of your TIME, that any engagement of the world or the music crumbles from the sheer frustrations of the questionable game design and I cannot, in good conscience, recommend Hollow Knight.

Hollow Knight is one of the best games that I've ever played, owing to a well-rounded presentation which satisfies many desires well. The main draw is in the gameplay, following the conventions of video games like Super Metroid and Castlevania: Symphony of the Night, you're given a large map to explore where you can essentially "go anywhere," though some sections will require specific items, spells, or movement abilities (such as a mothwing cloak which provides you with a dash ability). Every area has a unique design with original enemies and challenges; putting the player up against extensive platforming and fair, skill-based action in a side-scrolling environment. The world itself is a gigantic underground maze swarming with various insects, with some areas focusing on fungus and spores, and others around spiders and worms. The end goal is essentially to find new paths, conquer bosses, and pick up new items and techniques. It's simple, but on that front we can say it's simply elegant. I felt that there was a suitable level of difficulty, a strong sense of achievement, and the game was well-balanced. 

The other outstanding front in Hollow Knight is the visual design. Characters have a quaint and adorable hand-drawn motif, which limits them to straightforward looks that compliment their particular species well. The environment is extremely detailed by comparison, providing a nice contrast which makes keeping your eyes on enemies easy while also placing you in an enjoyable locale which will look unique compared to all the others you have visited. To top it all off, the game has an excellent soundtrack which becomes more intense and interesting when you're in a difficult battle or a hard platforming challenge, and provides great ambience when you're left to explore on your own devices. Little by little, the atmospheric sound design will be making the setting and moving you into the adventure. 

As for length, the game could take you anywhere from ten to forty hours, depending on how much effort you spend exploring and what things you decide to do; and thankfully, it has a good level of replayability with added challenges such as "permanent death." The only aspect I have ambiguous feelings about is the story, as I love the setting and the focus on insects and I think the plot is handled appropriately with well-crafted, somewhat mysterious lore; but I couldn't help but notice the entire game goes beyond a "homage" to Dark Souls to being so much of a rip-off From Software could claim copyright infringement. And it's not about the so-easily thrown around buzzword of "Dark Souls hard," but it essentially borrows way too much from Dark Souls' world design, and there can be no denial that the creators were aware of the series. Without spoiling too much, any player should be able to make at least thirty to fifty different comparisons off the top of their head from enemy design, concepts, locations, sound, story, characters, etc., etc. I feel that I may like Hollow Knight more simply because I almost have to regard it as a mainstay in the Dark Souls series--as if there was a miniature world of insects with their own struggles in the same universe as Boletaria, Lordran, or Lothric. 

Altogether, it's definitely worth its price and your attention. I think Hollow Knight is  one of the best games of the last ten years, and I'm looking forward to what Team Cherry has planned next.

Okay I'm going to start this out by saying that Hollow Knight is an AMAZING GAME! Seriously. There is so much to love about this game. From the art style, to the game play, to the lore, to the music, and many many more things I'd love to gush over.

HOWEVER! I'm putting my review as a "not recommended" review because I'm leaving this review for a very specific crowd of people and I want this to be more easily seen by those people. This is NOT marked not recommend because Hollow Knight is bad and I want to make sure that is clear. I'm NOT saying it's a bad game.

Let's continue.

I have a few reviews like this. Reviews centered around my disability. I have sensory processing disorder due to my being Autistic. Sensory Processing Disorder can effect a person in a variety of ways. One of those ways... is reaction times. My ability to react to something is hindered by 1 to a few seconds.

(Note: If you are interested, long explaination short, the time it takes is a bit longer for what I see to get translated by my brain and that signal to make it to my hands than the average person. I jokingly say that my brain lags. I see what I need to do but the signal my eyes are sending to my hands to respond to what I'm saying just takes a bit longer. This leads to, for example, when I try to jump OVER an enemy I jump INTO them instead because by the time my hands get the message to make me jump the enemy is already over my head.)

In a lot of games this does not matter and it does not change my ability to play and enjoy a game. In others those few seconds mean life or death in the game and can be the highest bar to entry that the bar could possibly be. In other games it very much does effect your ability to play the game and sadly Hollow Knight is one of those games.

I loved what time I did get with Hollow Knight. I absolutely adore it. The art style by it's self was what got me to give it a shot even though I generally LOATHE metroidvania games. Mostly because I can't stand the constant backtracking you have to do as you get new abilities among plenty of other reasons.

Despite that though I had a feeling I'd enjoy the world of this game enough to trudge my grumbling ass through it. I wasn't wrong.

At a certain point though I had to put the game down with the knowledge that this is a game I will never be able to physically finish and it pains me because I love the game. Placing it on the metaphorical shelf in my library with other games I can't finish due to my SPD like Fez and Senua's sacrifice (though those are due to auditory problems caused by my SPD).

At first my disability made bosses a bit extra annoying, but it was manageable. I was still able to play. The first time I fought Hornet though I could tell I wouldn't make it far. The fight against her was stupidly intense for me. The fight... really isn't that hard. I could look at the fight and know exactly what I was meant to do and exactly how to execute it. It was just so damn hard though because I'd react... just those few seconds too slowly... and take damage I shouldn't have taken.

I beat her though and it was exhilerating. I really thought that was the moment I would have to stop playing. So I was so surprised and happy when I beat her. Maybe I could do this after all?

I don't remember the name of the boss that I fought that stopped my playthrough though. He was kinda tucked away in an area I could only get to after unlocking the ability to get passed spiky walls. I think it was off to the left of the original area. I fought him several times and each time I lost. Unlike Hornet and the other bosses I had found increasingly difficult though... I could tell I couldn't beat him. I'm pretty sure he was optional. Optional or not though he represented the fact that my disability would get in the way of me ever being able to finish the game. I made the decision after my last fight with him to put the game down because I knew the moment was getting far to close where I'd hit a non-optional boss that I just physically couldn't get passed.

The combat in this game is very simple. It's not complex. You can see exactly what you are meant to do. The complexity comes with the bosses. This game is difficult for your average person, but rewarding. For someone like me though it, pun intended, left me feeling a bit hollow. Because I wanted to play it. I wanted to play it so damn bad. Maybe one day I'll give it a try again, but it's been almost a year since I opened the game.

It's always intensely upsetting for me when I desperately want to play a game and realize that I just can't do it.

So this review... is not a reflection of the game. The game is amazing. It's beautiful. Combat is fluid and fun and rewarding. The music is fantastic.

This review is a warning for people like me with reaction time issues and SPD. This game is likely going to be VERY difficult for you to play. If you are like me and hate to pick up games only to find out you are unable to get past a certain point... I sadly recommend against getting this game. If you don't mind not being able to finish a game and don't mind playing a fight a million times to get that bit of muscle memory going to get you past it, I'd recommend getting it knowing it will likely be incredibly hard for you.

If you DON'T have SPD though and like metroidvania's than get the game. Hands down. Get it. 10/10 worth the money. Worth the time. Worth the rage.

I cannot with clear conscience recommend Hollow Knight, it may be a good game under certain circumstances, but I disagree with the direction that was taken here. The game was marketed to me as a metroidvania with soulslike mechanics and while it may be a very good metroidvania and even that could be debatable, for example I had much more fun playing Unepic than this game, from soulslike games it took all the wrong lessons, which I will go into later into review. Compared to let us say Salt and Sanctuary, which should be the same genre, there is no really no comparision. Salt and Sanctuary copied everything about Dark Souls in a way kind of like a Japan with America after WWII with a mentality of I have no idea why this works so great, so let us copy everything. Hollow Knight took a look at Dark Souls and copied just a few surface aspects for no reason at all.

First of all, the story. Yes, Dark souls can get very mystical. Yes, Dark Souls gives you only the very basic information. Yes, Dark Souls story is very basic. All of these are true, but you get none of it in Hollow Knight. I have no idea why am I the MC where I am, why do I fight with all these monsters and even what is my end goal. You can say Dark Souls is the same, you can go whereever and do whatever which is true in a way, but the first NPC you meet tells you to ring two bells of awakening and afterwards you get another overarching objective of collecting Lord Souls and if you stray and explore you can get some cool loot or epic locations. In Hollow Knight you meet mysterious NPC at the start, who tells you nothing relevant and I am rewarded with mysterious hints when exploring, so there is really no reason to explore, since you are even getting punished for exploration by enemies instantly respawning when you leave and enter the same room again.

Next the currency. Similiar to Dark Souls you get a currency here for killing enemies. In Dark Souls however you may use the currency to upgrade your stats from the start, such a thing is not here in the Hollow Knight, only able to buy QoL upgrades like new pins on a map. What is then the point of losing this currency upon death in Hollow Knight you may ask? Well, there is no point really. If you die in Dark Souls it is a precious resource you can use to upgrade your power, so you do not want to lose that, in Hollow Knight you just shrug and move on if you lose it. Also even at the start since you can only buy QoL stuff, you will have nothing to spend it on, which can never happen in Dark Souls and if you lose it, you have only yourself to blame. Why then if you want to punish player for dying (which in itself is a punishment) are you not able to just load his last position at the bench with the currency he had back then, if you want to invalidate his progress and teach him to save frequently like was the norm in the games in the past?

And now that I am on that topic. Save points/benches/bonfires whatever you want to call that. Why do you have boss runs in Hollow Knight? In Dark Souls it works because you have limited amount of healing, which means anytime you get to the boss area with more healing items than the last time, you have an advantage that you gained by playing better, it is designed as a challenge every time. You gain nothing with boss runs in Hollow Knight, they are just pointlessly tedious.

I could probably even go on with problem solving that the game offers you with different weapons, blocks, parries, dodges etc. but I have not managed to get far enough to really comment on that, but let us just say that I do not have very good opinion of the combat and I think my point with the Dark Souls comparisions was made. There is nothing soulslike about this game.

I love how good this game look and it's art, the game itself is very immersive, I found myself playing it for hours on end. The atmosphere is so dark, yet so comfy. It's been a long, long time since I've 100% completed a game. 42 hours and 15 minutes it took me, and I've got 62 hours total at the time of writing this. Every minute was worth it. I hope you enjoy the story as much as I did.

The writing is very Dark Souls-esque in the idea that you don't ever get a direct telling of the story, you get bits and pieces everywhere from everything, and must put it together on your own. Storytelling aside, the game is not as linear as other Metroidvania style games, and definitely less forgiving. There are some areas and encounters that provide great story segments and achievements as well as some charms and item parts, and they are very hard the first time you attempt them. You will die, a lot. There's no way around them, either. You can just miss out, or learn to complete the challenges. 

The artwork and soundtrack are beautiful, and despite the opening to the game making you feel lik the color palette is weird, you end up in some very colorful areas, and the colors are done on purpose as well, to reflect the story of the game, as you'll come to find. 

For $15, this can't be beat. 60+ hours out of a $15 product is more than some people get out of a $60 product. As the old movie analogy goes, if I go to the movies right now and drop $15, I get 2 hours of entertainment. The value is here, easy.

Of all the 'Metroidvanias' out there, this is certainly my favorite. It leans more on the Metroid side. 

Great map design. You're always backtracking, and the game always makes the most use out of each area as it can with your new powerups, whether it's simply easier traversal, or access to new areas, or secret rooms. Upgrades and pickups don't come as often in this game compared to many others, which in some ways can feel disappointing. I have to say it makes those you find and when you find them feel very valuable, and really adds a cool feeling of accomplishment.

At the beginning, I remember missing my arm cannon (as I do in every Metroidvania not starring Samus). This game relies heavily on close quarters combat. It is difficult and unforgiving. You will fail often, even on simple enemies if you're careless or overconfident. However, it is very satisfying to finally get the hang of fighting different enemies. I do appreciate the style of combat now, and really enjoy it, and it's nice to be different than a Metroid game when you're not one, of course!

The art style is cool, without taking itself too seriously. Voices of characters can be kind of silly. It's all very fitting though. The story is spread very thin, and many details are to be observed by the player. Music is also very ambient. It's by no means bad, but for me personally it's not exactly memorable. It does work well, but I would have preferred some catchy tunes.

All in all, this is an instant classic. If you enjoy challenge, exploration, secrets, action, a healthy price-to-playthrough length-ratio (I spent 30 hours on my first run, collecting everything and never got bored), replayability, and quality, I can recommend this game to you. Metroid fans: absolutely play this game.

This game... oh god this game is really damn good. Everything from the art style, game design, soundtrack, etc is pretty goddamn good. but there are some minor problems it has from what I've seen.

1. Since the game is a Metroidvania, the map is really huge, ergo you'll occasionally have the "Where the fuck do I go" syndrome after the 3rd area or so. its not much of a problem if you just quickly pull up google and see what areas you need to go to in order to get shit to help you reach areas you couldn't reach before. Its a problem, thats for sure, but its a metroidvania, and these types of games are made for exploring. So I'll give it somewhat of a pass here. Plus you can now add pins to the map, so if  you find an area you can't reach yet, you can just put a pin on it.

2. Difficulty can, in fact, randomly spike for no reason a lot of the time. usually the areas that I've seen have this are ones that are guarding a piece of a heart, piece of a soul, or its just an area that you're meant to be in later in the game. Hell, some bosses can get pretty difficult too, since healing takes about 5 seconds in order to regain one heart, but the bosses do get into a dazed animation after a while of pounding on them, so it kinda helps to just stop and heal a bit during it, so it kinda balances itself out. also you don't have to kill all enemies on screen in order to get past them. just go around some of them. 

Everything else in this game is fucking fantastic though. If you like metroidvanias, then this is the game for you. If you like games that you have to pour a lot of hours into, this is for you too. 

TL;DR Amazing game with some minor problems of getting lost and difficulty spikes.

Such a fucking disappointment. The game absolutely doesn't deserve it's 97% rating, but hey, who am I to argue with such "reviews" as "best game ever", "Incredible." or "game good play game".

To be perfectly clear, the game is not that bad. It's that when I start playing something with overwhelmingly positive reviews, I raise my expectations bar for the game quite high, because I know shitton of games which, by my own opinion deserve to have this rating on Steam, yet, somehow, they do not have it. Yet this game has it.

The visuals is basically the only thing I can put into "The Good" category. Game looks very good from the start and all the way to the end. Environment, enemies, bosses, little details, everything is nice to look at. Different areas all have they own unique atmosphere and that is very good. Unfortunately that is the only positive thing I can say about the game. Everything else varies from mediocre to atrocious.

Now to the problems:
-There is no story. At least in the traditional sense. I've just finished the game (took 12 hours) and I have no idea what was happening during that time. Who are you playing as? What is your goal? Why are you doing what you're doing? Nothing. Yes, there are NPC's, dialogues and cutscenes, but, unfortunately, developers decided to take a "Dark Souls" way of narration. You have no idea what is this world and how it works and all NPC's just say random weird stuff. You notice an NPC, talk with them, get 2-3 sentences of text that give absolutely 0 valuable information and then you continue on.
-Soundtrack is forgetable. Which doesn't stop them from selling two of them, lol. For me the best way to understand if soundrack is good is ask myself "Do I want to listen to this music after playing the game?". No, I don't. Every composition I've heard in this game was immediately forgotten after I stoped hearing it. And even in game these scores just don't work. I don't feel anything listening to them while playing they do not enhance the experience, at all. An example of a good soundtrack for me is, for example, Blasphemous. I still listen to it's soundtrack after months since I finished the game.
-Exploration is horrible. The main problem with it is the map: it's ridiculously inconvenient. Why the hell can't I draw the map BY MYSELF?! Why am I supposed to buy a map for every new location and only after it I can finally start drawing chambers every time I sit on bench? Every time I get to new location I have to blindly wander around hoping that I stumble upon this humming fat fuck and finally unlock map drawing for this location. This is ridiculous. Moving on. There are no map notes in game. You cannot put a note on map saying "Dead end, don't go here." or "Need double jump.", instead you get some shitty colored stones you can put on your map and that's it. Good luck trying to remember what did you mean by this blue stone when you put it on map several days ago. This seriously impairs the desire to explore.
-Fast travel is awful. The stations are very scarce and located very inconveniently. Why not just give the player the option to teleport between benches?
-Ridiculous amounts of back tracking. Yes, it's metroidvania, and you're supposed to go back to old locations, but combined with previous two points it just feels so fucking slow and painful to go through.
-Health system is retarded. Instead of a bar of health you get several Hit Points (4 or 5 at the start of the game) and most of hits take away strictly 1 point of HP. What is the problem? The game doesn't give a fuck if you accidentally touched a small bug or got impaled by a spear - you will loose 1 HP and that is dumb.
-There is no upgrade system for your character. Levels? XP? Skills? Nothing. The only two things you have are trinkets and passive upgrades that you aquire during the game. Passive upgrades are mostly for exploration, except smith who improves your "sword" improving it's damage. Trinkets are horribly designed because they have a cost and if you want to equip one you will have to remove some other. That means that every time I get new trinket I do not become stronger or better, I just unlock new way to slightly diversify my gameplay. I've finished whole game with first 3 trinkets the game gives you and didn't felt any need to change them. Also you have ridiculously low amount of notches (points you need to equip trinkets). I finished the game with 4 fucking notches. FOUR! I have no idea how to unlock more and the fact that game wants me to google it is disgusting.
-No in-game journal. That's straight up could've resolved several problems, but no - either keep everything in your head or google it.
-1 goddamn weapon for whole game. Got tired of your sword? Well, fuck you. If you want to get 100% achievements, you'll have to play for dosens, hundreds possibly, of hours and all this time  you will be using one boring sword with tiny amount of different moves.
-Touching enemies damages you. The most retarded and lazy way of making the game more difficult. You have a short sword as a weapon, you have to get VERY close to your enemy to hit them and if you get 1mm closer, you get hit. Fuck this system.
 
That's mostly how I feel about the game. I could've easily written even more problems, but I already spend too much time for a review noone will read.

After spending 60ish hours on it, Hollow Knight has become one of my favorite games. As someone who never played many Metroid-vania style 2D platformer games, I found myself enjoying this thoroughly. The game made me want to go and explore, find out about the surprisingly deep lore through NPCs and item descriptions and get better at the combat. I really felt like I was part of a grand adventure. The game even has its own version of Pokédex called the Hunter's Journal, indexing all the various enemies you face, not all of which you can put away with a couple of slashes of your weapon.

The art in the game is truly something to admire. The combination of the rich color palette, mesmerizing soundtrack and neat level design across the diverse locations did an amazing job of putting me in a certain state of mind while tackling a platforming section or while battling a boss, or just sitting on a bench (which acts as the game's save spot) and soaking in the atmosphere. Be it a sense of rising tension, melancholia or an epic wave of heroism, I felt it all. The platforming, for the most part felt like a natural extension of the level itself, except for a few sections which were made solely as a platforming challenge, which did get a little tedious, and weren't always worth the reward on the other side.

The NPCs, thanks to their strikingly distinctive designs, dialogue that is intrinsic to their nature, and voices that are what can be described as vaguely weird syllabic gibberish, had me interested in them enough that I couldn't help but go check on them from time to time (that didn't end well for all of them, sadly). The combat mechanics are simple, but do require you to get better and adapt as you face different enemies and bosses. Using the melee weapon, in conjecture with intuitive movement (this is a platformer after all) and the magical spells creates for many satisfying (and equally frustrating) moments of combat.

The lore is sprinkled in trinkets across the breadth of the map and the length of the story. Taken in its raw and often ambiguous form, it is open ended to just the right degree, that I found myself playing sleuth and theorizing what must've happened. The scope of the lore (when fully comprehended, for which I watched multiple YT videos) easily rivals that of a blockbuster movie franchise or a bestselling fantasy book. All the NPCs have had some or the other role to play in the history of the game world. All the apparent character arcs seems to intersect one another at some point, before branching out into the bigger picture at the center of the story. There are no useless dangling bits or wasted subplots. The fact that there isn't much separating what would be "side quests/challenges" and paths that actually progress the story, I found myself ready to be tangled in whatever mess that came next, always being pleasantly (okay, not always) surprised.

I know the comparison with Dark Souls, seems extremely clichéd at this point, but in Hollow Knight's case, it actually makes sense. The game not only borrows the way in which the mysterious lore is unfolded to the player, but also some of the thematic aspects as well, in the form of a "once great kingdom, now in ruin".

The one gripe I have with the game stems from one of its obvious strengths. Every player is bound to experience the game differently, because after a certain point, you are free to choose which path to explore. In doing so, the game has to keep up with the rate at which the player gets better and acquires different abilities, by increasing the difficulty of the enemies in the order they are faced. It does accomplish this with some enemies, even managing to have a very challenging final boss. However, a few bosses in the middle, that were building up to be great battles, ended much quicker than I anticipated only because I had that one extra ability that gave me the edge or because my weapon was upgraded that one step further than it was supposed to be when I faced that particular boss.

So, for something that started off as a kickstarter project, Hollow Knight is a very well realized game. For me, it comes down to the way it balances its elements so well: it is quirky yet serious, dark yet colorful, challenging yet relaxing, the story for all the tragedy in it does have a hopeful side, there's moments of utter dankness and there's moments of pure heroism. Everything in it, is memorable. I do recommend this wondrous game at full price. When there are petitions from fans, asking the devs to charge more for the game as they felt like they paid very less compared to what they got, you'd think it's worth checking out what the fuss is about. Trust me, it is.

Oh, and yeah, free DLC.

So, you only get the full range of movement (dashing and wall jumping) after several hours of gameplay. Both of these actions demand you have the godly reflexes of someone who is raised on 20 years of Metroidvania. If you are absolutely new to this genre, you will probably be in the situation I am in.

I have reached the portion of the game shortly after you actually get your hands on the wall jump ability. It is here that I am stuck in this endless loop of dying, getting sent back to the bench, climbing up through all the enemies I don't want to encounter, killing my shade and getting my money back, only to die again shortly after and having to make this climb again.

At this point I realize my lack of instilled Gamer Reflexes makes this game pretty much impossible to experience in the fullest and every time I have vented about how I've been doing the same damn thing in the game for HOURS I've only been told to git gud.

This puts me in a bind where I like how this game looks and feels enough to WANT to continue, but also just want to refund it because it's just crippling me physically. Seriously, I've become exhausted trying to keep up only to be given advice like "well why don't you mosey on over to the charm shop and get such-and-such" which would be fine advice if I could get out of the loop because that would mean I could actually LEAVE the area at all.

Ultimately I'm deciding against better judgement and am keeping the game but deleting the save file I'm stuck on. Maybe I'll ACTUALLY learn something if I get out of the loop and start fresh. Until then I can't recommend this to ur average normie gamer or whatever, yeah bite me. If you have the reflexes from your childhood where you played nothing but this kind of game, I obviously recommend it. You'll have fun. Everyone else? Careful.

This game is one of the best games I've ever played. The music is fantastic and fits perfectly with the different areas. Speaking of the areas, they are all beautifully designed and unique from one another. There will definitely be an area that you like more than the others (and quite possibly an area you like least (cough White Palace cough)). The artwork uses standard, hand drawn 2D animation, and I love it... even if all the characters look like bugs.
The game itself can be considered challenging, but fair. You may manage to defeat one or two early bosses on your first try, but I guarentee you'll have to fight most of them at least a few times. The bosses may be stronger than you, but when you know all their moves inside and out, you can evade and strike at the precise moments and bring down these behemoths in glorious combat.
One possible negative point is that this game isn't for kids. The animation and art style may make it seem playful enough, but are some... rather creepy themes that this game deals with. Discussing them here would be a bit of a spoiler. The game can also be rather frustrating at times (cough White Palace cough cough) and some of the bosses may require, um... substantial reflexes.
The base game costs $15, which I'd say is well worth it. One of the DLCs costs an additional $5, but you can get it bundled with the base game and the $10 soundtrack for a very reasonable $25. If it's on sale, you have no excuse, especially since the other 3 DLCs are completely free.
Fair warning though: if you have arachnaphobia, steer clear. If you can handle the frostbite spiders in Skyrim, you might be okay.

For how amazing it feels to run into your shade on your own for the first time ever, dying on your way back to it is three times more frustrating.

In the Fog Gardens, I died atop a very tiny platform and there was no way to land on it to fight my shade, not with the recoil. So I tried to vengeful spirit it, hit a jellyfish, and its explosive core hit ANOTHER jellyfish, and THAT ONE hit my shade and i got it back. It was a brilliant three seconds and I was frozen in fear throughout. Best moment in the game so far, but it also kinda made me want to stop playing for a week.

The phrase "git gud" is a cop-out that allows people to disregard the needs of others. Yes, the difficulty can be part of a game, and here, clearly, it is, but you can also give people access to your story who could NEVER pass that threshold. Maybe they're disabled or maybe they just don't have the time to pour into mastering mechanics. Who cares? Other games do offer accessibility modes while letting you know that if you can play the game as intended, then you should, in order to experience the original vision of the creators. 

And literally nothing is lost in doing this. Everyone who Could have had a satisfying experience with the original in the absence of an accessible mode WILL STILL DO THAT, and now people for whom your GLORIOUS content would have been locked away otherwise ALSO get to experience the stunning design and wonderful music and the Hunter and Cornifer and Greenpath, the most beautiful place in the world.

I love Hollow Knight, but it hates me, and so I cannot, in good conscience, recommend it, but if what I've written above doesn't seem like it would ruin your experience, then it is a fantastic use of 15-odd dollars.

Hollow Knight is an amazing game with many stunning environments, quality abilaties and challenging bosses.
The game is in no way perfect and I will point out any pros and cons I know of.

To start off with is how you start the game. You start in a funneled tutorial area with one or two sectets, where one is one of the main collectibles in the game. Once you finish the tutorial and jump into the game you cannot return to the area until you get the wall jump ability meaning players who discovered the secret from the beginning are rewarded. Your starting moveset is simple, four directional swings which knock you in the opposit direction you swang, a jump and a heal which uses up a resource which you gain from damaging enemies. This basic moveset encourages you to get up close so you can harvest soul and heal or use a spell (more on that later).
The first area of the game is an abandoned village which slowly comes alive as you talk to characters which are in the area below. This gives you a sense of progression as you liven up the village with shops and citizens, whilst it still remains with its sense of being alone.
The movement in the game is great, you have three main actions by the end of the game, a quick sword strike, a sharp jump which doesn't leave you in the ari for too long, and a dash which can be used to avoid attacks. You also gain three more main abililites, which are a super dash which can cover whole horizontal strips, soul (healing and 3 powerful spells which attack up down and to the side), and a dream nail which allows you to rematch certain bosses in a harder version, challenge more baisic new ghost bosses or just view thoughts. The feel is great with subtle details like destoyable backround objects, vegetation which rustles when you pass it and a small cloud of dust when you land. You gain three more upgrades to movement in the game, a double jump, a super meatboy style walljump and a shade dash which gives you momentary invunrability (cannot be spammed). This moveset leads to challenging boss encounters and a feeling of progression when you reach a previously unreachable leadge. the atmosphere is beutiful and diverse.

The cons of the game are the way the start is slow with just your jump, swings and healing with the almost tutorial like bosses which reside in the first area, and the way you just mindlessly travel from point to point if you've completed the game and want to access the DLC. The way most of the DLC is hidden behind breakable walls is cheap and genrally makes it annoying to scour each nook and cranny of evry previouse secret for a key item which allows for DLC access someplace else.

Not so much a review but a personal story of my time with Hollow Knight.
I must admit, when I first played Hollow Knight I was not impressed.  It had a unique hand drawn art style but was visually a bit drab for my liking. Not only that, it controlled poorly and felt sluggish (hadn’t turned off v-sync).  As I wandered about aimlessly, in its not so inviting world, I found myself dying repeatedly. Increasingly frustrated, I couldn’t figure out what all the hype was about.  I’d be sent to the beginning after dying, losing all progress and currency I had so painstakingly earned.  Needless to say, after about 2 hours, I decided the game probably wasn’t for me and decided to move on.  Several months went by and I considered myself done with Hollow Knight.  Then the Switch version released. It brought along another wave of hype and discussions about the game.  I read a few articles and tips for newbies and realized other people had had a rough time starting out as well, but eventually, the game opened up for them.  Unable to ignore the high praise from just about everyone, I strapped myself in for one last play session. I focused on winning one battle at a time and after dying 30 times on the first boss, I finally beat him.  Just as people had mentioned the game began to open up and I found myself completely hooked.  I never would have guessed that 40 hours later, I’d be watching the credits roll.  This game goes down as one of my favorites in over 30 years of gaming.  It’s literally THE modern day metroidvania.

[h1]Artstyle alone won't carry a game[/h1]

First of all, I have almost finished the Switch version, sitting at nearly 40 hours. And before anyone hits me with a "bUt It'S a DiFfErEnT pLaTfOrM" - it's exactly the same game, cry about it. I'm still not done with the game. I won't let it beat me, no, I'll beat it and never touch it again.

It's Nintendo-hard, no other way to put it. Its challenge comes from facing situations that you wouldn't know how they turn out until you die to them. It sucks - doubly so - because I got it after a friend suggested it, after finding out I like Blasphemous (and after forgetting I own it on PC, I bought it on the [i]Switch[/i] of all platforms). It just feels like a slap in the face because [i]this[/i] is nowhere near as good as Blasphemous. 

What pisses me off about this is that it locks basic mechanics behind in-game items.

Basic mechanics like - oh I don't know - a dash with i-frames, your position being shown on the map or even the area map itself. Let's cut to the chase. This isn't some old Metroidvania, which could excuse its gameplay shortcomings. It doesn't shake up the Metroidvania formula enough to justify said gameplay shortcomings, unless you consider "shaking up the formula" that every character is an insect - which, no, doesn't count. 

[b]Editing to add the following:[/b] 

No boss HP bar. Listen, I'm not even asking for the basic of every enemy having a sort of HP bar that every other game has done, but admittedly not that many 2D metroidvanias did (but that two better games such as Blasphemous and Dead Cells do). Not knowing what kind of damage you do to a boss is [i]basic[/i] but I guess it wouldn't be like playing an '80s platformer but in [current year] so of course the devs neglected that. 

Treating contact damage like a proper enemy hit [i]on enemies that don't at all indicate they should be dealing damage by touching you[/i]! Basically every game that doesn't want to add cheap difficulty foregoes treating touching an enemy as a proper enemy hit. Blasphemous has contact damage... but it doesn't count as a proper hit.

Yeah and here's the thing. I keep mentioning better 2D platformers like Blasphemous and Dead Cells because first of all they're better games, period and because I keep playing Hollow Knight and that's all I can think of: how there's so many other, better games me or anyone else could be playing. 

Thing is, I keep playing out of spite. Whichever wannabe coded this mess was really - and I mean [i]really[/i] - banking on the writers and artists to carry this whole, absolute and total mess of gameplay mechanics that for a very good reason ceased to be included into games some 30 years ago.

[b]Following is unedited text[/b]

Let's just start with the Mothwing Cloak. It doesn't have i-frames initially, although it gets that after a while. You can imagine this is extremely annoying to deal with until you get to the Abyss. This game is trying to be 2D Dark Souls and they're giving you an evasion maneuver without i-frames. Let that sink in for a whole moment.

On the 2D Dark Souls thing once more. Usually, in every game that tries to rip off the Dark Souls formula, the currency is automatically deposited in the player's inventory - well not here. I know in Salt and Sanctuary you had to physically pick up gold dropped by enemies, but [i]that[/i] 2D "Souls-like" has tons more flat surfaces and the gold dropped also [i]doesn't bounce![/i] In Hollow Knight, not only are there far more platforms above damaging spikes, but Geo also [i]bounces[/i], often directly into said damaging spikes. Of course, Gathering Swarm is conveniently available for purchase and takes up one charm slot for no reason at all!

Getting the map of an area is tied to finding a specific NPC who becomes available after defeating a previous area's boss. I suppose plotting a map as you go is too advanced and you should just look at the pretty visuals and forget about conventions that have been established after decades of 2D platformers of various flavours.

You don't automatically plot a map as you go. I guess the fact that every other Metroidvania does this automatically, was an indication to Team Cherry that they should add an item in an in-game shop that doesn't fulfill this function, but instead fills in the map when you rest on a bench. Same with markers. Yes, you have to buy an item that allows you to set custom markers on a map and then ANOTHER item that marks points of interest (NPC merchants, Stagways, Benches) on the map. Because we're in the '90s and game length HAS to be padded out - no, we're totally not talking about an already massive game that takes upwards of 40 hours to complete.

Your position on a map isn't shown unless you equip the Wayward Compass charm, which you have to purchase from an NPC. So basically another one of your charm notches is taken up for no good reason, since this charm is essential to exploring... which is an essential part of a Metroidvania game, which of course means that it'll require you to spend resources for absolutely no reason. Keep in mind that the markers aren't a charm. Nor is the Lumanfly Lantern. But the Wayward Compass [i]is[/i] a charm - I suppose if it wasn't the game would be slightly less Nintendo-hard.

You gather [strike]white juice[/strike] SOUL by hitting enemies, which can be used for healing. It works OK while fighting mobs but is useless against bosses since they attack rather relentlessly and you're often forced in tight arenas. This is what you get when you mix Metroidvania gameplay with Dark Souls atmospherics (and a heaping spoonful of git gud).

Getting the comparisons to the latter series out of the way first:
- You need to reclaim your currency (and some max health) from where you died.
- You save at benches (instead of bonfires).
- The world is in a very dark, decayed state.
- The worldbuilding is primarily done through bits of lore and commentary from NPCs.
- The map shortcuts that unlock throughout the game remind me a lot of the interconnectivity of Dark Souls I's map.
- Difficult, but ultimately extremely fair.

Where it departs, though, is in losing the RPG elements in favor of the Metroidvania influence. Rather than hunting down keys and such, you'll more often find movement upgrades unlocking new paths. Instead of boosting stats and finding new weapons, you'll add new capabilities through spells and charms, which enhance or tweak your moveset (boost your spellcasting or your weapon attacks, modify movement options, etc). You're never locked in to a specific "build," because you can swap your charms out at any save point.

If you're looking for a challenge, you'll find plenty here. The White Palace and appropriately-named Path of Pain are cruel and spiteful platforming zones that would be at home in Super Meat Boy, the Trial of Fools in the Colosseum is a controller-throwing rage-inducer, and some of the bosses are a Nightmare. (I should note that I mean all of that with the utmost respect and love.) That said, all the "worst"-difficulty areas are optional (intended for completionists and masochists - but I repeat myself), though the regular bosses are still capable of giving anyone a hard time - until you work out the patterns, that is. ...And when you get to the Godseeker arena you'll find yourself putting yourself through all of them all over again (except stronger) and enjoying all of it.

This game is a bargain at full price, and I can't say enough good about it.

The Souls death mechanic is just bad. If you need to be punished IRL to be able to focus on the video game you're playing than you are an ADD addled retard or seven years old. It's that simple. If you are a normal human adult, who is able to concentrate on what they are doing, then this mechanic adds literally nothing but frustration. Hollow Knight is very good. Amazing graphics, music, atmosphere, game world. Fantastic controls, good enemy design, good platforming. But it is a challenging game, and wasting my IRL time to punish me for dying is absurd. This mechanic exists for, and is a result of, malignant narcissism. The only point of it is to make the people who play it feel good about themselves, because they feel "casual" players can't deal with it. If you are not mentally ill, or actually addicted to social media, then this mechanic is worthless. If you are unable to concentrate on the current thing you're doing, I could see how an extra impetus to pay attention would make you enjoy gaming more. If you are not BOTH paying full attention and having fun, then you should stop playing the game. I'm not someone who would ever feel "accomplished" by beating a single player game. The tasks and difficulty of said game, are entirely arbitrary and dependent on the devs. If you want to feel good about playing a game, play competitive multiplayer and beat other HUMANS. I play games for fun; anything that takes away fun is bad game design. Miyamoto proved this beyond ANY doubt in the 1980s. I like this game a lot and if anyone personally asked me, I would give it a full recommendation. However I can't give it a thumbs up when the gaming community at large is pretending that this death mechanic is good. It is the most cancerous thing introduced to gaming ever, competing only with microtransactions. Overall I would give this game a 8/10 and say that pacing is by far its biggest issue and being literally twice as long as any other 2d metroidvania was a laughably indulgent decision. It looses steam at about 20 hrs in, with about 15 to go. The end is just garbage frustration, with zero-fun bosses that are re-runs of bosses you've already beat. LAME, and incredible that people like this forced recycled crap. I would have much preferred 1 new boss than the re run through the old ones which seem to be in the game only as a length padding. Which is bizarre considering how long it is and how cheap it was on release. A good, but severely flawed game that is VERY FAR from being the best metroidvania of all time and is currently one of the most overrated games of all time. In my mind Super Metroid and Castlevania SOTN are clearly, transparently, and blatantly better than Hollow Knight. So I'm downvoting for the game being overrated and the fanbois being annoying.

EDIT: The music in this game is good, but VERY repetitive. Because this game is too long. And with the way the end game constantly trolls you with frustration, the slow atmospheric music is not fitting and, in its own way, is trolling the player. This sums up how I feel about this game; this game is far far far far less than the sum of its parts. And that is a sad thing indeed. And it's all due to design choices pandering to people's need to feel elitist. LAME.

So,

I love platform games and Hollow Knight seems like the obvious choice.

It's hard, but not impossible to play, or win its battles. It's very well designed, especially for an indie game (I think), and overall it's a very positive experience.

The one thing that sticks out and is very annoying is the saving system. The second thing is the mapping system, the combination of which makes things much harder than they should for purely design reasons.

You can only save on specific places in each area (the benches), and they do not seem to be placed very well within the game, meaning that some times you have to travel a few bloks in order to meet the enemy which will kill you and restart from the bench far away.

Things get even worst as you have to purchase a map for each area, which you then fill in with your own information. This means that when you enter a new area, you do not know where you are, nor you can find a bench easily enough. And if you die, you go back to a previous area.

These above two issues make up for an annoying experience and even though I've been playing for almost 5 hours and will keep on playing, I find these to be problematic design issues.

Finally, what I suspect is happening is that as you gain new abilities you have to back track to older sections/areas of the game to find specific paths that were inaccessible previously. There is a fast travelling system, but only works on a single screen in each area, making things, again... slow paced.

EDIT: As I've now finished (one ending) of the game, through 60+ hours of playing, STEAM, requested that I rethink my review. Well... it remains unchanged, as the above issues were a constant issue, to an, otherwise, superb game.

The other endings are so difficult to reach (which is why I've been playing for so long), that I've lost hope. The idea of carrying something across half of the world without getting hurt once, or dealing with all the bosses one at the time, at the pantheon is... too much, though it makes sense, really.

Let me preface by saying my profile picture speaks for itself, I am a diehard metroid fan, so when I bought this game about a few weeks after it came out, I was excited for a new 2D adventure akin to Super Metroid or Castlevania: Symphony of the Night.  However, I soon found myself putting my controller down after a few hours of play simply because the game was so brutally difficult.  I took it as a huge turnoff, and it dampered my first impressions.  However, about a year later, I hear of the intense critical acclaim this game received, and I just grew tired of seeing it collect dust in my backlog.  I picked the game up again to take another jab at it… and I just could not put it down.

First, I was playing on my new PC, which could actually handle the VSync, which made the game absolutely gorgeous with no skipping or lag.  The entire game is hand-drawn, all of the environments are surprisingly varied and colorful, and the soundtrack just sucks you in with its beautiful piano pieces, soothing guitar and orchestral works.  It is astounding what this game can accomplish with its presentation and atmosphere, and it makes the game feel so alive after a beginning that made the whole world out to be a dead wasteland.

The game takes heavy inspiration from Castlevania, Metroid, and Dark Souls (yeah it sounds cliché to say that as a reviewer, but it honestly does fit).  The game encourages exploration and is a borderline open world once you beat the first boss.  Of course, not every area is accessible after this point, as certain upgrades to your arsenal are required to pass certain environmental hazards and obstacles.  This is where the game draws from games like Super Metroid, as you thoroughly explore an area, gain the pickups and upgrades of said area, fight the boss (or bosses) of the area, and move on.  However, you are given little indication of where to go next, and how to access new areas are left up to the player’s intuition and prior knowledge of how the game works.

Where it gets all Dark-Soulsy is in the themes and the mechanics of death.  Benches in this game can function as bonfires, where you can heal, save, and change upgrades in the form of charms.  Charms are found all over the map and act as optional powerups, skills, and effects that have a VERY noticeable difference on playstyle and exploration.  I think the most fun thing in this game for me is facing a boss, dying to it once most likely (that happens a good bit in this game), then going back to the bench and tweaking my build to cater to defeating this specific boss.  But, before you can throw yourself at the boss again, you need to find your “shade” in the location that you died.  This will give you all of your Geo (currency) and repair your soul meter (magic meter/healing reserves).  If you die again before reaching your shade, however, you lose all of your Geo for good (sound like Souls yet?).  The execution of these mechanics is masterful, and honestly is the best thing about the game besides the drive to explore and uncover all of the world’s secrets.

Now, there are bosses in this game.  Over 20 or so big bugs to slay on this adventure.  And this is the point where I have some complaints.  The game seeks to provide an open world style game feel, so difficulty scaling is a very dicey proposition in a game built like this.  Certain areas of the game have enemies and bosses that are just not suited to be tackled at the point they are able to be accessed.  I can name one boss in particular—The Traitor Lord—that I suffered many, many deaths to simply because I really needed to face him close to the very end of the game rather than two thirds of the way through.  I know I’m alone in saying I think he’s hands down the hardest boss in the game (apart from a certain nightmarish DLC boss that will remain unspoken of), not to put down the other bosses in this game as being pushovers, oh no.  This game’s boss fights will leave you reeling, and they’re all yours to die to multiple times and throw yourself at hoping to finally learn their patterns and conquer them in nail to nail combat.

One last complaint could go to the platforming challenges, not necessarily the super meat boyesque White Palace (I personally found that place to be rather forgiving) but more the areas in the game where you are required to move through halls filled with spikes (you take a hit and spawn on the last whole platform you were on) and enemies hounding you in the process.  Crystal Peak is the worst offende of this, and I could go as far as to say that Crystal Peak is my least favorite area in the game.

TL;DR:  This game is phenomenal, it feels like a metroidvania distilled to its purest form, adventure through an unforgiving cave system that I haven’t experienced in years.  Yeah, there is some difficulty imbalance in certain areas, but that can be mitigated by proper preparation.  If you can get down with 20+ hours of 2D exploration and ball-bustingly hard encounters, this game is worth any price posted.

* There's way too much backtracking: if you don't kill a boss on the first try you have to walk back all the way from your last save point. Most of the time you'll spend as much time walking back to the boss as beating it.
* So far the platforming is lame. This comes from the fact that you have 2 moves so far: a simple jump, a dash, and a wall jump. It could be compensated by good level design / nice controls, but that's not the case.
* The NPC voices are _aweful_. It's so bad that I muted my sound during 2 boss fights, and I always do it whenever meeting certain NPCs. There is also a whole section of the game ( the crystal caverns or whatever ) that's very noisy and annoying. It's sad because the ambiance & music / lack of music is the sole redeeming quality for this game.

So the first half of the game is boring: the regular fights are a chore that you can't skip because you need money, the platforming is almost non-existent, and the boss fights are not interesting.

The other half might be enjoyable, I don't know yet, but I can't recommend this game.

This game is a masterpiece.

The music is just beautiful. At first I was kinda like, "eh i guess it's good". And then I hit the City of Tears and was greeted by one of the most amazing pieces. The combination of the rain and music was outstanding - and the music consisntently stays great.

The atmosphere is... gloomy. You explore this desolate world that literally tries to kill you wherever you go. It's harsh and the game does not pull back its punches, but everyonce in a while you reach an area where you just can't help but smile.

The map is jampacked with secrets. There's hidden charms, vessel and mask fragments, bosses, and other secrets around every corner. You could spend literal days just trying to look for these things. 

There is a story here and its actually really good. You don't learn a lot of it directly; if you just follow the main objectives you get the basics of what's going on. But if you want to learn about the world around you and the circumstances behind many actions, you have to dig deep and actively search for the lore. And it's pretty damned interesting. 

The combat. The core of the game and what really just solidifes the expereince. It's amazing and unforgiving. You can swing your nail (it's a sword, but looks like a nail) in four directions while on ground or in air. Your hits also knock you back a bit, which allows for you to do these combos where you dance on the enemy's head or you can use it to avoid damage when falling on spikes by keeping yourself in the air. The animations for your character and enemies are top notch, making you really feel the impact of your swings. On top of all that, you have charms - which allow for a very diverse way to play. There are dozens of charms that allow for various methods of play. You can spam spells, have familiars, augment your nail and so on. It allows for great replayability and a multitude of strategies. 

Even though I believe the game to be a masterpiece, nothing is perfect. There are some bosses that can be cheesed hard, and are others that are just bullshit to fight, but those tend to be the optional ones and aren't that bad once you get a strategy down. It's also very slow to start, especially with the lack of movement options and having to map out areas yourself (which i personally enjoyed as it felt like i was making great progression). 

There's also 3 free content packs currentlty and another one set to release soon! 

Overall, I've put about 20 hours in this game on steam and 31 on my switch, and for 15 dollars (or 10 on sale) I've well got my money's worth!

Really wonderful game. Amazing graphics and animation, wonderful sound effects and music and great level design.
However the difficulty... The game is extremely difficult.
I actually like difficult games but the problem here is the death system.
The death system is extremely punishing and annoying since once you die you will lose all your currency (geo) and the maximum mana (soul) decreases by one third until you go to your corpse so you are forced to go back to where you just died whereas in other metroidvania you could just say "Screw this area, I'm going somewhere else". Also when you die while there is a corpse the corpse will be destroyed and there will appear a new one meaning all your geo is hopelessly lost which is very frustrating, of course you could play it save by going to the bank (that you only unlock after about 4-5 ingame hours) but since the way there is very long because of the infrequent fast travel points it would make the game way too boring.
What makes this system even worse it that often times there is a boss but no checkpoint (bench) in the area. I found this to be especially frustrating with the Soul Master and the Crystal Guardian since it would take over a minute to get back to him after dying and fairly strong enemies would respawn along the way which could easily kill you again.

EDIT: After the first 8 hours of gameplay I just installed mods to spawn a bench whenever I want so everytime before fighting a boss or going through a really difficult section I could save. I also installed an enemy hp bar mod and a quality of life mod and now the game is actually really fun and not just frustrating anymore.
I will still keep the review as negative since having to install mods to have fun in a game is a bad sign for a game and obviously doesn't change my view of the base/vanilla game. If they ever decide to implement like an "easy mode" of some sorts with more save points and/or a less punishing death system I would change the review.

Hollow Knight is a Critically acclaimed game that deserves far more criticism than it gets. This game isn't anywhere close to perfect and there are quite a few flaws which can ruin the experience. For starters if you're not familiar with some of the techniques speedrunners often use in Metroidvania style games to sequence break, you don't need to read any further in this review. This game isn't for you. It will punish you. Hollow Knight is the absolute WORST metroidvania to pick as your first in the genre. But I'll discuss that more later.

Lets start with the good though, because the game does do a lot of things right. The Ambiance of the game, visuals and characters are all lovely. Its a well designed game with a lot of care put into how everything looks and how it feels. Personally, I'm not big on the art style but I can absolutely respect the vision and its execution. It very lovely. Lots of good little animations like when the player avatar looks down at the map when you open your map. Its details like this I absolutely adore! The goofiness of the characters is charming And I can see loving the game simply for its style. And from an artistic point of view, absolutely!

However, the Art style does have some serious problems with the world design. Because areas are very monochromatic and filled with a lot of negative space it can cause migraines for some of the audience that might want to play the game. I myself struggled through the game because the massive seconds filled mostly with negative space and its monochromatic style would give me migraines while playing it. It got so painful at one point playing the game I had to put down the game for several days because it hurt my eyes so bad. The only other games that have managed that were games styles after Atari or Nintendo Genesis era games so managing to cause this much pain for a more modern style game is impressive to say the least and absolutely not in a good way.

The Controls of the game are fine. They're standard for metroidvania games, other than the Nail bouncing there really isn't anything unique here. No Momentum for the character and we get a wall hope ability similar to Megaman X. I wish they took more from megaman X because that game actually has momentum in the movement with the dash skill. Hollow Knight's combat is perfectly fine for a metroidvania, but its also not impressive. And in some cases, its just inferior to combat in other games like Momodora or Ender Lilies. One might argue the bosses in Hollow Knight are better than both those games, but I strongly disagree. Only 1 boss in the entire game I'd say was actually all that good and the rest were just okay or outright terrible.

The Bosses in Hollow Knight are not all that good. A few of them are alright with a decent challenge to them while others are laughably easy. The best boss in the game I actually had fun fighting was the Mantis guys were I fight 3 of them in total. But I was either unimpressed with the rest of the bosses or the boss was so horrifically bad to fight with so much camera shake that it caused me so much physical pain to deal with I thought about quitting the game entirely.

Exploration of the world, usually my favorite part of any metroidvania game was an absolutely horrible experience at first and got tolerable once I learned that the game loves to suckerpunch you. What do I mean by that? Well, the game starts off and early in the game they have these enemies which lock onto you and slam into you at high speeds, even curving slightly. They're clearly inspired by the Skree from Metroid, however the Skree can easily be run past most the time in the first game and can be in the later games while in Hollow knight, their version will easily either come close to killing you or kill you outright. And they're positioned super early in the game before the player knows to expect rocket speed enemies which can kill you right off the bat. There's no warning for them, no time to realize you have to react to them, you just get blown up on right at the start. It gets worse starting out because it seems half the enemies explode in the early areas of the game, it seems like the game is not trying to be hard its just trying to punish you for not having precognition or cheating by reading a guide ahead of time.

Exploration gets even worse still because the Map and the compass are both upgrades. Because of the nature of the game and how exploration works the Map is actually something you can very easily miss. And with the fact the game takes all of your currency when you die and you need to recover it, added with the high price of the map you can quickly find yourself without a map and playing the game like the precursor to the entire Genre rather than a more modern Metroidvania which knows better than to tuck the map away from the player. Now, I don't necessarily think having to acquire a map is a bad idea. I think it can work in the right kind of Metroidvania. Hollow Knight isn't that Metroidvania and lacking a proper map for chunks of the game is dreadful. it was always "Okay, lets be cautious because I don't want to get cheated out of my cash before I find the map guy so I can afford it and get the map so I can FINALLY start to enjoy this game" which distracted me from what I really want to do, explore.

Exploration still gets worse and it goes into a topic I'll be covering next. You have this Notch system which is basically an equipment point system, its not the first game to use it it wont be the last but it is one of the worst versions of this system I've seen. How this relates to exploration is that in order to see your icon on the world map you need the compass which costs 1 point to equip. Now, You have very few slots throughout the game, even at the end a single point is a pretty big deal so to have one taken up by an ability that should be default is certainly a decision. A horrible decision that further punishes new players for not cheating by seeking outside help.

The Notch system itself is very poorly designed. Another point in its bad design is the nail length notches. This is something that honestly could have been its own upgrade. The player gets so few unique options with the notches as it is and a couple of them are actually just trap options like the thorn one.

But its not like we're done with the bad design either. If you die, not only do you lose all of your currency until you go back to collect it. Okay, that's fine, but we get 2 other punishments on top of that. First, you have to kill your shade to recover everything. Okay, that's not too bad, its not good, but its not the worst thing in the world. At least its not as terrible to fight as the Bat in Salt and Sanctuary. However it does do something WORSE than salt and sanctuary and that's the 3rd punishment which reduces your recovery pool until you kill the shade again. OH GREAT! So not only will you be struggling to get back to an area you die in for a reason but you're doing it weaker than you were before.

I mentioned above that you need to know how to sequence break to actually complete the game. Sequence breaks are typically the unintended method to completing a game faster than you're intended too. Sometimes Devs are aware you'll sequence break and will put Easter eggs for you like in Metroid Fusion, but to outright plan for the player to do it, this really takes the wind out of my sails. Rather than feeling like I've out maneuvered the game I'm playing it as intended and doing something a newbie to the genre might not even consider to try.

Now, I've ragged on the game long enough. I know a lot of people love the game, but the game is a hot mess. The game is hostile to the player and punishes you for not looking up a walkthrough. I went in blind and was heavily punished for playing it blind.

Even with my Criticism I do think the team behind it is talented and I'm cautiously optimistic for Silksong.
