TL;DR - I like a challenge, but I don’t like being forced to play until 1AM to avoid losing my progress. The developers don’t seem to have respect for your time.  Despite excellent  premise, art, and mood, the story is ultimately disappointing and thin.

I wish I could recommend broadly.  On the one hand, it's fantastic - beautiful and soulful.  But ultimately I can only recommend to those who like their games pedantically exacting.  I was largely enjoying it very much, but after putting in many hours and becoming quite invested, it became extremely difficult and nauseatingly repetitive at times.  The game gives no warning when you're approaching something perfectionistically hard.  This was usually okay, because you could usually stop whenever you wanted and be reasonably close to your goal, or just go do something else if you wanted to keep playing.  But sometimes you'd get locked into a do-or-die scenario, with the save points so sparse, and the goal so distant, that  I had no choice but to continue and spend hours trying to make it all the way through, or quit and have to go through these asinine obstacle courses all over again.  Hence me writing this review at 1 AM after having only wanted to play a short while tonight.  I was loving the game, but now it's left a bitter taste.

The narrative began promising, and exploring the new worlds was truly enchanting.  But after a lot of buildup, the story kind of petered out for me and ultimately felt diluted.  Full of bits of lore, but no real buildup.  It loses its momentum since there's no way of knowing whether a side quest is going to be relevant.  So you're given all these narrative threads that build anticipation, and you think they'll enrich the story, but instead they end up feeling mostly superfluous.  I spent hours trying to get through a certain point, thinking it would matter to the story; I finally gave up and found online that it has absolutely no relevance and just gives you some money.  Which was maddening.  I think having a more clear narrative throughline, and a more obvious distinction between what's narratively significant and what's not, would've helped for a more exciting, satisfying ending.

So it was like a themed roller coaster where the line builds tons of anticipation and sets a great mood, then the ride starts out thrilling, but ends by just spinning you in circles until you barf.

I would rather shove a fistful of needles up my ass than having to go through that stupid path.

This game is as close to perfect as can get. I've recommended it to everyone who owns a PC or a Switch. It's a typical Metroidvania with difficult, but fair and rewarding, combat and some clear Dark Souls inspiration. The game just feels good to play with very tight, responsive controls and a smooth difficulty curve. The platforming is just as fulfilling; just as the game has given you enough time to master a particular new ability, it gives you a new one and combines it with old ones so even the first exploration upgrade feels fresh, useful, and seamlessly integrated. 

For $15 (very frequently on sale for $10) you will find more playability and content in Hollow Knight than most $60 games today. The fact that the developer Team Cherry is a tiny, independent team really shows their love for quality gaming experiences (not to mention the free DLCs they've been releasing, including the upcoming Gods & Glory!) really shines through. That, coupled with the fact that Hollow Knight is their first full-fledged project, makes this game particularly special and certainly on many people's "Best Games of All Time" list. 

Think I'm overhyping it? Pick up a copy and judge for yourself. Its only criticism could be that some parts are too difficult. For people who play games casually, or people who aren't used to the "Dark Souls grind" (albeit a little more forgiving), some parts or bosses may frustrate them to the point of not wanting to press on. But for those who want to earn their satisfaction and overcome adversity: Hallownest awaits.

As much as I think Hollow Knight has the potential to be my favourite game, it's few but glaring flaws hold it back far too much. The art, music and fights are glorious, yet much of the game is spent wandering with little to no incentive or direction. I love metroidvanias but this game tends to feel more so like a walking simulator a lot of the time, and a singleplayer experience without benchwarp tends to be mind-numbingly boring.

If you want to enjoy this game, play it with mods. I would seriously recommend benchwarp (unless you're a fan of mindless backtracking) to speed up much of this games boring parts. I would also recommend HKMP because everything is more fun with friends, even the boring parts.

I don't regret buying this game because of the fun I had with the modded experience, but the core game tends to be too much of a chore to have fun with.

I really wanted to like this game, I love the genre, I love the aesthetic, but there are just too many player unfriendly aspects for me to enjoy Hollow Knight.  The developers give you a lot of content for the cost, if you can enjoy it.

The artstyle is great, the music and sound effects are good and fitting.  The mostly black and white aesthetic is nice, with subtle coloring for each region of the game to make each area a bit more distinct.

Exploration and discovery is an important part of the genre, but Hollow Knight makes this a lot harder and less enjoyable than most games.  Many passages within a region look similar, but you cannot use the map until you locate and purchase one from an NPC.  In addition, the compass has to be bought AND takes up a valuable build slot.  Maps do not auto-update either, only updating when you rest at a bench.  Markers also have to be purchased individually.  Overall, this makes basic mapping functions a grind.  One region you can get to fairly early, the NPC to purchase the map from is locked behind an object that needs a late game skill to reach.

Platforming works, but doesn't feel quite right, especially when compared to Ori or Celeste.  There seems to be a slight lag or delay before a jump happens.  Combat is quite difficult, especially bosses.  You will often need to die repeatedly until you learn the bosses patterns, and each time have to trek back to the boss room in a weakened state to kill your shade.  If you die before killing your shade, then you lose all your money.  Most of the treks back to the boss aren't too horribly long, but the Soul Master boss is horribly long, with very random teleporting foes in the way that make the run back even more irritating.

Overall, the game does not respect your time.  I wish I could like the gameplay, as I do love the aesthetic and what story bits I got so far.  I would only recommend Hollow Knight to those who greatly love the Metroidvania genre, and are masochistic enough to put up with the unfriendly mapping, the very difficult bosses, and the high risk of losing work and time.

So many anti quality of life design choices were made. Every death sends you back very far and makes you waste a lot of time re-playing same dozen or so rooms over and over. To get a functioning map you need to purchase a total of 3 items, one of which uses one of very limited passive buff slots. Even after collecting all 3 map items the map still won't update until you go back to save spot. And map is only valid for a single area, if you go to another one you are basically blindfolded again. Changing buff items also requires you to be in a very specific spot, you cannot just do it in the pause menu.

Music is inoffensive, but it's mostly ambient sounds containing so little actual melodies that there might as well not be any soundtrack at all.

Lastly, the combat felt very annoying at times. Often I would still get hit even though I was pretty sure I dodged the attack, or used my attack before. Also, many traps and enemy attacks were designed in a way that they basically require that you have played the game before and have very good memory, otherwise there is almost no way to anticipate them.

Hollow Knight feels like a true successor to the Metroidvainia greats like Super Metroid and Symphony of the Night.

The controlls remind me a lot of Rogue Legacy, which is an absolute joy to play. The movement and combat mechanics are very similar and quite snappy, so you shouldn't go into this expecting momentum based puzzles like in Super Metroid.

The environmental design adds a lot here. While there are a multitude of NPCs who fill you in on the story of Hollownest, the environments do most of the talking and reveal a great deal about this once proud kingdom.

The score is a joy to listen to, merging seamlessly with the environments and addapting to the gameplay situation, such as an ambush.

Considering this is only the first showing by this studio, the care and attention to detail impresses me greatly, and I cannot wait to see what Team Cherry has in store for the future.

It is a masterpiece!

[h1]Aspects[/h1]
[i]Hollow Knight[/i] is a metroidvania, that means, you have a map to explore, some parts you can access, others not... That kind of stuff.
[list]
    [*] The map: it is HUGE - just for a note, it took me [b]50 hours of gameplay[/b] to get every single piece of information in the map. To get a map of an area, you must buy it from the cartographer, and you can locate him by following the trail of papers or hear him sing - he will leave a note if he isn't there - for a metroidvania is crucial to get the map. However, the map is not that complete, has only some parts and you will complete it as you progress in the area. Even the map being huge, this isn't mean that the game is boring, on the contrary, every area is unique, it can show some piece of information, or it can show new enemies, or it can test your hability... There so much things to explore and discover in this game; 
    [*] The player: You control a bug (the Knight) with a mysterious past, in your hands, you have only your old nail and the hability to heal yourself. With your nail you can attack it in four directions and your heal magic uses white energy and you can get it by defeating enemies. As you progress in the game, you will get upgrades, for your nail and for you heal spell, as for more habilities like magic attacks and others userful habilities needed for some parts in the huge map of Hallownest;
    [*] Charms: Another part referent to the player, you can equip charms, which is a "badge" with a bonus. You'll have "charms points", called notches, who specify how many charms you can equip - there are some charms that consumes more notch than the others, so choose carefuly and see what is the best combination for the moment. You can find or buy more notches as you progress in the game. Each charm found has your own unique bonus and, too, you can combine charms to perform more powerful advantages;
    [*] Death: This game is "Souls-like", that means, if you die, you'll lose all your geos (golds). If you reach the same spot without die, you can recover everything, otherwise, you'll lose every geo.
[/list]

[h1]Story[/h1]
The game story is not given, you start in Hallownest and that is. As you advance in the game and complete sidequests, the story will unfold, you'll know new characters and backstories, every piece of information is needed to understand the whole plot. Some pieces of informations are easy to find out, others require some exploration and/or more experience, but all of these points complements to the game itself - it increases the imersion level. I will not detail any point of the story, it is better that you figure out everything for yourself, is another great experience in this game!

[h1]Controls[/h1]
I've heard some complaints about the compatibility with the Xbox One controllers (I hope these issues have been fixed), but I played the entire game with a Xbox 360 controller. I had no problem with, the controls are responsive and the default configuration is nice - the game displays the buttons correctly as well. For Linux players, no additional configuration was needed thanks to the [url=https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/input/xpad.txt]xpad module[/url] in the Linux kernel, just plug and play.

[h1]Music[/h1]
Just amazing, composed by Christopher Larkin, the music of the game really transmits the essence of the game.

[h1]Expansions[/h1]
This game expands with free contents, since this game was a Kickstarter campaign, some promises was made. Here all the announced expansions:
[list]
    [*] [b]Colosseum of Fools[/b]: This was a Kickstarter promisse, it was not founded, but is in the game. In this area you will test your habilities in the colosseum, fighting waves of enemies and bosses. There are three difficulty modes: first the easy mode, then medium and then hard. When you win each mode, you'll receive an item;
    [*] [b]Hidden Dreams[/b]: First expansion, adds two bosses, they are powerful and to deafeat them you'll need practice and experience;
    [*] [b]The Grimm Troupe[/b]: New location with new characters with a new quest! This expansion includes new charms and new bosses;
    [*] [b]Lifeblood[/b]: A new boss and some adjustments. This expansion fix some problems and optimize the game;
    [*] [strike]Gods & Glory[/strike] [b]Godmaster[/b]: From the devs, this expansions is the biggest yet, including a new quest, new bosses, a new game mode [strike]and more charms[/strike] ([i]there is no new charms, but there are interesting secrets which opens for more future content - like the Hornet expansion[/i]);
    [*] [strike]Hornet expansion[/strike] [url=https://store.steampowered.com/app/1030300/Hollow_Knight_Silksong/][b]Silksong[/b][/url]: From the Kickstarter promises, there [strike]will[/strike] would be a second playable character, planned to be in release of the game, but was postponed. At least, it is expected to be a completely different game - something like what happened on [i]Shovel Knight[/i]'s expansions. ([i]Indeed, initially planned as a DLC, now it was expanded to a fully new game. Hype![/i]).
[/list]

[h1]Issues[/h1]
[list]
    [*] Perfomance issues: three or four times happened, the fps rate drops from 60 to 30;
    [*] [strike]When going to another room, the game "loads". Sometimes, the loads takes too long, and no clear indication of it - in the most time, I thought that the game was freeze[/strike] - Fixed on Lifeblood update;
    [*] [strike]Locked achievements: the achievement progress is recorded both on Steam and in-game. I've already unlocked every achievement before Lifeblood update, although after this update, some of my in-game achievement was locked again, but they all still unlocked on Steam. Probably, if I replay the playthrough, they will unlock again... It is not a [i]real[/i] gameplay problem, but it is weird.[/strike] - Fixed on Godmaster update, all my old achievements are unlocked.
[/list]

[i]HK[/i] is an amazing metroidvania, with a decent Linux support and incredibly cheap for a game with such amount of content. Another example with devs dedicated with their work and with the community. I can easily say that [i]Hollow Knight[/i] was one of the best games of 2017!

Overall: 10/10

Hollow Knight is extreme well done and polished in many aspects
Weather that would be it's art style, it's music, and even it's world building

The game has contact damage, meaning every boss, enemy, and theirs mothers can damage you upon contact, and made even worse is that you start with 5 hit points
Then there's the Charms, almost 75% of them are useless, and 25% that are good are plain raw buffs, while another 15% of these 100 charms feel almost like they should be features and not charms. (Gathering Swarm, Wayward Compass, and Steady Body are good examples)
But I trecked on anyway, still amazed by the world, tho I only knew of the story due to a Youtube, the game is extremely Story-phobic, opting for the "Gameplay over Story" and "EnViRmEnTaL StOrY tElLiNg". Or better known as not telling your story at all and forcing the player to actively seek out even the most crucial of lore that could've been told to you in like 3 lines of dialog, but your forcrd to go through 8 separate lore terminals and find mostly useless information
Then there's the double damage, my fucking god the double damage. So have 5 masks right? Well you should be able to.. you know Upgrade your masks to take more hits, simple RPG stuff. Well say good by to that for the End Game of Hollow Knight, where all the enemies brought their Grand fathers, and they have gotten their Belts that collectively beats your ass inside out with Double Damage.
The Final Boss? Double Damage
The cool Nightmare King? Double Damage
The Traitor Lord? Double Damage
That random bug in the infected crossroads that explodes if you think about attacking it? (Double Damage I am not joking about this one)
That Infected versions of the Vengefly? Double Damage explosion on death

There is SO MUCH double damage, you could fill the ocean with it, and then blot out the sun with the rest. It's so annoying, and invalidates all of the hardwork you went through, just to increase you health

Alongside that, some bosses have the audacity to have DOUBLE CONTACT DAMAGE, they basically have Aid's with this. This game has SO MUCH I love, but honestly. it has SO much working against it that I legitimately cannot recommend this game, it's good if you put a mask on. 3. Double Damage literally negates the point of mask collecting, and renders healing useless, once again, you put on a 4 notch charm called "Deep Focus", also that charm makes your healing take twice as long, making it useless for faster fights like NKG

4. Contact Damage has NEVER been fun, no Soulsborn or Souls-like has had contact for a reason. If Miazaki, the man known for making hard games, isn't touching contact game in his games, it probably means your doing something wrong

While I've not played many Metroidvanias, this came strongly recommended to me as one of the best examples of the genre. The sense of atmosphere and artwork are really quite something, and the moment to moment gameplay is certainly compentent. However, if you are a completionist (these sorts of games typically suit that mentality and approach), this game really does not respect your time.

All throughout the game you will find yourself in situations where you have to press on to maintain progress, and quitting for the day before reaching a save point means you must pick up from the previous save point. Fast travel points add to this pressure, you feel you have to keep pressing on because backtracking can be arduous. Each time it is not so bad, but it is a constant irritation throughout the game.

As well, there are a few (presumably) infamous parts of the game that demand you play long segments of combat or platforming that can take from half an hour up to a few hours, with little warning. By the time you realise you've committed to spending that time, it is too late, and you must slog through the game until you are free.

Honestly, it is a pretty good game if not for this issue, I really wanted to like it, but the game kept getting in its own way for me. Maybe it won't feel that way for you though.

Christopher Larkin doesn't deserve to be associated with such a horrible excuse for a game, because his score is flawless.

- Beautiful artwork and scenery. 

- An amazingly atmospheric soundtrack tailored to each area. 

- Tight gameplay with controls that are instantly responsive ( on the PC ). 

- One of the best stories ( in a video game or otherwise ) I've had the pleasure of discovering. 

- Has one of the largest maps of any game in its genre with very few things being reskins of what you see in other areas ( I.E. lots of content and nearly all of its original/unique ). 

- The additional content that the game offers is completely optional.

- Every single enemy and boss in the game has a learnable attack pattern and spots where you can get in a heal ( I.E. while some bosses may be tough, none are "unfair" ). 

- Priced at $15 with 3 free DLC's. The smallest of these DLC's add more to the game then most ones you'd pay for in triple A games do. 

Quality and quantity at an affordable price. Hollow Knight has it all.

Warning: If you don't like backtracking and/or exploration then this game probably isn't for you. Personally, the sense of wonder and disovery I got from travelling through the ruins of the long dead kingdom of Hollownest and discovering its' many many secrets was one of my favorite parts of the game, but I know it's not for everyone. If you're looking for a quick game that you can beat in a day then Hollow Knight probably isn't for you. It's an experience that needs to be taken in to be fully appreciated.

When I first purchased this game, I got about 10 hours in, and then lost interest for a few months. That was a mistake. I came back, and have since clocked 22 more hours in a short time.
Coming from someone who loves challenging platformers like Shovel Knight and Spelunky...

Pros:
• [u]When you die, it always feels like it was your mistakes that did you in (which is rare for most games)[/u]
• Fastastic art style and music (as you can see/hear)
• Difficulty progression is smooth, and you can tell if you should return to a place better equipped later in the game
• [u]The combat is fantastic; by far the best in any platformer I've played[/u]
• [u]There is always, always, always a reward for exploration[/u]
• The game has a great sense of progression, as you accumulate more and more abilities
• Equippable passives add both an element of strategy and a bit of fun (coming up with goofy combinations is a simple pleasure)

Cons:
• The performance can be iffy on old-ish computers like mine (even with blurring and particle effects set to minimum, there were two or three times where the game lagged quite a bit)
• Jumping is a bit floaty, so precision platforming can frustrate you 
• The early game is difficult, enough that it can scare people off for at time (like me!); however, it eases up ~7 hours in

All in all, 9/10. Hollow Knight won't baby you, but it respects you if you demonstrate your skill.

I don't review games very often well to be honest this is my first game that I have reviewed. So here goes.
Hollow Knight is a Metroidvania with a strong emphasis on platforming and timing based combat. 
Overall I found this game rather enjoyable, I am a huge Metroid fan so this game was a lot of fun for me. Now I am going to review the individual parts for the game, the parts will be as follows: Gameplay, story, presentation, and soundtrack.


Gameplay: Now I must say that I feel like these controls are very comftorable and responsive. I honestly didn't feel like the controls really failed me. However I will say this: USE A CONTROLLER. This game even tells you as its loading up that a controller is recommended in the brief time that I did use the keyboard I had to deal with weird mapping of the jump and attack buttons, of course you can remap them however I feel like the controller is just better.  However while playing you will find that the jump is a little bit different from other platformers, while others will have more momentum based platforming, here your jump will last as long as you hold the button to a certain point and you will start your fall as soon as you let go of the jump button. Luckily the game teaches you this very early with the second "screen" of the game. The combat is more based on timing than anything, you reacting to the enemy and countering accordingly. You can use your basic attacks with your nail or four spells that you can use that work differently and use up your soul gaige. Speaking of your soul gaige that is another thing you need to be mindful about for it is the source of your spells and your healing. Starting the game you will have five health so minding your healing is very important in the harder parts of the game such as one of the hardest platforming challenges in the game: the white palace.  God some of the platforming sections in there are pure hell. However all in all very goog very tight and very responsive controls and gameplay.

Story: Now this is the weakest part of this aspect in my humble opinion. However that doesn't mean its bad it just means it isn't the best. The story is pretty simple: there is a kingdom under the town you have arrived at explore and solve the mystery of why it fell. On the way you will stumble across interesting bugs and find interesting lands. Overall the story is simple at first however if you are willing to look you will be able to unravel the story and find out why the kingdom fell and will be able to save it. No matter the cost. Even with a simple idea I love it however out of the four categorys it is the weakest but it is still enjoyable to find out the details and discover what happened.

Presentation: Hollow Knight is hand drawn 2d animated and it looks amazing. The animation is very fluid and awesome and the best part is that everything looks very adorable. Every creature looks unique and are memorable. My favorite type of enemies being the mantises.

Soundtrack: OOOOOOOH boy the soundtrack.....is freaking amazing I do not dislike any of the songs each of them fill the mood and atmoshpere of the game very well. Overall this one of the strongest part of the game.

Overview: the soundtrack, the art style, and the gameplay make this game very worth the play and I would even recommend 100 % the game on your first playthrough because the journey is so exciting and so fun. I know I enjoyed this and I hope those who buy the game after reading this review.

If you play with a controller, you might hate the game or controller for reading your jump input twice when you only wanted to jump once. But those are minor problems that are easily overcome. This game is not for people easily irritated by the ambiguity of progress or requirements. We have resources on the Internet, and I have used them for help on progression. You save much time finding out where certain things are and what you need to access various locations. However, using online resources or not is a personal choice you must make in deciding whether to play this game or not. I did, and so, the overall experience in the end was more than satisfactory. The amount of content for what you pay for is more than fair as well.

[End of review]

This game is comparable to Dark Souls, but you should not use that comparison in deciding to buy this game or not. It is only comparable through the essence of both games: Memorization of each boss' combat patterns and reacting to them accordingly, storytelling through NPCs, details of items, etc., the wide variety of enemies, and the return to your previous checkpoint after death and losing your currency if you cannot reclaim what you dropped on death. These are recurring ideas have been used in many games together or separately. The first games I played utilizing some of these ideas were from the Megaman Zero series. So, no more "It's like Dark Souls." There is much more to say about great difficult games than that.

Hollow Knight is a metroidvania with a plot & lore similar to a game like Dark Souls. It has a beautiful soundtrack, options for extremely challenging additional content (Path of Pain), extremely fun boss fights, customizable loadouts (charms), an almost Tim Burton-esq aesthetic, and a plot that, if you choose to explore it fully, tugs a bit at your heart strings. 

I haven't felt this immersed or obsessive over exploring & unlocking content in a game since I played Skyrim for the first time in 2011. I had fun getting lost, learning and mastering the mechanics of certain fights, and meeting all the bugs. This is a game that I found I wanted to actually read all the NPC dialogue (including all 57 of Zote's precepts, including all the special messages left by Kickstarter backers in the Shrine of Believers). This game is a testament to the power of crowd funding and hard work of indie developers. I truly believe it has something in it for everybody.

In addition to all of this, it has released several major FREE expansions that could have easily been charged money for. With that said, Godseeker is apparently supposed to be the last free expansion. I look forward to/hope for more content, because I will definitely be insta-buying it.

I bought this game on sale, but after playing it I would have bought it for full price. Hollow Knight is incredibly immersive, and the design and gameplay has kept me playing for hours upon hours to explore new areas, fight tough bosses, and unlock more charms. I had no idea just how big this game was until I realized that I've played 20 hours and probably still haven't completed close to half of it. And that's a good thing.

Other good things:
-Each area is designed to point you in certain directions without being obvious, and is still open-ended enough to allow exploration 
-Every boss you encounter is beatable no matter how difficult, you just need patience and strategy (which is good for casual players like me)
- Every time I discover something new I get really excited because of the possibilities of what that new thing will unlock. A new area? A new boss? A new ability that will allow me to explore an area that was previously unreachable and fight more bosses?
-Each character you encounter has a very distinct personality that appears in subtle ways, like the different ways items are described based on which character is describing them. It's not a big thing, I just think the attention to detail is neat
-The game just looks beautiful

You don't need to be a fan of metroidvania or platformers or whatever to enjoy Hollow Knight, this is just a really fun game that provides a lot for only $15. Play it.

I dunno how this BS game got the hype it did.  If you think Diablo is a deep game, then this one is for you.

I guess if you're desperate for a metroidvania, it fits the bill, but its systems intentionally waste your time.  Need a map?  Buy it.  Want markers on the map?  Buy it.  Want to reveal new locations on the map?  Buy them.  Want to reveal YOURSELF on the map?  Buy it and equip it to a slot.  Want to activate waypoints?  Pay for each one.

Want to do some exploring?  Better memorize everything, because despite the hyped up art style, everything looks the same.

Finally find a waypoint that lets you travel back to the areas you've already visited, after you decided to do some exploring in a place that you can access, but has artificial barriers halfway through, that you can't pass until you get the item?  PAY TO USE THE WAYPOINT, AND IF YOU'VE DIED, THEN YOU BETTER FARM UNTIL YOU CAN AFFORD IT, BECAUSE TRAVERSING THESE LEVELS WITHOUT A MAP SUCKS!

That's just the traversal and exploration.  Let's talk combat.  Attacking involves one button.  You press the button and a direction, and that's as much variety as the combat really gets, aside from spells which are situational, and burn through mana crazy fast.  

Let's talk about how they implement the combat.  When you hit something, you also get knocked back, and they setup the game with a ton of small platforms, with enemies on them, and death pits below.  Guess how that turns out?  Attack to a platform, and your reward for hitting the enemy, is getting thrown into spikes or acid or w/e.

This game is full of terrible game design, and if it was better I would be tempted to cheat, and just give myself infinite money, but the combat isn't good, and the story really isn't captivating.  Doom and gloom and dead civilizations and obscure storytelling aren't original, and this game has done nothing to captivate me so far.

Another fun thing.  Enemy variety.  It's something they actually hype up themselves.  Some enemies run at you.  Some enemies wait and the run at you, but they have a different model, so different enemy.  Some enemies wait, and then roll into a ball at you.  Different enemy.  Some enemies have shields when they run at you, so you have to jump over them and attack them from behind.  Some of the enemies that run at you are really big.  Maybe they get a bit more diverse as time goes on, but good lord, it does not deserve the ratings it has.

Ok when I first looked at the store page for this game I thought it would be a quick simple game for $14.99 man I was wrong, you know when you focus on a game so much or play really hard to win you don’t actually stop and enjoy it? Happens to me a lot but not for one moment did I not enjoy this game. It made me feel some kind of way about it I felt like I was in the story just a simple adventurer going on a quest for glory. I’ve never played a game that made me feel so different about how I see the world and that’s only after I looked at every piece of lore I could find. The art style and story is incredible it reminds me of studio ghibli that magical world that you never quite know how it started but than it pulls you in on it’s darker side of the story about a fallen kingdom and all that remains. I can’t tell you the fear I had when I first fell into deepnest I was relieved just to make into the next room! Never went back again until I was ready and that’s the best part you can do things at your own pace and you always get rewarded when you put in the time to look around. I always get excited when you get a required item to pass the next room or backtrack to another it’s so cool see that glimmering light and look at what’s around and think “hey cool! A new item”. First time beating was about 40-50 hours to complete (I play offline so my hours are a little weird) there is just so much to do in this game. I actually refused to play ANY other game until I had collected everything I could on my save like items, charms, the enemy log book thingy even all the pickups you could find to simply sell for some extra cash. A lot of people say this game is like dark souls let’s face it a lot of game are these days whenever I died I never felt like it was anyone’s fault but mine and while the death mechanic can be heartbreaking if you loose all dat sweet sweet geo it feels even better when you take it back. The game doesn’t exactly hold your hand for you have to figure a lot of stuff on your own but is feels good to do it without the help of the all mighty google like when you have one of those moments when your lost and don’t know what to do and suddenly you know where to go after triggering an event or thought I’ve looked a everywhere but here sorta thing. I loved backtracking with the help of the map, would never leave town without it. There’s really a deep sense of satisfaction here when you complete stuff but you always feel like your just on the verge of discovering something greater, it’s like when you read the first page of a new chapter in a book and you just wanna keep going. The boss fight were thrilling and intense, the normal enemies were thrilling and intense, the lore was thrilling and intense and the Grimm troupe was...........intriguing and creepy. The combat was exciting and fun I would always learn others ways of taking down your prey although I do love swinging my sword around above all else. The boss fights were hard but fair reminded me a lot of cuphead actually, there was a strategy to them where one way was easier than the others or you could just wing, practice and go all out on the guy. I actually wanted to fight them again because I got so pumped beating them and you can do that with some bosses, fight a harder version of it. Never have I seen a game so cared for and done right for a single player experience, since a lot of games are online and multiplayer based nowadays. Honestly this game is super cheap for what you get in return one of the few games I bought at full price and thought was best money I ever spent, it was a steal. If you made it all the way down here thanks for reading I appreciate it! But stop reading and start playing hallownest awaits!

Had I played this game before Dead Cells, I would definitely have recommended it. But Dead Cells has raised the bar for side-scrolling dungeon exploration, rougue like games. The controls in Dead Cells are ultra responsive, and the liberal placement of teleportation gates gives the game very good pacing.

Hollow Knight is a much slower pacing game, which is fine. But ultimately I could not give it a recommendation because of its controls. Particularly the way jumping is unresponsive. There always seems to be this lag in movement for jumping. I've lost count of the times I got hit or missed a jump because of the unresponsiveness (the character does not jump after hitting the button). Maybe Dead Cells ultra tight response has spoilt me, but that seems the standard to meet. A game character should be an extension of the player, and the character should move at the speed of thought of the player. Every time I miss a jump, it sucks me right out of the experience.

Another reason I cannot recommend the game is the way it's designed linearly while giving you the illusion of openness. Without spoiling the game, you are NOT allowed to venture into other areas, even though the path seems open, you have to follow a certain order. But the game does not funnel you towards it. So you waste tons of time trying to find the correct path to unlock the next item that allows you to proceed into the next areas.

This sort of design is not new, but there are usually several paths to take while some are blocked. In Hollow Knight, particularly the first areas, are completely linear.

There are a lot of things that annoy me in this game - the fact that you get the dodge i-frames so late, the fact that it has a cooldown that doesn't match your dodge, the fact that you have to beat a boss again and again just to fight the actual boss...
But theres more i love about it - the Dark Souls in it, if you will. The atmosphere is great, and uncovering the story little by little is lovely. Many of the characters are top notch.
If it had taken a bit more from dark souls and focused on making a better combat system, it would have been much better, but for what it is, and for that price, it's very good :)

Hollow Knight, despite being a visually appealing game with its massive map and artwork, has a significant design flaw. While the game's map structure allows for freedom and various ways to progress (there isn't even a traditional quest list because of this), you cannot advance in the game without obtaining a single essential ability: Crystal Heart. Once you reach a certain point in the game, you cannot continue without acquiring this ability, which is only obtainable at one specific location. If you overlook this location, you'll have to traverse the vast map repeatedly, revisiting the marked and inaccessible areas. Hopefully, they will address this issue in the sequel.

This game is hard. I do not mind hard. What I do mind is when the game specifically teaches you something, then decides to break those rules in the middle of a boss fight, resulting in your death. Don't forget that there is a 3-5 minute slog back to the boss fight from the nearest bench, so hopefully you can make it there with a full health bar. 
Boss tells are very minimal. Do not expect an audio cue for different attacks, because there are none. The bosses (the part of the game that is supposed to be the most fun) all have 2-3 moves that they will do over and over. Once you learn them, mostly the fight is a cakewalk. But some of them have one move that will hit you based entirely on luck. Where is the projectile going: above, below, or into me? No way to know, and if you made the wrong move, you'll get hit, there simply is not enough time to react once you see which way it intends to go. This is not "hard", this is bad design.

If you're coming into Hollow Knight looking for a monotonous slog of uninspired platforming to neat looking, but ultimately disappointing boss battles, this game is for you. Everything gets old quickly in a game that parses out the "fun bits" extremely sparingly. Incessant backtracking, lame upgrades, labrynthian level design filled with traps you can't see until you hit them, and one-trick enemies that sometimes blend into the environment entirely and act as more of a nuisance than anything else.

This is one of those games who I wish there was a neutral option for steam ratings. it's a solid metroidvania with some awful design decisions crammed in. 

The graphics are gorgeous, and this is probably the biggest good point about the game. A game however, doesn't run on graphics alone.

The music is... I don't know, it's not good but it's not bad either. It's just, there. I guess I'm in minority thinking that but it's just not a soundtrack i have found particularly good or bad, it fits the game but it's frankly mediocre.

The movement system is really basic in this game compared to other metroidvanias. It's a common indie game trope to have bad floaty jumping and this game follows suit, but its not too punishing in terms of platforming. People who pretend this is some of the best platforming they ever played have never played a mario game and should be ashamed of themselves frankly. To add to the frustration of the platforming, if you die while traveling you lose all of your money and have to go kill an enemy to get it back. It's tedious, unfun and is my biggest gripe with the game. It's a downrihgt awful design decision that adds nothing to the game besides tedium.

The combat is fine but the hitboxes are wonky and not that obvious. It's not the best playing metroidvania, and it's souls-like influences also detract from it. Why can't I spawn back in the boss room? Oh, right, because this game is padded entirely with tedium.

Tl;DR... Good? But bad too. It's a great game if you have patience for bull but I don't.

The game has pleasantly surprised me time and time again. The artwork is detailed, distinctive, and beautiful. Controls are very fluid and polished (better with a controller than keyboard but either way is viable). The world is considerably larger than I had expected, and level design is oh so clever with such a satisfying flow to it. The difficulty level is spot on in that new enemies, bosses, and areas are a real challenge in the first few attempts, but the player can quickly learn to adapt and overcome.

They creators have mastered the little twists necessary to make the hours you invest a fresh experience. A new ability having more dramatic effects than anticipated, some comic relief in the Royal Waterways, thinking I had probably seen most of the world only to check the Wiki and find that I had only seen a little less than half of it. Environments are consistently works of art.

Simply put, the game succeeds in making you yearn to progress. To unlock that new ability, to go back and investigate that area you might be able to access now, to see what role that character plays, to find more charms to suit your ideal playstyle.

I had been in a slump for a while, looking for something like a sandbox style game to get into. Ended up buying Hollow Knight on a whim, and though it is not a sandbox, it has won me over with its scale and execution.

However, Hollow Knight also features heavy platforming elements, and the ghost sometimes ends up in the wrong place in an expanse of hazardous terrain. Whether that particular variety of frustration adds or detracts from the overall satisfaction in gameplay is probably a matter of taste.

It's really no matter though. I'm a cheap SOB who usually waits for sales, got this one at full price, and am so glad I did. Hats off to Team Cherry.

I have tried SO hard to like this game, but after sinking 16 hours in it, I have to admit that I just... don't. It's got a really unique personality, with quirky characters, a strange underworld to explore, and cute little voice snippets. But despite all that generally adding up to what I should be majorly into, I just couldn't get into it. 

I think it's in large part the clunky map system, as well as the repetitive combat, and surroundings. At least it felt repetitive to me--I know lots of people love it. But for me, it didn't feel fun challenging, but artificially padding out gametime "challenging". And despite initially liking the setting, I found my just plain -bored- circling through endless greyscale caverns, and then backtracking to get map areas. And exploration is usually my favorite part of a Metroidvania! 

I won't say it doesn't deserve its reputation, since a lot of love clearly went into it, and a ton of people found it to be just what they wanted, but for me, it was a total miss.
If you like metroidvanias, you will like this game. Everything about it is polished to a shine.

Hollow Knight is absolutely phenomenal. 

The developers really know how to create atmosphere. Everything about the hand drawn graphics and the beautifully orchestrated music works perfectly. Every area on the world map offers a unique feel so that things never get repetitive. Speaking of the world map, it does a fantastic job of creating a sense of adventure. When you enter an area for the first time, you are completely blind. Luckily, the game guides you where you need to be while still giving room to explore. As you explore you'll come across elements of the game's lore and hints towards the bigger story. The characters you meet along the way have simple yet satisfying arcs.

Gameplay wise, the game also does most things right. Hits, both by you and against you, have tons of weight behind them. The combat is quick and challenging, but never unfair. Your character moves swiftly between swings and various upgrades give you new tools to fight with. You can even heal mid battle if an opening comes. If a fight is ever too hard, you can always switch to a different part of the world and come back later once you're stronger. Slowly learning from your mistakes and figuring out the enemy patterns is extremely satisfying. The difficulty curve is reasonable although there are definitely harder areas.

Where does the game go wrong? Personally, I hated the saw blade location. That one is required for the "good" ending and was just frustrating. Other areas were mostly forgettable but not as actively bad.

Ultimately, the negatives are nitpicks on what is otherwise fantastic. I can't wait to purchase the in progress expansion whenever it comes out.

I've read a few reviews before playing Hollow Knight for the first time. I did expect to like the game because hey, it's a Metroidvania after all. And those are rather hard to screw up. But wow, I didn't expect to experience such a gem of a game after playing through it.
For most people [i]Super Metroid[/i] (or [i]Symphony of the Night[/i]) stands at the top of its genre and I'm inclined to share that opinion. I'll give it some months and a few playthroughs more but Hollow Knight might just become the new king of Metroidvanias. For me at least.
---------------------------------

Story: 9/10 
[i]A hollow vessel emerges, moves toward Hallownest, and battles its way through the infection to reach the hollow knight. All that to become the new shell of an old powerful entity, the Radiance, and thus to save the entire bug civilization.[/i] 
Let's just say this short summary does not make the initial story enough justice, there's a lot of lore behind the curtain and most won't get it all while playing the main game only. Neither did I.

Sound: 8.5/10
The sound is most of the time the biggest minus of a metroidvania for me but here it is actually...fitting. At a lot of points of the game like really good, even. Good one.
   
Graphics: 9.5/10 
Hand drawn graphics are rare to come by in video games. In this case the world, monsters and bosses just look beautiful and there are just a selected few (Cuphead,...) who can beat Hollow Knight in that regard.

Presentation: 9.5/10 
- Hollow Knight's world is beautiful,it is gigantic, with each new location you visit you'll be astonished how much detail has been put into the game.
- The presentation of the story is just as good as the story itself and it expands as you go further down into the void. But here lies also my one and only gripe with the presentation, the complete story is hard to comprehend without explanations and interepretations found through the internet. Even when you reach the ending (and the true ending as addition), you'll be baffled and ask yourself "What was that?" with a high chance. 
The pieces of the story can all be found ingame, but to combine them into one is really complex.

Gameplay: 9.5/10 
Hollow Knight might belong to the harder to finish Metroidvanias, which is a good thing. 
- Medium challenging platforming passages (with optional really hard passages as well), tight but fair battles and a big pallette of bosses which are increasing in numbers with each new DLC.
- Controls are perfect, in no circumstance I had the impression that a passage was unfair, mistakes always had been on my own end.
- customizable protagonist: there are currently 40 trinkets you can find in the world of Hollow Knight. Depending on the trinkets usefulness you can equip about 2-5 at the same time at end-game.
- a lot of extra skills you find during the game, double jump being on of them to name an obvious one.

Replayability: HIGH
I don't have the exact numbers but you can finish the game without killing half of the game's bosses. In which order you finish the obligatory tasks are, to some extent, in your hands as well. This gives you, the player, a lot of variety. You can play as you like, choose different routes, choose different skill settings, there are enough choices for more than a couple playthroughs.
Additionally there's an unlockable hard mode, where you must not die. Otherwise you'll have to start the game from the very beginning.
-----------------------
[b]Final rating: 93/100[/b]

One last thing, for the measily price of 15€/15$ this IS a must buy, no questions asked, no money lost. Don't wait for a discount, you'll feel bad with that decision afterwards. Because to be fair, for what you get here this should at least cost 40€/$

Hollow Knight is quite possibly the most beautiful and well-structured game I have ever played. With a runtime of about 35 hours for a normal playthrough time and about 60-75 hours for a completionist one. The world of Hollow Knight is a rich one. 

Almost all of the game's art is gorgeously hand-drawn, and the vast majority of the enemies move in seamless animation, allowing you to know when they will attack. Each area is full of surprising secrets and places not accessible until later in the game, allowing for hours of exploration. Although backtracking may be obnoxious, the art and fast travel stations found in almost every region of the game shouldn't make it too much of a hassle.

Hollow Knight implements a fairly unique charm system, with charms that do things such as increase damage, increase soul (mana) generation, automatically collect money for you, summon minions, and other such abilities. This system is expanded upon further by creating charm interactions that occur with certain combinations such as a charm that summons minions combined with a charm that increases movement speed will increase the speed of your minions or a charm that makes your sword range greatly increased at full health combined with a charm that doubles damage when the player is on 1 health will reactivate the ranged ability at 1 health. This can create some enjoyable moments when you find your two favorite charms compliment each other's strengths.

 Every boss is somewhat different, some orderly and precise, others more unpredictable (which can be a problem for impatient or inexperienced players) in their attacks and behaviour. Everything about this game is interesting in it's own way, and that's pretty rare.

Your max health, damage output and mana meter can be upgraded to increase their effectiveness, as in most games, with moderately difficult challenges required to obtain the components needed to do so.

My only criticism for Hollow Knight is that some bosses have buffed re-fights that have nothing to do with the main storyline, which is something that somewhat ruins the creativity of the game. To me this just felt a tad cheap compared to the rest of the game, though this is just my personal opinion.

Now that Hollow Knight's final update, Godmaster, has been released I do have some additional praise and criticism for this game:

This update allows for bosses you've defeated to be fought as many times as you want in The Hall of Gods, an area you gain access to about 2/3 of your way into the game, and you can  unlock more bosses to fight as you encounter them as you progress. This area also has 5 boss rush "pantheons" that get progressively harder as you go along, incorporating bosses you've fought along the way in easiest-to-hardest fashion. 

Personally, I like this feature, as most of the bossfights in this game are in some way enjoyable, with an odd few being "that boss". Replaying them in newer renovated arenas is a fun way to burn time and have some fun. 

HOWEVER, once you have beaten a boss in The Hall of Gods, you unlock the ability to replay them at a harder difficulty (i.e. more health, faster movement, double damage, altered arena, a new attack, or a handful of these buffs). Pretty cool at first, albeit frustrating, but once you do this you unlock another difficulty:

Do it again. Hitless this time.

Now, tell me to "git gud" in the famous words of our beloved Hornet (if you want), but this is hard as all fuck, and kind of ruins the integrity of the bossfight. I still love the game, but this update seems more like a fan-made contribution than an expansion.

I really wanted to like this game, and I had heard a lot of good things about it. But a few hours in and I mostly find myself frustrated. My opinion may change once I get further in, but right now, if I could I would give it a "meh" rating. 

My main problem is that they made the navigation incredibly frustrating. In order to get a map of a new area, you first have to find the map seller. Once you buy the map, it is only for parts of the area. AND THEN you have to buy several upgrades to do basic things, such as fill out the rest of the map, and even see where you are on it. This fullfills no purpose other than frustrating the player by wasting time. Furthermore, there is no indication whatsoever where you're supposed to go, so you're left to wander randomly until you happen to stumble on progress.

The platforming and combat itself is fine thus far. The design of the encounters is a bit annoying in some cases though, where you are fighting on small platforms, where a small mistake will at best set you back a minute or two of climbing, and at worst drop you in a pit of acid. If you die you respawn at the closest Bench (i.e. rest spot), which could be 5-10 minutes of progress you now have to do all over again...

All in all the game is far from bad, but I think it will appeal most to players who enjoy  random exploration and also are not afraid to die and do the same section over and over again.

Personally I enjoyed the Ori games a lot more, with their more directed and narrative-driven gameplay.

I'm not usually one who like to jump on bandwagons, but I have to say that this is a fantastic game.  It took a little getting used to, the way that the camera moves when you jump felt a little off when I first started, and there were a few cheap moments in the opening area (stalactites falling on platforms) that interfere with the difficulty curve, and it feels stressful traveling in unknown areas with no map or marker, and it was confusing trying to find out how to go back to the starting area, but after a few hours everything starts to gel, and it is a fantastic experience.
As of writing this review I am over 20 hours in, and I highly highly recommend this game.  I have been playing it on a tv with Full Array Local Dimming, and the result is truly breathtaking do to the beautiful aesthetics with inky blacks and blooming shafts of light.  If you have a high end tv with either FALD or AM/OLED displays, I suggest you hook them up to your computer while playing this, and play with a controller (or joystick!  The movement in this game is digital, so a joystick would be a viable option to play this with!  I haven't tried hooking up my x-arcade up and playing this with it yet, I've been having too much fun playing with my PS4 controller, and hooking my headphones up to the controller [my computer headphone jack is noiser than the PS4 controller's.])
Anyways, I wasn't planning on writing even as much as I already have, but I just want to say that this game will be looked back on as a masterpiece, and should be played by everyone.  Much is made about its difficulty, but honestly the issue isn't the sheer difficulty, but rather the difficulty spikes.  If you've been able to beat any Mario, Metroid, Castlevania, Megaman, etc., then you shouldn't have much difficulty with this game.

Hollow Knight is THE contemporary Metroidvania. True to the genre, "ability gating" leaves you passing by unreachable ledges, unnavigable poison ponds and the like, teasing you to return when you acquire the necessary abilities. Because of this, backtracking and out of the way exploration are integral to the game, and this can be a put off for some players. Couple that with a cumbersome map system and you might ask, why bother?

Bother because unlike many that try, this Metroidvania delivers on reward for exploration. Rich with secrets and rewarding challenges, the world is robust and beautiful. The combat is tight and will push you to execute properly and punish you when you don't. You'll think you're near the end of the game before you reach the halfway point, unveiling the sheer scope of the game in a way that only excites you to play it more. Unique and gorgeous aesthetic plays second fiddle to the amazing movement and combat.

The game is rich with content and atmosphere. If you're at all a fan of the genre, this is a must buy. If you find the early game dragging as your limited abilities and unfamiliarity with the world seem to hinder your enjoyment, muscle through it, because once this game picks up, you can't put it down.

Hollow Knight is fine, but it's not amazing.

With the deluge of adequate 2D indie action/platforming games out there already, it's hard to be enthused about one that does nothing especially well. The soundtrack is fine, the dialog is fine, the art is fine, the combat is fine, it's all perfectly passable. Many people will buy this game and enjoy it, and for that reason I could easily give the game a recommendation just to fall in line with the "overwhelmingly positive" general consensus. However, there is one glaring flaw in this game that made me put it down and stop playing it entirely, and that alone is enough reason for me to mark this as not recommended.

There is simply *no good reason* for this game to have the bloodstain system from the Souls series. I don't see how it fits thematically with the story, nor do I see it fitting with the rest of the metroidvania-style presentation. Its inclusion feels like it was done solely for the purpose of appealing to the Souls fanbase, so they could scream "IT'S LIKE DARK SOULS BUT 2D!!!!" in every single review. I myself am a massive From Software RPG fan, but I'm of the belief that the bloodstain system as it exists in the Souls series is fundamentally flawed, and that it needs changes to be a truly effective system. Not only does Hollow Knight not do anything to improve on its implementation of the mechanic, but it arguably makes the system worse by way of not having your recovery spot in the same spot that you actually died in.

To explain why this is specifically bad in Hollow Knight, let's first address what makes the mechanic flawed in the first place. The Souls series hails from a long lineage of old-school game design, and historically the punishment for death in old games was to simply lose *all* progress completely, often having to start all the way back at the beginning. This sort of punishment for death is often more disengaging than it is helpful, and since game devs do want you to actually play through the games completely, many different ways of softening this punishment have been developed over the years. The bloodstain system is one such method, as it serves as a way to punish player for death and encourage them to play cautiously, but still gives incentive to try an area again after failure. The flaw in the system is that progress in an RPG often depends on increased stats and items, and increased stats and items depends on currency, so when you permanently lose that currency from failing twice in a row, you're stuck having to grind to make up the difference. Repeating content is, generally speaking, not fun for most players, so forcing them to repeat content because of failure becomes a huge negative incentive to keep playing. Some players still enjoy the challenge, but many end up quitting the Souls games after one too many instances of losing 1 million souls because of an accidental misstep.

Now, when it comes to Hollow Knight, say you've just died in a room that you never fully explored. Because of the way the bug-ghost-bloodstain generates, when you go back to that room, not only do you not know what is in the unexplored areas of that room, but you don't even know where your bug-ghost-bloodstain is, making it significantly more dangerous to try and collect it. If you then die again just trying to just figure out where the hell your ghost went, then you've just lost all of your progress in a way that feels cruelly unfair. At the end of the day, all this mechanic ends up adding is some artificial padding to make playing the game more tedious, and there's nothing particularly interesting or special about the gameplay to make that tedium worth it.

Will you enjoy Hollow Knight if you buy it? Probably, but it depends on how much time you have to spend re-grinding up currency to make up for progress you lose from dying twice in a row. You might like it, but frankly I have a lot better things I could be spending my time on.

Not sure what else I can say about this game which hasn't already been said by so many others, but I will say this is single-handedly the best Metroidvania I've ever played. I adored the sound design, visuals, gameplay, and it has some of the greatest bosses I've ever fought in a game. 

This is also one of the hardest games I've ever played, but to me, it never felt cheap. It's the perfect balance of difficulty, and I can't express enough just how satisfying it was defeating some of the harder bosses and getting past the more challenging platforming sections.

The lore of this game is also super deep and I would highly recommend checking out Mossbag on YouTube if you want to learn more about it. (Just be weary of spoilers, of course!)

As for negatives, the backtracking could get quite annoying at times, but it was never unbearable for me. Many people have criticised the map system, but I personally never had a major problem with it. I can 100% understand why it would be a deal breaker for some though.

Hollow Knight gets a 9/10 from me. Would highly, HIGHLY recommend to anyone even remotely interested. And it's cheap, too; Only $15.00 when not on sale, and trust me, that is the very definition of a steal.

I was really excited to play this game after listening to years of hype. However, after 2 hours of gameplay, there just a few features which have left a sour taste in my mouth.

I have only made it as far as the fungal wastes, but so far I find all the environments to be fairly samey (the ubiquitous  use of black silhouettes, etc.), with the soundtrack doing little to add tension or feelings of anxiety that are so important in metroidvanias as the player descends deeper and deeper into the unknown. Yes; they are easy to distinguish from one another, but this is largely achieved via simple palette changes.

In terms of difficulty, I have found enemies lacking in challenge and mechanics; combat often feels as technical as a flashgame from newgrounds.com. The most common instances in which I find myself taking damage are the split second of confusing particle effects upon taking or receiving damage, in which it is difficult to instantly correlate damage taken to a specific attack animation. I also find it disconcerting that so many enemies damage the player upon simple contact, ala Mario Bros 1983. For a game that clearly borrows heavily from the Sous series (putting it generously. Why is the meter that build upon striking enemies called soul?), it falls short in the direct relation between enemy attack animations and player damage.

 Finally; blind jumps. There are simply too many areas where the player can't be sure whether they can make the distance over a set of spikes, etc. without simply experimenting. There have been a couple of instances where, after trying a blind jump, I've found it unclear whether that area is as of yet untraversable, or the fault was on my execution.  Before trying again, I need to go to another screen, hit enemies enough times so that my 'soul' charge is sufficient to recharge my health and have another attempt. I feel a lot of these traps could've done with the removal of spikes and hazards, and simply show the player through other means that this area cannot yet me traversed. The same goes for vertical areas, which often dont allow for blind downwards traversal because of inexplicable puddles of acid or spike beneath. These, like the enemies who, after defeating, the player must wait a few irritating seconds for their corpse to explode, slow down the pace,  which needlessly impact upon the player's pace.

Obviously, this game does have a lot of positives to offer, but I personally found myself very disappointed, due to these obvious short comings which seem overlooked by other reviews.

Overall, probably great, but too many moments of needless frustration. What I find more odd, is that these features are not the result of developer oversight, but are intentional features from a team who fail to strike the balance between challenge and just being plain irritating.

In summary, it might be a fun game for other people, but if you can't stand enemies that instantly respawn and stand in the way of exploration, this game isn't for you.

At first the game felt really good, then really awesome, and then it got worse and worse the closer I got to the end.
Since you've probably already read a lot of good about the game, I'll mostly skip that part.
Most of the good things you heard about the game is probably true. I think it has good art and music, even though I don't think it's stellar, it is definitely original and creative and I wouldn't be surprised if most players loved it. The story is also good for this genre. The enemy designs are also interesting, especially the bosses.
I like the challenge as well, but I don't like wasting time... sadly this game is better at the latter than the first.

But I wanna start with something else, which you'll notice right at the beginning. The main, and hardest enemy in this game, that will haunt you from start to finish, is the effects of your own attacks. It's so big, bright and sometimes screenshaking that it becomes more annoying and distracting than anything else in the game. And it's twice as bad when you parry.
The slashing effect will hide enemies and projectiles, sometimes even your own character.
Not to mention the size and duration of your slash animation is not at all representative of where and how long it does damage.
A smaller problem is that this is true for enemies as well: they can hit you through walls and outside of their own attack animations, and will do contact damage even if in fact you did not seem to touch them.

Another thing is the unresponsive controls. Sometimes your keystrokes are ignored. I thought if there's 1 thing this game has to do right, then it's controls: just fast, neat and responsive. But so often I press left and my guy still dives downwards into spikes, or hits in the wrong direction that it really gets in the way of having fun.

I'm also not happy about the performance. The menu animations are slow, unnecessary and you can't turn them off.
The loading times are also ridiculously long for such a game. The different sections that you can access immediately are very, very small, and there's loading between those as well, there's even more loading between regions.
I only mention this, because the game is designed in such a way that highly encourages players to go back to the main menu and load the game just to respawn at a certain part of the map.
There are also lags during boss fights and challenges, which can kill you.
And you could blame my PC for that, but I don't really have this kind of problem in other games I play, which you can check if you want.

The amount of backtracking you're forced to do for both the storyline and especially collectibles is beyond reason. And all that while enemies are constantly respawning. Fast travel is minimal, it's just enough to not make you just leave the game. And sometimes it requires several tries just to unlock the option to fast travel.

So while for the most part your experience and abilities grow at a steady rate, always ahead of the enemies you face (even for me, as a casual, the game felt mostly easy), the bosses and certain events will be a huge spike, which is expected.
The problem is that you have to travel through huge chunks of the map just to face the boss again if you die, while dealing with the constantly respawning mobs. It's not a challenge, it just slows you down. Potentially they can damage you in which case you either won't start the bossfight in full strength, or you have to go back to heal again, making the enemies respawn.
And when you killed a type of enemy 10, 20 or 50 times and you're trying to get to a boss for the 3rd time, then it's not a lack of skill that makes you get damage, it's boredom.

There's also this soul (energy) you have which should be maxed at the beginning of every boss fight, if for nothing else it can give you a bit of extra damage, but it can also save your life. Every time you load the game or respawn you have to charge that manually by hitting enemies. Even if you save your game at max energy, when you load it's on 0. So you have to go out and hit the same enemies again for 10-20 times. It can get mind-numbingly repetitive.

Hollow Knight is the best metroidvania I've played and I recommend it to everyone who likes the genre or other similar games.

Hollow Knight is an artwork whose purpose is not always to be fun or entertaining. While as an artwork it never fails presenting gorgeous graphics and mesmerizing soundtracks, as a game it is often tedious, frustrating and player-hostile, on top of an otherwise healthy level of difficulty.

The binary recommendation system doesn't really work for this game. I would definitely recommend it to people who are ready to put up with tedium/frustration in exchange for a deep and engrossing experience, but for people who play to have fun and might be limited on time this game has a few problems.

The biggest one is the lack of save points and backtracking to places where you died, often a boss. HK has absolutely no respect for your time whatsoever. Bosses and difficult platforming segments are always several screens away from the nearest save point, often with lots of lesser enemies along the way.

Team Cherry wants you to think of "the boss, and the path before it, as a single challenge" but the only function of this "path before it" is to waste your time and loosen the feedback loop, which is a form of adding artificial difficulty. A tight feedback loop is one of the most important things when building muscle memory, and these forced pauses in practice can make the experience of improving against a boss very frustrating.

If you want to try a bit of the Hollow Knight experience think of one of your favorite melodies, then search the internet for an online piano and try to find the notes of your melody with only the mouse - except that if you get a note wrong you have to close your browser, open it up again, go to a web search first and search again for online piano, wait for the site to load and then try again.

It is unfortunate because otherwise the overwhelming majority of bosses are really well designed and can be mastered and defeated in a relatively reasonable amount of attempts. It's just that those attempts are padded out both in raw time and with a loose feedback loop. This is most evident with the final boss of the game where you have to defeat a different boss that plays completely differently each and every time you attempt the final boss.

It has to be said that you do eventually get an item that allows you to teleport to where you placed it, but it's a late-game item that you get well past the first 10 hours and you have to defeat over a dozen bosses for it, and it slowly uses up the same resource that you are fighting some of the bosses for.

HK also needlessly wastes your time in other ways, a very good example being dream bosses, which are more difficult versions of some regular bosses. You don't have to backtrack to these bosses, but if your current item loadout is not working for you, then you do have to backtrack to the nearest save point to be able to change it and then go back to the boss, which can add up to several minutes. Team Cherry wants you to have "time to reflect on the loss" but that time would be far better spent in the inventory looking at what other items could be more useful.

Traveling to vendors also takes a long time. There are some very powerful items that break if you die and you have to pay to repair them. The money is not the problem though, but to get to the merchant that fixes it and get back to where you were could easily take up time in the range of 10 minutes. Which is to say, repairing the powerful item isn't paid for with an in-game resource, but with a real one - your time.

This is because you can't even teleport between save points like in Sekiro, instead, you have to backtrack from a save point to a fast travel station, which are even more scarce, then travel to the merchant on foot, then back to the fast travel station and back to where you were.

In the end though, Team Cherry perfectly explains what's happening in this paragraph:

"We don't have full control over the placement of things in Hallownest. The world informs us where objects are placed, and how things are built and laid out. It abides by it's own logic, and we have to take care not to bash it into place just to smooth it out for players"

This makes it very clear: Hollow Knight is an artwork first and foremost, and a game only in a secondary role. The primary intent here is for you to experience an artistic vision through the gameplay, and that will need sacrifices on your part, backtracking, frustration and lots and lots of time so that you can experience the vision in as pure a form as possible.

Some of the most difficult bosses in the game are also gods or divine beings in the lore, and people say their difficulty is supposed to reflect their divine nature. Through the lens that HK is primarily an artwork, this also makes sense. After all, to defeat the most divine being in the game and get one of the endings you actually have to complete an over 40 minute long boss rush that you naturally start over from the beginning when you die. I mean, this has to be some kind of artistic decision because as game design it would be completely crazy.

As such I would not recommend this game to average players who play to have fun. Even though the game is often lots of fun it will always mix with a feeling of tedium and frustration. However, I would absolutely recommend it for people who are willing to go through The Path of Pain so that they can experience a wondrous and immersive world which plays by its own rules and does not pretend that you are not actually the character in the game, but rather a human trying to have fun in a potentially limited window of time.

Hollow Knight is a pure jewel of a game.

It's not just its gorgeous art direction and its beautiful, beautiful soundtrack, that deliver this intimate, moody atmosphere. It's not just the tight controls, either, or the perfect metroidvania gameplay, with its upgrades and skills and the perfectly tuned charm systems that lets you develop countless builds and playstyles. It's not just, either, the extensively interconnected map and its thousand lore hints and mysteries.

It's all those things together, and something more:

Hollow Knight is a game with a [i]soul[/i].
This is a masterpiece.
