After hearing much praise of this title and thoroughly enjoying the Ori games, I decided to give it a spin while on sale. Was it worth it?

TL;DR No. Unless you're a dedicated metroidvania fan, play Ori instead - it does everything better. The serious beauty of Team Cherry's game world is overshadowed by the ridiculous amount of anti-player inconveniences and low quality of life of gameplay.

I am not going to waste much time talking about the beautiful artstyle, music, characters, and arcane story that the developer has woven together to make an incredible game world. Everyone has written to this effect and I will simply note that I agree. There is something truly special here.

The truly special world is undone by what I can only describe as endemic annoyances. For example:
1. Basic quality of life elements that would be the default in any other game are missing or require grinding out, like the simple ability to see your location on the map.
2. Fast travel points are few and far between.
3. Checkpoints (benches) are  few and far between. Unlike in Dark Souls, there are very few shortcuts or ways to make the "graveyard run" quicker or more enjoyable. This creates a very severe problem where if you die because you fail to learn something (perhaps a boss fight or using a new ability or platforming), rather than the game helping you learn to overcome that obstacle, you spend 10 minutes running back to the obstacle, which is far longer than it takes to learn the obstacle! In other words, in my three attempts on one boss, I spent 5 minutes fighting the boss and 15 minutes just running through areas that I had easily cleared. It is boring, repetitive, and simply unenjoyable. I am sure legions of fans will flock to the comments to post "git gud," but understand this: I killed the various bosses I struggled with. I learned their attacks and got good. I just did not like spending the overwhelming amount of time just running back as opposed to fighting the boss! 
4. The combat is somewhat clunky. There are upgrades, but as explained in the quality of life grievance, the baseline combat of a game should not require obtaining upgrades to feel good.
5. The game is very stingy with upgrades early on (other reviewers have commented similarly). As points 1 and 4 assert, it just makes the game feel less fun because of how barebones the player's toolkit is. I've blitzed the game pretty quickly and I'm around 1/3 of the way through judging by online walkthroughs and I still feel weak. 
6. There is noticeable input lag on mouse and keyboard. Other reviews have commented on this too if you wish to see supporting evidence. This wouldn't be that big of an issue, except for the fact that an unnecessary death results in a lengthy run back to your corpse-shade. 

I understand that I am not the target audience for this type of game, as I am typically a top 1% FPS player, but that does not make me an unfair judge. I'm not a metroidvania fan or a platformer fan or a roguelike fan or a Soulslike fan, but I still greatly appreciated Ori and Dark Souls. Ori is more polished, more fluid, more approachable, and more fun. For most gamers, I think that the issues I identified above will make Hollow Knight more frustrating than enjoyable.

It's next to impossible to definitively pick a favorite game, taking into account every possible factor.
But this would be a VERY worthy contender for me. Even if it's not #1, it's impossibly close.

I was recommended this game by a friend, and so I tried it. I did not enjoy this game. 
I have put almost 40 hours into it, and I still haven't reached maximum completion (112% ?). I stopped playing due to how much time I had put into it, and just how frustratingly difficult portions of the game are. 

Don't get me wrong though, not every aspect of the game is bad. The art style and music is amazing, the game play however, I cannot get behind.

Some of the platforming can feel very unfair, and you'll probably spend a long time painfully trudging through different areas. 

The combat can feel awkward at times, and having the *same* enemies spawn in the *same* spot in *every* room is very annoying. 

New enemies typically aren't introduced well, they're usually just thrown at you without any way to know what you're facing, leading to deaths most of the time.

All enemies usually have some kind of gimmick to them, which combined with the level design, makes nearly all areas an absolute pain to deal with, due to projectile spam or a crap ton of enemies rushing at you in different styles.

The bosses aren't usually well balanced, you either beat them first try or you get stuck on them for hours. That, and most of the difficult bosses require you to beat them in order to make any progress, due to most bosses giving you something to explore more areas.

Exploring can be either calming or very annoying, depending on the areas gimmick. Some areas force you to platform, some just require you just kill enemies, some you get to learn a new ability in. Something else about exploring, it just feels aimless; I have no idea where I'm going most of the time, and in order to see your own position on the map you have to pay for an item and keep it equipped with a system that has limited slots.

There's no difficulty settings, everyone plays the same game here. If you aren't so good at fast paced combat or are looking for a casual game, this game probably wont be for you.

This game isn't very expensive and has recieved free expansion updates. The story is very confusing and uninteresting IMO, but thankfully you don't need to worry about it too much in order to play the game. 

Overall, I just dislike the gameplay loop and level design. If this is considered one of the best games of its type, I'm scared to try whats thought to be below it.

This probably wont be a popular opinion but whatever. I LOVED this game. The art is phenomenal, the story is superb, the platforming was pretty straight forward, and its absolutely gorgeous. Like the final Boss took me about two tries to beat in under 5 minutes tops. So if that was the only thing wrong with it, why would I not recommend it? Because to get a final boss fight worth it, you gotta go through some arbitrarily hard jump puzzles for what seems like an asinine amount if time to get a final boss fight that's even worth playing the game in the first place. It feels like a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ EA pay wall where you gotta pay this jump puzzle skill check tax to actually fight something that's worth playing the game for, and if I would have known beforehand that I would have had to do it, I probably wouldn't have even played it. This isn't really a gitgud moment either like the DS series, its just some wonky ass jump puzzles that contribute absolutely nothing to the game or the lore just to even fight a boss worth fighting. I would give everything up until the final boss a 9.5/10 but fighting the final boss is actually easier than the first boss encounter in the game and terminally anticlimactic. Its a shame because its a REALLY good game but what the ♥♥♥♥ was that ending. If this is your first platformer, everything you're going to get out of the game will be before the final boss encounter. The final boss is about as hard as stealing a half eaten sandwich out of your drunk Uncle Tim's fridge as hes passed out on his Lay-Z Boy. 

tl;dr: Make your way through an amazing and beautiful game just to get a 2nd month's game design intern's rendition of a final boss. Then you gotta pay the "I've been playing platformers all my life" Skill check jump puzzle tax to even get a boss worth buying the game during the winter sale.

The game is well made but suffers from some flaws in the game design. The lack of being able to save the game means that a lot of time will be spent backtracking which makes the game play tedious and boring for too much of the time. People thus claim that the game is difficult but most of the difficulty is artificial, it just takes longer time to finish the bosses than in other games due to the backtracking, with a normal save system this game would not be harder than usual games. The overall gameplay is in general easy. 

Backtracking is a part of the metroidvania genre, but this game does it bad making you trod through easy but boring section for no reason.

The combat is simplistic yet requires skill, which I would say is the game's biggest positive.
This doesnt really make up for all the negatives though. The bosses are far too "on-rails". They do the same pattern repeatedly. I think I got through the first 4-5 bosses before I even died to one of them. The difficulty can be a matter of preference though so take that for what you will. The exploration however is terrible all around, which is a cardinal sin for a metroidvania. Most important areas youll find early on will be locked behind "ability" walls, such as spawning platforms or double jumping, but the problem is that you get these abilities far later than when you run into these areas.
This makes it so that the vast majority of places you find have random dead-ends which makes exploration feel pointless and unnaturally gated.
The last problem I had was the lack of any kind of reason or guidance given to the player. You will not know who or why the little bugman you are controlling is doing what he is doing, nor will you have ANY idea of where you need to go next, and yes, i mean not even a single phrase or sentence as a clue. Absolutely unguided, random exploration is basically the first 70% of this game. Some people may enjoy that, but I just find it to be a waste of my time considering ill spend 2 hours looking for a boss only to defeat them after two tries within 10 minutes.
I would recommend getting it on sale if you don't care for spoilers and plan on using a guide or if you have far more patience than me.

I like to play games that make me feel empowered. The combat in Hollow Knight is great at this. It's simple to play, with one swing of your weapon in any direction and some spells being your only means of attacking. Yet it's hard to master because of the enemy variety. Their attacks are quick and frequent, yet obvious, making combat based entirely on reflexes, not luck. You feel badass when you play well. 

Yet Hollow Knight's map system is designed to leave maps feeling obscure or incomplete. You have to find the map to a new area hidden inside of it before your minimap displays anything. Even then, new rooms you explore in that area won't update on the map until you visit a save point. This is designed to make you feel lost on purpose; you're a small life in a dangerous, mysterious world. You're isolated, weak, and struggling for survival.

I think that has a certain appeal for others. For me, though, getting lost is the opposite of feeling empowered. This means Hollow Knight is really fun when I find a new area, find the map right away, and can make progress towards the boss while enjoying the combat. But those stars have aligned maybe twice in five areas. I wound up quitting.

For those who want the isolated atmosphere of being alone on an alien planet in Metroid, this is your game. For those who want the badass atmosphere of being a vampire killer in Castlevania, this isn't your game. I am the latter.

To my unfortunate surprise, I really disliked the game, I've kept coming back to it everyday but couldn't spend enough time on it. I was coming back after forcing myself to inhale hopium.
The movement is wanky and unsatisfying as it feels you are moving a cube across the screen with the keyboard buttons without any weight or physics. 
The combat is basically just hit and fall back then hit and fall back. (I've not gotten to meet enough bosses in the game to figure out if that's the case for all of them, but) from what I've seen the all the enemies are pretty easy to get beyond with just hitting and evading in a very repetitive pattern that is not satisfying because of the lack of weight to the character and because of the ease that prevails on these encounters. Like I wasn't on the edge of my seat fighting any of the enemies in the game.
The story seems to be non-existent up till now. Just a bunch of npcs that sell you something or the other (a shit load of markers for the map, or the map itself) and it just felt so slow and boring of a game.
That being said, I have full understanding that this is a widely highly regarded game and people just love it. I was hoping that I would enjoy it too but it didn't work out for me. Maybe you will enjoy it though. PS: I mostly enjoy fast paced games. so if you are anything like me I'd say skip this one

Where to even begin with this masterpiece of a game? Hollow Knight at a glance might seem like just another platformer with metroidvania elements, but after finishing this game you'll wish you could experience it for the first time again, because it's like reading an amazing book. It's an experience, it stays with you for the rest of your life and you come back to it over and over. Not to mention it offers so much content you'll easily put in 40-60 hours to play through the full game. All that for only 15$!

This game just does everything right. The movement and combat are smooth and responsive, and to make sure you utilize these features, the game throws a massive range of different enemies, and some very challenging bosses, and platforming challenges at you. The story is fantastic, albeit (intentionally) obscure and very dark, the music is phenomenal and I saved it on Spotify the same day I played the game for the first time.

By far the best part of Hollow Knight is the freedom of choice. This game is non-linear and the route you take to arrive at the end can vary from other players by a huge margin. There are very different combat playstyles to choose from, whether you prefer to fight through spells or just hack away at the enemies with your Nail (sword), whether you prefer to play defensively or more of a glass cannon, or whether you want an army of pets to do damage for you.

And this freedom compliments another great part of this game: exploration. The map, for the most part, only displays paths that you've already traversed, which really helps build the feeling that you are exploring a living, breathing world for the first time. There's a certain magic to games that excite your curiosity through mystery and make you feel like an explorer in a new world, and this game does that just right.

I've played way too many games in my life and over time of being exposed to similar games over and over, you tend to get cynical and desensitized and it becomes hard to be impressed by new games. But Hollow Knight made me feel like a kid again. I couldn't stop thinking about it, I recommended it to all my friends and I'm recommending it to you as well.

Easily game of the year 2017, go fu(c)king buy it.

Metroid-Vania meets darksouls. I'd describe this game as a "Souls-Vania" because you have no gun. And the upgrade path is waaaay less linear than that of a Metroid game. 
Pros: open ended exploration by the bucket load. Simple, yet deep gameplay mechanics that require you to measure every move, analize your environment and think on your feet. TONS of bang for your buck here. The map is gigantic, the enemies very varied, the music and voice acting are top notch. This all helps to create a solid backdrop for the story you're playing. They story is delivered with enough subtlety for you to invest however much you want in it. If you're more gameplay oriented, then you can breeze through the story (which I do not recommend); but if you're a completist, you can submerge yourself and get lost in this haunting lore the developers have so intelligently laid out in all kinds of forms 
Cons: open exploration might be too extreme, as it can be tricky, to know where to go next. You might spend hours just going back and forth the huge map. The hero walks rather slowly "a-la-Alucard" so thighs might be frustrating until you get the dash upgrade. There are several ways to progress, so there is no clear upgrade tree after a certain point. And some people might find annoying that for most of the game your one and only weapon feels pretty weak.
Overall I'd say Hollow Knight is one of the greatest 2d games to have ever existed. You either love it or you hate it. But you have to give it a chance.

For now, I cannot recommend this game to any person who is looking for a fun (since that's what I've noticed is lacking in a large portion of my playtime of this game) souls-like experience.

I'll start with the good: 
The sound design. Very well composed and simply enjoyable. I never felt truly alone in my time in Hallownest, thanks to the palpable dread created by the ambience in some of the more dangerous early areas. I applaud whoever was in charge of that portion of the experience. 
Art style. Simple and succinct at times, detailed and elaborate at others.  It conveys both the simplicity of your mission and your physically insignificant position in this oppressive realm. A perfect fit for a rotting world and its facets.
Pacing of combat. There is no game I have ever played that is quite as well paced as Hollow Knight is. It's exactly what I was looking for in my next souls game. I might even like it more than Sekiro's, and that clause alone proves that my relationship with this game isn't one of total hatred.

All that said, this game suffers from two major flaws that can quite literally not be ignored.
I think the best way to put this is the following: It plays like a 2011 flash game. In short, the main character, who I shall henceforth call the 'knight', doesn't give two shits about what you want him to do. I played this game on an Xbox controller, with default keybinds. Nonetheless, I said to the knight 'face the right way god dammit' more times than a toxic Dead by Daylight Nea does whilst fishing for flashlight saves. I found myself blaming the game for what in most cases was my own, unrealised failure, which is not a healthy relationship to have with any game, or anything you have to rely on at all, really. The way I would describe the way you have to play this game (and play around its flawed control system and platforming mechanics) is a wide berth. You have to take into account every single idiosyncrasy of this game's controls, and that simply doesn't sit well with my playstyle, and more importantly, the flow of the game and it's fast paced combat. You NEED to have your inputs actually register and matter in games like this, and Hollow Knight suffers from a plague of unresponsive movement. I've died more times to spikes and pits of piss in this game than I have to an actual enemy's weapon. That reminds me; the hitboxes are good, no problems there, if you learn an enemy's moveset, and your positioning is good (and the game doesn't cuck you) then you can actually exercise skill for once. Like you can in that kind of situation in most bossfights. Which let me remind you, are the highlights of the game.

My second problem isn't even really a problem to do with game design, it's a product of my own entitlement from Dark souls 3 and Sekiro. It's the absence of fast travel between checkpoints, which there are not many of anyway. I'm not even gonna complain about this one, but it's good to include as much information about the game as possible, even if just to warrant a negative review. Oftentimes, the level design of Hollow Knight will compliment the requirement to use your legs, however the janky movement of the game can make traversing these environments quite a chore. It's a me problem, in short, as is every problem I have with this game, I feel.

Overall, this game does everything right, however I feel like it is being held back by the movement and how the player traverses the world. On a scale of playing Stardew Valley to listening to Justin Bieber, this game requires about as much patience as sitting in a room for 15 minutes, once a day, for the rest of your life; meaning it gets more annoying as you realise the pointlessness of getting annoyed at it.

I'll keep the comments on for this review, and I'll be sure to update it to a positive one once I've had my mind controlled into enjoying the game by the fans of the game on Reddit. I want to enjoy this game, but so far, I can't ignore the fact that the movement is this janky. I'm sure I'll learn to love it, one day.

I'll start by saying that this is one of those reviews where I have enjoyed the game, but I don't recommend it on the basis that it's also causing me a lot of irritation. A lot. A huge amount.

This game feels like 5% Pikimin, 20% limbo, 75% bad Castlevania. I love all of these games, but Hollow Knight seems to bungle it all up in a bad way.

Strengths;
Great mood and atmosphere. Characters are light but intriguing. Visual content is extremely good; consistent, immersive and interesting. 

Weaknesses;
~ A bad walking simulator. The environments aren't interesting enough to justify making the player walk through them over and over and over. This is compounded by the next two issues.
~ Poor layout. Whilst the general assembly of the map is great, it has some significant flaws. Firstly is something castlevania worked out a long time ago. Bosses are tough. You can't just beat a boss knowing nothing about it, you usually have to try a few times first. This is why respawn points were often very close to boss battles in Castlevania. In Hollow knight, no. You have to walk for 2+ minutes almost everytime.
~ Poor Layout. Levers that unlock entire areas can sometimes fairly easily be missed due to being tucked away, so even when you're in the right area you can miss them. The result is sometimes 10+ having to head the wrong way, realise the mistake, head back and hit the level (in this case through the entire city of tears to get out).
~ Poor Layout. I'd wager at least 40% of my deaths have been from falling into acid or spikes I couldn't possibly have seen beneath the screen. Sometimes there are visual clues but the general position of platforms doesn't seem to be very considerate towards potential long distance falls into hazards. This is compounded by my next issue.
~ Bad platforming. It's bad. I've had so many issues with this. I should be able to enjoy a game with as few teething problems as possible. Hollow Knight doesn't seem to have any of the UI leeway even games as old as early mario games had. Everything seems to be exact at the moment of input, so it's VERY easy to misjudge timing on a jump and fall of a ledge whilst trying to move around quickly, which you'll probably want to, because this game makes you travel a lot.
~ some enemies are fantastic, but a lot are kind of boring an repetitive. There doesn't seem to be many unique mechanics or environmental interactions for enemies, which would've been nice.

Not a bad game at all, I'd give it 6.8 overall. Castlevania's (which is over a decade old) combat and platforming is about 40% better whilst Hollow Knight nails atmosphere and setting about 20% better. But for me it's far too irritating and wastes far too much of the player's time, and hasn't done a great job of learning from the games that came before it.

Fantastic art design, tight controls, and great music.

It lasts about 15 minutes...

You ever watch a movie that had an amazing intro, style and sound design and were all impressed, then proceeded to pad the next 70 minutes with meandering nothingness until the epic conclusion at the 90 minute mark?  Congratulations.  That film is the cinematic equivalent of the Video Game Hollow Knight. 

This game does everything in its power to waste your time.  They REALLY wanted to say that they have a game with a long runtime, and they were more than content to sacrifice enjoyment and quality to do it.

It is painfully obvious this game wanted to be Dark Souls, only with 45% of the run-time being backtracking and staring at a poorly made and not-descriptive map.  And I don't mean running back to your body, or running back to a boss arena to try a difficult fight again,  I mean literally going back many many many screens because the fast travel system is highly flawed, and you finally found a key, or a double jump, or a wall jump, etc. that might or might not allow you to access something literally 76 screen ago.  There are a few shortcuts that the devs mercifully added for this reason (where they bothered) but these account for likely less than 3% of the rooms and are generally located in places you don't have to backtrack through anyway.  Also, if you die in dark souls, weather you collect your souls/body or not, you are not permanently gimped until you do, like you are in Hollow Knight.  Sure, you can use a rather rare item to summon your body back to a location if 1, you know about how to do it and 2, have unlocked it, and 3 have one of said rare items of which there are a fixed supply... 

I'm okay with difficult games, im even fine with unfair odds being stacked against you, gotcha-deaths, etc. being someone that has played through every Soulsbourne title and beaten them all.  There was a feeling or reward or accomplishment with those.  With Hollow Knight its just... yup... you did that... now move on... 

Seriously, this game is overly stingy with its quality of life elements, down to even what the very few fast-travel points are even called, forcing you to warp to a number of areas incorrectly before you get to the right one.  If I am going to the ONLY warp point in Map X, WHY NOT CALL IT 'MAP X'. Why does it have to be called "Forbidden Genome" or something equally inaccurate?  The more I play it the more it feels like it is just wasting your time, like it's goal it to try to rack up hours of time played, instead of hours of time ENJOYED.  

Why can you not even begin piecing your own map together in areas that you've explored until you randomly stumble upon a mapmaker, who the devs also seem to have placed in the most random and impossible to reach locations?  Also, you cant tell where you are without using active trinket space for your compass.  Are.  You.  Kidding.  Me?  

It's at about this point where you also start realizing that the map is huge... which can be awesome... but this is NEEDLESSLY huge.  As in huge just for the sake of getting you lost, and without a map or compass or any idea how to get back.  Add in difficult platforming that you have to do over and over and over and over again and then start also realizing that there really isn't anything to gain from these maps.  They're empty.   You only feel like you can BEGIN exploring an area AFTER you've found the Map Maker, BOUGHT an INCOMPLETE map from him, AND found a checkpoint so you can fast travel back should you need to leave and come back.  Until then, you will spend hours on end going back and forth through nearly identical rooms, sometimes dying, sometimes not, and after a while you realize you've barely gained any spendable currency, there's no leveling system so you haven't gained any XP to be applied toward a level or unlocking a spell/kill/ability, etc... You just wasted a crap load of time!  This is the first game in over a decade that by the end of my play-though just started googling the Map Maker and Checkpoint locations when I stumbled upon a new area, because I was just sick of aimless wondering and wasting my time.

I don't know if I want to call it a "Rip-Off" or "Homage" to hear the map maker hum the 2nd stage music From Super Ghouls and Ghosts.  Knowing this team, I think I'll decide on "Rip-Off" seeing how they basically ripped off just about every other element of this game from other actually good games.   They also ripped off the Dark Souls storytelling style of not really telling a story at all.  

This title had amazing potential.  It pissed all of it away for needless time consuming padding and some very dubious game design choices.  

I can recommend this game as much as I can recommend the Film Empire State, by Andy Warhol, the 8 hour and 5 minute film that is literally a single slow motion shot of the Empire State Building.  It's a film in that its on a screen, has visuals and is watchable... technically... this is the Video Game Equivalent to that, only Empire State wasn't ripped off from anyone else, and they called an Art Project, while Hollow Knight... made specifically for your money.  Spend it and your time elsewhere.

Hollow Knight doesn't do a whole lot to shake up the formula.

What it does do, however, is take the broken pieces of older games in the genre and piece them together into a masterfully done game. Great characterization, shortcut porn galore, satisfying combat, and a story that isn't shoved into your face but will make you care anyways.

I'm sad I didn't discover this game sooner but, having not even completed it after this many hours, I feel like I have a lot of content on the horizon to play through. $15 is a steal for this kind of experience.

(Side note: If you have arachnophobia, this game may be a pass on your end. Not because of the cartoony spiders, but...).
Frustrating platforming, extremely frustrating boss for Ending 3. I'd rather replay all of Dark Souls and Elden Ring at the same time.

In my opinion, a masterpiece. I'm no expert when it comes to games of this genre, but Hollow Knight has everything I look for in a game, and I can't think of any flaws of things I disliked.

I love the art style. The graphics are great, and the game feels smooth, which I guess has to do with the animations and framerate. So visually, it's fantastic. The music is beautiful, and together with the art style and the story (i.e. dialogue and interactions with other npcs) creates an amazing atmosphere. The atmosphere is kind of haunting and sorrowful, but your character is kind of like the light of hope in this dark, dying world.

Gameplay and combat is great. As I already said the game feels smooth and fast, and the action is good, it's fun and tense, and I like the movement (fast and fluid). I'm a fan of any game that has a way for your character to become stronger as the game progresses, through items, equipment, skills, levelling, etc. Hollow Knight has that, and it's well done. And I still haven't even finished the game (due to my poor attention span and lack of time - I tend to play games a little bit at a time)!

Lastly, I want to mention Team Cherry. They are the only people I know of that have released a game at the level of quality as Hollow Knight, at the price of Hollow Knight, and continue to release dlc of the same quality FOR FREE! I still can't believe it. Team Cherry, you have made a fan out of me, and I intend to continue to support you as long as you keep putting out this level of quality.

Best game I've played in a while!
Also the best metroidvania-style game I've ever played (I don't really see Ori and the Blind Forest in that category).
Yes, like in all those games there's a lot of backtracking, visiting areas you've been before. But in this game it's not a bad thing for two reasons: 1. The countless different enemy-types and their attacks are so well made and the controls are so spot-on that fighting is actually fun instead of kinda annoying (looking at you, Metroid) and 2. you never really hit a dead-end. Or rather, if you do, there's always several more spots on your map where you can obviously get further. Also, the new skills you get over time plus well- but (almost) never ridiculously well-hidden secrets always make coming to old areas rewarding (also a nice game for completionists).
It also helps that the drawn graphics are just gorgeous, the game world very atmospheric, the soundtrack beautiful and the sound effects perfect. Speaking of sound effects, the voice acting is some of the best and funniest ever (Divine!) :D

The story and lore is (like some other aspects of the game) inspired by Dark Souls, in that it doesn't throw the story at your feet. Most is told by fragments of the things the NPCs say, by the environment, the atmosphere, etc. and even by the names and looks of bosses. Altogether it's an atmospheric puzzle that forms a story and world much broader than you'd think (it's worth watching one of those longer story and lore videos on youtube after you've completed the game; seriously).

The game is hard, but it's really not as hard as some on the net are saying. It's not in a category with Dark Souls.
Yes, you'll die. But not a lot. Some bosses kill you once or twice. That makes it challenging and keeps you on your feet.
There's a few bosses which are much tougher, but they're rather optional.
There's one place which you'll have to get through if you want the "real" ending in the game. And for many, that place will be extremely frustrating. Especially those who haven't played games like Super Meat Boy or The End Is Nigh, because this is some tough-as-nails-platforming. For me, I died a few times, but it wasn't too frustrating.
The only place that's f***king too difficult even for me is the last pantheon in Godhome which is a boss run full of every (?) boss in the game, even the toughest ones. That one's just not fun, you'd have to train some bosses for hours before even attempting this. The game developers should at least make check points so you don't have to do ALL of it again every time you die there. However, this place is more or less not part of the main game anymore; it's after-game-stuff, so it's ok.

Apart from that last part being too difficult, I can't really find any real flaws in this game. Get it for whatever price, it's worth it! (it's part of the Humble Monthly Bundle this month, btw.)

On the metroidvania spectrum, this game tends more towards Castlevania than Metroid, not only because of the emphasis on melee combat. Item collection, exploration, and upgrading feels more like the former than the latter. The game also features collectable equipabbles to customize your gameplay, a staple of the Castlevania games. Most of the "metroid" is in the platforming.

Tons and tons of content, many hours. Exploration feels properly rewarding. Hollow Knight can be a very long game, but it has excellent speedrun potential. The controls are very tight, responsive, and intuitive (Best played with a controller). As for plot, its as you can expect for a metroidvania, with some storytelling styles taken from the SoulsBourne franchises. Art direction is fantastic (some of the best I've seen, in the league of Mirror's Edge and Rayman Legends), it astounds me that everything in this game was hand drawn. Ambiance is fantastic, capturing the eerily beautiful nature of the game really well. Soundtrack serves the game well, but its not super memorable; pass on getting the OST. Sound effects on the other hand, hoh boy, I can still hear the satisfying sound of bugs exploding in my head. The game is well optimized; my PC build is about 7 years old, and I never dropped below 60 frames.


The game isn't perfect. Now bare with me here, I'm reaaaally cherry picking, but the paragraphs below this can be summed up as "the free DLC is not as well designed as / is a breakaway from the base game."

There are several non-obvious mechanics, paths, lore, etc.. When I mean "not obvious" I mean, in all likelihood you'll need a guide to find them, instead of just exploring normally / casually. The guides are fine, and this game plays well with them, but some paths or quest lines break the mold that the game uses to hide secrets. [spoiler]For example, activating the Grimm Troop DLC questline requires some arcane order of tasks. Another example is talking to the mushrooms requiring equiping a certain item. Another example is that some DLC hidden paths from breakable walls are not indicated by the typical cracked wall.[/spoiler] Because of the DLC, some of the backtracking can get pretty insane, and its not particularly fun to revisit past areas and [spoiler]refight past bosses with just more health and slightly altered attack patterns.[/spoiler] A few of the DLC bosses just aren't fun to fight because they're geared for endgame.

I also don't appreciate how a few of the achievements require multiple runs on an already long game. If you want to get them, either replay several hours of content or save scum. 

The game is not completely bug free (pun intended), I managed to break a questline and some entities got stuck while platforming (including myself), but these were countable on one hand over about 50 hours worth of gameplay time. Fortunately, the game does come with its own save backup system if you really need it.

Most of the time you fight boring enemies, that die too slowly and require you to wait for right time to strike. That is bad because when you die in some interesting scenario (for instance during boss fight) you have to go through those boring weak enemies to get back to the good part. This is not difficult, it is punishing and boring. At beginning you also don't have any interesting move options that would make sprinting through map easier and faster.

The money (geo) system also does not make sense to me. At the beginning I had to buy map elements (like buy upgrade to map that makes me see on the map check point), which makes no sense. Why stop there, why not make me buy upgrade that lets me see how much health I have at the moment? Why not make me grind for every game element?

Every time you die you have to go back to place of your death to recover money that you had when you died. If you don't feel like going through the same boring places for the third or fourth time wright now you lose your money. This has led me to situations in which I hated getting money because I knew it will led me to repeating this vicious circle. And at the point of my rage quit I didn't even had place to spend the money I had (I already bought all map upgrades) so game asked me to grind for some abstract number.

Also developers didn't follow basic rule that the most efficient tactic should be also the most rewarding/fun. Examples that I encountered: you have to lose one charm slot to see yourself on the map. So in later game you will have to choose weather to know where you are on map (fun tactic) or have that slot for something more useful (efficient tactic).
Also first charm I got is attack spell that uses the same energy that my healing uses. So now I can either kill boring enemy quickly with spell (fun tactic) or spam them with my basic attack (nail), regenerate energy and therefore
later heal myself (efficient tactic).

Difficult to play this without making comparisons to the original Metroid games which this game is based on.

The first few hours of Hollow Knight are monotonous and dull slogs through endlessly similar looking dark caves with ruins. Metroid has much more variation in scenery, even between single rooms in the same environment, which makes exploration exciting and dynamic. Hollow Knight mostly has you transition from one dark ruin to another, over and over.

Inclusion of RPG elements like a shop and an equip system is also unwelcome in this genre. Badges can be bought and equipped for (mostly) uninteresting stat buffs which require you to pause the game to equip, breaking the flow of gameplay. Player progress is also often gated by finding keys or gathering money, which is a big no for the genre in my book. Metroid handles 'gatekeeping' far more elegantly by encouraging you to find new abilities (which is a fun task to do since your character is also becoming more powerful in the process). Nobody likes searching for keys.

The art is good in places, but almost everything is a bug wearing a white mask, or a run down looking city object (because all modern games must be set in a post-apocalyptic setting, apparently). Controls handle well, but close range combat feels iffy compared to shooting foes from afar, and isn't friendly to people with fat-fingers. 

Maybe Hollow Knight gets more varied and interesting as you get deeper into the dark ruined city, but 4 hours in and I was basically still exploring the same bit of samey cave, killing samey enemies (all bugs) to grind money for uninteresting items to help me find gate keys to progress. I ended up losing interest and stopped playing.

I suppose I was never going to compare Hollow Knight favourably to my hundreds of hours spent playing Super Metroid though. The latter is over 2 decades old and still unsurpassed in the genre to this day. It's just a shame that the developers of Hollow Knight failed to analyse the gameplay elements which made that game so successful.

A couple of years ago I played this game for a few hours and left a negative reviews, citing high difficulty and high cost of dying. Now that I returned and put into the game another 20-30 hours, I see that I was mostly wrong. I was just unlucky to get stuck in one of the few bottlenecks in the game.

Bosses in Hollow Knight are indeed relatively challenging, but most of the time you don't need to kill them right away. If you have troubles, you can go away, explore other areas and then return stronger and more experienced. This is not true only for the first two bosses that have to be done in order while exploring the first two areas. Unfortunately for me, during my first attempt at the game I got stuck on the second boss, Hornet, just before the whole Hollow Knight map opens to you.

The scale of the game is amazing. Multiple times I thought "surely I've explored the majority of the map", while in fact I saw less than a half of it. They have very different feel to them, but all of them are very non-linear, allowing the players to find many secrets and shortcuts.

One other complaint that I had when I played the game for the first time was the cost of dying.  Similarly to Souls games, when you die, you are leaving all your money with your corpse, and you have to fight back to your spirit to retrieve them. This is rarely an issue. In the normal exploration you die quite rarely, and it's usually relatively easy to retrace your steps. The only time it becomes frustrating is on some boss fights when you die several times in a row in the same place and have to fight your way back through the same path. Usually (though not always) you are not forced into the boss fight again by retrieving your corpse. And even when it happens that the corpse is stuck in a difficult location, there is an in-game NPC which can retrieve it for you.

This game...oi, where do I even begin laying down my thoughts?

I'll keep this nice and simple: Team Cherry, you guys have created something that, I think, is genuinely amazing. Old school feel, with some nice additions, make this game one worth playing out the whole way through. I died, a lot, I'm not ashamed to admit it, and I spent 21 hours exploring your game before I decided I was done and wanted to finish the game. And not a second of it, I felt, was a waste of my time.

The NPC's are all well done and have character to them, the story is dark and leaves so much to a player's interpretation, and the symbolism and implications all of the player actions are so well thought out. In addition, the hand-drawn style of graphics brings out the very best that this dark kind of game deserves, with painstaking work put in to creating something truly beautiful and worth ogling over every detail of.

And the gameplay is so buttery smooth! A bullet-hell style mechanic put into a Metroidvania to make for an incredibly difficult but rewarding combat scheme makes this one of the best playing Metroid-Castlevania style games that I've ever played, and I've played all the Metroid games, most of the Castlevania games, and I'm still exploring all kinds of Metroidvanias, so this is saying a lot, coming from me. The combat is a great balance of methodical and frantic, and punishes mistakes heavily while still letting the player experience the idea that the combat is still entirely possible through dedication and learning. Nothing hit me that I felt shouldn't have, most of my victories felt earned, and as a whole, this was a treat, rather than a chore, to experience.

The high difficulty peak might be a turnoff to some, however, as this game starts very hard. There are some moments of uneven difficulty balancing as well, things that I would expect to be infuriating battles are instead easy, or battles that are early are perhaps too difficult for a novice player or a player that hasn't experience the game to really enjoy. The feeling of powerlessness early on is also both a blessing and a curse, offering both a sense of real need to learn the world of Hollownest to survive, but since it takes so long to become empowered in any sense, it can feel like a "about time" rather than a feeling of wonder at more power.

The other thing is that this game is obtuse, and while that works so well for it, it also has so much that leaves a player feeling lost until the magic item is found. Other Metroidvanias have done this, of course, but for Hollow Knight to do it seems deliberate, and frustratingly so. That sense of "oh, I can go all these places now" sometimes was replaced with "Well, now I know how to get to this place I've been struggling for hours to get to". 

But if those are two flaws that I can find in this admittedly short review (by my standards), that's a game doing well for itself, considering there are tons of other flaws to be found in other games that I can't find here as easily. I feel like I could go all day if I wanted, but I wanted to hit the points that stuck out the most to me.

TL;DR Play this game, seriously. It's got a couple of flaws that might be glaring to some, but the overall experience is surprisingly lengthy and very well worth it. Give it a shot and see where you come out. Like it or hate it, but don't forget this experience, something that should be attested to as real quality and passion in a project.

A beautiful and immersive action-adventure platformer. It can get quite challenging but the difficulty curve ramps up gradually and the game does a good job of helping you improve with very little hand-holding. Critically, it never makes you grind to progress and it rarely feels tedious even when re-traversing the same areas. The characters are charming and the story, while always a background element, makes the world feel real and interesting.

I will say that I sometimes found Hollow Knight to be quite frustrating. Sometimes this was due to a particularly difficult platforming section or boss fight, which made me think "This is BS, how can they expect me to beat that?". I didn't mind those too much, because with some persistence and some strategic use of charms they can all be overcome and I ended up with a better mastery of the game because of it. However, there were other sources of frustration with no benefit at all. In particular, there are a lot of "gateway" sections in the game where you can not progress without a particular ability or item. There is nothing that tells you: 
a) That you are hard-gated for this section,
b) What you are gated by, or
c) Whether the thing you are gated by is obtainable or whether it itself is also gated by something else.

So on your first playthrough you are left to trial and error (or risking spoilers in the wiki) to figure this stuff out and it's hard to shake the feeling that maybe you just didn't try something right. I got stuck like this twice in my first playthrough, once where I was gated almost everywhere and hadn't discovered the remaining accessible sections yet, and didn't know where to look. The other was where I wasn't gated but thought I was, because I didn't figure out a particular interaction with the environment.

Don't let that turn you off of the game though. If you get completely stuck then it's worth risking spoilers and taking a look in the wiki. A few friction points will soon be forgotten as the overall experience of this game is very enjoyable.

I'd also say it would be very hard if you have a condition that limits your ability (eg. reduced motor function in your hands).

Below a certain threshold of skill, it's basically impossible to build on your progress and advance in the storyline. You earn a currency whenever you kill a monster, but you will lose everything you've earned so far in the game if you die, and you die very quickly (each hit from an enemy costs you a "heart").

Sadly, I kept on playing in hopes instead of refunding in the hopes that I would improve and be able to access more of the game, but it looks like I'm stuck at the very beginning constantly dying and reviving. It's a nice game, and the quality is definitely worth the price for those who can advance in it, but it's completely unplayable for me. I'm only leaving a negative review so people can find it.

TLDR: This game is a masterpiece. Get it.

Hollow Knight drops you, as the titular Knight, into the vast, dying kingdom of Hallownest with only the nail on your back and vague directions to descend through a well into the dangerous ruins below.

At first sight and sound you know something is wrong here. The art style and atmosphere of this game seems an impossible achievement in a 2D game, but Team Cherry pulled it off in spades. From the bumbling Fungal Wastes to the unsettling drone of the Abyss, Christopher Larkin's score is nothing short of epic, with memorable themes that suit the respective area and boss music that makes you sit up and grip your controller tighter.

I can't decide whether combat or exploration is more fun. Both are so tightly tuned that, once I got accustomed to the controls, I never doubted the precision of my attacks or movement. With few exceptions, damage and death will be your fault and rarely feels unfair. Exploration is always rewarding, whether you're finding imprisoned grubs, mask or soul shards, new abilities or hidden bosses. 

Hallownest is enormous. You will get lost. You will die. You will get frustrated. You will probably stumble across the first boss by accident like I did, and proceed to get even more lost once you get past that ability gate and the world expands some more. Which it keeps doing. Even when you think you've explored every inch, your map (both mental and in-game) is complete, there will be more breakable walls, more secret areas, just... more. This game feels endless. Yes, this can be overwhelming, and backtracking can be time-consuming, but the seamless fluidity of movement, especially once you aquire additional abilities, helps keep this engaging.

A lot of gameplay revolves around the standard metroidvania fare: along the way you'll pick up shards and vessels to increase your health and soul, new or upgraded abilities, charms to modify your abilities, and a sprinkling of ability-gated areas. It should be noted that even the ability gating is thoughtfully designed and you can complete the game with only one or two required (excluding a particular boss's requirements). 

As I mentioned, combat is fast, fluid, and precise. It's a good thing too, because there's 100+ regular enemies, all beautifully drawn and animated, each with unique behaviour. With all the DLC, there's 20+ bosses, most of which have a more difficult dream version for extra challenge. With a few exceptions, all the bosses feel well designed, presenting a challenge without being unfair. Each enemy suits its environment and each boss adds a bit more to the story.

And speaking of... the story of Hallownest is vague, engrossing, and fed to you crumb by tragic crumb. The Knight was called back to Hallownest and it's up to you to discover why. The fate of this crumbling kingdom rests with you. Will you make the same sacrifices as your predecessors and maintain the world or face the source of the infection and break the cycle?

I cannot say enough good about this game. At this point it's the only game I've 100%'ed. If you like tightly tuned metroidvania games, if you like consumer-friendly practices and developers that treat their fans with respect and gratitude, get this game. It deserves a much higher price point for the content and polish, and I can't wait to see the Hornet DLC and whatever else Team Cherry comes up with.

This is one of those things where on paper this should be a game I absolutely love, but in reality it fell short for me. I absolutely think this is a well-crafted game by most objective standards. The mood is wonderful, the music is good, the art is good, and the controls are tight and responsive. However, I simply grew tired of it and eventually didn't care. The combat is often praised but I found surprisingly unfluid. Just felt like I was poking or swiping at things without a real sense of rhythm or momentum. Just poke, wait, poke, wait, poke, wait. Didn't ever really feel like I was doing something genuinely cool or exciting. Just poke and wait for an opportunity to poke again.

It's a Metroidvania but I felt the backtracking was really arduous. It just got tiring at some point. That and the really annoying game design decision to put save points far away from the start of bosses to artificially increase the challenge. It doesn't increase the challenge, it just increases the annoyance. I hate this in other games as well. You will die a lot to some bosses -- that's part of the game -- but getting teleported back far away just gets so irritating. I would rather have a more difficult boss but shorter trek, if it came down to that kind of game balancing. Beating difficult bosses is rewarding, of course, but at some point I just didn't care to see it through to the end.

All in all, it's a well-made game and I don't have any trouble seeing why it's beloved, but it just didn't do it for me.

Pros:
- The art, phenomenal. Simplistic, beautiful, inspired me to carry on with my art.
- The music, especially Greenpath and City of tears, fantastic. 
- The gameplay itself is wonderful, immersive and incredible.
- I adore the charm system. I do wish for a few more alternatives to increase speciality. I managed to get them all. I like gathering stuff like that.
- The placement of save points are ok by me, The deeper you go, the less you have, makes perfect sense. Deepnest is a shithole though, and I still don't understand the point of the Mask guy haha. 
- I liked the money system (Geo from kills, mines, bosses, and Grubfather). I lost 3k at one point and that pissed me off, but I shrugged it off and carried on. My shade got stuck behind one of the bosses, and I lost my Geo, when I couldn't get it back, oh well. 
- The general feel of the game is really nice and well designed, animation is excellent and fluid. 
- I loved the boss battles, especially the weird creepy stuff they did, like Soul Master, Collector and Nosk. I got to Flukemarm before I realised that upgrading the Nail past 2 to maximum was extremely important. 

Cons: 
- In the beginning, it was a where the fuck do I go game. 
- It took me a long time to unlock the map to Fog Canyon and some of the switches I needed help from guides to find. 
example. - The Queens Gardens has a lot of blocked off walls, so I had to use guides. 
- I want more mana (yeah I know it's soul) for spells. Having that attached to my healing was challenging. 
- The white knight challenge zone was all spinning blades and spikes and shit, it felt like super meatboy, hated that. Although I got a really nice charm using the Hive charm and some of the Lifeblood charms. Just waited to regen. 

Other stuff:
- I've read some of the reviews posted by other people and they've gone to town with all the negatives. Hollow Knight is what it is, and it's really really good. You can't have all powers like a god at the end. That'd be overkill. 
- I have maybe 3 important things to do before I feel this game is fully complete. I want to kill Grimm, Radiance and the final trial. I love the arena and trials. I want different versions. Like beast arenas or something. 
- One of the reviews mentioned overpowered monsters. In the beginning maybe yeah, but when you get Nail 3 or 4 you can clean up easily. With a few charms you can have a nice killing and exploration "build".
- Yeah the map location charm is bollocks. That should be a default ability or something.
- I wish for more 'endless' content for the NPC's like farming certain materials for potions or something. 
- The flower quest was nice, I want to do more of them challenges. Didn't feel like 1 delivery quest was enough.
Too much backtracking, very little reward and progression.

This game is an absolute steaming pile of shite!


What makes it worse, is that for the first 2-3 hours it showed a lot of promise. It was challenging sure, but felt fair and well paced.

But oh boy, it soon turns into a festering pile of donkey turd.

The levels are a confusing labyrinth, and in their infinite wisdom, the devs thought it would be a good idea to make it so that you have to use up a valuable charm slot just to actually be able to see your position on the map!
Why?? This isn’t fun, it just adds a level of frustration, for absolutely no reason.
I haven’t got a problem with having to find the maps for each level, and actually enjoyed hunting around to find the nooks and crannies where they were hidden.  But needing to use a charm to actually show where you are in the map is ridiculous!

And what’s the harm in actually pausing the game whilst you are checking the map?
It’s something that you need to do a lot in this game, so just pause the damn action, so the user can actually get 5 mins to get their bearings.
Just another example of adding in frustration for the sake of it.


The platforming is god awful too.
Many times I found myself loosing all of my lives, just to try to get from one side of the screen to the other.
In most other platforms, if you landed on a spike, or fell into the water, it would start you on the last ledge that you reached, or maybe a couple of ledges back at worst.
But no, this game makes you go all the way back to pretty much the start of the screen, just so you can try and fail again and again.

There are also times when you have to drop down into the screen below, just to land on a spike or in some acid etc. all because you have no way of knowing where you are going to land when you step off of the ledge.

There are another sections where you have to use a pogo stick style attack to bounce from one enemy to another, but you have no idea where the next enemy is, as they are off screen, so you end up missing and falling onto the spikes below, again and again and again.

There isn’t enough difference between the background and the foreground in some area, so you end up trying to jump on a ledge that doesn’t actually exist and is just part of the background.


I’m sick to death of this trend of just making games difficult and frustrating, to extend their play time, rather than actually putting in the effort to come up with a good well paced and thought out game.

I’m all for a challenge, but it needs to be a fair challenge, not just difficulty for difficulties sake.

I even modded the game to give myself invincibility for these annoying platform sections, but still turned it off because it’s soo frustrating to play.

It’s like the devs played other games that used these ideas, but didn’t actually understand what made the ideas good, so in this game, they ultimately fail.

Save your money, I wouldn’t even buy this game on sale.

This is my only negative review of a game. (so far..)

And the problem is I like this game! The story and atmosphere is awesome, and I love exploring it. But most of the time it's like the game doesn't want me to have fun, whenever I've gotten the hang of the weird controls and am actually starting to enjoy exploring the beautiful world, it'll throw some over-powered boss or an infuriatingly designed platforming section at me. 

Something that makes fighting bosses really difficult is that there is no health bar. I get why the devs did this, it certainly fits the 'style' of the game. But it also means I have no idea how close I am to defeating them. Are they 1 hit away, or 10? There isn't really any way to know.

I see that most people like a challenge. And so this is presumably a plus for them, but personally I don't enjoy slamming my head against a brick wall in the vague hope that it'll maybe break. And the worst part is that the game forces me into situations where I basically have no choice. I HAVE to hit my head against that wall.
Basically, when you enter a boss room, all exits will be covered. So you can only escape after defeating them, okay. Whats the problem? The problem is that when you die you spawn a 'shade' who you must kill to get your money back. What this means is that when you die in a boss room and go back to get your money back, you not only have to kill your shade. But also the boss! This results in what I call a 'death-loop' where I am constantly killing the shade, getting my stuff back, getting killed by the boss, Returning so I don't lose all my money. Repeat.
..Which isn't very fun

Basically every time I close the game is in a rage-quit, and then a couple days later I'll try it again. Start having fun, game'll say NO. Repeat.

So, for now at least. I don't recommend it.

The unreasonable difficulty of an oldschool Nintendo platformer from the "20 hours endlessly retrying the same part is 20 hours more entertainment for your money" era, combined with the unreasonable difficulty of a Soulslike game - drop all your money on death, with the possibility of it being lost permanently if you don't find it again.

But wait, there's more! The game takes place in an enormous maze made up entirely of slightly smaller mazes, and wholly expects you to memorize every single nook, cranny and meandering route from point A through mandatory points B-F until you get to point G, and that's just describing a very short walk from anywhere in the game to somewhere nearby.
You also generally have no clue where to go, as the game does the Metroidvania thing where you unlock powers and abilities of various kinds, and these help you overcome obstacles - but as previously mentioned, the game world is enormous and convoluted as can be.
Thus you will need either a walkthrough ([i]highly[/i] recommended if you decide to subject yourself to this.. experience) or some kind of cybernetic brain implant that provides you with the equivalent of an eidetic memory. Or just an actual eidetic memory. Or a deep love of backtracking through the murder-maze that just makes you want to run back and forth through the place forever and ever.

My point being, if you try to play this game through natural exploration, you will run out of new places to go, first nearby and then anywhere in the world, whereupon you will need to go through every single place you've been (ideally in your memory) and figure out where you'll be able to get further using a recently acquired ability or two.

Okay, so finding your way is a mess. Is the combat any good, you may ask? Well... sort of. Technically it's pretty refined. In practice, relative to the average human being (particularly a non-hardcore gamer, or indeed a... mildly hardcore gamer, like me, who in fact quite enjoyed Dark Souls, Bloodborne, Salt & Sanctuary etc.), it's far too fast and demands far too much precision. This is not a family game - give it to a kid only if you really, really hate the kid.

Quick example, if you find yourself slightly (and I do mean slightly) underprepared for certain boss battles (and you will be unless you took a youtube correspondence course in Exactly How To Play Hollow Knight And Where To Find Everything), you can expect to have a bad time, then a worse time, then carpal tunnel syndrome in your hands and wrists (if using a gamepad, not sure if the keyboard is any kinder), then general despair as even your improving skill at that particular boss fight is not enough and the fight never seems to end other than by your own death no matter how much damage you dish out, and then the adrenaline from the anger at the boss who keeps zipping around the screen and murdering you wears off, and you really start to feel it all.
Your only healing ability is channelled and interruptible, meaning it's useless in many of the situations where you need it most. You charge up your "soul" (mana) by hitting things, which works well, but you lose the soul cost of a heal spell even if it's interrupted. Did I mention some bosses like to endlessly spawn flying, homing enemies that can and will hit you while you're trying to heal? Yes, there's that.

Oh, and remember that murder-maze the game takes place in? It's divided into segments, right, and to even start on a map you have to buy some paper and a quill. You can only update your map with new info at a bench, the rest spots where the game saves and where you return upon death. These are extremely few and far between, by the way, because what really ruined Dark Souls? Too many bonfires, too easy to find! No-bonfire run, hardcore, rah! Do it now, bros!
...Have they gone? Lovely.
So, as I was saying, the map very realistically only updates when you rest at a bench. But that's not enough. Too easy! You also need to find the map salesman, right, again and again, [i]in each area[/i] and buy a new map before you can fill in that area's vague geography! Don't have the cash for it? Tough luck, no map updates in that area for you! Have fun finding your way through an unmapped murder maze. And you know how in games like Castlevania, your map tells you pretty much the actual shapes and sizes of rooms, how they connect, and so forth? Well, screw that! Hollow Knight has nice vague maps for you, so you can strain your brain trying to remember if this nondescript blob of color was the room with the seven spiky intestine-tunnels or if that was the nondescript blob of color a long, long detour to the right.

Oh, and it looks very pretty. Don't get me wrong, I have to give them points for that. There's some lovely color and lighting and gorgeous scenery and... well, the characters and animation are there too, yeah. Points for aesthetics.
The thing is though, to see those aesthetics, you'd have to play the game.
And the game?
It's not fun to play.
It feels sort of like work, and if it was your job to play Hollow Knight, you might be looking for a new job with Amazon. Y'know, one of the premier corporate slavedrivers. That's about how much fun it is to roam Hollow Knight's endless murder-mazes, trying not to die before you've seen all the pretty.

At times, it kind of flows, it's fun and dynamic and you make progress and there's a glimpse of The Game That Wasn't.
And then you're back to The Game That Is. The game that thinks the equation of fun in a video game is as simple as "Harder = Better". The game that is full of literal insects, yet half of them make weird grunting noises or hum or speak gibberish languages or do other non-bug-like things to the point that I, a not-particularly-fond-of-bugs sort of person, would frankly rather they just chittered, chirped, or rubbed antennae.

The music is frequently a little too fond of itself; it sounds like it thinks it's accompanying a grand adventure of some kind, but the thing about grand adventures is they flow. They don't grind to a halt and have the hero(es) run all the way back to get the Reflective Spatula from the Kitchen of Doom after obtaining the Cutlery Immunity Seal in the Hole of Random Interjections. And then the same route again, and again, any number of times... if you should have the sheer gall to die of any of the million things down there that very much want to kill you. Okay, the items and places are very slightly more plausible than that, but you get the idea.

Hollow Knight is so eager to cater to the hardcore "I'm a real gamer unlike you filthy casuals, you're not playing it right, the game is perfect, lol" crowd that it very obviously forgets about everyone else. I would not object to the hardcore crowd having a one-life hardcore mode to play in (they do have that too, on top of everything else), or a hardcore difficulty... but the entire game is structured in such a way that an unreasonable amount of dedication and specialist knowledge of this one, specific game, are required to progress.

I miss the days when people sometimes made games that you would play just to relax.
Or just for fun.
Games that didn't give you carpal tunnel.
Games that weren't Hollow Knight.

P.S. I did eventually complete the game. Not every single collectible, but the main path, all the abilities and most of the upgrades. Again, at times, it's a fun game. Beautiful to look at. But it is torturously, sadistically hard in more places than you can count - it literally turns into a lengthy Super Meat Boy style buzzsaw jumping puzzle marathon towards the end, and while there is a lot of elegance in platforms being placed just right for a precise maneuver or sequence of moves, it doesn't excuse the fact that checkpoints are so few and far between that you're constantly repeating challenges you've already proven you can overcome. Repetition is not fun. I remember from school! It's also kind of depressing, overall, despite the beauty.

This game has zero respect for your time and investment.  Very soon into the game it abandons it's main method of healing to make your journey tedious.

It tries to add too many gameplay challenges on top of each other for a game that doesn't warrant it or, frankly, is not designed to handle.  It stacks Dark Souls, Mega Man, and Metroid on top and you can feel the weight. 

It's Dark Soul without the good level design so when you can't get maps you're just lost.  The same punishing results for failing a fight, but there is no way to run or dodge as rooms are too small and often enemies' attacks fill the whole corridor. You lose money the same way, and since the levels aren't clear and you can't avoid enemies to get back to buy more maps you lose your money and continue to be lost.

It's Mega Man in that any contact with anything harms you significantly and healing is hard to come by very soon.  It's also dark everywhere so it's not completely clear when you can dodge enemies, which start to cover every room.  Soon the game just becomes an obstacle course where it is simple dumb luck to get through without significant damage.

And it's like Metroid, because you slowly get abilities to traverse the map, but the farther you go the more tedious it becomes, and what should be an interesting journey into a lost world is obscured by you constantly having to figure out where you are and constantly losing your money.

This game also has many of the traits of games lately, in that it requires constant fast twitch reactions to survive any fight.  It feels more like an unwieldy Street Fighter II with one button to hit rather than a Dark Souls or 2D adventure game.

It's just a bad mix of things that are put there to waste your time rather than enjoy the story, explore, have a good and interesting challenge, and enjoy the great artwork.

Too many copy-paste enemies. A very backwards revive and health system which removes any sense of adventure and progress and forces you to take small bites whenever you want to journey and play conservatively to a fault. The game feels like it was a first project by game designers and lack polish and creativity. 

The "RPG" aspect of the game is also very weak and probably rushed. 

The game has athmosphere which lasts for about 10 minutes until you start to experience the gameplay which is a mixture of boredom, frustration, and repetition. 
Oh and the worst part? if you die twice without "recovering" your sould you lose ALL coins (or seashells or whatever they are), with no way of getting them back and no chest or bank to deposit them in before going back out. 

I recommend this game to no one unless you're into chronic CBT. Keep your money and buy a good game instead.

Probably my favorite game to date. Plenty of detail, challenging, yet fun, gameplay, extremely rich storyline, the ability to customize/modify your playstyle with charms, a breathtaking landscape, and numerous endings put this very high on my list. If you find a place too hard, and no matter how many times you try it always ends in failure, you can just come back to it later when you have more abilities and/or power, but otherwise, many areas can be accessed from the very beginning as long as you can survive. The game also encourages exploration, hiding various items and other secrets in every corner. By far one of the best metroidvania games I have ever owned.

Also, it's filled with insects, arachnids, and all manner of other things that crawl, and many of my favorite animals are in that group, like the adorable stag beetle shaped knight you play as, making it even more of a favorite just for personal reasons. I would recommend this game if you love a brilliant story, and insects.

For me, that's a 10.

Before the Lifeblood update, I was considering straight up downvoting the game because of that. Now the situation is much better, but make sure your pc meets the minimal requirements.
The game is beautiful and immersive. Nothing short of amazing!

Such an amazing game! Its role as a metroidvania is a very well played out, the pace of the game is layout perfectly. There's a very good relation between the amount of world you can explore and the time it takes for you to get a new ability. Combat is very good too, the amount and diversity of charms make it possible to have a very different experience during combat throughout several phases of the game.
Story is very good, the characters have very distinct personalities. The whole lore is left very open which definitely suits the ambience of the game. Once more, the story is very well paced, there's a very good dictomy between moving through the world and learning bits and pieces about the story. 
One of the greatest things about the game is the graphics, it is so goddamn beautiful. Some of the areas are breathtaking and the characters are really pretty too. Sound effects and soundtrack are very good too.

(Spoiler) Probably the only thing that bothered me during the game is that there are several choices you can make that at the time seem somewhat meaningless but turn out to be the difference between getting an achievement or another, as what happens with the Nailsmith for example. If you want to get that simple achievement, you have to play a lot of the game again just for that. 

In general, I loved this game, had a ton of fun with it. Once again the graphics are beautiful and the whole movement and combat feels really good. Highly recommend it!

This game is fantastic, and I can see why so many people like it. It has a great atmosphere, and gameplay-wise it is difficult and punishing, but fair.

Unfortunately, if you're not very dexterous, it quickly becomes incredibly frustrating. Platforming sections where you know exactly what to do, but you just can't pull it off. Boss fights where you're not fast enough to dodge the attacks despite knowing the pattern.

This is not an issue for most people, but if you're one of those people that just isn't very good at games or not very patient, maybe skip this one.

If you'd told me before I played it that one of my favorite games I've played in recent history would end up being a cartoonish Metroid-vania with a cast of intelligent bugs, I would have given you a very funny look. I was on the fence about this title, looking at the art style and the subject matter, but finally I gave in to the enormous amount of praise I'd seen for this game and bought it on the last Steam sale.

Wow. I'm glad I did.

If you like Metroid-vanias, you're going to love Hollow Knight. The controls are tight, the platforming is well done, and the combat has just enough depth to be satisfying. The world is deceptively large with many, many hidden secrets. You get a very satisfying feeling of character progression as your little knight grows in power and capabilities, though the suite of movement enhancements is nothing unusual (with the exception of one particular ability I won't spoil). The soundtrack is excellent, bringing each area to life the way I remember Castlevania: Syphony of the Night did for me the first time I played it. On top of all of this, the developers hve continued to add new free content far past what I ever would have expected to see provided without it being relegated to paid DLC, so kudos to them.

In short, this is a great game. You should buy it, play it, and enjoy getting lost in it for a while.

I find the sound design of this game insufferable. All of the characters sound like they have mental impairments and they NEVER. SHUT. UP. I swear if I have to listen to one more minute of that charm vendor giggling non stop, or those anthropomorphic mushroom enemies trying to sound threatening by making baby noises, or the city infantrymen huffing like idiots 24/7, or the map-maker's wife sounding like the most unpleasant, entitled person in the history of ever, or...

The sound in this game makes everything feel like a farce. An incredibly annoying farce. And it's part of a larger issue, which is the DISSONANCE between the art style - which would be right at home in a Teletubbies game - and the superficially dark and grim themes that the game insinuates, but never actually delivers on.
It's like the game is trying to please babies and goths at the same time.

I find some of the mechanics questionable too. You essentially heal by hitting enemies, and you're granted a grace period after you are hit, so you can basically "brute force" almost all enemies, because you heal and finish foes faster than they can inflict damage on you.

But my main complaint is the sound. Gods, if this was Dark Souls, I'd slaughter every single character just to make them shut up, but of course they won't let you do that to their four-limbed, plump, baby-friendly "insects"...
