[b] TL;DR [/b]
Game is a 6/10 at best, not what I was advertised or what I was looking for.

Heard really high praise for this game, was recommended by many friends as "The greatest MetroidVania ever made". I love the genre and was excited for it.
This game has amazing art style, animation, atmosphere, music, the world is interesting and varied. It's gameplay however is luck luster at best, intentionally infuriating at the worst. Many decisions in regard to the gameplay seems to have 2 primary questions, "Will this be difficult" and "Will this waste the Players time". If the answer to both questions is "yes", then it made it into the game.

[b] Examples of minor time wasters that compound [/b]

Your Shade (Bloodstain) does not appear on the exact spot you died, but has preset locations per room. You also must kill the Shade to retrieve Geo/Soul Cairn, but the Shade is based on your power level so it will always die in a few hits, so why bother making it an enemy? No Coyote Time is ridiculous, especially because ledges often have grass or some other decoration masking the proper edge, leading to numerous falls when 99% of platformers would have allowed you that jump. Close to no I-frames on damage despite enemies being numerous and often quick, leading to chain deaths that are often impossible to break before you have the Dash. Fast Travel being separated into 3 forms each requiring their own unlock and use conditions.

[b] The Charms [/b]

Wayward Compass should be never have been a charm, at the very least make it a tool like the Lumefly Lantern. Nail Master's Glory is all but required to make Nail Arts usable, so why does it take a a slot instead of being a final upgrade from Sly? Stalwart Shell for damage I-frames is also ridiculous, as it should be a mechanic by default.

[b] Bosses [/b]

Keeping it simple, the bosses suck. I can count on 1 hand the bosses I enjoyed fighting, the other hand the bosses who were pushovers/tutorial, and the rest sucked all enjoyment out of the game. Nightmare Grimm/Grey Prince Zote/Nosk/Galien/Markoth in particular were awful. Most bosses end up in the not fun category for a simple reason; You spend most of your time dodging/chasing them down. The fights are a drag and personally I felt no sense of accomplishment after beating them beyond "Thank fuck I never have to do this again."

[b] The Map [/b]

The map is obtuse and mostly useless. Needing to rest to fill in the map only really serves to slow down exploration, since it will get filled in if you die and respawn anyway. Having to buy tokens individually and also limiting custom tokens to 6 per color unnecessarily limits and stretches out the navigation phase without making the game itself more fun. Finding each zones map inside the zone would be fine, but you also have to buy them once there, and the map itself doesn't mark room structure or completion. The compass being a charm (Albeit only 1 slot) is wholly unnecessary and frustrating when the map has so little information anyway.

[b] Expolration [/b]

Backtracking in this game is horrible, even by MetroidVania standards. Only 1 area changes over the course of the game and it has the least amount of content in it, areas are long and tedious to traverse with fast travel spread thin. The sheer amount of backtracking is absurd, the amount of times I had to go back to Deepnest or Kingdoms Edge, or pass through Fungal Wastes made me hate the zones. Add onto that that many "shortcuts" you unlock are 1-way, and thus cannot be used for quick traversal on return, only exit.
There is also next to no reward for exploration. Mask Shards/Soul Cairn pieces are few and far between, with Geo Deposits being the primary reward and occasionally a Charm or Grub. Many charms feel useless or won't fit an individuals playstyle, and only collectors will care about the Grubs. Money is plentiful and farmable very early, so Geo Deposits are meaningless for the majority of players. 
There was several times I explored an area, and got stuck with no option but to kill myself or Save&Quit. In particular, there was a passage in Crystal Peak where you need the Crystal Dash. I did not know this was an ability yet, so I tried to traverse the room with Dash/Pogo/Mantis Claw. I made it 3/4 into the room, and found I could no longer progress with my current tools, but also found that I could not return the same way I entered.
Many times I would have to jump down but be unable to see anything below, forcing a blind drop. Landing on spikes from these drops was not uncommon, and frustrating since the only way to know was take the initial risk.

[b] Conclusion [/b]

As a Platformer, this game fails to be challenging (Ignoring the Path of Pain, a completely optional and out of place difficulty spike) or engaging with it's movement. Lack of Coyote Time and poor visual clarity makes traversal frustrating.
As a MetroidVania, the obtuse map system and excessive backtracking remove most of the fun of exploring, and the lack of meaningful rewards/discoveries makes it a chore.

If you are looking for a MetroidVania or Platforming experience, I would never recommend this. If you want a Platform/Bullet Hell hybrid built on the boss fights alone, then this is your game.

This is another instance of a long tradition of games that are only praised because they're "hard". In reality, this game isn't exactly difficult; it's just very dull, tiresome, arbitrary and unpleasant to play. The character movement feels slow. heavy and cumbersome, which is especially egregious in a game that's all about exploration and maneuvering. The levels look and feel like a porridge-like mess, and they're not fun to explore and walk around. Even the combat is dull and unsatisfying, which is fatal in a game that's trying to hard to be a kind of watered-down 2D Dark Souls. Most of the "difficulty" of this game comes from wearing down the player with repetition and enemies that are irritating to deal with (e.g. those that teleport away when you try to hit them). I believe it says a lot about the mindset behind this game when, in order to reduce repetition, you have to pay in-game currency to unlock a save point--which, in the game's own terms, means you have to buy a bench. What kind of game does that?
In all, this entire game feels like the designers never asked themselves a very simple and important question: "is this fun?". It's a game made to be stylish, deep, difficult and indie, but NOT fun. Certain design decisions just weren't made with the player in mind: considering you have to learn to read and memorise enemy attack patterns, it's very bad when half of the screen gets covered by an ugly flash of colour when you get hit, thus preventing you from visually understanding what's going on. And really, considering how much the game tries to be profound and indie, it looks and sounds really bland and unimpressive. Even the music is generic and faceless.
If you want something 2D and difficult, stick with something like "Super Meat Boy" and "Dustforce"; and if you want exploration-based platformers (you know, that kind of "genre" that's named after the combination of two other games, thanks to people with no imagination), play "Ori and the Blind Forest". This game is just an unfortunate chore to play.

Not gonna waste time explaining basics of what this is and how it plays on paper, I'll assume you know how Youtube works and have already seen enough gameplay footage to get a good general idea of what Hollow Knight consists of.
So please enjoy the following stream of consciousness about a game I haven't played in more than 5 years at time of writing.

Whenever I think about this game, two specific quotes by Yahtzee Croshaw immediately come to mind. To paraphrase the first:
"The thing about exploration is that the joy lies in the finding."

The traversable area of the game is huge but for all the game's optional nooks and crannies there are three or for halfway decent character upgrades actually worth picking up.
There really is no reason to go anywhere, just look up a walkthrough and make immediate beelines to the required progression points. Any detour from that path will just lead to tedious slogs into nowhere and the discovery of some useless bovine droppings. Given that usually "exploration" is one of the main selling points of metroidvanias, Hollow Knight essentially falls flat on its face right out of the gate.

Navigating the world is needlessly convoluted. All the fast travel points are so far away from anywhere you'll actually want to revisit I genuinely don't understand why there even is a fast travel system at all.
Apparently the game's fandom has taken to ironically calling it a walking simulator which I admit is good for a chuckle but unfortunately in the long that'll do nothing to suppress ones' urge to work the level designer's private parts with a soldering iron.
Hollow Knight has, hands down, THE most poorly designed game map I've ever seen in any metroidvania, period; beating out the previous title holder Harmony of Dissonance (yes, that one) by such a ludicrous margin any actual potential reader of this review is probably incapable of comprehending the quantified amount unless you hold a doctorate in higher mathematics so I'm not even going to specify.

Lots of other negative reviews seem to make a big deal about losing money and I don't really understand why.
When I eventually dropped this out of sheer boredom I had something like 20000 bugdollars or whatever they're called over and absolutely nothing to spend it on.

At some point, somewhere, I found a statement from the developers essentially explaining how Hollow Knight is influenced by the Metroid series and decidedly NOT the Castlevania games. In retrospect this really shows in their final product and if I'd been aware of this from the start, I'd have known better than to ever pick it up.

The saddest thing is that basically, half of the game's fundamental design issues could have been immediately solved by including instantly usable items for combat and/or an experience based leveling system.
It would, however, still maintain the massive issue of basic movement: Large chunks of the game are designed without a proper dodge move in mind since you'll only acquire that upgrade some ways into the game and the second upgrade that gives you invincibility is an optional late game hidden collectible. This leads to the paradoxical situation where some enemies are borderline impossible to avoid without invincibility-dodge, leading you to wonder why it wasn't given to you as basic move at the very start, but once you DO get the upgrade it completely flips around and you essentially will never take damage anymore.

Overall, Hollow Knight is a game that really was never properly thought out. The basic mechanics of "what your character can do" and "what the gameplay is (supposedly) about" are massively disjointed.
Get Grime instead. It's not too far off from this in the broad strokes, only with every element of proper game design missing from  Hollow Knight being a prominent feature of the core gameplay.
I'm well aware that this isn't really a review but more of a frantic disjointed ramble but at this point I simply refuse to exert more effort in regards to this game than the developers and their army of flying monkeys ever did.

Soundtrack is rather neat though. The fact that I bought that along with the main game is also the only reason I haven't voluntarily removed it from my account permanently.
Which brings me to second Yahtzee Croshaw quote I teased in the beginning:
"Deleting a game just doesn't give you the same sense of closure as microwaving it"

..okay. good job. you managed to sit through a paragraph of someone who doesn't know what a metroidvania game is complaining about problems that aren't there;
here's my REAL review of hollow knight. and yes, i do recommend it, i marked it as don't recommend to keep the illusion up, haha.
where do i even begin? oh i know, the most unnecessary part, since every review loves to treat graphics like an integral gameplay element, even though games like undertale exist, which was LITERALLY made in ms paint and still has incredible gameplay.
enough of my tirade on graphics culture though.
hollow knight's graphics are absolutely stunning; i've heard from friends that it's all hand drawn and animated frame by frame and i'm HERE for it. the fact that every environment was meticulously drawn from the ground up is the best part for me, 
gameplay is interesting, the devs nailed (haha) the slow trickle of progression that i adore in games alike to this. you only get upgrades to yourself after defeating bosses, or discovering such things around the map, which encourages exploration and gives a motivator to battle bosses.
certain mechanics are not taught to you but must be figured out on your own, which is not necessarily a bad thing in this case, as it's executed incredibly well. nailjumping for instance is something i didn't know about until it was required in fungal wastes, at which point i just started experimenting and learned it on my own, making me feel like a galaxy brain glowing genius when i figured out how to do it properly
the bosses were very fun and compelling, the promised rewards aside, every death felt like it was my fault and not a problem with the game's design, making me want to pick up the controller again and give them another shot, which is something i love in games. when a death feels like an issue with the game and not the fault of the player, it's infuriating because you don't know what to do to get around it. but when it's your fault; well, one phrase echoes in your mind immediately; "GET GOOD!"
and that should really be the motto for this game, honestly. there is never a moment where something is the fault of the game, and if you feel like there is, you're coping and just need to get good. for me, i thought that the final boss was complete bs, but it just took some charm setup restructuring and practice to beat it, and every time i went through to fight it, those two words were yelled at me before i entered the arena, reminding me of what i had to do to succeed.
of course, my perspective is not flawless. metroidvania games are NOT for everyone and all of the reviews complaining have every right to, but calling the game bad and saying it's the fault of the developers is completely stupid. the game isn't for you. be nice about it and move on to your next game. you dont have to dogpile the game because it's not your kind of genre.

[h1]An absolute Masterpiece.[/h1]

(no, seriously, not one of those IGN [i]masterpieces[/i])

As of writing this review, I've yet to try beating the final boss - I wanted to complete the game as a whole (100+%) because I haven't enjoyed a game this much for a long... long while. Will be sure to beat the boss shortly.

This game shattered my expectations. I bought it while it's on sale but it deserves the full price. The amount of content for a 2D platformer is enormous (40+ hours for first playthrough w/o all DLC)... And that depends on your ability to learn from your mistakes. It also serves as a warning. While the game is fun, it can be equally frustrating and feels unfair more than it doesn't. But to me, that's all the more appealing since it makes it more rewarding to finally figure out the solution and beat it.

[h1]The Atmosphere[/h1] of the game is simply put, magnificent. The darker, sad atmosphere is matched with equally sad tone (props. to the amazing musical composition) and despair. However, there are many, many other themes with equally matching characteristics (without spoiling anything). The background is multi-layered and dynamic: It's focused in the middle of the painting, which destructible and non-destructible details complement and at the same time there's various effects that move and glow constantly, making it a very lively experience. The NPCs and the main character look adorable. The monolgues are short, philosophical and uncover bits of the lore. The game seems to also have its own language that the bugs speak, which reminds me a bit of Scandinavian and Japanese mixed together. From aesthetical point of view, this game set the bar so high that it's hard to best it in any other 2D platformer. The ambience in music is perfectly timed during some sequences of entering the level - it's that refined.

[h1]Gameplay[/h1] is very smooth. Every hit that you receive and deal is shown in a very clear manner. Since you have a couple of lives, there's a [i] thud [/i] that increases in volume if the damage takes more than one point of health. At the same time when you deal more than one instance of damage, the sound loops itself so you can recall how many hits you dealt. Controls are sensitive. Slightest misstep or mistiming can cost you a health point, character moves exactly where you issue. Although I'd have to say hitboxes for getting hit have a priority which in some cases make it a bit unfair as there's no chance of recovery even if the input registers during the same time. Visual clarity is refined however audio cues aren't exactly consistent in all the cases, still consistent enough to use them.

[h1]Customization[/h1] exists in the form of playstyle. Which is presented through Charms. Charms increase or empower the basic mechanics you unveil and have from the get-go. Makes it all the more interesting to challenge yourself to beat the game with a certain playstyle.

The best way to play this game is without no prior knowledge about how it plays. The game slowly opens itself up. Just when you think you've seen it all, new places open themselves up to you until the very end. And even then the game tells you that you have more things to discover.

The learning curve is literally a slash ( / ). You start with no knowledge, basic platforming skills and the game slowly teaches you how to master it, the more you learn the more addicting it becomes. It has very similar characteristics to Souls games, the bonfire mechanic, fast travels and at the same time, a classic Metroidvania. There's no guide what to do, but there are hints. Leave no stone unturned or no cave unexplored as there's always something to find. The game is roughly 35 hours of pure exploration.
The game tries to gate your advancement, but doesn't do it for nothing. The reason why you can't enter some areas become clear after you enter them. In that way, the game's progression is very well thought out (although, i entered some areas pre-maturely, using the game's mechanics).

[h1]Final words[/h1] for the game itself: If you have free days that you wanna spend gaming in solitude, Hollow Knight is one of the best solo gaming experiences I've ever had. A perfect balance of challenges, reward, atmosphere and gameplay that got me hooked instantly and ended up blitzing it through in a week.

However... There's something that they manage to do which actually drove me to the point of frustration. A particular DLC area feels like lazy game design that completely removes the aspect of the game you learn to love and replaces it with a different game entirely. A mini-game becomes the game itself for next to no reward. I had no complaints about the game until that. It is an absolute torture and requires absolute perfection which requires trial and error until you learn to time things to a T. It's a challenge and i'd categorize it as preparation for speedrun-strats. I sort of regret doing that, but my curiosity beat me to it. Although, in the end I did beat it. I just felt... Hollow (no pun intended) and had no desire to continue playing the game that day.

That said, the area is almost hidden (had to wiki it). It's a free DLC and it's your own responsibility of going through it. So can't really point fingers.

Hollow Knight is the main reason, in addition to Rivals of Aether and Castle Crashers, I got an X-Box One controller and a Blu Tooth adapter for my computer. In total the controller was about $50, the adapter was about $10, and Hollow Knight itself was about $10. But was it all worth the hefty price? Absolutely. A massive world, tight controls, and stunning music all come together to form my favorite game of  2017. And thats not mentioning the Hidden Dreams and Grimm Troupe content updates. Best game ever made 10/10 fuck you if you think otherwise.

This game is definitely in my most favourite games of all time, and I've been gaming for over 12 years.

As you explore Hollow Knight's world you immediately get sunk into the story. Snippets of lore through ancient tablets, desolated rooms reminding of what once was, ... The music is incredible and makes every area feel completely different than the last.

The variety of different areas in the game is astounding, Hollow Knight achieves a complete picture in a very particular sense. This meaning that you may enjoy the calming, sometimes thorny gardens of Greenpath while you might also find yourself in the ghastly halls of the Resting Grounds. Every area has a different kind of setting and conveys the emotion behind it in a very subtle, smooth way. While occasionaly you can enjoy some lightheartedness through the little Grubs you save, searching for Cornifer the mapmaker, Zote the Mighty, ...

The endless combinations of charms that can be found keeps the gameplay varied and interesting. Bosses often force you to step up your game, change your strategy completely and continuosly reinvent yourself in order to beat them. 

Team Cherry has truly achieved a masterpiece which sets an incredibly high standard for other Metroidvania-titles out there.

This game came highly recommended to me from a friend. He knows I’m a big Metroidvania fan and said that this was the perfect example of the genre. 

It’s an okay game. It has a decent story. What I wasn’t prepared for was the sheer amount of time you have to waste to get anywhere. The mapping system is maybe the worst I’ve seen in the genre. I got lost constantly only to suddenly walk into a boss fight I wasn’t prepared for. 

In fact, I’m 7 hours in and have never felt like my character has made any progress. You feel so weak for the majority of this game that it gets frustrating to play. In most metroidvanias you get incremental upgrades and skills that become really handy in boss fights. In this game, the only skill I really feel is useful is the wall kick. Everything else either costs too much mana or takes to long to execute. The power dive is completely useless so far, making me feel like it was a giant waste of time and effort to get. 

If you like Dark Souls or Elden Ring, you might like this. I’ve played both those games and had fun, but I’ve also played all of the castlevania and Metroid games, blasphemous, and the mummy demastered. I feel like I know how a good metroidvania should play.

This game feels like it hates you. You can get through it by pure willpower and “gettin’ gud” but do you want to? Do you want to be lost for 20 minutes? Do you want to find out where you were supposed to go only to suddenly fall into a pit and die? Only to have to start from your last check point miles away from your corpse holding all your money?

If you like wasting time, this game might a good choice while getting through a season of tv or a long podcast. Other than that, find a better metroidvania.

I really, really, REALLY wanted to enjoy this game because of how much praise it gets from pretty much anyone. Is it a bad game? No, just a very disappointing one from my perspective. I'll start with the good:

-The art is really good, obviously.
-The music is kinda nice (though forgettable).
-The lore is amazing? (from what I played, it seemed kind of interesting, but given my opinion on the game, I'm not gonna dive into it just for an opinion).
-I really like the dialogue in general.
-Many bosses are very enjoyable from the first time you fight them (you don't need to farm them for 5 hours just to have fun during the fight), my favorite ones being the Grimm King (bot fights) and Hornet (mostly the second fight).

Now the things I disliked about the game (note, if you aslo despise several of this "features", I strongly advise you NOT to buy this game. Save yourself the time and money):

-A SHIT TON OF BACKTRACKING. I know very well what metroidvanias are and what they offer. But if you're gonna need me to travel through the EXACT SAME portions of the map because the fast travel and respawn points are just about wherever the fuck they want, then thats a big no, no for me. Even worse: the game isn't clear about what you need to "beat it" (specially during the first half), so good luck trying to find that new zone, boss or item that probably doesn't even exist.

-Some really BULLSHIT secret passages that are REQUIRED for the 100+% completion. One thing is to hide an easter egg or special item within a room that requires some precise parkour to reach, and something very differently is to hide A WHOLE ASS MAP SECTION (yes, I'm talking about the motherfucking bee hive) behind a fake wall you'll most likely only notice if you hit it by complete accident, if your brightness is at 100%, if your sight is godlike or if you straight up look it up on a uid or something.

-Shitty hitboxes and some boss attacks which are SO FAST (in terms of how quickly they "start") you should genuinely pray to god that you're not unlucky enough to get combo'ed straight to the bench a whopping 5 seconds after beggining the fight. No wonder hitless runners and tryhards always try to kill the bosses as fast as possible: because actually fighting them is sometimes a chore and sometimes a coin toss.

-No real rewards for endgame quests, challenges, etc. What do you get from saving all 46 grubs? A shitty charm you'll problably use exactly once before throwing it away. What do you get from getting all the charms? A dumb blessing that refills your focus on benches when, by this point of the game, you are more than able to get from killing anything easily (and it is fucking useless in pantheons because benches are always next to a hot spring). What do you get from the hunters journal? Spoilers: absolutely fucking nothin! (The other two at least get some credit because they're needed for completion, but the hunter's mark is not, so go figure).

-A disappointing ending. And I don't say it because of the conclusion to the "story" (which is ok, I guess) or anything like that, I refere to the last boss. Even a terrible player like me could beat it first try and only healing myself like 3 times, which is extremely disappointing (and the fight itself isn't even that interesting). The thing is, there's actually a "true final boss" you get to fight AFTER this "final boss" if you do some specific stuff. And while this other fight is definetely more interesting, it feels kind of cheap and even mean that I had to do some other stuff in order to reach a satisfying conclusion.

-The Pale Castle (or however the fuck it is named) and the Path of Pain: genuinely the most MISERABLE experience I've had in ANY videogame ever. Guess what? The reward for the castle is (half of) charm that, though interesting, you literally have to get rid of in order to reach the true final fights. And the path of pain? Nothing (surprising, I know).

Overal, this whole game just felt like a huge ass waste of time for me. I probably had some good moments with it. But I say "probably" because my memory is just filled with mush and other uninteresting and unrewarding stuff from this game.

But if you like hard games you may enjoy it.

For the life of me, I can't figure out why this game is so well-liked. Recently, I've played (and very much enjoyed) a few other indie exploration/combat based games, so I thought it was time to play Hollow Knight, which many consider to be one of the best games of the past 5 years and an archetype of the MetroidVania genre. I had never really been drawn to it before because it didn't strike me as appealing, but I went in excited and with an open mind. Several hours later (and after forcing myself to restart after quitting from boredom a few times), I can't say I like the game.

In my mind, the thing that separates a good 2D platformer from a bad one is the controls, and Hollow Knight's controls are not good. Movement is slow, attacking is imprecise, and nothing is explained well. The attack feels bad and unintuitive to me, and I was unable to get used to it. Several hours into the game, I thought "there must me some form of upward/downward strike even though I was never told about it", tried to figure it out for a bit but couldn't, and gave up, thinking it was crazy they didn't implement something so common in this game. It was only later after coming across something that *really* looked like it needed a downward strike that I looked it up and found a bunch of online posts from people who were in the same position as me and had to be told many hours into the game how to do a basic attack. And when I saw how to do it, I still can't even do it consistently because the controls are so bad. This is a sign that you have failed as a game designer. I've played many Flash games that have better controls than this.

Also, the combat itself is not even fun. You essentially only have one attack type (you run out of magic very quickly and it's super OP) so the strategy for every enemy in the game is "run up to it, hit it, then run away before it hits you". There's no depth, every boss is the same. After the first few minutes, I never felt like I learned anything new about how to succeed in the game (except for the aforementioned upward/downward strike issue). None of the enemies are really that interesting either, they just try to ram/shoot you until you hit them enough times that they die. Boss fights gave me little gratification because I didn't do anything special to win, I just hit it enough times with my sword. Again, I've played many Flash games with more interesting combat than this.

And the level design, the thing that I hear the most praise about, was the worst part for me. I can work around bad controls or combat if I'm actually interested in the game, but nothing in the level design actually made me care about what I was doing. Every room is the same drab and featureless box, with no real landmarks or points of interest other than a reskinning of the same tiles in different biomes. Enemies are strewn around haphazardly with no real purpose, and boss chambers are not telegraphed at all, leading me to end up in several boss fights I was not ready for. Furthermore, there wasn't anything that was drawing me to go further in any direction other than just filling in the map. There's no semblance of story structure, promise of reward, stimulating puzzles, or other compelling reason to set a goal for yourself, so what's even the point? I felt more connected to the randomly generated caves of Terraria than I do to these worlds because at least I knew I would get something valuable, whereas I feel like the only thing waiting for me in the next room is more uninspired enemies I have to hit with my difficult-to-use sword. Unsurprisingly, I've played many Flash games with more engrossing level design than this.

So what is this game? It's a 2D platformer Flash game that got enough budget to make it tens of hours long and failed to come up with a reason why. Improve the controls and attacks (and actually explain them). Make combat more than just a one-dimensional "I hit it with my sword". Give the levels some type of intellectual stimulation like real differentiation, puzzles, or story structure. I guess I'm glad everyone else had such a good time with this, but I'm just going to stick with Flash games.

Played a lot of platformer games lately.  I've seen and heard a lot about this game.  It is inspired by the metroid, megaman, and darksouls, yet it fails to have anything remotely similar in feel.  Once I got into the game it just didn't feel good.  The responsiveness of the controls and the pacing feels slow and boring.  The skills are spread out with minimal impact.  By the time you receive the crystal heart (a skill that allows you to zip around the map quickly) the game is at a fairly slow point and it's a little too late for an item that saves that much time moving around.  Qol in that respect is poorly designed and the feel of combat is bad.  The game a fun grip in the beginning, but slows down 4 hours into the game. 

Dash is an animation that isn't cancel-able in any way, which feels very restrictive.  A huge reason why this gets a bad score for me is having some improvisation in combat allows for a more interaction.  The music is mediocre, and the art is average as well.  

I will finish the game eventually, but there are 10 other games like this that are far better and should be played and bought before this.  Dust, shadow complex are far older and still better, astlibra and hell, just play the original metroid and megaman games imo. Super Metroid and Megaman X came out on the SNES, many years ago and this game is inferior in every way to those games.  

The currency and impact that it has is not really felt.  This is a 6.5/10, not worth the hype.  The boss fights do not get much better and fail to feel unique and fun.  When the controls and skills don't evolve much the boss fights all kind of blend into each other and are too similar.  This is a long game where you will play twice as long as you should with metroidvania qol acquired over time.  Some qol in the form of fast travel and shortcuts just come too late.   If you want to play either Megaman X or Super metroid for the snes get on an emulator it's free and way better
If you want to play games that take from their the predecessors and improve in unique ways, play Astlibra, Dust an Elysium tale, Shadow Complex, new strider titles, Bloodstained ritual of the night, etc. this is just not it.

I spend almost 50 hours in the beautiful, chilling world of Hollow Knight, and after over 100% completing the game (not gonna spoil anything else about that), I can safely say that this is my favorite game. Period. Team Cherry poured every bit of their hearts and souls into this game, and have truly outdone themselves. Everything is top-tier: atmosphere, art, pacing, compbat, character designs, sound design, character customization (to an extent), and sheer wealth of content. There is a plethora of secrets and collectibles scatered around the world. You'll find yourself becoming familiar with the world, its characters, and slowly piecing together the history of Hallownest. You're heart will pound with adrenaline as you defeat immense bosses with quick, deadly attacks. You'll expand your arsenal of skills and abilities, as well as your own capacity to use them. You'll explore a huge living world, and encounter a wide variety of unique enemies.

This is the only game that I have ever reviewed on Steam to this point. After defeating the final final boss and watching the credits play, I felt pulled to this game.

It's worth every cent of the $15 price tag, and I got the game on sale around its release. Whether you've never played a metroidvania, or have scooped up every one that came onto the market, Hollow Knight is well worth your time, money, and heart.

Thank you.

I'll admit, when I just started playing Hollow Knight I was put off by it. You're very weak, your weapon's range is very short, there's no clear direction on where to go, the map system is very confusing at first (you have to buy maps and a compass), and to top it all whenever you die you lose all your money and one third of your mana pool until you backtrack to where you died and kill your "shade". But, once you push on enough and the game opens up it's an amazing Metroidvania experience.

First of all, the game is [b]massive[/b]. While most Metroid-likes take only 3-5 hours to 100%, Hollow Knight will easily take you 30-40 hours and more. There's so many locations and all of them are unique and enticing. You never feel like you're going through some padding. While the difficulty slowly ramps up it mostly stays quite hard. The game does require some tricky platforming and studying enemy patterns. It's mostly quite fair though, I was able to defeat Soul Master (the first big difficulty spike boss) on my 2nd try by just keeping my cool and dodging his attacks. And if you're stuck on a particular boss, no biggie, just come back later.

Because a really cool thing about Hollow Knight is that there's no single set path, once you acquire dash and wall jump (and maybe also double jump) you can pretty much go anywhere. Pick a location, go through it, acquire new charms and upgrades, meet some new NPC's. There's [b]many[/b] optional areas, bosses, various nooks and crannies with hidden goodies. There's a regular ending, and then you can try finding a secret location and unlock a secret ending with an extra hard boss. There's been numerous free updates with new content. And there's even more content planned including an all new second playable character.

Hollow Knight features gorgeous 1080p graphics with exquisite hand drawn caverns, forests, lakes, dilapidated cities. And the soundtrack only enhances the experience as it fits the vibe of the post-apoc "bugs world" perfectly. Sit on a bench under a gothic street lamp and give it a listen.

This is one of the most beautiful games I've ever played. The art is beautiful and consistent. Honestly, it's more than worth the asking price just to SEE the game's world.

The platforming controls are tight as can be, which is great news considering that both navigation and combat rely on them. I've completed the vast majority of the game's content (103%) with a keyboard without feeling hobbled, so don't worry about having a hard time just because you don't have a controller. There's an innovative healing system that I've never seen done in quite the same way. It rewards aggressive play and careful attention to when it is and isn't safe to heal.

Hollow Knight's world is one of the best-designed I've had the pleasure of exploring. The game follows the classic metroidvania formula. You discover new powerups, shortcuts, and abilities that let you access new regions of the massive kingdom of Hallownest. You get a map, but it only fills in new areas when you rest at checkpoints, and you have to buy the template for each area from a delightful npc. Did I mention the npcs? I love each and every one of them. The developers have filled their world with incredible characters -- friend and foe alike.

The story is probably the main reason Hollow Knight gets compared to Dark Souls. Things aren't straightforward. You have to piece the details together from the environment and its inhabitants. Don't pay attention, and you just might miss the point of key scenes. It's fun stuff to read up on and argue about with your friends.

The most incredible thing about Hollow Knight is that it's so inexpensive. 15 dollars got me 50+ hours of quality content. There's dlc, but it's all free. Yes, you heard that right. Free. If you buy this game, you'll be supporting good business practices, indie developers, and getting a product a hundred times better than half the stuff AAA publishers try to sell for 60+ dollars.

I have very few criticisms of Hollow Knight. The "pogo" mechanic, which lets you bounce off enemies by jumping over them and doing a downwards attack, occasionally feels too inconsistent to do what the game asks of you. A few of the optional bosses related to the Hidden Dreams dlc have fairly long runs to get to them each time you fail. They really don't feel necessary at all. And the invincibility period after taking damage can behave a little strangely when you get hit by an enemy and touch an instant-kill hazard like a spike in rapid succession. But these are all minor nitpicks that barely affected my enjoyment. 

Highly recommended. Thank you for reminding me that video games can be art, Team Cherry. Eagerly awaiting Gods and Glory <3

I haven't played a game this good since I played the original dark souls back in 2013. The art style, atmoshphere, level design, soundtrack, combat, animations, currency and item systems, characters, and story are top notch. I also want to praise Team Cherry's attention to detail. The games story is all around you and even hidden in the games music and the sounds you hear as you move through hollownest. There is so much hidden in plain site that when, after 10 hours of playing, I began to peice things together, I felt almost giddy. I was extremely happy and surprised that I found a second game that can give me that feeling of wonder and discovery. 

Theres also tons of little details in the enemy design that are easy to miss but shake up the game play and allow tons of room for creative freedom. You can parry an enemy's strike if you swing at the same moment they do, you can hit solid objects that are thrown or shot at you like a baseball (the more brittle objects break), you can bounce off enemies with the jumping downward strike and land behind them, you can dash foward, strike, and dash out, you can short hop over attacks to land a blow to the face, and you can bounce off or up walls for superior positioning. I constantly feel like I'm getting better and the better I get the more fun I have watching my little bug gracefully slay enemies like a kung-fu movie. It's not something thats new or innovative; it's just done very well.

- After about 2h of play, the game suddenly drops to ~15fps, making it unplayable. This is only fixed after a restart of the game.
- Soul Master puts the game into a softlock on death
- Sometimes hardlocks on screen transition

[h1]Movement[/h1]
The movement system of the game frankly feels unfinished.
- The game lacks any sort of input buffering.
- Lots of actions put you into a stagger (dashing, healing) that isn't visually conveyed in any way. Combine with no input buffering and it eats your inputs.
- Since the input for focus isn't buffered either, interrupted focuses do not restart. Same with opening the map
- Getting hit puts you into a long stagger, so often you are put into a hit loop you can't escape.
- When dashing, the dash immediately starts in the direction you are facing. This means you cannot press dash and Left arrow to dash left.
- If sliding on a wall, you automatically face away from the wall, no matter what you input. This means if you want to jump slightly under a ledge and attack a monster standing on top, there is a window where that works and then suddenly you face away even though your inputs did not change
- There is no indication when you can dash or use the double jump although the delay is not constant (charms and air-time)
- There are 0 coyote frames (nobody has 0 frames input latency)
- When holding the jump button, there is a phase where your height increases perfectly linearly. This looks and feels really awful

[h1]Random annoyances[/h1]
Hollow Knight [i]really[/i] loves to obscure your screen. You know, the one you play on. There are bosses that derive their difficulty solely from the fact that you can't see their attacks sometimes (of course random). The screen is heavily obscured if [b]you are low on health[/b].

The game relies on you learning but actively prevents you from doing so by teleporting you far away from the challenge or boss. You have to fight all the monsters on your way again and then you have to fight against your corpse. Only then can you retry. Sometimes you have to fight the boss and your corpse simultaneously.

[h1]Story and World Building[/h1]
This is where this game shines. Not only is the art great, the world building is awesome. The characters make the game feel alive and when the weather effects don't cover your enemies completely, they really add to the ambience. The music is very good.

So yeah, this game feels like a beta, especially after playing Celeste, which gets its movement right, without any of the issues. It feels like I am constantly trying to fight the game and not the bosses in it. Together with the bugs and usability annoyances (can't disable screen shake, choices default to 'No', ...) this feels like a beta. It would be a very good game for a beta but not a passing grade for a released game. I would like to play a version 1.0 but that isn't happening I fear

You'd be insane to not buy this game. It should last you even in a minimalist playthrough atleast 30 hours which already makes a 15$ game sound like you're stealing. If you like metroidvania's you have to buy it. If you don't, you should still buy it. Maybe you might not like it as much as other people, but unless you literally quit instantly there's no way you can say that it wasn't worth it. 


Whats good?


15$

*It takes the metroidvania concept and bumps it to infinity with a ton of enemy variety, atmosphere, and amazing bosses. Metroidvania's are infamous for tedious backtracking (looking at you SOTN) and hollow knight gives you options to reduce the downtime to a reasonable minimum without straight up going instantly where you want to go. The gameplay like most games in general is slow and linear at the start to teach you the basic mechanics and then takes off. After a certain story event, the map opens up and you can go wherever you want, similar to breath of the wild. 

*It also rewards you for being creative and taking challenges. I've played video games for awhile and know metroidvanias are about combat, exploring, and getting power ups. In one point of the game I noticed a wall that clearly was meant for me to use a wall jump ability, but through learning the game I knew that you get a slight bounce when hitting certain objects downard and got to a unique boss fight. Granted with this creativeness, I wasn't suppose to be there, so that's where I got my first death, but after coming back I was rewarded.

*When it comes to not gameplay, hollow knight is great, but not amazing. Music is fitting and emphazies what you're doing, but there are very few tracks that I would personally listen to out of game. The enviorement you are in is one of the best parts that isn't gameplay. Exploring all these area's filled with bugs gives the game's world a sense of being alive, compared to most metroidvania's that are just focused on their gameplay and have boring overworlds.


Whats bad?


*Obviously if you traditionally know you don't like metroidvania's you're enjoyment will be worse than mine. I personally think Hollow Knight is the best metroidvania in gameplay and aethstetics if that helps you're decision. Like I said in the pro's everything that isn't gameplay is good, but I wouldn't say they should be the sole deciding factor to buy it, unless you really really dig the artstyle.

*Story.... kinda. It's obviously not a heart jercker for sure. My problem with the story is that you can understand what's happening around you while playing, but you don't understand why. Granted with the confusing poetic lore tablets you find around, you could, but after searching online after beating the game, it turns out I was missing a whole lot of what was happening. It's more interesting once you learn what actually is happening in the lore, but I shouldn't have to look on youtube to find out, speaking of looking up stuff the next bad part of hollow knight is....

*The DLC
So I should point out that the content of the dlc is good infact it's really good adding some of the best boss fights in the whole game. I mean it's free anyways and comes installed on the first download. You can relate the dlc to BOTW'S dlc where it's a optional part of the main campaign essentially, except for Gods and Glory.

My problem with the dlc is finding the dlc. Not liking the story is one thing, but I honestly didn't mind since the worldbuilding made up for it. This issue however is a big problem. Normally in metroidvania's if you keep a sharp eye out you can find secrets to help you out, but hollow knight dlc is litreally hitting some random wall. This would be fine for easter eggs in which the dlc does add some, but for a essential part of the game *cough* Grimm Troupe *cough* it's dumb that I have to look parts up on the wiki and potentially get spoiled (Which did happen btw). I really hope that after they finalize the patching on Gods and Glory they make all the hard to find dlc that aren't easter eggs actually noticeable. I understand that most of the DLC is late game, but if they don't want people taking on a challenge that's unreasonable for their skill level than give them a hint later on.

If found the aspects above impeccable and yet I hated playing this game. No reward felt like a real reward, because it was either useless, or just made to allow "stage" progression.

I think that Hollow Knight is great as an art piece; the music, artwork, and the snippets of lore you pick up along the way set the tone quite well. While the controls are tight and responsive, the moment to moment gameplay other than a few bosses felt really underwhelming to me, which is an issue when about half of a blind playthrough will be backtracking through known areas to get to where you actually need to be.

Apparently, the developers claimed that making fast travel stations few and far between was an artistic choice for the sake of player immersion. I feel like this reflects a lot of design decisions about the game, where player immersion seems to have been held at a higher importance than fun gameplay. The fast travel stations, benches, and vendors are spread out in locations that make sense in the world of Hollow Knight, but that are also a chore to visit. There were multiple times where I had an extreme amount of money on me, but I didn’t want to make the 10-15 minute round trip to visit a few of vendors, so I would simply put it off for later or I would call it quits for the night and plan to do it first thing the next time I played. For people who are easily immersed, this probably wouldn’t be a problem, but for me, it made me want to stop playing.

I genuinely had fun with a few bosses, but most enemies were more of a nuisance to fight. Common enemies can usually be defeated by just whaling on them, shimmying their attacks and hitting them while they are recovering, or by bouncing on their head with downward slashes. The ones that can’t be defeated these ways are typically the easiest enemies to avoid, so there isn’t really need to figure them out. Your main arsenal of attacks are just slashes and a projectile, anything beyond that is more situational and not really worth expending resources that could be used for healing instead, so the enemy design feels basic to match the fairly basic means you have for attacking.

Having to pick up your money from your specter when you die felt like shaking down a kid for their lunch money or hitting a pinata without a blindfold on. It was an interesting spin on the souls genre trope of having to reclaim your main resource after dying, but not well implemented in my opinion. Granted, most of what I experienced through my blind playthrough wasn't hard. If you’ve played through any of the challenge levels/bonus content in Celeste, Hollow Knight won’t be hard for you either. For people with a general familiarity with platformers, I would say Hollow Knight will probably be challenging, but not too difficult.

(Spoiler warning of 2 bosses and final boss.)

[spoiler]I loved the way that the Mantis Lords were designed, with having you face one of them to help you get a feel for their patterns and then throwing two at you. It gives the player the opportunity to learn the attacks before ramping up the difficulty while still using the same attack patterns. I think they were just well designed and interesting to fight.[/spoiler]

[spoiler]The Lost Kin was fast paced and really forces you to play based on reactions instead of falling into a pattern, it was the only boss I had to attempt more than twice and I was on the edge of my seat the whole fight for every fight. The only problem I had with the fight was having the slime ghosts spawn on top of me, which was basically unavoidable if I was already attacking or trying to heal and couldn’t get out of the animation in time.[/spoiler]

[spoiler]Finally, the Hollow Knight was interesting not because of the fight itself, but because of the 
feelings conveyed during the battle. I was a little disappointed when I realized he was basically a ramped up Hornet, but when the music turned somber and he started stabbing himself with his nail, I realized that the Hollow Knight was being forced to fight when he didn’t want to. Every time he staggered or hesitated after that, I felt sorry for him, and it made me want to win for him. That alone made it a great boss.[/spoiler]

Hollow Knight has some real gem moments, but considering that these three stand out fights were maybe 10-15 minutes of a 16 hour playthrough, the majority of which I can’t exactly say I had fun playing, I don’t think they make up for the rest of the game. I know that there was more that I could have done if I had explored more, but I was already on the edge of being burnt out on the game and knew that when I dropped it, I wouldn’t be picking it back up. I wanted to write a review and if so, the least I should do is finish it, even if I don't 100% complete it.

If you are a huge fan of the metroidvania genre or you get easily immersed in a game’s atmosphere and you weren’t dissuaded by anything in this review, give Hollow Knight a shot. If you are having any doubts about whether or not to get Hollow Knight, regarding the backtracking, difficulty, overall gameplay, or anything else, I would not recommend it.

Normally I don't play metroidvania games due to their annoying puzzles and prisise jumps, but at the time I was playing Metroid Dread and was really enjoying it and wanted to give this a shot due to the positive reception Hollow Knight was getting. What I came to experience was just boring. There is no main goal or destination, which I'm with, some of my favourite games have this but Hollow Knight dose not use it well. I ended up just wondering about the world and because it doesn't fully explain the worlds history I felt disconnected. I also like how this is classified as a soulslike game, its not really. Soulslike game are designed as being extremely difficult but fair, they are designed to kill you countless times but you will begin to analyse their attacks and eventually come out on top. But Hollow knight's bosses and enemy's are unpredictable and irratic, which can frustrait many people because its not the players fault that you died. And when you die when exploring be prepared to redo 10 min of walking and jumping to get to where you are because the benches(spawn/save points) are very infrequent. And when you embark on your walking and jumping simulator you may think the world is full of useful things to use to help you on your journey. No most of the time its a dead end and occasionally you may find a useless talisman. This just frustraits the player when they have put 40 min into exploring an area, dieing multiple times only to be greeted by a wall or a tool that is as useful as the knockoff PS1 controler used on the Titanic sub.

Would have been a great game, if it wasn't plagued by an insane (and I'm not using the word casually) amount of spiked-up difficulty bosses in advanced content. And there is actual content locked behind those bosses; items, lore, and endings, so it's not like "I only want to play it for the story, and I'll skip extra challenges that are there just for their own sake"... if you want the full story, you'll have to beat those challenges.
They require truly too much time and effort for this to be classified as a game. It becomes work. Unrewarded work that only steals your time.

If you're not someone with too much free time on your hands, you won't enjoy having to die dozens of times to learn to beat one boss, and repeat the process for the huge number of bosses there is in Pantheons, also considering that in those you have to fight powered-up bosses in uninterrupted sequences.

In short, recommended for masochists and people who are either blessed with mad skills or cursed with having no life; definitely not recommended for anyone else. Just watch playthroughs on YouTube or whatever; the atmosphere and lore are good and they're the true value of the game, along with its art direction, but if you don't feel like dying inside wasting your time for nothing, don't actually play it. Or play it "partially", and watch the rest online, if you don't usually have the compulsion to finish what you started like me.
Personally, I regret buying Hollow Knight and wasting in it the hours I did. Winning a fight after fifty tries gives me no satisfaction, only emptyness. Ponder how such things work for you.

This s*** good. The gameplay was confusing at first because of my experience in other 2D platformers, because, most of them are momentum based. Hollow Knight, is not. It's not building up speed or whatever, it's precise, clean, and a pain if you're not used to it. It's a sick system for the combat, which is fun and interesting, as there's strategy in setting up your loadout, and accomplishment in learning to exploit attack patterns in new ways. The ability to heal yourself and the soul system are right up my alley, because it means that even when you make mistakes, you can make up for it by doing well next time. The movement is fun, and sometimes it makes you feel really cool.

The aesthetics, though. Oh my, the art is beautiful. The soundtrack is stunning. The sense that your character really IS a character interacting with the world is fleshed out to the last detail. Every background is so nice, each one is unique, and I could stare at some of the areas for hours. The music is emotional, even when it becomes energetic for the fights, and I'll be getting that soundtrack as soon as I can. And, as for my last point, let me just use some examples. 

Every time you hit a wall or ceiling, little rocks and pieces break off. When you take damage, the music dampens greatly to emphasize the shock. When you hit something made of metal, sparks appear. When you defeat the third boss, the Mantis Lords, every mantis in the village becomes docile, and will not attack you. In fact, they no longer deal contact damage either.

That's what I could think of off the top of my head. Anyway, I really enjoy this experience, and I hope you will too.

Hollow Knight throws the player in a cavern at the beginning without any context, which can be seen as saving the player the trouble of skipping cutscenes and go straight to the juicy gameplay, but the gameplay itself at the beginning is not even that compelling. Hollow Knight is a stellar game. From the tight gameplay, beautiful hand-drawn artsyle, and spectacular soundtrack, most everything about this game is amazing. Of course, like most games out there, it does have its problems. But in my opinion they're far outweighed by the positives.

Pros:
+ Extremely fun and addictive gameplay: The game can be difficult at certain times, but it seems fair most of the time. I was able to 100% the game, despite having no experience with metroidvanias other than Ori. There are some parts which just seem cheap in terms of difficulty *cough cough* trial of fools *cough cough* but none of them are required to finish the game (one is required for the true ending though).
+ Beautiful Art: The game has a very unique look to it that you sadly don't find in many games nowadays. After cuphead, I'd say this is my favorite game of 2017 art-wise.
+ Soundtrack: The soundtrack is really good. If you love violin music like I do, you're in for a real treat with this one. Even if you don't buy the game, you should at least look up the OST on youtube so you can hear it all.
+ Story: The story leaves a lot to the imagination. The protagonist is silent so NPCs and looking at the world are the only ways you get information. It's refreshing to not sit through 10 minutes of backstory before a playthrough.

Cons:
- Some parts of the game can feel unfairly difficult: Thankfully there aren't many, but those parts can be FRUSTRATING. Only one is required for the "true" ending, but you need the others for 100%. 
- Tiny glitches: Many of these have been fixed by team cherry, but there are still some in the game. They can range from a button imput not going through to getting stuck in the wall and having to restart.

TL DR: I would highly recommend buying this is you like very tricky action platformers.

I want to like this game so much, its got so much going for it. The world it creates is so fascinating and intriguing and beautiful. I love exploring it and seeing what there is to find. This game is extremely tightly designed, the level design is really intuitive and rewarding, and the power scaling is super well-tuned; I always feel the right level of power for the area I'm in. Theres a lot to like.

But...
I'm sorry, but I just can't tolerate the moment-to-moment gameplay. The combat just feels super clunky, annoying, and repetitive. Every single combat encounter is exactly the same: catch the enemy's agro, wait for it to telegraph its attack, back up so you don't get hit, then attack while the enemy is vulnerable. There is absolutely no strategy, its just a test of reaction times, basically a quicktime event. And the number of enemies in each room and their respawn times is just ridiculous, it means that 90% of the moment-to-moment gameplay is combat and evasion is near-impossible. Also the movement feels unpolished, (why is the jump arc a triangle?), it makes the platforming feel floaty and unsatisfying.

I'll probably come back to this game at some point, and if I change my mind and end up liking it I'll leave a positive review, but for now I think I'm done putting up with this game.

As a life long lover of metroidvania style games, I was excited to try Hollow Knight out. Unfortunately, taken as a metroidvania, Hollow Knight is terrible.

Exploration is a chore. Your currency drops when you die, ala Souls. You will likely spawn far away from your body and have to slog back to it, taking care not to die again along the way or everything is lost. Your map is basically useless until you find the merchant that sells maps in each area and buy a plan. However by the time you find said merchant most of the area will already be discovered on your own. Even then, for navigation to be useful at all you have to waste a charm space just to have an icon on the map that shows you where you're at in the area. 

The weapon variety is non-existent. You will be swinging the base nail for hours on end until you find a nailsmith that will upgrade you to a slightly more powerful nail. Considering that a lot of the fun in a MV is item and equipment unlocks and progression this is absolutely unforgivable. 

The graphics and soundtrack have a charm to it and the controls are mostly tight is about as much of a compliment as I can give to this game. 

The rabid community that goes along with this game is completely obnoxious as well, btw.

It is absolutely not worth the hype. Two thumbs down. Hard pass.

Sure you can blame me and say that I'm just pathetic and casual and need to "get good" but I see no need to suffer just to get to the enjoyable part of the game (the combat and bosses). I got to a point where I don't think I'll be able to finish the game because in order to reach the next (final?) area you have to do some insane platforming, and I'm not willing to suffer for 5+ hours just to get to the next part to try finish the game.

Hollow Knight is everything you could want in a metroidvania style game. The art and soundtrack are incredible, the controls are responsive, and there are tons of things to do and find. You can easily spend hours playing the game to do everything (and there is more coming still).

10/10. Definitely worth picking up, especially if you catch it on sale, but it is absolutely worth the full price.

Okay. This game is damn, one of the best I've played in a long time, and it's extremely well crafted. I'll start this off by saying one thing. If you want to truly enjoy this game, do NOT look at a guide for your first playthrough. For this game it's very easy to spoil things unintentionally, since it's very open.
Pros:
1. The music. The music sets the mood for each area extremely well, and loops well. Each area has its own unique song that fits not only the visual aspect of the area, but the lore behind it as well.
2. Fluid and natural controls (once you turn on the controller adaptation in the options menu). I was initially frustrated in the delay between my input and the game's response, but the adaptation fixes it perfectly. The reason why I'm praising this (I have a policy to not praise fixing something that shouldn't have been broken) is because the controls are perfectly suited to each ability to start with, but the devs allow you to customize them should you want to. It's a little thing, but a lot of games don't offer it.
3. Open storyline. Once you have two manueverability upgrades you can access basically every single area in the game, and there are no real guidelines as to where you're supposed to be going and how to get there. It makes the game feel a lot more like an actual world and made me feel a lot more immersed than I otherwise would. I loved encountering a new area with new enemies, and every upgrade I acquired I felt I had truly earned.

Cons:
EXCESSIVE backtracking. Once you have the abilities to move around better, there are a lot of areas to go back to and get secrets. So much so that it's nigh impossible to find them all without using a game guide, which kind of spoils the intent of the game, at least for me.

Overall, I give it a 9/10. Would definitely recommend to anyone, and I'd even go so far as to say to NOT play the game when you're mad.

Other bosses are uncreative pathetic jokes, like the trashcan they named "Flukemarm", whose whole boss fight consists of the same single attack, over and over; beating him essentially requires have your weapon upgraded. Of the difficult bosses, the challenge does not reside in your ability to consistently execute your attacks at correct times or quickly dodge attacks. The challenge is - almost invariably - in how many things you can simultaneously track. 

None of the basic enemies pose virtually any threat. Moreover, 90% of the games enemies can simply be ignored to no consequence whatsoever. The only real point in the normal enemies seem to make quickly traveling around a little more annoying.

On the topic of traveling, this game does not only excel in making the map annoying, it also excels in making getting around the most fucking unbearable endeavor. Quick travel stations are seldom, and placed in mostly inconvenient locations. Same goes for the respawn locations, which are either placed in the middle of fucking nowhere or two rooms away from a boss.

A few other things which got on my nerves but I don't necessarily consider a flaw:
-Hidden walls are dumb
-Some bitch just steals your bank money
-Hidden bosses are dumb

On the brighter side, this game is very pretty and holds a high care to detail. Rarely, and object in the foreground can blindspot an enemy. The combat can also feel rewarding, the colosseum was enjoyable.

[spoiler] Lastly, the final boss was absolutely terrible. Expected a boss more difficult than the Failed Champion, but all I got was a crappy boss I could heal through and killed on the first try. The ending was a 10 second cutscene, so the story was another joke by the developers. [/spoiler]

Played 88% of the game diligently, not worth my time.

Appearing deceptively simple with its beautiful yet admittedly cartoonish visuals, a grand orchestral soundtrack compliments a fluid combat system that rewards precision and skill against the game's most challenging bosses. Exploring every inch of the game's vividly detailed sceneries was more of an adventure than a chore and ultimately led to a fantastic game. While the game is fun on PC it feels more at home on a handheld console like the switch, where I beat it.

After 40 hours I realized that I did not enjoy this game. I wanted to, because I like Metroid, Indiegames and all. Controls are awful in this game. They are unresponsive on occasion and there is a delay between commands which makes it extremely difficult to do some things with precision. I shouldn't really use the term delay since the command doesn't get delayed; it gets straight up ignored. 

Some enemies are also downright cheap and unfair. Teleporting enemies can teleport right into you, other enemies can easily trap you somewhere and just keep you pinned without being able to get away, some enemies will hit you multiple times without the ability to dodge, and some enemies will hit you when you hit them. 

One of the worst offenders in this game is hitting something from above and having it hitting you while you do (I'm looking at you, Grimm) or not having the attack even work in the first place and just slamming into the enemy. 

Gorgeous game but can't recommend when the controls are this bad. The enemy stuff can be dealt by learning the enemies and their patterns and anticipating the problems with but not the controls. The unresponsiveness will get to you eventually unless you can input as perfect as a speedrunner.

I came back to this game a few weeks ago because everyone I spoke to about this game said that I must be wrong and hadn't really played the game, despite the fact I had beaten the Radiance. I will say this, you can go through most of this game, even up to the first ending without too much trouble. The world is fun to explore, the lore and characters are interesting, and the artwork is phenomenal. They'll even be some really enjoyable bosses too. But the more you complete the game beyond the first ending, the more the problems show. Honestly, had I stuck with just doing the first ending, I might have enjoyed it, but going further is just a punishment on oneself. Godhome reeks of this. Lost inputs are extremely common when you're trying for split second timing constantly. Cheap attacks that have ridiculous dodge circumstances are commonplace now. You'll need to grind your ass off to know how to exploit the AI to give yourself time to dodge. Pogoing, a main mechanic, is basically harmful to use here. If it even works, (which a lot of the time, it doesn't; you just take damage) you'll more than likely be punished by a boss attack that's unavoidable while you're up there, an extra mob will be spawned on you, or if they're the Watchers, they'll just stay in that nail-impervious ball form. The shadow dodge is also very unreliable, and seems to be the input that gets ignored the most, which is terrible since a few fights require it to successfully dodge certain attacks (extremely bad design). I've tested the input with two different PS4 controllers and a keyboard in three different USB slots. Inputs are coming through to the PC but for whatever reason, the game ignores it sometimes. Even worse when it fires, but doesn't actually work as a shadow dodge so you just run into an enemy or attack (Yes, when its off cooldown, uses that cooldown, and still looks like a shadow dodge). 

January 28th, 2023 note

Ever since I played Symphony of the Night I was really excited to play another highly praised metroidvania style of game again. However sadly I didn't enjoy it as much as I thought I would. Is the game bad? Hell no! Far from it! This game's presentation is top notch! The graphics, the visuals, the atmosphere it's a 10/10. The characters are interesting and fun to interact with. The boss battles are fantastic!(most of the time, few of them I found kinda cheap and not very well designed) 
And the soundtrack is very beautiful! Well, all these things are all well done, however my problems with the game was with the gameplay and the exploration. Though the gameplay is pretty simplistic, I had a lot of trouble with the controls, especially with the wall jumping. A lot of times I swore I did press the jump button from the wall on time, but I still fell down most of the time, giving me a lot of frustrating moments, and this mixtured with the harder boss fights and hard platforming sections, it was very tedious. There were also other little issues like this that were just frustrating overall gameplaywise. And then there's the exploration, in the beginning it's not too complicated, whenever you got a new upgrade you knew where to go next. But towards the end of the game, I just couldn't tell where to go, so I had enough and had to look up on the internet, and it turned out I had to go through a hidden passage way in order to progress, I probably wouldn't have been able to figure this out on my own, or maybe I could've, but then again, that would've been just wasted time of my life.(which is the cancer of gaming if you ask me) This wouldn't be a problem if the map indicated which way I could go, but it showed me as if I looked around everywhere at that point.
And after finshing the game, I still had few other items to find for a 100% completion, but at this point I simply could not be bothered anymore. I was bored because during my exploration, not even combat held up the fun factor anymore.

So yeah overall I didn't enjoy it as much, there were couple of pretty frustrating parts, and by the end I was kinda bored playing this. But it was still a fun game for one playthrough. I do recommend it playing it through at least once, especially for this price! 7/10

Hollow Knight is a 2D platformer metroidvania which is very challenging and fresh.
Ive heard it had some resemblance with Dark Souls, and it was tough, and since DS is probably my all-time favorite saga, and I like hard games, I thought I should give it a try. 

When I first started playing it I had my doubts, because I hardly every play 2D platformers and I thought maybe I wouldnt enjoy it as much because of that, but the art style and the level design made up for it, and I ended up liking it.
The main issue for me in my first playthrough was that the world was a bit too open, and you required a lot of upgrades and new moves to be able to explore it to its full extent. There was one time when I discovered 3 new areas at once, and that made me feel quite overwhelmed.

But, to be honest, the only part I disliked about the game was The Grimm Troupe DLC. (Note that all the DLCs are free and they are downloaded together with the main game) This DLC just adds layers of backtracking to areas you thought you wouldnt need to step into again, and the final boss of this DLC, in comparison with all the other bosses in the game, it is a bit unfair and frustrating, to say the least.

Other than that, the game is great. It has a very good level design, although a bit too open, the OST fits the game quite nicely aswell, and the areas and bosses are usually fair and challeging, and you can beat them all just with some patience and adapting to each different enemy and enviroment. The combat is simple, yet intense and the story is quite interesting aswell, even though its kinda hidden, and you have to explore thoroughly if you dont want to miss any part of it, and the art is very well done.

Overall, a great game, and if you like challenges, this game is probably for you, just dont let its looks deceive you at first, haha.

Absolutely stunning game!! If you're a fan of Ori and the Blind Forest you will love this one. The aesthetics, theme, style, and music are very very similar but the gameplay is far more focused on combat than platforming.

Things I liked about the game:

- Absolutely stunning visuals, sound and music
- Sound Design is incredibly good (audio matches the environment you're in and makes it very immersive)
- Very original art style, characters and story
- LOTS of content, a hell of a lot of content. It took me roughly 23 hrs to finish the main game and 10+ hrs to complete it fully and get all possible endings.
- Well worth the price tag
- Multiple beautifully crafted environments with their own unique themes
- TONS of interesting and unique boss fights with multiple options of how to approach each fight
- Lots and lots of secrets and hidden content that if found is very rewarding
- Game is very challenging at times, but not so much to the point where it's punishing
- Unique and memorable characters
- Smooth gameplay, polished controls, minimal glitches/bugs, overall a super well optimised game
- Supports 144Hz refresh rate

Things I disliked about the game:

- The death mechanic in this game can very punishing as you need to return to the place you were killed in order to get your ingame money back (used for buying items to progress as well as character/weapon upgrades), this is especially frustrating if you die in a particularly far away location from your last checkpoint.

- The story is very complex, abstract and layered at first glance, at times can be a little hard to follow what is going on and what you're meant to be doing. Although this is more of a personal gripe, some people may like the way they made it. I personally found it all very confusing and hard to follow or understand fully.

- Some of the exploration relies on uncovering essentially 'secret' areas to progress. Although this adds a rewarding feeling for discovering an entirely new area that you first thought was just a small secret, it can be a little frustrating when you get stuck in the game only to realise you missed an ENTIRE section because it was so well hidden (ie The Hive, and Kingdom's Edge). Although then again, with the internet what it is today, I highly recommend bookmarking this game's wiki for guidance.

- I know they went for a very free-roam approach with this game, but it would be nice if the game would have more of a progression, or at least some form of guidance on what you should be doing next, in terms of events that unfold rather than relying on the player to happen across everything. It's easy to miss things.

Overall, the negative aspects of the game are really more down to personal preferance and brutally honest nitpicking based on my personal experience. The positives of this game completely outweigh any shortcomings it may have, it's a phenomenally good game.
