If you ever wanted to play a Metroidvania where almost everything is 10/10, this game is for you. The art style is awesome, the soundtrack is awesome, the story is awesome, the world building is awesome and the gameplay is awesome. 

The moderately hard difficulty makes it so Hollow Knight poses a challenge for those who seek one but still isn't impossible to beat for those who might want a more regular, casual gameplay experience. What I especially like about this game is the fact that the [spoiler]Charm[/spoiler] system provides a whole variety of different and interesting play styles for everyone, I'm over 45 hours into this game and the gameplay still hasn't gotten anywhere near stale. The story [spoiler]with multiple endings[/spoiler] that slowly and naturally unfolds while you play the game without the need of overly long enduring cut scenes is something I really enjoyed as well, not to mention that there are some really unique and lovable characters. Hollow Knight also gets constant updates with new content, there's so much different stuff to explore other than the main story, so many additional bosses to beat, so many collectibles to find which means that you can always come back to this game after a while and enjoy a new and refreshing experience of gameplay content. Last but definitely not least the beautiful art style and soundtrack is the icing on the cake, watching things move on their own while listening to some really fitting music is a joy to the eye and ear already even when there's no actual gameplay involved at all. 

The only black mark I'd give this game is the fact that at some point progressing might become almost impossible without a guide, not because of the difficulty but rather because of how little information the game gives you regarding where to go. It's still definitely better though than all of those modern AAA titles that constantly hold your hand with a way point mapping system, tempting you into turning on autopilot mode and not actually playing the game.

All in all I can comfortably say that despite this slight flaw, Hollow Knight is a 10/10 game. To all the die-hard Metroidvania fans out there: This game is for you!

It's been a long time since I have been so immersed in a game. When I first got this game, I sunk 40 hours into it without even trying. That feeling of being lost, wandering and discovering, learning, and slowly building yourself to be this unstoppable force never felt so rewarding. 

What originally drew me to this game was the art style, but I fell in love with every part of it. The music, the atmosphere, the characters, the enemies, the world, everything. I found myself surprised at how much I liked it. I went in completely blind not knowing anything other than it was from a small indie studio.

What seemed to be marketed as a simple 2D, sidescrolling adventure unravelled into a suprisingly dark and emotional journey. It is hard to put into words exactly how I felt throughout the game, save for that fact a sense of accomplishment when I finished it, and that rare sense that something was now missing now that there was nothing left to do. One of those rare feelings that you wish you could have your memory wiped so you could experience something for the first time again; that's what I felt with Hollow Knight.

And, the fact that it is only $15. I am sad that I did not know about the Kickstarter before the release, otherwise I would have been happy to help fund this project.

Bravo, Team Cherry, bravo. I eagerly look forward to see what other things you are capable of.

I'm giving Hollow Knight a negative review based on my opinion that its review score is too high right now. 
Honestly, I'd still give Hollow Knight a 7/10 or so, but I don't think it's the 10/10 game everyone has been saying. I can appreciate several points of Hollow Knight, but it's just not my kind of game. 

And now I'll explain why: 
Soundtrack: 9/10
Art: 10/10
Gameplay: 3/10

Okay, okay, put the torches and pitchforks away please. The atmosphere of Hollow Knight is great, there's no denying that. The creepy-cute bugs that you meet are all interesting, and the soundtrack just puts you in an adventurous mood. However, I hated all the housekeeping that the game makes you do just to play that game. The thing that absolutely got on my nerves the most is that you have to take several steps to just use the map. You need to buy items so that the map will draw itself, so that it will show your location, so that it will show fast-travel points or shops, and if you don't find the map maker in each area, you can't make the map for that area at all. It just seemed like this was holding back the progression of the game for no reason. And the map in Hollow Knight is pretty huge, so you will definitely need a map at some point. 

The gameplay is fun, but a little repetitive. Lots of the common enemies you face are more just annoying than a challenge, but I enjoyed fighting the unique bosses that have lots of different moves. 

I'll admit, I dropped Hollow Knight after only about 5 hours, but I have very little understanding of the story so far. I know about the setup and that's it. I'm not sure if the story gets better later in the game, but from what I understand, Hollow Knight is more concerned with you beating some challenges/areas than driving a story. 

If you are into challenging games that you may have to pour a lot of time into, Hollow Knight should be easy for you to fall in love with because of its challenging but different areas, great artistry, and musical score. For me personally, I play games for the story, and there was just too much hindering the progression of the story to make me feel it was worth my time.

Original review written 5 hours into the game:

Really wish I could like this more than I do. The artstyle, graphics, animation, soundtrack, just every technical aspect of this game is excellent. But the gameplay just isn't fun.

I've played for around 5 and a half hours right now and just don't feel any motivation to start the game again.

Bored of combat that seems to boil down to the worst of Dark Souls' "dodge and punish" style encounters.


Revised review after many more hours of gameplay:

The first 10 hours of this game were exactly as I described. Just a total slog to get through, genuine 2/10 experience. 
As you unlock more movement tech, the travel gets less tedious and as you get more adept at the combat you tend to just breeze through old areas a lot quicker.
Unfortunately the combat really doesn't get much better than described in the first iteration of this review. You dodge and then you punish you enemy's miss with an attack of your own. Sometimes you can't figure out an enemy's attacks and die until you learn their patterns. This happens a lot with bosses in the second half of the game, where you just feel like you're getting totally screwed over by patterns you have zero chance of predicting or reacting to on your first try.

Context for the next part of this review: I'm the kind of person who feels almost forced to 100% games and go for every achievement listed on a game's page no matter how stupid it may be. Keep that in mind for the following complaints.
- Having an achievement that requires me to beat your game in under 5 hours sucks.
- Having an achievement that requires me to 100% your game in Steel Soul mode (whatever that even is; knowing this game it's not pleasant) really sucks.
- Locking me out of most of the good endings (or so my expert friend told me) unless I play a totally out of place Super Meat Boy area is awful game design. I'm not really bothered by the difficulty of this area but the way that difficulty ties into the systems of this game. Because the game is a Metroidvania with a health system that simply doesn't lend itself to tight punishing platforming. I didn't mind most of the minor platforming challenges during the rest of the game but building and entire area around that just showed me how unpleasant Hollow Knight could become.

So overall I guess if you can look past a really unpleasant first third of a game and are able to ignore non-fun optional challenges, this game is probably for you. 
But with the first *and* last third leaving a sour taste in my mouth, and the middle third only being an 8/10 experience at best, I really can't recommend this.

Wanted to love this game. Just a few issues that compound and complicate gameplay.

Incredibly large map with loads to explore, but that makes significant progress difficult. Occasional fast travel locations alleviate some of these stresses, but exploring new areas doesn't really give much room for such conveniences.

Navigation is strange. At first all you're given is a brief idea of where you ought to be going, until one significant event that then unlocks an important key ability and also marks three goals on your map. Then it's just up to you to figure out how to get to those locations and even whether you have the necessary abilities to do so.

The corpse run trope is in play here: when you die, your Shade remains where you die, along with all of your money. Also, your magic container breaks and only holds half of the maximum amount of magic unless you make your way back to the point where you died. Heaven help you if you die near a boss battle. This corpse run mechanic seems very unnecessary.

Combat is tight and intuitive, if very basic. This reviewer experienced significant input lag issues which, according to firsthand research, have also been occasionally reported by other players. Buyer beware.

A fun and incredibly challenging game. The combat at core is very simple but can be tailored to your liking using a system of charms. You can go full on offensive swordmaster, dodging and attacking left and right, a spellcaster with various abilities to use, or even a defensive tank where you just receive and return the damage to your enemies. Want a general all round build? Or a mix and match from other builds? Go for it! The opportunities are vast, and each boss and map encourages you to use different builds depending on the situation.

The visuals and audio in this game are absolutely on point. I love the graphical effects when in combat. The music takes you to another world, always keep track of time or else you'll be lost in this game. Use of headsets is recommended!

In general, the difficulty of this game is a bit on the hard side. Mechanical skills and reflexes are trained in this game. Take this game's nonlinearity, and it can be even more difficult. It can be frustrating if you're exploring and suddenly meet a boss when you're not correctly equipped or haven't unlocked some abilities yet.

All in all, even though it is challenging, it feels so satisfying when you finally beat the bosses in this game. I highly recommend this game!

Well, Hollow Knight is one of those rare games that comes along and is the exact opposite of that--it's an antidote. I didn't like Hollow Knight and I spent 175 hours trying to find the fun in it.

The combat was very simple and passive. There's a lack of agency in combat as your enemies are the ones that dictate the pace of the fight and the actions available to you pretty much entirely. Put simply, combat is based around avoiding enemy attacks and using windows of vulnerability to retaliate and I rarely, if ever, felt like I was the one taking the initiative in battle, so I found fights tedious at best and frustrating at worst.

I've beaten all the bosses, cleared every pantheon (end-game boss rushes) with all bindings, found all collectables, beat each boss without getting hit etc etc. Basically, unless I lucked out completing all the game's challenges without finding new strategies, I don't think I feel this way about the combat because I'm bad at it or because of a skill issue. I'm not bringing this up to brag or impress anyone with my epic gamer skills but to prove my experience with and knowledge of combat in this game. This game has a reputation for being difficult and as a result, criticism of the combat, the majority of content in the game, is far too often deflected as being a result of a "skill issue". In this way, it's a bit like Dark Souls.

Hollow Knight is a well made game and clearly my opinion places me in the minority of players. It has some good platforming and the art design suits it very well. I wouldn't say this is anything near a bad game, and I can see the love and care that went into every aspect of it. However, I'm not playing Silksong if the combat is going to have the same philosophy behind it as Hollow Knight's, and I definitely don't plan on playing Hollow Knight again as it just isn't an experience I can enjoy.

A wonderful game, but mostly for a hardcore gamer.

Hollow Knight is polished to perfection. The music, the graphics, the Lore, tight controls. Every little detail was treated with attention and love. And it really shows in this game. 

While this game has an outstanding following (go to yt to see some speedruns to see how many things you don't know about this game!), and it's really gorgeous in all aspects, there is however one caveat that makes me not recommend it: DIFFICULTY.

This game was clearly built with a hardcore gamer in mind. To actually finish it, you will have to mostly know all the areas by heart. That means you will have to traverse them many, many times, dying a lot, retracing your steps to die again and again. The map system in this game is really annoying and doesn't really help you (in most times, you get a map of the area AFTER you explore it). The boss fights are really punishing, and if you play without a guide, they will be HARD, really, each taking at least several attempts. 

While I get the point, that the difficulty level and so few save points in the game are by design. However, this means that if you're a working adult, and simply can spare 1-2 hours a day to play, Hollow Knight will be simply frustrating. Unless you mean to spend the whole weekend exploring the areas.

Somewhere around halfway through the game I was fed up with it and simply hacked the app memory to freeze my life points. So I had an invulnerability of sorts (there were still insta-deaths when falling out of bounds). This allowed me to complete the game, and I am really glad I did that. Some areas (White Palace!) required for a "true" ending were frustrating even with this cheat enabled.

So while I think this is a wonderful game, I truly cannot recommend it, unless you are a hardcore gamer or want to be frustrated. Or you can cheat the game like I did. Then it becomes a much more pleasant experience.

[b]Gameplay [/b]
The gameplay part of it, in its core, is very straightforward. You run around, jump and swing your sword. Seemingly nothing fancy, but I found it does the trick throughout the game. You learn some more mobility skills, spells and attacks on the way. While some of them are necessary for progression, some upgrades are totally optional and you can finish the game without even discovering them.
Apart from attacking and moving around, you also get a healing skill that's going to be your companion and aid from the beginning to the very end. If you get enough soul (mana), you may heal. As simple as that. Though it does require time to activate and you might find it impossible to restore your health in some tight spots, even during boss batles there are certain windows of healing opportunity that you can use.

[b]Combat and stuff [/b]
Hollow Knight is mostly about platforming and totally about combat. There are dozens of unique enemies with their own attacks patterns. It's mostly quite easy to work them out, though visiting a new area will definitely pose some difficulties or even result in dying once or twice. It's fine, the game is fair in a way that it gives you all the information and it's all about your own skill level and execution. It's never the programmers' fault, you are to blame and you know it. "Why did I do that" was the phrase I used so many times while dying again to a boss monster, it made me realize the game was not at fault, I was. I suck and I know it.
Having that said, you get better with time. For me it was hours, for some it's less.

[b] Visuals and Music [/b]
The game is gorgeous and the music is amazing. For me personally, the game may look meh, I'm not picky since I don't own a nice gaming PC. Music, however, is a game changer.
The visuals in the game are just gorgeous. The game is beautiful, kept in more dark tones of blues and blacks, occasionally greens in certain areas, but there is a feeling of melancholy if not sadness and desperation. 
When it comes to music it's just perfect. Not pretentious, quite simple to be frank, but sets the mood for the whole experience. There are certain leitmotifs that one quickly recognizes, and several pieces that simply stand out. I love video games soundtracks, being in love with what Darren Korb composes for Supergiant Games and what Nobuo Uematsu (and Yoko Shimomura) created for Squaresoft. The soundtrack for Hollow Knight will forever be in the pantheon for its simple emotional tunes.

[b]ETC [/b]
Now, if you haven't noticed, I fell in love with Hollow Knight. I'm not the best at it, but the game is extremely satisfactory. Every goal you achieve pays off and makes you feel great about yourself. You feel accomplished.
The lore is incredible if you have enough time to investigate it. In not, use the wiki or some really good youtube creators who have deciphered a lot of it (just play the game first, at least as much as you can, because spoilers and stuff).
AND, most importantly for me, it runs on my 7y.o. potato laptop. If you don't know if it's playable when you meet the minimal reqs - yes, it most probably is.

Go grab the game while it's on sale or not. You are not overpaying, the game is ridiculously cheap for what it is even when you pay the full price. You will love everything about it. The plot. The visuals. The music. The challenge.

So in truth. I always try to focus and look at the good in games. But this game right here is just... Well even if I try to focus on the good, I rarely like to use the term "Masterpiece." 

Hollow knight is by far one of my favorite games. It just is. I love it. It's so good. And I would definitely put it into the "Masterpiece" Catagory, but it just barely makes it into the catagory. 

So, what is Hollow Knight? Well, welcome yourself into the unforgiving world of dark and devoid bugs, oddities in a land of the strange, and an adventure that will have those of you hooked, stuck playing for hours without even a moments respite. The story is captivating, game play intense, with every fight on your toes waiting and wondering. If you're a fan of the Dark Souls-esque grind and punishment style games. You've found your platforming match. The game is just point blank good. Team Cherry did an amazing job.

The graphics are a consistant style, dark and gloomy, but they really just stick to you. You feel like this is a world that must be saved. You reallly see the longevity of the story's grasp on in every single area. 

Backtracking, boss fighting, exploring; it's all in here. And it's all well done. 

THE MUSIC IS AMAZING! I listen to the sound track over other games, wishing the game I'm playing was as good as Hollow Knight. The beauty of the game paired masterfully with the music. Oh it is soo good. 

My only gripe is that I'm not as good as I wish I were on this game. I'm decent, no doubt, but I could definitely be better. And that's something I personally love about a game. Every player has the ability to play a game, but only those who really work to hone the skill and talent it takes to be good at the game, really see their skills thrive.  

Every area in the game feels crafted with loving detail, and nothing feels left out or devoid. Hollow, if you will.

I know I'm in a minority here - just look at the "Overwhelmingly Positive" in the top right of the page. There's so much to like about this game: a gorgeous art style with plenty of environmental variety; a great soundtrack; a varied upgrade and build system; solid controls and combat.

Personally I just couldn't handle the frustration of it. When you arrive in a new area, you have to scramble your way around without access to a map or any other navigational aid. Even when you do pick up a map, large areas are hidden from you and important landmarks aren't indicated. It's very very easy to miss crucial areas - like fast travel points and save points - and as a result you spend a huge amount of time backtracking and retracing your steps. It even inherits the mechanic from the Souls games of confiscating all of your currency when you die unless you trudge all the way back to your corpse without dying again.

The level of repetition required on every death, and the ease of getting completely lost again, incentivise a highly risk-averse playstyle where you never experiment with what's available and indeed you often dodge and weave through enemies rather than even fighting them if it's been a while since you reached a bench.

There are two scenarios in which you'll really enjoy this game: 
- You're willing to go in completely blind and die over and over again until (I suppose?) you get good enough at exploration and combat that you're not worried about dying or getting lost anymore
- You don't mind heavy reliance on external guides to tell you where to find save points, fast travel points, item stores and powerups

Unfortunately I'm not in either category, and this game really isn't for me.

First things first: this is not a hard game. The only "souls-like" thing about it is the fact you drop your money on death and have to go back and get it because you also lose half your max mana/estus equivalent (they're the same resource), adding a layer of unnecessary tedium on top of getting sent back to a checkpoint.  Every boss can be defeated on your first try because you have 5 hits before you die on top of your replenishing heal. It's like if you started Dark Souls with twice as much health and estus, and estus refilled a charge every three attacks you landed. 

The first bit of the game is spent doing something that no experienced developer would ask of the player: you have to manually purchase the ability to update your map, then separately purchase each of the markers for shops, save points, fast travel points, etc. As opposed to the industry standard of maps that update as you enter areas. Apparently Hollow refers to the main character's skull. 

For the first third of the game, you essentially don't get any upgrades. By which I mean the ones you can get in this time aren't really additions to a functioning character but rather remove debuffs you begin the game with. 

I.e.  The first thing you'll notice is that by default successful hits knock you, the player, away from opponents. Even start-of-the-game 2-hit crawlers. This breaks the fluidity of movement since many platforms contain a crawler, so you have to wait until they're far enough from the edge that you'll be able to both attack and not fall down onto a hazard (either spikes or water).  You're essentially given Sonic the Hedgehog's knockback punishment, but here it's a part of successful attacks. The first charm you'll probably want is the charm that removes that feature. Second is that there's a lag between your attacks that could've been solved with a more fluid animation, which you don't get the charm to fix until much later. 

It's like the early Metroid and Castlevania games where aiming diagonally was a power-up, and games in this genre shouldn't be making this mistake any more. 

The level design leaves basically no hints for progression, along with that bit about needing to purchase map markers. Where every Metroid game had special colored doors to let you know what weapon you needed to break them with, this game has no such thing. The map being a big part of the issue since there are no markers to indicate places you can probably go with the next movement upgrade are item acquired. NPC's aren't any help either, as they're scripted to move at certain undefined points and offer no information a majority of the time. 

There isn't exploration in this game. That's a bold-faced lie. What there is is looping, because the map and level designs are so samey and there are no notes about where to progress.

For $7, it's an okay, clearly-indie first attempt at a metroidvania. I wouldn't pay $15 for it.

One of my favorite games of last year, Hollow Knight is an indie Metroidvania developed by Team Cherry, a 2 person studio based in Australia. You are a masked bug thrown into the beautiful hand-drawn world of Hallownest, with only a dull nail to defned yourself. However, as you progress, you acquire more upgrades to help with navigation and combat. The boss fights are challenging, but not unfair (at least, unti you get to a certain Trial later in the game), and reward skillful mastery of the game's mechanics. For the hardened veterans and achievement hunters, there is also Steel Soul mode, essentailly New Game+, which now presents permadeath as a condition in the campaign. The controls are tight and responsive (gamepad is recommended), and the game comes with every DLC released thus far (2 as of this review, with a 3rd announced for late August), all for free. If you want challenge, depth, and value for your money, do not hesitate to pick this up.

Hollow Knight is a good example of why Steam's review system is flawed. Don't get me wrong, it's a good game, and I would definitely recommend it on sale... however, it's not really as good as some reviews have made it out to be, and I feel a simple 'Recommend/Don't Recommend' won't do it any justice.

There are some moments where the game is very engaging, and that's when you've discovered something new. There's a ton of polish put into this game, and everything is satisfying when you have a 'goal' - the combat is solid, albeit a tad buttery, the art style is great, and the exploration (when you actually have a path to do so) is very compelling.

However, once you've explored as much of an area as possible, there are much longer periods of the game spent merely on backtracking. This is where Hollow Knight's grind comes in. 

You spend ages going through rooms that you've already seen, looking for a potential entrance you might have missed, until you eventually notice you spent an hour doing nothing of interest, and you switch to another game. Then, you see other people online praise this game heavily, come back to it, finally find a new area to explore, and the process repeats. For me, I went through this process 4 times, before deciding to write this review.

I think that Team Cherry intended for players to play this game in longer sessions, like 3-4 hours worth, when you've got the ability to go through the map with a finer comb, and relax while doing so. In those scenarios, I can definitely see the appeal. Unfortunately, I rarely have this liberty, and likewise, most casual gamers probably won't. And when you have an hour of spare time, and plenty of other games to play...

Now, considering that I've only played 3 and 1/2 hours of this game, it's worth noting that I might be just stuck in an early-game grind, and it might get better later on. I might play this game some more later, and if my opinions change, I'll change the review. Let me repeat - if you find this game on sale (which it is!), it's actually a great deal, and if you're leaning towards a purchase, you could do worse than this. However, it's not worth all its hype, and people on the fence should do some more research before buying.

If you like games that give you a challenge, this game is for you. If you like games with a Dark Soulsian cryptic story, this game is for you. If you like cute little insect people, this game is for you.

This game went from being a refund to one of my favourite games of all time.

Hollow Knight . . . I didn't think much of it when I first saw it at half-price for Steam Winter Sale 2017. As of writing this, I just can't remember what got me to try it out, but I assume it was its stellar reviews and high ratings. I had never tried any metroidvanias prior, so this game was a completely-new experience. 

From start to finish, Hollow Knight was gorgeous. It sucks players into an intricate world, brimming with distinct characters, enchanting atmospheres, fantastic music, and a mysterious overarching story. Fueled by sheer curisostiy, you will find yourself exploring the breathtaking land of Hallownest for days on end, gradually piecing together its lore.

Not sure if other players can relate, but Hollow Knight possesses a very unique energy, quite similar to Dark Souls in a sense. Regardless, the game is NOT a Dark Souls clone, nor is it "Souls-like" in my opinion. The combat is not as slow and methodical (for the most part), and there aren't a wide range of weapons (it's just you and your trusty nail :D). Instead, it captures that feeling of triumph after beating that boss/enemy/area you struggled on for awhile. Likewise, characters that you meet throughout Hallownest will share their stories with you, and thus, give you fragments of the game's lore (that you are expected to piece together, but feel free to watch Hollow Knight Youtubers; my favs: Mossbag, Reylea, Rusty). Although, if you are a Dark Souls fan (like me), I do recommend you give Hollow Knight a chance, and I gurantee you'll love it. 

Ultimately, BUY HOLLOW KNIGHT ! Also, in the wise words of Hornet, "GIT GUDDDDDDDDDDDDDDD." Thanks for reading guys ! 

TL;DR Pls try out Hollow Knight ("No cost too great");  DOMA DOMA DOMADOMADOMA !

I really don't care if people make fun of me for writing this review, but I don't enjoy Hollow Knight. I really don't.
And I know my opinion probably doesn't matter on the internet, but I don't care. I'm going to explain my reason anyway.

I only have 1 reason in particular why I find Hollow Knight to be so un-enjoyable and headache-inducing for me, and whilst yeah, it's only 1 reason, it's a pretty big reason that, if it were to be changed, would require a lot of work to the core concept of the game.

The lack of any direction.
I know that the lack of any direction is an intentional game design choice by the developers, and that the whole genre of "Metroidvania" involves this, but it is a very flawed design choice as it can lead to players getting frustrated at how confusing it is to figure out what your objective is (and, particularly, where you're actually meant to go.) This also means, for example, that at the very beginning of the game, you may accidentally stumble upon a boss battle that you aren't meant to be at yet.

So yeah, I don't know if I am just utter garbage at the game, or if the game is just "not for me" (which, in all fairness, it probably isn't), but I think my reason for why I dislike the game is an understandable reason.

"Hard" is one thing. But this game puts stuff in that's just un-fun punishment. Bosses with homing jumps that change direction in midair to punish you for trying to dodge, but don't try to dodge and the same move *doesn't* change and hits you anyway. Projectiles that are moving in one direction in a pattern suddenly have one that goes against the pattern (even though the pattern continues further) to punish you for trying to dodge. It's just feel bad after feel bad. 

You don't feel like you're learning the fight, because moves are indistinguishable from each other and resolve based on what *you* do. You tried to attack this time? Boss charged forward and hits you. You tried to anticipate his charge? This time it stayed still so your leap actually make you hit him and take damage. It gets really old really fast. Ultimately, you can't help but feel like the designers are snickering at you.

It's a pity, there's a lot of cool stuff going on with the rest of the game.

hollow knight just loves to waste your time.

this game could be great: fantastic atmosphere, tight controls, engaging combat, excellent soundtrack. but if you're making a "difficult" game (this one's not really that difficult, but you ARE gonna die to some of the bosses or surprise-enemy-waves), why in the world would you make the player do repetetive, boring and tedious stuff over and over and over again to get another try? this annoys me to no end. this game makes you run through multiple screens repeatedly, fight the same boring trash mobs and do the same very basic plattforming over and over again just to get another try at the thing that killed you. this gets annoying, boring and repetitive very quickly.

there are times when you enter a room unsuspectingly, get locked up and have to fight several waves of enemies. the last bench you sat on (=checkpoint) is >9000 miles away? have fun running back and wasting your time. oh, and be careful not to die a second time before you get to your corpse to pick up your money - which can easily happen due to rushing through the rooms because of sheer boredom - because you will lose all of it on you if you do. this also leads to not wanting to try anything new and risk dying, casually explore new territory or just enjoy the atmosphere, because the next timewaster could be just around the corner.

also, the amount of backtracking and just running around trying to get to somewhere you haven't been before is just ridiculous. this game has way, waaay too few quicktravel spots so that even if you take the closest one, you will sometimes run 10 minutes. i am currently at a point in the game where i have ~10 unexplored "open ends" on my map. getting to each one takes an eternity running through already explored territory and most of the time those open ends really are just dead ends for me since i'm lacking an attack, an item or something else that would let me progress.

and that's a shame. if this game wouldn't love wasting the player's time as much as it does, i could easily recommend it and would actually enjoy it. but making the player do basic stuff over and over and over again just to get another try at the actual interesting part or making them backtrack through already explored territory for hours is just very bad game design in my oppinion. and i am kind of puzzled why they would make such a bad design choice in an otherwise great game.

Hollow Knight is an oddly incredible experience. It does take a page from the silent protagonist trope. It works for this game. If the character was more like Peter Parker the mood would not have been terrifying. The stoicism was necessary for this character to make it to end of the game. I played and got really tense, nervous, or some other form of fear. The game has a way of pulling you into the unknown. It does a really good job with the level design, but I do have to say I would have preferred having the dash at the beginning of the game. It would have made the pacing and traversal better. It is odd for a game that came out last year to not have controllable dodge distance. I still hold this complaint because some of the enemies will teleport into your dash, which you have no control after dashing, so you take damage. It would be fine if there was no contact damage. It would have also been fine if the dash was controllable, then there wouldn't be a need to complain about the contact damage because I could easily avoid dashing into an enemy. It really doesn't help that the second upgrade for the dash is one invincible dash every couple seconds. It would work better because the dash is not controllable, therefore the dash can't be spammed entirely. Following traversal is the combat. The game only has for directions of attack, yet developers have already done eight way shooting a decade or two before this game. Sure, the attacks do have some AOE effect, but there are clear sections of the attacks that don't count as a hit. Level design is a bit bland because there aren't any slopes, but the game makes up for it with environmental hazards, puzzles, or traversal. Highyl recommend playing this game. I was just pointing out nitpicks. It's mainly gameplay aspects because the narrative is few and far between to discuss, also spoilers. I do like this narrative, so I don't want to spoil the narrative because it is good.

I originally didn't like the game after I beat the first few bosses, got lost for two hours, and stopped playing. Fortunately, I tried starting a new file a few years later and have now joined everyone else in thinking this is one of the best games of the decade.

 - Great unique world design
 - Great interconnected map and consistently good variety of areas
 - Great enemy and boss design
 - responsive combat system with many movement options which allows you to pogo off enemies
 - rewarding exploration
 - innovative charm items which change gameplay drastically

My one criticism is that is does start slowly in terms of unlocking the movement/combat abilities, so the combat feels quite simplistic and less dynamic in the early game, especially if you end up getting lost and need to backtrack a lot it can get tedious.

Overall, highly recommended even if you don't usually like metroidvanias

Hollow knight can be an enjoyable game with good personality, due to it's focus on the miniature world of beetles, and interesting exploration through well designed and aesthetically good, although often similar, areas.

However, the combat is quite simplistic and soon becomes repetitive (largely just slashing at enemies). Also, it can be tedious travelling through the map, even with the transport system, as finding the next area is often frustrating, partially due to the inventive combat system. I found I spent a lot of time wondering around the map, checking every entrance to advance and getting little reward for the exploration I was doing. Bosses were also fairly forgettable due to the combat system's simplicity.

However, I wouldn't say this is a bad game and I sure many people could enjoy the exploration aspect more than I did, which could make the game more interesting. For me, I can't recommend it due to the reasons above but if you like this genre a lot, the game might be worth a try.

tl;dr A good action game packed into a bad Metroidvania. 6/10.

The good:
- Tight, responsive controls
- Simple yet satisfying combat mechanics
- Well-balanced, challenging boss fights 
- Charms give you a lot of options with respect to customizing your playstyle
- Excellent atmosphere, sound design, and visual style
- Lore is distributed throughout the world very well, between NPCs, readable inscriptions, and item descriptions. One of my favorite parts of the game is [spoiler] the Dream Nail, which if used on NPCs and enemies lets you read their thoughts which adds a ton to making the game world feel complete. [/spoiler]

The bad:
- Area design is uninspired, with most areas just being flat platforms strewn about in a seemingly random manner. There aren't as many unique platforming challenges to each area as I would like.
- Areas have many seemingly useless dead ends and loopbacks, making it easy to get lost and forcing backtracking.
- Fighting basic enemies is often boring because even if you get hit, you can use the i-frames to kill them and get back enough soul to heal any damage they dealt.
- Level design combined with the map system makes exploration a chore. I love Metroidvanias (SOTN is in my top 5 all-time), but I just don't feel motivated to explore in this game outside of finding the way to the next boss.
- Enemies often blend into the monochromatic backgrounds and the particle effects can obscure projectiles. This is a HUGE problem because the game expects you to read and avoid incoming attacks or die.  
- Some of the vocal performances are pretty annoying. I often avoid using fast travel because I find the voice acting for the stag beetle character grating.
- While the base art style is great, I feel as though many of the character designs don't do enough to differentiate themselves from the rest of the game and stand out as memorable.


Overall, Hollow Knight is a game with amazing highs, but one that will require you to slog through hours of poorly thought out level design. I'm the type of person that likes to explore every nook and cranny in my Metroidvania/adventure games, but in HK I have more fun just rushing through to get to the next peak.

So, this game is good - well made, smooth, nice artwork etc.  Very well done.  You might be curious why I gave a thumbs down.  There are multiple reasons, here they are:

1.  This game is not meant for people who have normal lives.  You have to have a lot of free time on your hands to complete this game.  I went into this game thinking I could beat it in just a couple days or less - no chance.  Maybe it's my taste in games, but I'm not crazy about games that take 30+ hours to beat - I like games that have a definitive start and finish. 
2.  It's very dark.  You will play it for awhile and come out of it feeling like you've been sucked dry of all your energy, which leads me to my next point.
3.  The game turns into a grind.  If you die, you must traverse a long range of obstacles to get back to where you were.  If you want to explore another side of the map, you may spend half an hour or more just getting there.  
4.  I had to stop playing this game because I found myself with nowhere left to go except to battle against bosses - bosses that are seemingly overpowered.  I ran out of desire to grind my way through these bosses.  I tried picking the game up again after a few days, died a couple of times at the same boss, and was left with zero desire to keep trying, even on a different part of the map. 
5.  This game will cheap shot the shit out of you.  
6.  Most of the time you won't know where to go or what to do, and will waste a lot of time running around in circles in search of a path that you can traverse at your skill level.

Overall, it's a good game and I recommend it to people who have nothing better to do other than run around in circles grinding their way from one area of the map to another.  I also recommend it to people who like dark, depressing games.  

I do not recommend it for people like me who appreciate more upbeat games that instill energy, not deplete it.  I also wouldn't recommend it for people who like games that can be beat within a couple of sittings - this game will rob you of your time if you expect to get anywhere with it.

Hollow Knight is one of the best Metroidvania games your money can buy. My initial playthrough took me 40+ hours (and I STILL haven't beaten the true final boss or completed all of the sidequests), which is excellent value for a $15 game. 

You play as a silent protagonist descending into the ruins of an ancient city. The lore of this game is incredible, with lots of interesting characters, both past and present, inhabiting the kingdom of Hallownest. The game has awesome visuals, and a thick atmosphere: you can feel your heart jump when a new enemy pops up seemingly out of nowhere, or your heart racing as you try to find the mapmaker whenever you enter a new region.

The game's health machanic is very interesting: you fill up your SOUL meter by killing enemies, and you can spend SOUL to focus and refill lost hitpoints (though it's not instant, so it's tricky to do when you're in combat). There are a variety of charms (perks) that you can collect and equip during the game, and you can make a really powerful (if situational) build with the right combination. For example, against bosses, I like to use a build that [spoiler] replaces your health meter with lifeblood, which means you can't focus to regain health, but the tradeoff is that you have more starting health, and it's difficult to focus while fighting bosses anyway.[/spoiler]

For players seeking a challenge, there is a very high difficulty ceiling. Within the your initial gameplay, you have the [spoiler] Trial of the Fool [/spoiler] and the [spoiler] Dream Bosses [/spoiler] to test your combat prowess, and then the [spoiler] White Palace [/spoiler] to test your platforming skills. If that's not enough, then you also unlock SoulSteel mode upon completing the game: can you beat the game without losing a life? Best of luck...

To address some of the weaknesses of the game, I've seen some people complain about the combat, that it's very repetitive. Personally, I don't really see that: during the first few bosses, it may be the case, but even with just the ability to strike enemies with your nail (sword), there are a fair variety of techniques or at least flair: do you strike them from the side, or do you jump upabove them and slash them from the air? More notable, in my opinion, is that the game does involve a lot of wandering around. Any game (particularly a Metroidvania) has a strike a balance of how much to guide the player, and Hollow Knight definitely errs on the side of "less": there are very few times during the game where the game directly tells you where you should go, and so there may be a numbe rof hours in the game where you make very little progress. But I will say that, for me, it wasn't really a problem until [spoiler] I was trying to reach the true ending, and I didn't know where to go to access The Abyss and find the Shade Cloak. [/spoiler] For that part, I eventually had to consult a guide.

Still, I wholeheartedly recommend Hollow Knight: it is a standard that all modern Metroidvanias should be measured against.

Hollow Knight is one of those games that I find myself returning to over and over. Beautiful world design and music make for an immersive experience. The gameplay is challenging and the replay value is high.

Hollow Knight is a game that I started out hating and being annoyed at for a majority of my first playthrough. However, there is something about this game that is addictive and makes you want to continue to play it. It wasn't until my second playthrough that I started to fall in love with this game.

The art and character desgin are quite beautiful and the music is an additional NPC character. But the game sucks you into its world and makes you want to keep revisiting it.

All the frustrations that I had that led me to want to rage quit, seemed trivial on the second playththrough.

I liked who I was controlling and the journey he was on. 

During my second playthrough, I ended up defeating bosses that on my first, seemed nigh impossible. But the patterns are there for the patient and I have to say that by now, this is one of the best games that I have had the honour to play.

The control of the character is very responsive and acquiring the abilities to access the areas which could not be previously reached, is well paced.

The bosses (although on their introduction) were fun once you worked out what their pattern was. And even though I wanted to just finish the game, something kept driving me to play it again. I attribute this to the world and the lore.

Anyone that is a fan of the Metroidvania style games and wants a challenging experience (during which your skill level and attention to detail will improve) can't go wrong in buying it.

This went from a game that I thought I would never finish; to one that I played through again.

It isn't without its flaws. No save points next to boss fights stands out in particular. But the overall experience in playing it and the love that grows from playing it will make you forget them and focus on the skills that you need to improve to progress further.

At the time of writing, I have 106% and it if was the only game in my library, I would replay it again.

Do yourself a favour and buy this game as Team Cherry have lovingly created a world and experience that will stay with you for a long time to come.

Hollow Knight is a rare breed. Not only is it a Kickstarter success story, but it is also a masterful debut of Australian indie dev Team Cherry, and quite possibly one of the best, most well-crafted games to come out in recent years.

You take the role of the Knight, a bug arriving in the lost kingdom of Hallownest, once home to a race of intelligent insects and rumoured to hide great treasures and secrets. After gathering what little information you can from Dirtmouth, the small town on the surface, you plunge into the town's well, your nail (tiny sword) at the ready, and begin exploring the vastness of Hallownest and its many depths.

At first look, Hollow Knight resembles Dark Souls thematically. A large, interconnected world that encourages exploration and backtracking (as befits a metroidvania), the grim tone of a kingdom fallen from grace, the intentionally vague story and lore that is found by exploring the environment, the massive variety of enemies and their patterns and strategies...Hell, you can even rest at certain spots on the map, only instead of Bonfires they're iron benches. That's about where the similarities stop though, as Hollow Knight has its own artistic identity and follows it to the letter. As an underground kingdom of insects, Hallownest is built into the earth following a sort of geo-gothic motive, from the caverns that are barely traversible to the signs of majestic civilization.

Despite this depressive atmosphere, oftentimes the game will allow for moments of lightheartedness and veritable rays of humanity courtesty of its many NPCs. The jolly mapmaker who constantly moves around as you uncover new areas and sells you their maps, the antipathic knight-braggart you constantly seem to run into, the hopeful Stag beetle used as transportation on the Stagways, and many more each help to convey a sense of fullness and emotion to the world. This is further helped by the incredible soundtrack, which gives every area and boss their own identity.

On the gameplay side of things, the platforming is smooth, the controls are tight and responsive and the combat is utterly satisfying, every blow of the Knight's nail impactful, every enemy different in its own way and requiring different tactics to overcome, and every boss demanding as much skill as one can give it at that point in the game. However, the Knight is not alone with his nail in this endeavour, as you may also find Charms in your adventures. They each take up a certain number of Charm Notches, of which you can buy or find even more, and grant benefits from a higher attack range to faster running and some more outlandish effects as well. Optimizing Charm combinations is a big part of success in the game, but even so it is a smooth experience, easily done at any Bench the Knight can rest at.

In conclusion, Hollow Knight is a beautiful piece of work, its many cogs intertwined to offer a compact package of ideas and design choices that is incredible to behold. And on top of all this, Team Cherry is still updating the game with new content, for free, and their biggest update is yet to come. For only 15 dollars, nobody should miss this masterpiece.

EDIT: The Gods And Masters *free* DLC has arrived, and it's done wonders for the longevity of the game after adding boss challenges. There's still talk of another DLC featuring a new playable character. As such there will never be a time not to buy this game. So do it now!

EDIT #2: 'Oh, we wanted to make that into a DLC, but we made it into a new game instead. Oops!' And that concludes the lesson of Why We Don't Deserve Team Cherry.

Atmosphere, sound, music, worldbuilding, story, combat, exploration, characters. 10/10 Hollowknight is just about perfect in everything it sets out to do. The level of polish is undeniable. Almost everything is as it's supposed to be and it shows. The only exception is the charm combo system, certain charms provide benefits when combined and there is no ingame way to know when this happens (other than possibly feeling it, or sometimes visual effects).

Some personal preference stuff. It's a gloomy soulsesque type of world, very detailed and full of secrets that you are likely to miss unless you pay close attention. There are a few super-meatboy-tier platforming segments hidden away, they are very wellmade and I would have liked more of that (though I get why they didn't make more, lots of crying noobs over those parts).

Main complaint against this game seems to be that noobs think it's too hard. This is a good sign, it means there is depth to the mechanics and that a certain degree of mastery is expected if you are to get to the end. Hollowknight is designed on a learning curve that turns a normally-abled person to a legit gamer, and later on in the optional super lategame your eyes will stretch out and your forks will bend into chopsticks that's how gud you get. If it seems easy early on just enjoy the atmosphere, it's a really chill experience.

There are [spoiler]many different endings, the "better" endings are hidden. The radiance ending [/spoiler] is hinted towards through gameplay and lore if you pay attention and it's extremely welldone imo. But [spoiler]the godmaster ending might be a bit too [/spoiler] well-hidden. There are no clues that hint towards it existing outside of [spoiler]having a spare key if you have already collected every key in the game.[/spoiler]  Even so there are no hints at all towards [spoiler] the location, it's in the royal waterways incase you were wondering. [/spoiler]

Hollow knight is probably one of the best games I have ever played and I'm not even done with godmaster yet. It continues to be supported by the devs, recently the last free dlc came out but there is still more content to come. The full game and story is complete, 
[strike]next dlc is going to be fanservice in the form of a secondary playable character. [/strike]   it's a whole sequel now called Silksong. I'm looking forward to it.

Edit - added spoilers for the lore section

Hollow Knight a game by Team Cherry. there's nothing I can say that Team Cherry has done wrong. 
This game, this amazing game. it truly is hard to know where to start. Hollow Knight has so much to offer.

Difficulty - Hollow knight's difficulty is.. well to say the least hard. this is no run of the mill platformer. Sure it's a hard game, but its that type of difficulty that people of the gaming community should enjoy and love! Each and every time I have failed at a boss fight or died during my exploration I just wanted to continue and beat my goals set in mind. When you die it can be heartbreaking, really. But it fills you with the power to go back and keep trying until your hands cramp from holding your controller. Yeah, it's a hard game unless your skilled in platformers but its fun to the point where you want to keep trying.

Exploration - when you start you see your self as a traveler looking for something (i can't say much about the story, I don't want to spoil it) you are thrown into the game. A cave that is just outside Dirtmount, a dying town. after going through the dying town you jump into a well. Now this, this is where the game takes place. I can't even get over how much work it put into exploring the ruins, it is truly beautiful.

Graphics - My God, the amount of time Team Cherry put into making this game look the way is it today, its magnificent. I'm not the type of person to care about graphics most of the time but it is absolutely beautiful. the work that Team Cherry put into this game must've taken hours and months and years. You can really tell that it was all paid off in the end.

StoryLine - The storyline for Hollow Knight to some people it just might not be there. For me in order to really see the story you have to pay close attention to every NPC you meet and read ever log from the Hunter Journal to really see what has happened to this once beautiful city. trust me you will not forget the story of what once happened to Dirtmount and their people.

To those of you who really cared about what I had to say about this great game, thank you for sticking around and reading to the end. my words for this game is nothing. For anyone to really see how great this game is you have to play it for your selves. I actually feel really bad for Team Cherry. This amazing game is only worth CDN$ 16.99 I think the amount of time and effort they put into this game it honestly should be worth more. Team Cherry even has DLC's I know what you're thinking. "Pfff this game doesn't have any DLC" I'm sorry to break your hearts but it does. Team Cherry has made 3 "DLC's" Hidden Dreams, The Grimm Troupe and Life Blood. I went into Hollow Knight with high expectations, and needless to say, it certainly delivered. Hollow Knight is truly a masterpiece in my opinion. A captivating story with a wide array of interesting characters, beautiful area and character design, and a brilliant soundtrack combined with highly-responsive controls and wonderful gameplay make this game a must-have for any metroidvania fan. 

It took me a bit to get used to the controls, to be honest. I wasn't really used to them. Once I did, however, I was having the time of my life. The game never felt like it had a low point. Not even the White Palace or the Path of Pain. There were ocasional roadblocks, of course. A few bosses took me over an hour or two to beat, and the above-mentioned White Palace and Path of Pain did cause me some trouble (Mostly the path of pain, I had this game open for *three days straight* trying to beat it!). In the end though, I managed to get past them all and achieve 107% completion. This game has made me feel more accomplished that any game made me feel in a while.

I could say many, many more things about this game, but I'm afraid it would simply take too long to write out all those compliments. I will say, however, that this is one of the best games I've played in my life. If you have the money to spare, I urge you to pick this game up. You will most certainly not regret it.

I spent some 30 hrs on this game before giving up. I did really enjoy it at first, but the more I played, the more frustrating it became. I have played Dead Cells (completed 5BC recently) and Bloodstained: Ritual of the Night (100% complete) and enjoyed them a lot more.

This game is very beautiful, and you can tell that the devs put a lot of care in every single detail in the game. The map is expansive and there are lot of contents. So if it is your type, you will probably like it a lot. 

Not for me, unfortunately.

Perhaps no one cares, but here are some reasons why I didn't like:
TRAVEL
I had some huge trouble with this. The map shows you the overall "areas", but not the exact passages in those areas, leading to heavy reliance on memory (or am I supposed to memorize everything?) The game has a fast travel mechanism, but IMO the travel points are too far apart, and movement speed is kinda slow. So whenever I need to get to a place, I have to study the map to find the quickest route, and follow up with a lot of walking/dashing/jumping. The scenery is beautiful, but after a dozen times or so walking the same path, the fun is just gone. And with this kind of game, you need to go to a lot of places to unlock all contents and to fight difficult bosses that require multiple tries.
STORY
This could be because I am not a native English speaker, but honestly I just can't understand the story. I tried to read every single dialog -- like I always do with every game -- but they just seemed so... vague. Thirty hours spent and the only thing I know about the story is that it has something to do with dreams (lol). After a while, it felt like wandering around just to complete/unlock everything.
PLATFORMING/COMBAT
Some platforming puzzles are questionably difficult, and rely on this downward nail slashing move, which is novel, but difficult to perform consistently. Boss fights are generally good, but the RNG can sometimes push you in a corner with no escape route, which is ugly when it happens, eg you need to jump over the boss to avoid a dashing attack, but the jump path is blocked by randomly falling debris. Also some boss fights cannot be won if you weapon is not strong enough, almost regardless of how skilled you are.

That's all for today.

(Minor Spoilers ahead)

Story: 9/10
I can't say too much here, for fear of big spoilers, however: the world building is incredible, and the plot is unique (though I hear it takes a lot of inspiration from Dark Souls, which I haven't played). I also love the way the game drops you in without telling you anything - you're left to figure out stuff by yourself (again, from what I've heard, a very Dark-Souls-y approach). I very much prefer this over games that hold my hand the whole way through. I also love the way the game leaves the lore just vague enough to hold many different theories/conclusions plausible.

Gameplay: 9/10
Let's take a moment to applaud the combat in this game. There's so much different ways to take down an enemy or boss, and it's all left up to the player to pick their combat style through the charms system. The charms are my favourite part of this game - you can combine them to create unique charm builds that best reflect your style of combat. Some of the charms do feel a bit cheaty, but hey. Whatever gets the job done.

The gameplay itself is pretty solid, and I don't have any criticisms here at all. It's tight, fluid and never gets in the way.

Enemies & Bosses: 8/10
There are so many different things that can hurt your character in this game, it's unbelievable for a game that costs so little. Each area has a different set of unique enemies. And the bosses... incredibly well designed and choreographed, and some of them truly made you feel like a true master in combat when you finally get the hang of them. There are a few fights you can cheese though, and that makes the experience a little less satisfying. Still, the harder bosses brought out the salt A LOT so I guess it balances it out.

World Design: 9/10
The world design was spot on. The game's location, Hallownest, is vast and expansive, but not too overwhelming due to the fact that you needed to obtain cool items™ to access certain areas. I also like how some areas were accessible without said cool items™, it just required me to think outside the box a little. Secret areas are pretty damn well hidden, and this game always surprises me - when I go back to explore areas I've seen before, there's almost always something I've missed.

Graphics & visuals: 10/10
The visuals in this game are stunning, from the extensive artwork right down to the neat little particle effects. I also adore the use of colour to emphasise certain moods or even themes. Not much of a visual artist so I can't say a lot here, except for "I thoroughly enjoyed it."

Music: 10/10
Being a composer/musician myself, I rarely ever give soundtracks a 10/10. The soundtrack of this game seriously impressed me. It was memorable, original and absolutely stunning - it covered a wide variety of contrasting styles and emotions while still maintaining a consistency in timbre - i.e. you can tell all the tunes are from the same soundtrack. Each piece of music fit the scene perfectly, and whilst I noticed it, it wasn't intrusive at all.  Amazingly, it didn't even end up getting that repetitive for me, even after spending loads of time in certain areas. This is pretty much a perfect soundtrack. Well done Mr. Larkin - I'm now a fan.

Audio: 10/10
Some of the sound design in this game was phenomenal. Deepnest was by far my favourite in terms of sound design - the sounds make the area come to life in a terrifying way. The Abyss was a close second. The sound design during the boss fights were really well done. Also damn, the voice acting was cute, and extremely effective. Never once was the audio out of place. 

OVERALL SCORE: 9.5/10 (points gained for pure awesomeness)

In closing: If you're a fan of platformers, Hollow Knight is well worth your time. Like I said, it's packed with so much content for a game that costs such a small amount. It took me around 78 hours to get to the true ending (because I suck) but I pretty much loved every moment of it - I reckon you will too.

There's good difficult and bad difficult. The "give me god of war" difficulty for GOW is a great example of the former and Hollow knight is a clear example of when games become incredibly frustrating and an unrewarding waste of time. Much has been said about the virtues of this game of which it has many, so I will not spend more time further exhausting that subject. I am fully aware and appreciative of the many things this game gets right and yet, I don't suggest you play it. 
You will often find yourself feeling miserable and worthless as you trek across the map for the hundredth time, thinking what else you could you be doing with your life? 
What makes everything worse is that while this game has a lot of lore, it doesn't have a story. The main character is impossible to care about since you don't know much about him and don't get to sympathize with his wants or needs. The only reason you care about him is because his death would mean more meaningless repetition. I have sunk 50 hours into this game and I can't tell you what it's all about if my life depends on it. I don't know who's who and what I am looking for and why I'm doing the things I'm doing. In fact, for the better half of the game it was nothing but good old "lost cost fallacy" that made me keep playing. 
Despite all its greatness this game is one to avoid. after all, we don't play games to feel miserable.

Just finished the Deepnest area of the game... that was some of the most frustrating gameplay I've ever experienced. 

The world, atmosphere, story, and characters of the game are amazing. But the gameplay is seriously lacking.

The combat is unsatisfying, not fun, and downright frustrating about 80% of the time. There are numerous enemies in the game that are just not fun to fight. Shining example of this are the large bugs in the Deepnest that have an armored front side that take no damage when you hit their shield. To actually damage them, you can hit them from the back or get one hit to their front when they attack before immediately having to dodge out of the way. Now, this wouldn't be that annoying, except that they are in an area of the map where you CANNOT JUMP OVER THEM TO ATTACK THEM FROM THE BACK... I think there were 2 places in the entire area where you are able to jump behind them (out of the 8 or so times you encounter these creatures. More when you die and have to repeat the area you just went through and the enemies respawn). So, running into one of these guys was just a "Oh fantastic! Another one of these guys..." followed by about 5 minutes of: wait for them to attack, hit them once, retreat, reposition, and repeat until dead. You can damage them through their shield with your ranged magic attack, but it takes >5 hits of this to kill them and the only way to replenish your magic attack is to painstakingly slog through slowly hitting them one attack at a time to refill the gauge.

10 hours in and I've gotten one (marginal) damage upgrade to my main weapon (your sword), one exploration-found health shard (you need 4 to increase your health by one hit, so I'm still at the starting amount of health), and 1 upgrade to the most useless ability so far in the game which is a ground dash that allows you to break through the floor and is pretty impractical to fight enemies with... is all of that my fault? Possibly. But I've been doing a good job of exploring and still haven't found many useful things besides currency and items that only exist to sell them.

Speaking of exploring, the in-game map is downright terrible. It shows just shaded-in blocks of areas you have explored (not to mention the newly explored areas of the map don't even appear on the in-game map until you've returned to one of the widely spaced save points...) and not anything detailed. Hope you can remember what arbitrary symbol each of the merchants spread throughout the game have, because the game doesn't give you a key for what they are. Your spirit (which is a ghost of yourself that appears *roughly* where you died, that you have to return to and fight to restore your collected money and mana gauge back to the full amount) will be in the general vicinity of where it appears on the map. By no means is it an accurate location, just a general direction to head in to find it. One time the map icon for the spirit was shown to be in a part of the map that was outside the game... that was cool. I had to explore around the circle of the map that was actually a part of the game to find it. Only for it to be about 2 level transitions up from where it appeared.

I want to love this game, I really do. I've heard nothing but good things about, but it's just not there for me. I'm going to keep playing this game and see it through to the end. If for no other reason than I'm currently staying in a hotel for work with bad internet where I can't play anything online.... But as of right now, if you've actually read this far and haven't raged that I need to "git gud" or "you're just dumb", just play the Ori series. It's the same concept (adventure platforming mixed with combat and storytelling) that does all of the good things that this game does just as well, if not better, and the parts that this game does badly, Ori blows it out of the damn water.

So if you want a game that is a mixture of: Dark Souls + Spelunky. This game is for you!

I played, beat and loved Ori and the Blind Forest, Shovel Knight, and Dark Souls 3.

I should've loved Hollow Knight. I didn't. Here's why:

First, the art style is uniquely beautiful. Not my cup of tea, but I can appreciate the work here.

Now the bad.

Combat is irritating and not fun. I feel like a dodge roll would've helped. Bosses could've all taken 25-50% less hits and it still would've felt hard but not been as tedious.

Progression is soooo slow. You have to use an upgrade to see yourself on the map?! Fail. I don't know if they were trying to scale the game up to take much longer to complete or what.

Quality of life sucks. Venders are scattered all over. It's a chore to go back to them with no pay off along the way. No fast travel. WHAT?!. Benches are just too far apart too. They punish you for playing this game and not in a good way.

One thing I know for sure, I hate procedurally generated stuff. And Hollow Knight's level design is close to that feel. It's not quite random gibberish but it's pretty pointless. It's ok the first time you are going through an area, but with the amount of back and forth this game forces you to do, the lack of creative level design really shows.

So let's recap. The combat is irritating, the progression too slow, travel is tedious, level design is boring... I'm 15 hours in and the sunk cost fallacy is in full effect here.

Hollow Knight is a well thought and realized game by a three person team.  The story of the game is told through the areas that you go through and the actions of bug inhabitants that you observe throughout the buildup of the protoganist. Hollow Knight is a best described as a Metroidvania inspired game with Dark Souls characteristics.  If you are difficulty adverse, then this game might not be for you. You will definiitely need a bit of persistence to get through it on the normal difficulty.

Although it's not as difficult as Dark Souls or (closest 2D relative) Salt and Sanctuary.

For example:
Save points are sparse and few.  If you die, you lose your "money" = "souls"
Combat is about timing which means learning enemy patterns so you can dodge or attack accordingly.

As for the Metroid side of things:
There are many quests to search for "keys" -- some of these are abilities that allow you to traverse areas that required those abilities and some is "plain" keys to allow you access a different door. 

There are quests that can be given by NPCs but for the most part, you are allowed to go off on your own and you will wander through areas in awe what what you are seeing.  

Exploration is a sensory and progression reward.  The music and the art in the background punctuate the visuals making the journey all the more worthwhile to go through. For the most part, players can get through the game with obtaining the "keys" but there are plenty of incentive to look for the optional upgrades such as additional health , energy, sword (aka "Nail") and spells that can augment your survivability and fighting options but are not necessary for the progression of the game.

All in all, Hollow Knight is the complete "Metroidvania" package and I look forward to Team Cherry's next project.

I cannot overstate my disappointment with this game. An incredible art style, phenomenal music, perfect tone, and gameplay that is a complete and utter disrespect of a players time.

 I have a bit over 26 hours of playtime as of writing this, and at at least 20 of them have been nothing but dreadful backtracking, not after death mind you, just how the levels are setup seems to have been built to be as annoying and forgettable as possible. People praise this game for it's "exploration and adventure" what that means is the map function is a complete joke and you'll be wandering aimlessly around areas you've been in for hours, and if you're lucky enough to stumble into somewhere new, chances are you'll die and get sent back to god knows what bench (bugged for me frequently, would respawn at the wrong bench more often than not). A majority of all areas are just dead-ends at the time you'd naturally be going to explore them, but it's not like you have a choice with the useless map and no direction for where you are actually supposed to go. Exploration my ass, this game is an overhyped walking simulator (I hate Deep Nest, I spent a goddamn hour there for literally nothing, why am I even let in if there's nothing but a headache for me at the time I find it? Is there genuinely any reason to go to the Howling Cliffs?)

Make no mistake, a large majority of your time in this game will be nothing but traversing the same areas back and forth, hoping that a small hole in the wall is somehow the intended way to go, and not just a still-inaccessible path or worse; a useless NPC - Mask Maker, Midwife, Bardoon, etc.

Everything having the same style and palette means it's pretty common for enemies to blend into the environment, ESPECIALLY when it's a small, flat enemy spawning out of the ground when there's foliage or foreground decoration, which the game loves to do. Another issue of this is it can legitimately be hard to tell enemies apart from friendly NPCs - multiple times I approached what looked like a neutral character to only be damaged when I walked into them, often dying because I gave something I had snuck up on the element of surprise toward myself.

Enemies legitimately just become a chore to fight after you've killed a couple of them, meaning even interesting encounters quickly become stale backtracking with more steps a few rooms in. Bossfights that become braindead endurance tests when you master their ~3 attacks in the first minute, or just annoyingly difficult by blatantly reading inputs to catch you mid-air without possible time to react (multiple bosses that you will die to only a single attack, because it becomes clear it's the only one capable of hitting someone who's paying any attention). I'm not good enough at 2D platformers or metroidvania games to warrant these bosses taking 5 minutes and I only get hit 3 times during the entire fight, that's just wasting more of the players time.

But by FAR the biggest and most egregious waste of time absolute spit in the face to players is the Colosseum of Fools - the first two rounds are more complete fodder to mindlessly space out to for 10-15 minutes each, while the third and final round is just 15 minutes of absolute mind numbing nothing immediately followed by multiple waves of the same enemies and without the ability to heal. Telefragging enemies mean it's not a test of skill as much as just remembering where they spawn for the 7th time you have to play through it again when the first dozen or so waves become an unskippable cutscene.

Yes it is all optional, but it stands as a testament to the design philosophy and general competence of the game when the bonus challenge minigame is so numbingly boring and painfully hamfisted that you start yearning for walking around a map you can ignore because at least that doesn't demand your attention to be actively un-fun.

I've seen praise of the game for there not being any bugs, but I've encountered multiple in my playtime, most annoying of course being respawning much, much further away than I should. I've also had issues with coins falling inside of walls, enemies not having sounds/particles, and maybe worst not being able to heal during bossfights (I start the animation but never gain any health, I just sit there consuming soul until I usually get hit).

All in all, as much as I've played I've enjoyed maybe 20% of it, and the rest has absolutely not been worth it. This game disgusts me for the praise it gets as such a horrid addition to this genre, for gods sake please go play Shovel Knight instead.

I ended up 'burning out' on this game before I got to the end. Possibly even before I reached the half-way point of this game. Not because I of reasons that would identify this game as 'bad'. Rather, not for me.

I kind of like the controls. They are tight and in combat it makes me feel like playing 'footsies' (fighting game terminology) with the enemies for most of the time. At first I enjoyed that... only for it to grow stale over time. It felt like most common enemies you either combat using footsies, or you can just button mash to death. Some may need a bit of maneuverability... But it started to feel samey at some point. Felt like a grind. Left me feeling I'm wasting my time and no longer enjoying myself.

The visuals are '50 shades of gray' in a non-sensual sense. It's all so desaturated; grayscale with sometimes a hint of color. Causes enemies to sometimes blend into the background; Dark foreground against dark background. At time the foreground can obsure what's happening too. Something I quickly started to resent and dislike about the game.

Final nail into the coffin for me, was the style of metroid-vania implemented in this game. It's actually kind of nice that are multiple paths to progress. However, I happened to have missed a particular corridor to a special skill needed to progress. I was stuck aimlessly exploring regions for what I may have missed, feeling like I've wasted my time. The map didn't help in that regard all that much. Had to get a walkthrough to figure out what I'd been missing. Which was eventually killing blow that caused me to quite. Maybe I'm too spoilt with quest arrows / markers pointing me were to go next? 
I don't know, I felt like my limited time I have available to play games was not being respected. I felt there was too much timewaste, in an environment that didn't facinate me with a gameplay loop that grew stale for me.

I hope the experience will be different for you. If you think it won't, you may want to look for Ori and the Blind Forest or Dead Cells instead.
