The good: visuals, animation, soundtrack - all top notch.
The bad:
1. Ugly map system. There is no automap. Areas that you visit are not automatically mapped unless you buy the area map. The map only updates at checkpoints. Why? Because you must suffer.
2.  Continuation of #1. Objectives are not marked on the map. -Dear devs, where do I need to go now?  -Just continue to roam the area, you'll find it eventually. Or not. Because you must suffer.
3. Checkpoint placement. Absolutely horrendous. Checkpoints are rare even in the starting locations, but further into the game you can wonder around for half an hour and still don't find any. And when you visit the thew area you don't have a map, remember? Not even the automap, so you cannot backtrack. You've got 2K Geo on you and you are low on HP? Well, too bad, pal, but you'll most likely die and loose everything, because there's no checkpoint in the 100km radius. Yes, when you die your money is left on your ghost. But what are the chances of finding and picking it without the area map?
4. Continuation of #3. Let's say you're in a boss fight an you die. You'll respawn at the last checkpoint (the last bench you were sitting on). But as the checkpoints are rare and far between, you'll have to travel quite a distance before you can get to the boss again. And yes, enemies do respawn upon your death. So you'll end up fighting through the same enemies (which are sometimes quite tough) over and over and over again just to get to the boss. Suffering is a must, remember?
5. Ghost. When you die, you leave behing a ghost which holds all your money, Dark Souls style. Well, as I've mentioned before, oftentimes it is impossible to retrieve it. But that's not all of it yet. When you die, your mana gauge (the Soul gauge) gets crippled to around 60%. So if you die in a boss fight, you'll have to fight your way back while being crippled to 60% of your normal Soul capacity. And you'll have to do that many times. Thank you, devs, this is such a nice move. Oh, and you spawn with an empty soul gauge.
6. Oh, so you can heal using Soul, which you absorb from enemies? Then we'll just fill the entire area with critters that you cannot kill. Because we can. And no, there is no checkpoint around. And yes, as you have just respawned, your Soul gauge is empty.
7. 3 hits with an upgraded sword to kill a single flying crystal-spitting thingy? Seriously?

Bottom line - there are right ways of making the game difficult and there are wrong ways of making the game difficult. Being an a-hole towards the gamer is a wrong approach. Seriously, if I wanted some frustration, I'd just go and play Super Meat Boy or something. So if you want someting Ori-like - stay well away from this game.

I had some credit and bought this when it was $10, I knew I wanted to play it so I made the purchase eventhough I had not a computer to run it with.  Fast forward to now and I've since broke down and installed the game on my work PC (I work alone in an office and have a lot of free time) and it's been great! 

I'm not one to enjoy super challenging platformers, the rebooted Rayman games (Origins and Legends), SPELUNKY and Hollow Knight are pretty much the only challenging ones I've liked thus far, I do grow tired with some bosses in Hollow Knight that persist even with the strongest equipment acquired, but the overall gameplay and settings are wonderous!  I really do love how you can explore to your hearts content and not feel like you're missing out on story or major events, you really can come back to them when you're ready.  

The controls are not as tight as a game like SPELUNKY but they get the job done, it could just be my controller though I use an original Xbox One controller attched by the stock USB cable and I was surprised the controller worked right from the get-go (I game on PC almost never).  There have been a few times where I've taken damage or died simply because buttons do not respond, this isn't a consistent issue and I've tried to recreate them while troubleshooting with no luck.

One of the best parts of this game is it's characters, not everyone is out to get you some are just exploring as you are, they help fill in the history of the land and help in guiding you on your personal journey.  One could say this is what NPCs are supposed to do but too many games create filler and become vapid in that sense.  Apart from finally completing physical challenges you've worked so hard on, getting to see certain characters grow along side you is even better!  After realizing how much time you spend alone, it's nice to find people who are happy to see you and are thankful of your presence.

Overall, I would suggest Hollow Knight to anyone in need of a platformer with depth, challenge, emotion, humor, and great visuals, it really shows that the developers have put in a lot of time to make this game the best it could be.  I feel lucky to have gotten the chance to play such a well crafted single-player experience, the game does feel like a perfect mix of old and new!

Gloriously rich in well thought out, meticulously detailed storyline and made vibrant with stunning graphics, Hollow Knight is a game that drew me in and held me tight. The content seems almost limitless, with a vast map to explore and many secrets to discover on your journey. However, I will say this: If you are looking for an easy game to while away the hours, this game is not for you. Bosses are hard, platforming is harder (some areas more than others), and there is no hand-holding. To use the phrase "Dark Souls of metroidvanias" would probably make me crnge hard enough to shrivel up, so i'll sum it up simply. The reward of this game is the result of the effort you put into it. The difficulty makes your accomplishments  worth it, and although a fight may be gruelling the satisfaction you feel on completion is immense.

[h1]Hollow Knight -[/h1]
One of the best (if not [b]the[/b] best) metroidvanias of all times created by a small-time indie game developing team mostly known for their success with Hollow Knight. Keeps you playing for at least 50 hours your first run with all of its immersive world design, a plethora of collectibles and rewarding progression system (the story, which can be beaten in less than 5 hours, even by an amateur, when delved deeper has so many side quests and interesting lore to think about that it got me sucked up for 80+hrs on my first save file). Also features remarkably difficult and original boss fights, taken even further with 4 free content packs which easily add around 20hrs of playtime on top of everything. The game can even be modded and has a very polished wiki for everything you might have missed during your first playthrough. Although getting so far as to having every single achievement in the game can be a daunting task which still, upon finding great enjoyment in the game for a long time, shouldn't be left without attention since the more you play Hollow Knight the better you become.
 
Basically if you've played 2d platformers and/or metroidvanias in the past and enjoyed them, you should try Hollow Knight. It gives you a completely new and unique experience within a small but story-rich universe.

Editing comment:
I haven't changed my mind, Hollow Knight is a good game, but it's overrated! You'll probably like it, good for you. There are things I liked, but I was disappointed overall with some points:
1) Controls. Constantly missing inputs. Until the character fully landed, the jump will not count (perhaps even a short time after landing). The same goes for jumping off the wall (especially after the Crystal Heart ability). Same to dash. This leads to the constant sliding on the spikes and the inability to dodge enemies.
2) Mandatory boss rush for one of the endings, a very questionable thing (especially with not very good control, see point 1). This is not a complaint about the difficulty!
3) Once again, backtracking is fine for a game of the Metroidvania genre! but.. Combined with the fact that the game is constantly choking you, it turns into torture. Maybe that's just my experience with HK. I've never had this problem in other games of a similar genre, even with the more complicated backtracing system.
4) The complaint about the "map" is removed. I just did not like its implementation. No complaints about the world structure, it's good.
5) Boring gameplay, even with different spells, you will not find a great variety of builds here. Especially considering the way these builds are talked about in the positive reviews. Builds are there, but it is a choice between different types of warrior, that don't change the gameplay much.
6) Good reviews can misinform you about the advantages of this game, so don't read them and start playing. That's where you decide whether you like the game or not.

Maybe if a patch had come out that improved the controls, the review would have been positive, but the conclusion: a long stuffy journey with not very good gameplay.

P.S. Perhaps if I had learned from a few reviews about the problems of this game, I would not have bought it, so I leave a negative review for those who are still in doubt whether to buy or not. Perhaps it will help someone.

P.P.S. As pointed out in the comments under the review, I will try one day to give the game another chance and maybe change my mind, but not now.

P.P.P.S. Fun fact, 1 boss was defeated by about 70% of players, while about 21% finished the game. Bored or choked by the game? Sure, some people are still exploring the world of the game, but the very large percentage difference for a good game confuses me. Idk, conclusions are up to you.

I grew up playing Super Metroid and the original metroidvanias and tried really hard to like this game. I've refunded it once already and bought it AGAIN to try and give it another chance just because it's praised so highly. After a few areas and a handful of bosses I had to give up on it, I was just not having any fun. I think the art direction alone covers for what I consider mediocre gameplay and there are some core design sins in HK that ruin it for me.

Too many dead ends in exploration with no rewards. Chopping away at currency nodes is not rewarding. The correct path forward was frustrating to find when it should have been fun to find. Most of my short playtime was spent wandering around looking for the correct path and when I eventually found it I wasn't happy I was just pissed off at how much time I wasted. There is a complete lack of design to facilitate guiding the player in some way to the correct path forward for progression, some people might like the complete directionless exploring for with no rewards, I don't.

The physics is another reason I don't like the exploring. Some games are amazingly fun because of their physics alone. In HK you fall very quickly, it's very tight, almost too responsive. This doesn't sound bad right? Too responsive? What? Since this is a platformer physics that feel good are extremely important to me and having too responsive controls can be just as unwieldy as controls that are too floaty. It just didn't do it for me.

Combat has knock-back every time you strike an enemy. Add this to the really touchy physics and it gets very annoying. Now you can remove this knock-back if you use some equipment, but if you do that you take up important slots that could have been used for more interesting abilities. Why would the developers create the combat like this then create a system to remove it? It baffles me.

Now the aesthetics. Beautiful looking game. I thought the art style would click for me sooner or later but the other aspects of the game that kept me continually frustrated couldn't be saved by how good looking it is. There is also music. It must be very background, ambient, because I can't recall any of it. They were going for a very particular feel when designing this game and I don't think I'm the target audience.

Obviously I'm in the minority here. This game has found much success but I think HK's lesser known contemporaries do a much better job as metroidvanias with satisfying exploration and combat. If the combat and physics were more to my liking I wouldn't mind running around in this game. Unfortunately I just wasn't having fun playing and nothing about the game was going to redeem it for me. If some day I ever complete this game I might update this review.

Wow. Just wow. This game is probably the single best game I've ever played. It's an absolute masterpiece. And here's why.
TL;DR:
Story 13/10
Gameplay 11/10
Characters 11/10
Controls 12/10
Bosses 15/10
Content for the Price 100/10

The Story
Fantastic. Now the game does not straight-up tell you it's story through it, so you might not know all of it or even half of it by the end of your first playthrough. The game does an amazing job at drip-feeding the story to you and only giving the pieces of it piece by piece, so you can complete the puzzle by yourself. 

The Gameplay
Honestly, the game has a little of a stiff start. It's quite confusing at the beginning, not knowing where to go and searching every little place to go for a new path, only to eventually find you have to beat a boss to get an ability, etc. In the beginning the game is pretty linear, with only one way to go. In the late game is where the game truly shines. You just got a new ability, and suddenly you just remember all these places where you couldn't go before and find out if it's true. It becomes a bit of an open game by then (although still not very much). Oh, and when you think in this game: What if I went up that ledge? Most of the time, you will be rewarded.

The Characters
[spoiler]CLOTH 
WHYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYYY[/spoiler]
Yeah the characters are pretty good.

Controls
I like the base controls, so jump Z, slash X, and the rest around that. Eventually you slide through everything so smoothly touching your keybourd so good with those keys that you no longer think you're human.

Bosses
Everything is perfect, except [spoiler]Absolute Radiance[/spoiler]
Some bosses unlock new abilities, while others just give you items. The best thing is that most of the bosses are optional and only some are needed for the new abilities, and thus for progressing the story. There are quite some bosses that give the really good feeling when fighting with them. [spoiler]Mantis Lords and Grimm[/spoiler]

Content for the Price
Honestly, this game is worth like €60 for content alone. And after release they even made four more content updates that make up for like 50 hours extra of content (especially Godmaster). I myself haven't beaten the game fully yet though, right now I'm working on the Godmaster stuff and speedrunning the game :) SUPER hyped for the upcoming Hornet DLC.

Hope you enjoy the game, it's awesome.

Hollow Knight is a special game. It is difficult to find something negative to say about it. 

The controls are some of the most responsive I have seen in these types of games. It makes traversing throughout the world very satisfying, and it becomes even better as you unlock more movement abilities. The game is listed as a platformer but challenging platforming sections are very few. The main point is exploration. You explore until you reach a wall. You explore somewhere else. You find an ability that allows you to break through that wall, you find new places to explore and so on. You unlock stag stations that allow you to travel between each area, but in my opinion there could be even more, because some areas are massive.

Combat is fluid and simple. You have some sword attacks and some magic. The variety comes in the form of enemies. There are over 150 different types of enemies to keep you on your toes. Bosses are well designed and offer a good challenge even for veterans. Builds come in the form of charms, and although a lot of charms are good, some of them are a bit lacking, and you will most likely find yourself using the same charms everyone else uses by the end. 

The music and the artstyle perfectly complement the story and the lore. Hallownest is way past its days of glory, so the NPCs are few. However, in addition to the charming designs and voice acting, each of them has a story of their own and it makes progressing through the game even more exciting when you see that your friends are also progressing with you and even helping you at certain instances.

Lastly I need to mention the content as a whole. Hollow Knight is massive, and it will probably take you around 100 hours if you want to experience everything that it has to offer. The fact that this game costs $15 and goes on sale frequently is absolutely absurd. So absurd in fact that fans of the game have actually asked Team Cherry to increase the price.

This was the first big project Team Cherry has published and there were certainly a lot of limitations during production like time and money. Even with that in mind the result was still a 10/10 game. Now they are developing Silksong without any of those limitations, which means they can let their imagination run wild, and who knows what the result will be. With Silksong being expected to release before June, now is the perfect time to play Hollow Knight.

Hollow Knight is one of the greatest games ever made, and I included it all in a pros and cons.

Pros
The game's art style is absolutely amazing, it manages to look hand drawn yet amazing and immersive at the same time.

The game's combat is a very simple concept, the bottom line is jumping and attacking. You can hit left, right, and up, and you can jump and air hit left, air hit up, air hit right, and air hit down. Those simple options open a gateway to massive potential. Each boss has a very different attack pattern and fight style that makes you rethink how your going to go in each time. As you progress you get more and more attacks and abilities, and the bosses get harder and harder, ending with some of the most intense and fun combat iv'e ever seen.

This game has a charm system that is unique and game changing. Through out the game you will get more and more charms. You can equip these charms for abilities and effects. For instance, there's a charm that will  make it so you can dash quicker, deal more damage, summon minions, and just about everything in between.

This game also has a very cool system called the "Soul system." When you hit an enemy you will gain soul in your "soul tank." You can use this soul for the later attacks I mentioned, or to heal. This creates a very interesting option of using it too execute a high damage attack or replenish a heart. This comes into full effect when fighting bosses.

The world of this game cannot be praised enough. The whole game is 2d, yet it has an open world. The world is big and honestly fun to explore. Each area felt like so much time and love went into it that I got honestly excited when I discovered a new one, yet had a feeling of satisfaction when the map was complete. Exploring is so much fun because about half of each new area you can't actually get to when you first discover it, you have to get abilities and come back to get to new areas, making the big world feel even bigger. Curiosity is rewarded, seeing that a section of the wall that you usually run by looks funny and smashing through will reward a new charm, or a piece of soul tank. The enemies scattered throughout the world add an always present since of danger in this game.

Bosses may be my favorite part of this game, each one is so amazingly well done. There is over 30 bosses in this game and each is done with care. The great fact is each boss is different in it's own way. Some are tanks that will absorb your damage with no problem but are easy to dodge, some are very fast and heavy hitting that don't take much to die, some lack speed and health but do an insane amount of damage, ect.

This game is hard, That's just a fact. It has a steady difficulty that I would describe as "challenging" but there are times when this game will make you cry, and im putting that under pros because I hate games that are too easy.

There have been four huge updates for this game since release that could of EASILY been dlc's but they were released as free updates and that gets a spot in the pro list in my book.

One of the updates is "Godhome" and I love this addition. This is the very endgame, almost towards the very last boss fight. Godhome is basically a place in the game that you can fight every single boss on different difficulties. Although this DOES become redundant it adds tons of hours that can be spent in this game.

I would recommend this game for 15$ any day, that's a fair price.

The world is so creepy and beautiful, the story and lore are subtle, and battle is both straightforward (kill stuff before it kills you) and complex (the charms you can equip are situationally helpful). In my opinion, it's a great example of how an indie developer can produce something that perfectly merges gameplay function and artistic flourish.

Also, believe the hype - this game is no cakewalk. Like Dark Souls or Bloodborne but far less infuriating due to a more forgiving checkpoint system. Yes, you need to *get gud* to survive in the world, but that struggle is so worth it! And when you do finally beat that boss that's been giving you grief for several hours, it's a great feeling. I wholeheartedly recommend this game if you're looking for a beautiful challenge.

[h1]A Quick Note[/h1]

This is a full rewrite of my original review, which was posted shortly after I started playing about three years ago. Since then, I have played ~500 hours, earning all achievements, completing most of the extra challenges offered in the game, and completing a number of self-imposed challenges both with and without mods. In short: I'm [i]much[/i] more qualified to write this now than I was then, and have a ton of detail/color to add :)

I'll be doing my best to avoid any spoilers in this review, but they will be marked as such where I simply can't. Read at your own risk; the world of Hallownest is best explored at your own pace the first time!

[h1]Summary[/h1]

[i]Hollow Knight[/i] is a must-play if you enjoy extremely atmospheric games with rich lore, extensive exploration, and rewarding combat that is easy to learn (but difficult to master.) For an indie title with a price tag this low, it stands up tall when compared to the giants, and that's no laughing matter.

In short: Play. This. Game.

[h1]Pros and Cons[/h1]

[b]Pros[/b]

[list]
[*] Impossibly good value ($15 for a 60+ hour game is absurd)
[*] Beautiful atmosphere/art/music/lore
[*] Simple, fluid combat
[*] Interesting NPCs
[*] Rewarding-to-overcome challenges
[/list]

[b]Cons[/b]

[list]
[*] Huge difficulty curve
[*] Multiple areas that feel incomplete
[*] Limited early-game movement
[*] Markoth (IYKYK)
[/list]

While most of these lists are self-explanatory, there's a few things I want to expand on:

While the challenges the game provides are indeed rewarding to overcome (in an implicit way), I do think some of these challenges don't offer sufficient in-game rewards. [spoiler]The Path of Pain[/spoiler] in particular is notorious for this; the entire reward is a short cutscene and Hunter's Journal entry, which can easily make it feel like an extremely difficult waste of time. I've seen much of The Discourse about this particular challenge, and my main advice is this: If you don't want to do it, don't do it. You aren't missing out on anything, the devs made it clear it is a TRULY optional challenge; there's not even an achievement to earn. There's no reason to be angry about its existence; you never have to even see it, so how does it make the game worse?

As far as the difficulty curve is concerned, I think the ramp-up is a bit too steep. False Knight, the first boss, is completely tankable. This, I think, encourages a style of play that isn't sustainable for the vast majority of the game - as soon as you get to [spoiler]Hornet[/spoiler], the next boss, this strategy completely breaks down. I've known many people to bounce off the game because of this second major fight, even though overcoming it is what really teaches how the combat works. I think it would be better to leave False Knight out entirely, making the City Crest a free pickup and leaving in [spoiler]Failed Champion[/spoiler] as a late-game secret encounter.

Finally, my take on limited early-game movement: For a first playthrough, only being able to jump at the start is perfectly fine. Subsequent playthroughs, however, which you'll need to do if you're achievement hunting, become a tedious slog until the first major movement upgrade. I would propose providing either [spoiler]the Mothwing Cloak[/spoiler] or [spoiler]the Mantis Claw[/spoiler] as movement available at the start. This provides a serious improvement to either vertical or horizontal movement, which is seriously lacking when you can only jump. I understand this has implications for the room/region designs - But it would make the tedium of starting fresh a bit less awful.

This game is certainly something magical. Coming from a background of only touching similar games such as Super Metroid and Cave Story, I found this game didn't take much time to get acclimated to it. It works good on the keyboard and mouse but even better on a controller. A controller like a DualShock or Xbox controller is preferred (mine bricked)- but I managed to make it work on an SNES USB controller. As of writing this, I'm sitting close to about 29 hours. I knew that this game looked interesting but didn't quite realize just how good it was until two hours in.

PROS
Controls are very tight.
Atmosphere is dark and moody.
Lore is sprinkled to you through similar to how one would get it from Souls game
The design of the characters and enemy type are very unique. Bugs. Lots of bugs. Some cute, some more sinister.
Mechanics are interesting
Plenty of power ups and other useful collectibles

NEUTRAL (Good&Bad)
The mapping system takes a little getting used to. You find the map maker in a zone, buy it from him, and you can purchase parts of the mapping system from his wife in town. Progress is made when you sit at a Rest point and it updates the map (assuming you bought the upgrades) with where you have been. You're required to go and seek him out in new areas in order to get the map of that zone. It can be tedious at times but it gets easier as you familiarize yourself with the game.

CONS
This game has a lot of backtracking. This is to be expected from a game of this nature but it certainly has A LOT of backtracking. It can be tiresome fairly quickly especially if you don't have the best memory. Once you get the ability to double jump, climb walls, and other neat tricks you can slowly go back to previous areas and get to locations you couldn't be in. I don't mind it as much but one should be prepared for it going in.

tl;dr game is fantastic and does most of everything right; however, the shear amount of backtracking and the mapping system are an acquired taste. Absolutely buy this. It is fantastic. And they keep putting out more bosses and other content FOR FREE- with "Gods & Glory" coming out later this year.

Is this game fun? Yes, absolutely. Though difficult, more often than not it comes down to one thing and one thing only; precision. The levels are oozing with enemies, obstacles, rich atmosphere, and intriguing plot.
So why am I voting this game down? 

Because more often than not the developers decided to go for design choices that prolong the game in often tedious, boring ways. Ask yourself this; During the Mantis Lords boss-fight, did you have fun? Of course you did! They're fun, fast, exciting, a real 'on the edge of your seat' battle with killer music and exciting gameplay. Their attacks are manageable, and force you to adapt to a barrage of lightning-quick attacks. 

Now, ask yourself; Did you have fun in the White Palace? Did you feel like you adapted and overcame something that was both well thought out, and fun? Did you feel accomplished finishing something that didn't reward ingenuity, but rewarded your ability to fulfil specific movement inputs? 

Final review: 6/10. Great game, marred with poor decisions.

Hollow knight is an incredible game with a ton of value, so much so that I felt bad buying it at a discount during the most recent winter sale and bought it a second time at full price on the Switch. Between those two different purchases, I've gotten around 100 hours out of this game and I'm still working on some of the new content. There's so much to explore, so much to fight, and so much hidden lore to dig through, if you're interested in that kind of stuff. I love the art style of this game, and it has a lot of attention to detail for players who keep an eye out, a lot of it is totally unecessary, you probably wouldn't have cared without it, but the fact that it's there shows how much care went into this game.

Difficulty wise, this game is challenging overall, but some of the later (usually optional) content can get insane. There's some optional content that doesn't even really reward you with anything, it just exists to challenge hardcore players and provide bragging rights. Again, this wasn't needed, but it's not unwelcome and provides players who really want to challenge themselves with something to do.

I would highly recommend this to just about anyone, but especially fans of Metroidvania games, this is one of the best games of that genre. There's a huge amount of content, and now that the Godmaster content pack has arrived, the game is complete and there's no reason to wait any longer.

I haven't been desperately looking for a good metroidvania but boy does it feel good to play one after such a long time. Salt & Sanctuary, Axiom Verge, Guacamelee. Those are the ones i last played and while i can't say they were bad, there were just some things that didn't click with me and i never finished any of those. Hollow Knight doesn't have that. First time i completed the game i had clocked in 16 hours. Then i continued searching collectibles, beat new bosses and areas and unlockecd more endings. 27 hours in i was satisfied and started a new game and began an Overcharmed run. It's really hard for me to find anything bad about this game and i feel like a nitpicker if i do say anything negative.

Many metroidvanias i've played have had underwhelming bosses. Usually they are too easy due to the player being able to level up way too much or because they are just a one trick pony that require no effort to beat. You just power through blindfolded while mashing the attack button. In HK it is highly recommended you learn the enemies' patterns unless you want to constantly keep getting your ass handed to you. Sure there are a few bland ones but never before i have actually been exhilarated to fight a boss in a metroidvania. Nightmare King Grimm is without a doubt the best boss i have ever faced in a 2D game.

Even if the music wasn't too memorable for me, it's still good, if that makes any sense. I might not go to youtube every month to listen to HK's soundtrack but i still like it because it fits the game's world so well. It's like a part of it and playing the game without music is out of the question.

Speaking of the world of HK, i found it very similar to the Souls games'. You meet NPC's all over the world and through them you uncover the story. There's enough ambiguity and mystery to keep me interested and wanting to search (almost) every nook and cranny to learn more what's going on. The finely crafted world helps alleviate that occasional frustration of having to travel to some far away place where there are no benches or fast travel points nearby.

I play with a Dual Shock 4 and not once did i blame the controls for my deaths. The gameplay is pretty much perfect.

Not quite what I expected. I love Metroid games and this game is marketed as a "Metroivania" style game. I would like this game if it cut out 75% of the maps and areas. The maps are disguised as HUGE but realistically 75% of them are simply there to cover ground, make the game longer, or optional. I put 30 hours into this game and it was 25 repetitive hours and getting lost. The other 5 hours had good content and cool design and bosses.

Most of the maps are just huge maps where you do nothing but move across it but twerked to look different. A lot of the sections and areas aren't even worth exploring. More than a dozen times I would think I "discovered" something, spend a ton of time exploring it and in the end getting some currency or something I can sell for more currency. 

You don't really get better armor, weapons, or gear. You get a few moves that aren't very useful and you will use maybe 5 times. Your main skill is literally the attack skill you start with and a dash skill you get early on. 

You get some equipable "passives" but you can only use about 3-5 at a time. I believe there's 25-30 total but you'll have to spend a TON of time hunting them and most of them aren't even worth finding. I used the same 5 throughout the whole game out of the 20 that I found or bought. There's a TON of room, NPC, interactive objects that give random dialogue but nothing else. If it doesn't give an upgrade, a crucial puzzle, an important part of the story or lore it's just wasting time. The point is that there seems to be a TON of optional things that has weak incentive to actually do them while the main story and game seems to be weak and pushed aside.

Some of the monsters are flat out annoying. Not challenging. Annoying. Not only are most of the maps already huge for no reason but some of the monsters make it worse. You don't gain experience from killing them and killing them give negligible amounts of resources but a lot of times you're forced to fight them anyways, whether because they're in the way or they're just too fast to ignore.

The map system is a mess. Similar to Metroid games you have to find the room to unlock the map for the area. In this game it's an NPC that you have to pay currency to. Except that he only unlocks some parts of the area.... AND get this, it's in vague scribbles with no distinct landmarks, rooms, or markings. To do that you have to buy individual components from the shop in town... do you want to SEE where the fk you are on the map?! that takes up an equipable passive slot after you find/buy it. Do you want to see where save points are on the map? you have to buy that. Stations? buy it. Trams? buy it. You have to unlock your map and after doing so it's still messy.

Overall there are things that this game does right. Some of the boss fights are fun. Some of the levels look interesting and beautiful but I feel like there's way more quantity than quality. I think if they cut out 75% of the maps that are there to add time, cut out all the maps and paths for the optional items and finds and add them to the main path and branch out from there instead of making whole areas dedicated to useless optional things. Just cut out all the distractions and show the shorter more refined game. 

I would recommend this game for completionists. People who like to collect things or who like to explore without a real story, motive, or reason. If you can enjoy your own time exploring just to explore and collect things for the sake of it you'll enjoy this game. If you're looking for substance and something more fleshed out you'll feel bored and disappointed.

The bread and butter of this game is exploration in a huge world with many unique regions, each one harboring dozens of secret nooks and crannies that will require advanced skills and knowledge to access. The lethal inhabitants you'll encounter along the way don't usually pose much of a threat by themselves, but the bosses can be really intense and often had me experimenting with 3 or 4 different equipment loadouts before I found a successful strategy. If gameplay is all you care about, Hollow Knight has that base fully covered.

Now, onto the small things that make this game so charming:

* The world has a rich mythos buried in its dusty corners. Normally I'm not a fan of fragmented, obscure storytelling, but it works surprisingly well in a Metroidvania. It's possible to arrive at the final boss without knowing much other than that something really bad happened to the world... but if you poke around a bit, there's a ton of history and background characters to discover.

* Every NPC is interesting. They're all endearing in their own way, even the bristly and depressing ones. And many of them are worth revisiting several times.

* The soundtrack is fantastic. It's often mellow and bittersweet, great for exploring, but the boss music is on another level. Seriously, there are multi-million-dollar-budget games that don't have music half this good.

* If you're into ridiculous platforming challenges, one of the optional late-game areas is nearly impossible in my opinion. I gave up and swore it off after a few hours and hundreds of failures.

All in all I don't have a single bad thing to say about Hollow Knight.

Ok so, I'm kinda bitter and requesting a refunds as I type this.

So I got into the game and was starting to get my feet wet. Trying to do the explore thing. Which felt oddly boring actually. Just trying to get from one room to another, killing a bad guy, dead end, go back, try another way, area that requires you to find something to progress. I didn't find it exciting at all.

It literally is a game of "Find the cape that lets you dash across wide gaps that will let you move to area C so you can find a hammer that lets you smash the floor so you can go back to area A to access area B that you couldn't access before. You'll find yourself constantly going around in circles trying to find that entry way that's on your map but not sure which of the 3 doors it is.

The killer for me was this though, I was about 3 hours into the game and had not died once. I dropped into this room with two really obnoxious guys that killed me. When I respawned, all the money I had accrued was gone. My "Mana" pot was broken so I could carry less mana for less abilities, and it had spawned me somewhere 5-10 minutes back so I had to make my way all the way back over again. This is not a punishment system I like at all. Particularly the money loss. I might have forgiven the broken mana thinking that maybe I hadn't found a shop yet or something to repair it, but what does it matter if all my money is gone? Now the game is harder, and I keep losing money. I hate it.

Ok now that I've gotten my general gripe out of the way, I'll try and toss in some good points.

The game is absolutely gorgeous. I love the art style. Its kinda dark with a halo affect around your character kind of what you saw in Diablo II. Really sets a solid ambiance.

There's great music! One of the few games that I actually left the music on to enjoy instead of turning on my pandora.

The combat feels pretty smooth. You have a lot of control over your character so combat feels fair. If you mess up, its usually your own fault. There's none of those "OH COME ON THERE'S NO WAY HE HIT ME!" moments. Its pretty damn clear they hit you.

Overall though I found the gameplay unengaging. Most of the time I was just trying to figure out how to get from one place to the next with no real idea which way I should be going. I ended up doing a lot of backtracking which I just hate. I want to feel like I'm constantly progressing in a game and backtracking just sucks me right out of it. If its your thing though, give it a whirl. Its fairly priced at only 15$. So..... eh.

I'll start this with simply saying this game is a masterpiece. Describing it as just a good "Dark soulsy" game is nothing but narrowminded and ignorant. But that's what gaming journalism is nowadays.

The game shows how much attention to detail was given by team cherry by creating this game. The game design is very unique and the game characters have a certain charm you wouldn't be able to find anywhere else. 

The game rewards exploration. Side quests are very smartly done and rarely feel like side quests because of how well they fit into the world. The game completion up to 107% simply makes sense and is designed to give you the full experience of hollow knight. However there are many things that do not contribute to completion percentage. I finished the game on PC and switch up to 107% and there are still things to understand about the lore.

- The soundtrack by christopher larkin is simply beautiful and each area's music fits perfectly. My favorites will always be path of pain, colosseum of fools and Nosk (fits in a very creepy way).

- The game difficulty is well above average if it's your first playthrough. But it's not a merciless type of game. Just difficult enough for a great challenge.

- The lore is the only thing I would compare slightly to dark souls. However it's not that simple. There are so many tiny details and unique characters that make it stand out. 

- Gameplay is the embodiment of simple but perfectly done. Very fluid and responsive. The charm system is great.

I really wanted to see this game expand even more. But turns out the upcoming gods and glory DLC will be the last. The game could take you 25-30 hours if you're rushing for the credits to roll. Could take up to 60-80 if you're looking to get 107% without help.

One of my favorite games of all time. Thank you Team Cherry.

So as I write this Im actually playing the game on my Nintendo Switch and I have about 22 hours of gameplay in and I believe im probably closing in on the end of the main game, though Im told im about 60-70% of the way done.

I grew up loving a lot of Metroidvanias, the Prime series, Metroid Fusion, all the GBA and DS Castllevania games, but somewhere after playing Order of Ecclesia I fell out of the genre, maybe my patience changed, or my tastes, maybe they jsut stopped making good Castlevania games but I hadn't felt the interest in them for some time.  I heard a lot about Hollow Knight and I loved the art style for the game, when I picked it up it didn't initially click, I wasn't sure where to go and put it down.  Fast forward months later to the Switch release, I give it another go.  And damn, I cannot put it down.  Team Cherry nails that feeling of progression so well in Hollow Knight.  Every ability you earn just makes the game feel more and more open, I remeber first getting the claw that lets you wall jump and immediately thinking so many areas that I could now check out.  As happened when I earned the double jump and the dash.  

And that's the beauty of Hollow Knights progress system, while a lot of games in this genre just give you a tool that allows you to interact with a specific door or lock type (Dead Cells, and many Metroids) Hollow Knight gives you specific skills that youll use constantly that allow you to travel to new locales, you dont get a sword upgrade that lets you open purple doors, though you do get one that lets you interact with every NPC in a unique way.  You get movement skills that youll use constantly as well as allowing you more freedom to explore the games world.  

The games world itself is vast and growing, as I moved on I was continually impressed with the size of the games tunnels and hallways, each area being distinct and interesting in art design (though all the areas generally follow a similar pattern of open spaced and closed space) all the enemies, music and art is unique giving each area it's own feel and challenge.  Travelling almost never feels like a chore because new connections are always being made, the entire game world is connected much like Dark Souls 1, I was often surprised to explore somewhere and later find out I was back in another area by total accident.  It makes discovery organic and fun but also genuinely rewarding as you find special shortcuts you didnt see before.

Combat is simple, fluid and fast.  Enemies have tells and patterns, overall everything can be overcome with patience and focus, you have a basic attack, some special ones and spells you can use to overcome any enemy and the charm system allows for unique character builds that allow some freedom in playstyle.  Simple enough for newcomers but deep enough to truly master.

Hollow Knight is a masterpiece of it's category, a steal at it's price point and a genuinely beautiful and captivating piece of work.  If you're a fan of the genre, it's worth every penny and much much more.  I would give more money to Team Cherry if I could.  Buy this game.

I love Metroidvanias. Love them. Hollow Knight fits the definition of one, but it serves for me as a cautionary tale of what happens when you screw up the fundamentals of the genre.

It has a rich aesthetic that seems to try a bit too hard to skate a line between creepy and endearing, like some kind of bootleg Tim Burton joint. At the end of the day though, it's not ugly. I can love a game where the art doesn't do a lot for me. But really, what sucks the life out of this game for me are two things: atrocious, joyless movement physics and the unshakable feeling that every single thing you have to do as you progress in the game is a dismal chore. 

Movement has no weight whatsoever. Your character and monsters have total air control. No resistance. No sense of momentum. Utterly joyless and unrewarding. It's hard to overstate how detracting it is to the overall game, seeing as it's something you're doing the entire game. As though it's not bad enough that movement itself isn't enjoyable, the double whammy is the tedium of backtracking as implemented. Seeing that it's a staple of the genre, you'd think that faster travel across a vast labyrinth and save spots closer to bosses would be top of mind for whoever was handling level design. Instead, the dev team thought the best way to make the player spend their time was to spend most of it joylessly traipsing about to explore. It only gets worse as you progress. There are very few meaningful shortcuts that tie together the expanding labyrinth as you progress. The game ends up feeling more like an uninteresting sprawl than an interconnected world. The further I got into this game, the more tiring doing the simplest tasks became. It's all one big chore. 

This could've been pretty great, my opinion on the art notwithstanding. I bought it a couple years back and finally got around to playing. I was even kinda stoked to do so. Unfortunately, I don't think the dev team knows what makes a good Metroidvania, let alone an exceptional one. They couldn't nail the cornerstones of the genre, but went on to build a hellish sprawl of a game around their fundamental failures anyway. It's a big oof out of 10 from me.

The Metroidvania genre of games is one I am quite fond of, they're the games that reward players like myself who explore every nook and cranny. Hollow Knight offered me an interesting world to explore, with memorable characters to find and meet as I played through the game. Throughout the game I noticed certain thematic elements I enjoyed in other games, like Demon's Souls/Dark Souls. Such as the various wandering NPC's you encounter and help along with their stories. And that wonderful melancholy that places like Dirtmouth have. The world building is similar to a souls game, in that nothing is told to you directly, only bits and pieces, that you assemble on your own.

The boss fights are memorable and fun, with tougher variants of some of these boss fights also available. Multiple endings to try and unlock, and many key items and collectibles to hunt down and further imrpove your character and the areas they can access. The free additional content for the game has been excellent so far, and I look forward to trying the Godmaster add-on and any paid content that becomes available afterwards.

i was going to wait until gods & glory came out to review hollow knight, but i think i'll just do it now
i'm a big fan of super metroid and those types of games, but i won't compare this to those games too much because i'd like to talk about its own strongsuits

put short, this game is incredible. when i paid about $9 (it was on sale), i was expecting a decent metroidvania that would keep me occupied for a little while. i did not expect this game to grip me as much as it did, both in appreciating its details and in the number of hours i put into it, as well as the hours i've yet to dump. i knew almost nothing about this game other than "it's a good metroidvania". i didn't bother to watch any trailers for it, and i went in completely blind. for just $10, this has been one of the most enjoyable and unforgettable video game experiences i'll ever have.

there's a lot to love in this game, from its hand drawn artstyle to its tight controls and everything inbetween. the music in this game is beautiful; christopher larkin (an actor and musician whose work extends outside hollow knight) did an amazing job capturing the feeling of each area and battle. i can't get enough of his soundtrack. all of the areas in the game look very pleasant, with so many different set pieces and backgrounds to gawk at, and all of the pretty particle effects that go with it. one complaint i have is that some of the foreground pieces can obscure your vision, but not enough to completely mess you up. in the case of deepnest, it's obviously supposed to make you feel claustrophobic and nervous, but this isn't exclusive to that area. i've noticed this in queen's gardens. all of the dialogue is well-written, which makes the characters seem more believable and their text boxes more pleasant to read, and the gibberish language that they speak when you talk to them helps with the immersion as well.  it almost seems like a real language, with some of the words being consistent throughout the game with different characters. 
the world is huge and full of content, but it doesn't suffer from this "bloat". everything is placed with care and attention, which makes all the areas and the entire map feel more alive. exploration in this game is a joy, and some of the best that this genre of video game has to offer. there's hardly any linearity, other than the required events and the upgrades you have to pick up. but once you get another new item, the world just opens up even more, with lots of goodies to find and things to fight. anyone that complains about the world being too big to explore or having too much backtracking is probably just a scrub lol

combat in this game also feels nice. everything works as it should, and the swinging of your nail as well as the sounds and effects that play when hitting enemies feels good. it's all properly bug-like. using the downward slash over and over is a little too good though, as enemies might not have a way to strike upwards. some bosses are a good challenge, and very exciting to fight. 

overall, this game is just great. i don't think it's worth the $15 price tag, since it can offer over 40 hours of playtime, has had multiple free dlc updates, and has so much love and care put into every nook and cranny. it could easily cost about $30 and still be a worthwhile purchase. just going through every little detail and seeing the thought that was put into this game makes me appreciate it more and more, and every time i felt like the game needed something, it gave me just that. there's a lot to see in hollow knight, including the best character in the game, the dung defender. if you like metroidvanias then you should love hollow knight, and if this is your first footstep into the genre, other titles may pale in comparison. there's a lot i have to say about this game but i have that problem where you end up speaking only like 50% of what you have in your brain, so i hope i did a decent job of telling you how much i like this game

Hollow Knight is painfully simplistic for its runtime. That isn't to say that it's a bad experience, but it doesn't change all that much over time.

You start with a slash move, a heal that takes time to use (we'll get back to this), and a ranged attack that requires the usage of a resource called SOUL.
It's not that complicated, soul is simply a resource you collect by attacking enemies, and it can be used to do certain spells such as the ranged attack, and to heal, "creating a dynamic" where you need to decide whether you will heal or use a spell.

And it's here that things already start to fall apart. It's blindingly obvious that every boss fight was designed with the time it takes to heal in mind. You have little to no breathing room in many fights, and as a result risking a heal often results in a net neutral or worse getting hit multiple times, due to the decision to make the bodies of the enemies act as both a hitbox and a hurtbox. Getting grazed by a hit is not uncommon because the models for the bugs fill up a lot of space, and touching them hurts you, and every hit you take interrupts whatever you were doing.

This isn't necessarily bad on its own if the boss fights varied more during the game, but I can confidently say almost all of them ended up presenting more or less the same challenge. Spam your single attack move over and over on the boss, dodge their body and attacking projectiles until you can attack them again. If you get hit, you might have a short window where you can heal one point of health, but that's most of it. Charms allow you to offset this interaction somewhat, but not enough to change the overall pattern. No amount of making the execution of the task more difficult makes it much more interesting or allows you to optimize beyond taking advantage of the narrow attack windows presented.

It speaks volumes when the most significant upgrades to combat are the ones that change your stumpy fast slash attack into a faster or longer slash attack. Your movement options don't really change the experience of combat, because you'll either be so mobile they become trivial or you'll realize they were a crucial element for that encounter to make it more manageable as the fights get faster and more hectic. Your attack moves remain limited, because the "nail arts" you get, amount to charged attacks that suffer the same problem as healing. You have to hold a button for a prolonged period of time where you'll probably get interrupted before you get a chance to use the move. So your best, and for most purposes, only option is to spam your single attack move, or use a spell that is competing with your heal meter.

Exploration is arguably where Hollow Knight shines most, but I found myself glazing over at how blocky and rigid all the environments were. It became more an exercise of navigating geometry then forging my way through a hostile world, and it made me incredibly aware that I was playing a video game. On top of this, enemies respawn when you switch screens, so you'll be blitzing your way through chumps the whole time, and that soul bar will be used near exclusively to top up after getting nicked by some random bug you accidentally touched.

Or you'll be using it to heal after encountering a jumping puzzle for the umpteenth time, this time with spikes, or sawblades, or some enemy sitting in the way. Which will take life from you every time you fail and reset you back to a checkpoint. By itself this isn't so bad, but it gets compounded by the death system which will lock away a portion of your soul meter, and force you to fight a shade of yourself to get it back that might have spawned in an inconvenient location. And jumping puzzles do have sections where you'll be stuck between spikes, and you'll either make the next jump, take damage and reset, or die.

These all sound like small issues, but they add up over the span of the game because it's all you'll be doing over and over. And unless doing platforming across and around rectangles with a sprinkle of hyperfocused single attack combat does it for you, then that's really it. You won't be able to progress without accepting that this is the reality of the game.

I don't like being mean, but I need to be honest; I wasn't impressed with Hollow Knight. I can see that a lot of work went into the environments, creatures, and expanding the map to include an exceeding amount of content for its price tag (all things considered, Hollow Knight is an excellent product). But the execution of its ideas is lacking in flavor. This game shines as an example of how increased difficulty doesn't add depth, it only forces near optimal execution into being a necessary way to play. And if those strategies and interactions don't have variety, and the gameplay boils down to muscle memory and repetition, then the experience becomes homogenous and tedious -- for me, and that often doesn't add up to much.

With so many people praising this as the pinnacle of the metroidvania genre, I knew I had to check it out. My first time around however, I managed to fall to the bottom of deepnest without warning, after almost having saved up enough money for the lantern. After a grueling hour of trying to get out, I had lost all my money and quit the game entirely. Coming back to it a year later, I started a fresh save and managed to enjoy the game more because I knew the map layout and I specifically avoided deepnest until later. Because I got the main ending this time though, more glaring issues popped up that I didn't realize were huge detriments before. 

First of all, the upgrade system is slow and badly staggered. The upgrades themselves (wall jump, double jump, dash, etc.) aren't bad in design, but the timing at which they're delivered is. Sometimes you'll play for hours without approaching a new one, but then towards the end you'll get two within 30 minutes of each other. 

Second, the map is too big for its own good. Around mid-game, you'll have 15 or so open paths around the world that need checked once you get a new ability. Even with the map markers, it's near impossible to know which ones require which ability off the top of your head, so it requires a lot of backtracking just to find your next destination. 

Third and last, losing all your money upon death is an awful addition and ruins the core of the game for me. Sure, I lost all my money to deepnest the first time around, but that's not common. What is common is wanting to explore a metroidvania's world to the fullest. I would have loved to do this if it wasn't for the constant fear of losing all my precious savings to exploring an unfamiliar (and most likely deadly) area. This made me want to only take baby steps throughout the world, retreating at anything that could truly have a chance to kill me. And when I do die, sometimes it's in an area that I don't even want to go back to, but I have to in order to collect my money again. If this mechanic was entirely removed, I could overlook the other issues. But as it stands, it completely ruined the exploration for me (until late game I guess).
Simply put, this game is amazing. I have nothing else to say just buy it and you'll find out.

This game has a nice atmosphere, cute characters and beautiful music. However, I found it too frustrating to play. I thought I would be diving into a slick platforming experience like with the Ori games, but I was pretty disappointed on that front. The movement is not very smooth, and the directional attacks are bit janky. I found myself unable to reliably perform the mid-air downward strikes. This is unfortunate seeing as this particular attack is essential for things like jumping over impossible gaps by hitting an enemy, or just for getting additional height from some bouncy mushrooms. I couldn't play this game for more than five hours because I was screaming at my screen every time I fell into a pit because the game decided to ignore my key-press. Before anyone says anything, yes I did try it on controller too, which in my opinion actually felt worse.

So in conclusion, yes it is a beautiful game, but it is not for those who are easily frustrated (like me.) Do not be fooled by the cartoony aesthetic and do not buy it just because it's a classic that everyone is talking about. It is a hard game and it might not be worth the trouble.

I waited until this game was fairly cheap, because the art style had turned me off for a long time.

HK is a pretty game, with very nice music, and lots of polish -- it's obvious that much passion went into this project, but it exhibits some issues of being in development too long. Some things have been *too* thought out - specifically the deliberately annoying, huge, confusing areas, and the enemy designs that aren't really fun to fight because they mostly involve waiting for openings and not being able to accomplish much in the meantime. If you die against a boss, there's usually not a save point anywhere nearby, so you have to spend a long time getting back. I don't mind a cryptic game where you have to find your own way, but the mapping system is frustrating as well. Money drops are inadequate for purchases and one of the mandatory purchases is extremely expensive. There's just questionable design decisions all over the place. I really wanted to like this game, after hearing the near-universal praise for it, but it's just been an exercise in frustration for me specifically. If I had more time to master the game and spend backtracking and running back to bosses I've died against, maybe I'd find the game more enjoyable.

tl,dr: Avoid if you have a day job, or otherwise limited free time, because this game does not respect it. Otherwise, go for it.

THIS WILL CONTAIN SOME SPOILERS !!!
I started playing the game, and had this problem right away, right on Greenpath, there's a hole in the wall. In order for you to get to it you need to slash some rope and drop a stone in front of it. Except, i had no indication that that was possible, making it pretty easy to miss, specially when it clearly looked like i needed some dash or double jump ability to get to it, as in any other metroidvania. So i tried to look for somewhere else to go. Ofc after exploring everything i could, i didn't find anywhere else to go, and had to google it up. There i started having a bad feeling that this was gonna happen recurrently throughout the whole game.

Happily, it didn't happen too much. After that, the game started flowing better. Although the map system doesn't help, the minimal amount of teleport locations doesn't help, the fact that important locations on the map look just like everywhere else and are very forgettable also doesn't help, i managed to have some fun during most of my 20 hours of gameplay.

I specially liked the bossfights, that require you to be very aggressive, but attentive and focused. Has some of the best bossfights i ever played in any game. The platforming isn't that great, but the bossfights are, specially the hardest ones.

But then i got to the second occasion where i got completely lost. Now i have explored 80% of the whole map. It's a very big map, and I've explored it plenty of times, not knowing where to go next. I got some upgrades i wouldn't have gotten if i haven't explored the map very extensively, but even so i didn't find the next main area to go to.

So i google it up again. I turns out that i need a key to get to the next main area. A simple key, that i had once before, i bought it on a shop, and used it on a door. [b] This was a very good indication that there's only one of those keys in the game.[/b] But no, there are another 3 keys that are scattered in some random places, that I've been to before more than once and just missed them.

I really feel like the game wants you to be lost on purpose. Wasting time being lost in a game isn't fun, and i really don't like games that make you waste your time on purpose. I'm giving it a bad review. Maybe if you have plenty of time to waste on a game you will like Hollow Knight, but imo, there are way better metroidvania's to play, like Ori for example.

One of the best games I've played, ever. I was gifted the money to buy it by a friend and, gosh, I'm so happy they did. Sorry if I repeat anything or if it's a little messy, this is my first time writing a real, in depth review and I love the game to death.
Before I begin, this game will be so much better if you don't know much about it. Have you seen one or two trailers and are interested to look at more? Buy it! Don't delve deeper into anything outside of the game. Hop in now. Though I have tried to keep this review completely spoiler free.
If you already know tons about the game, then play it anyway, because the gameplay will make up for the loss of some mystery.
Pros -
This game has a stunning artstyle and lovely characters. Even some of the enemies I just want to stop and appreciate. You can see the love and care that went into the backgrounds and animations. Clean, fluid, and despite how different they all are, they all fit the aesthetic of the game, especially the animations. As someone who has dabbled with animation in the past, I've seen how tough it can be, but the animations here are perfection. It's masterfully crafted and a joy to see.
The gameplay is endlessly fun, simple but unique. It's easy to pick up, but as you get more powers through the game you start getting more intricate with how you control. I play with a keyboard because I don't have a controller, but I've been told it's a lot better with the latter. So far I haven't run into trouble with the controls, though. Only dumb mistakes on my part because I hit a wrong button or something similar. You'll have to get used to mistakes like those in your first few hours, but take care and you'll improve in no time.
Hey, can I talk about the characters again? They're all charming in their own way, and they're all very memorable. I'm always excited to run across familiar faces while exploring, they feel very alive and are very well written, you can feel their unique personalities, even after talking to them maybe once or twice.
Each area has its own aesthetic and, most of the time, colour. This makes them very distinct and easy to tell apart, sometimes they even leak into each other and you know they're leaking in because you see enemies or background props from them, or you know if you're approaching a new area because the colour scheme has changed. This struck me when I had only entered three areas. That's how distinct they are. And they're all as beautiful as each other.
I didn't play the game before the DLC was added (besides Godmaster) but I didn't even realise the DLC content was DLC until I checked because of how nicely it was crafted in. Perfectly placed and blended in, though I haven't run into much of it. But that's fine, just means I have more to explore! On that note...
So much content! The map is huge! I'm 16 hours in (though by the time anyone reads this I'll probably be much deeper!) and I've discovered so much more than I thought I would when I first started playing, but from what I can tell, there's so much more to discover, so many more characters to meet, so many more bosses to fight... I want to see it all!
The soundtrack (and sound design!), just for this review I'm listening to it outside of gameplay for the first time. It perfectly captures any emotions you're meant to feel. Peace, tension, fear, adrenaline, wonder and horror, and so many more, the amount of feeling the game mixed with this music can bring out is unbelievable. Everything is made expertly and placed in the right place, but it's still amazing on its own. Props to Christopher Larkin for making and adding something beautiful to the already beautiful game.
The game has steady progression and you'll never feel like you haven't discovered something in a while, unless you're really stuck (more on that later). Each major boss gives you something to use in the future (at least so far) and discovering new areas is the best feeling, possibly one of the best in the whole game. Just when you think you've found the most unique one, you find another!
The lore, the lore, the lore! Or, the mystery of the lore! Both! As an arachnophobe, I never thought I could enjoy a story about little bug things. But I am. So much. Every time I see something minor, a statue, a name, a new item, I want to see everything about it! I find myself making little theories on the go. The way it's laid out is.. the best. I love games with mystery in them but you rarely see it done well. Here, though? It's some of the best storytelling I've ever seen, possibly in any medium. Not so vague you're confused, but not so in-your-face that it's not a mystery. Always looking forward to more.
This is the midpoint between pro and con; the difficulty. If you're not one for difficult games, then it's a con. But if you like a good challenge? It's a definite pro! The bosses (at least from where I am) aren't so painfully hard it's impossible but aren't so painfully easy that it's just "oh. that's over.". It's the perfect level of struggle, and I've felt amazing after defeating every boss, whether it took me one try or ten tries.
Cons -
The only con I could possibly fathom was sometimes you'll get stuck or lost. The map is large, which is amazing, but also sometimes hard to navigate if you're mapless. You may have to use a walkthrough a few times, but it's no large trouble.

I have endless respect for everyone at Team Cherry and everyone that helped make this game a reality. It's one of the best game experiences I've ever had and I'm so happy I was given the honour of playing it. I'm always excited to do more. I very, very highly recommend it.
Now if you don't mind, I'm going to go play it some more....!

[h1] First, the good: [/h1]
Gameplay - very good
Controls - nothing wrong there
Challenging - hell yes
Music, Art - really nice :)
Story, lore - as good as you can except from this type of game I guess, I liked it very much actually :)  Too mystical though (you don't ever really get to know what's going on and why - unless you google it )

[h1] Bad:	[/h1]

As said by many people before me - you only get a few predetermined set of safe points (benches). Every time you die you start up from there. Now this isn't bad of its own, but when you get to one of the boss fights and you have to get back to him from the bench every time he kills you - now that'll get annoying really quickly.  Sure, most of times the bench is close to the boss but come on - WHAT's the point of forcing the player to go through some meaningless area, kill some meaningless enemies, just to get another shot at that boss??
Would it really be that horrible to add a little pop-up window saying "You died. Would you like to try again? Yes (try it again right away) No (go to bench)" 

Stuff like that is just making the [b]game longer artificially.[/b] It doesn't make the game harder, it just make the game longer to play.

To be fair, you get the ability to create your own "safe point" later in the game - although this will ony help in exploration parts of the game, it won't help you in boss fights at all.

But that's not why I can't give this game a 100%.
No, it's the last two boss fights. I am now at the very end of the game and I am not sure if I'll ever finish it.

Ones I'm talking about:
[b]Nightmare King Grimm[/b] - the name is veeery fitting.
[spoiler] [b]Radiance[/b] - yeah.. it's a secret boss, I get that. But it's like 5 times harder than the "official" boss Hollow Knight.
That's a little too much, don't you think? I've managed to beat Hollow Knight on first or second attempt and can take him down with numerous charms combinations. Radiance on the other hand - I am lucky if I can get to phase three.[/spoiler]

[h1] Overall [/h1]
Boss fights in this game are all about timing - that's okay. Unless you take it to the extreme.
Taking down these two requires lots and lots of attempts just to get the timing right. And of course after EACH attempt you have to get back to the fight from the bench (well, in case of Grimm it's not from bench but.. doesn't matter).
And watch some intro animation. 

I get the logic behind difficulty of Grimm - it's a DLC and initially after few attempts I just thought - okaay, I'm not gonna beat this one. But the second one? Different story.

So please - patch those two a little bit, or just add that pop-up window I mentioned. 
Otherwise this is really beautiful game worth of playing.

Contrarian mode activated: I hate Dark Souls and what it did to Metroidvanias in particular. Metroidvanias already had challenging bossfights in them and there really is no reason to keep adding Dark Souls mechanics to every game now and make them even more challenging. 

Hollow knight often feels like it is funny to have you navigate a spiked death room before even allowing you to fight a boss. So every time you die you have to redo everything. 

Because that is what most pisses me off about Dark Souls inspired games: that their idea of difficulty is just to waste time as much as possible. Limit fast travel, limit hp recovery, limit everything and yeah sure you made it harder and extended the play time but it also is no longer fun to play.

Straight up: if you could just instantly retry the bosses in this game I might be incline to forgive it more but having 5min+ breaks every time I die just to die again on the next attempt cause I lost focus and getting more and more annoyed ruins the game. 

You can't gain power that much either. You start out with 5 HP and very very late game, way beyond what I played for you will have 8 hp. Yeah no. So basically that means you have hitless every boss in the game. Which is very easy with the first boss as that guy has windup times for days but then you come to stuff like the Mantis Lords and see that the bosses haven't even enough gaps in their patterns anymore for you to be able to heal because that is accompanied with a long animation of you just standing still. 

To conclude F*** Dark Souls and everything it stands for. Especially F*** the players that think obnoxious difficulty is the only metric to measure the quality of a game by. Because from an artisitc stand point Hollow Knight is very good. Visuals and sound design are great, so is movement and combat. If they didn't fall into the trappings of the gitgud crowd then this would be a really fun game. Hashtag bring back difficulty settings!
