[h1][b][i]Hollow[/i][/b] Knight[/h1] wow.

To state the obvious, yes, the art is pretty and painstakingly crafted (even if I don't personally find it very appealing), and, yes, the value for money here is excellent. The sheer ludicrous quantity of content for the price tag would make this game an easy recommendation, if not for the fact that most of it (whilst not remotely as lazily copy-pasted) suffers from the 'Ubisoft' effect. 
The thing is, whilst the game does do (heh), and have, a lot of things, rarely any terrible, and most very serviceable, little of it is remarkable enough to warrant ur time. And ur time, this game will take a lot, from repeating (painfully) slow (& tedious) animations often, to placing check-points too far (from boss fights/challenges), to even just simply navigating the menu, the game doesn't respect the player's time. (It even commits the cardinal sin of putting a checkpoint before a ~'cutscene' [spoiler]The Lost Kin[/spoiler].) Moreover, some areas of the game, the worst among them being 'Greenpath' are, downright, a boring slog to play thru, especially when u start out, though it doesn't get any better after u unlock more movement abilities either). Similarly, much of [spoiler]Fungal Wastes, City of Tears, and Kingdom's Edge[/spoiler] feels needless, like padded out empty space (in-b/w-places to mindlessly slaughter thru to ur destination, as quickly as possible preferably). [spoiler]Howling Cliffs, Royal Waterways, Ancient Basin, and Fog Canyon also, set themselves up brilliantly with their atmosphere but ultimately had little pay-off[/spoiler]. It was rare that I felt adequately rewarded for enduring thru the game's bullshit & tedium.
Furthermore, as it turns out, the game isn't particularly difficult either (so far as I've played), with most boss fights being a matter of pursuing upgrades or equipping the right charms. There were only a couple odd standouts (namely: [spoiler] The Hornet Protector, The Mantis Lords (tho really the strategy here was to upgrade ur damage and health), The Soul Master[/spoiler]) of the dozens I encountered (as the game likes to boast), that were actually fun/rewarding to fight against. Speaking of the charms, tho they maybe numerous (once again as the game likes to boast), few are really worth the precious notches? slots? (u get the idea), and most are either downright useless, direct upgrades over others, or solutions to (wut feel like) artificial hurdles.

Finally the few remarkable things, [spoiler]Crystal Peak[/spoiler] feels excellent to play through. Not only does it have, arguably, the best ability in the game (especially cus it instantly opens up the map and allows u to access other powerful abilities), but the pacing, check-point spacing, and balance of all elements in this area is [b]*muah*[/b]. Its unequivocally, imho, the best area in the game, tho unfortunately, its also one of the shorter ones. The Forgotten Crossroads is also a very well paced introductory area, and [spoiler]The Deepnest, Queen's Garden, Soul Sanctum, and Fog Canyon (without the advanced movement abilities)[/spoiler] together, make for some of the most fun I had in this game. However, they are, once again, some of the shortest areas.
So, not having much positive to say about the game, Why did I play 50+hrs. of it then?
Wellp, being my first foray into Metroidvania & Dark-Souls-esque (fetishised masochism) genre of games, and having heard a lot of praise for this game, I kept pushing myself to play a little further, hoping that the game would reward me for my patience. I wished to play thru to completion to experience wut it feels like to beat the 'Final Boss' and master the game or wut have u, but I realised, quite late, that if I wasn't having fun at 50hrs into the game, I wasn't ever gonna' have fun. (I stopped shortly after discovering the Stag station, for context)
 
[h1]Conclusions:[/h1]
I certainly had higher expectations for this game; to me it feels like an effortful take on the 'Ubisoft formula' (impressive quantity of barely engaging content). Maybe this isn't the type of game for me, maybe its another instance of the charm of this game failing to work for me, whatev it maybe, my recommendation for the sequel would be to approach with a narrower scope and greater emphasis on quality. Or just go 3D like ROR2....

Mentioned below are a few solemn moments that delivered a genuine heartfelt experience. If u wish to play the game, I'd recommend discovering them urself.
[spoiler] 
The Midwife trying to turn me into a quick snack, one of the few completely unexpected moments.
Sitting on the Rostrum, simply, listening to the Songstress' melody.
Looking thru the telescope in the Watcher's spire and seeing the world in 3D for the first time. [/spoiler]

Damn, I'm pretty late to this one. Either way, this game is fuckin great; it's got a nice cartoony artstyle (which I'm glad they didn't go the "oh I'm an Indie so I must use pixel art" route), interesting characters with strangely realistic personalities, and a somewhat depressing "macabre-esc" feel to the entire game. Though, even with it's mysterious and sad plot, the game still does not fail to portray a sense of wonder as you explore the beautifully crafted kingdom of Hollownest. The gameplay itself is nice and tight, and though there is a badge system, the game doesn't get any more complicated than: press the "attack" button to attack, press the "focus" button to use magic, and hold the "focus" button to heal (and some other moves that you'll probably end up forgetting). As a metroidvania, the game really stands on it's own, since in most games of the genre they give you a vast assortment of weapons, magic abilities, badges, etc. etc. Speaking of metroidvanias, another thing synonymous with the genre is the crazy batshit difficulty that a lot of the games have. Hollow Knight is no exception, this game gets fucking evil by the time you get to the late-game, and due to the simplistic combat system, unless you got some OP badges you are fucked.

Overall, Hollow Knight is a surprisingly solid addition to the metroidvania genre that I highly recommend to any fans of the genre (as well as some that don't enjoy these kinds of games). This was easily one of the best indie games I have played (despite playing it about a YEAR after all the hype around it died out) and *was* a good contender for GOTY 2017.
Though the game does actually have problems (voice acting is charming but a little hard to get used to, the game slows down around mid-game, etc.), it's still extremely fun and entertaining. 9/10

Don't get me wrong, the game looks good and is in general a very nice experience. It is a basic metroidvania, which means you have an open world that you traverse by getting new abilities like double jumping, dashing and so on, which opens a lot of new areas. And this works pretty well the most of time. Additional to that they introduced some kind of Dark Souls system where you have to find your Geo before dying a second time.
However, the bosses in this game are just overall bad in my opinion. Instead of making the bosses interesting, they mostly just included other stuff to worry about, either being hazards that you have to dodge around while dodgin the boss or just several minions that can even respawn. In combination with a small 2D area, you will often find yourself in situations where it is just impossible to not get hit, you can only decide where you want to take damage. In general, this alone wouldn't be too much of a problem, because the game has a system where you get energy for attacking enemies, that you can then use to heal yourself. So by just attacking the boss, you should theoretically be able to heal these unfair hits. The problem is now, that most bosses have attacks that reach from one end of the arena to the other. Combine that with the additional minions and you usually don't have any time to heal yourself (and I even used the item that increases the healing speed). This leads to a lot of frustration while fighting these bosses, because it feels never fair. And all of this doesn't even include that some bosses can just do double damage with certain attacks.
Cranked to eleven got that when fighting in an arena. Not only do you have to fight around 10 minutes of spawning enemies, sometimes even without ground to stand on. That alone is hard enough, because you are not allowed to make many mistakes, but at the end, they also introduce a boss that you have never seen before and that consists of two enemies, one having an attack to get from one side to another again, meaning no chances to heal. And after loosing you have to fight these 10 minutes of small enemies again, which is already hard enough as I said.

In summary, while I really loved the exploration part with all its secrets, the bosses in this game are just weak in a lot of cases, which leads to a lot of frustration and in the end, this was the feeling that the game resulted in. So if you don't care about something like that and like exploration, the game is perfect. But if you don't want to try a boss 30 times (which is the amount of arena battles I tried to that point), then don't buy it.

Hollow Knight is a great metroidvania game, it uses exploration and rediscovery characteristics in a proper way. The difficulty level might be considered as medium, for experienced gamers it doesn't represent a challenge, for occasional gamers it could be difficult to beat the game for the first time, and it offers continuous challenges for beginners. The deep and dark story is a great element for the game, as it is optional to follow it people with no interest in it or people who are speed running can avoid getting into it with no further repercussion. The astonishing grafics, smooth sound effects, and surrounding music are well worked elements.
This game made me learn that I don't find 2D metroidvanias particularly enjoyable, and I have to thank the game for that.

This is a bit of a tough one. The game should be great. I love the art style and the music, the controls are tight, the fights challenging, and what I've seen of the story hints at something well worth exploring. The only problem is that the game isn't fun to play, and I think that mostly comes down to tedium.

The areas are beautiful, but you're traversing them often enough that they become boring. The bosses are challenging, but that's mostly because they have a lot of health -- you learn their pattern, it's simple enough, but ultimately you're going to have to hit them a hundred times and avoid getting hit more than five times. In close combat. With what is effectively a very short sword. While they have longer reach. And when you die, you have to walk back to the boss fight from a dozen screens away because there was no save point directly before the boss and fast travel doesn't exist.

There's a way to do hardness right, like in Super Meat Boy, where you have to do a hard thing once, and when you fail your next attempt starts immediately. That kind of game is immensely satisfying. Hollow Knight, on the other hand, wants you to do a medium-hard thing five times in a row without making a mistake, or else you die and your next attempt is five minutes away. That kind of game is immensely frustrating.

I should point out that I only ever got to the second boss, so mabye it gets better after that -- certainly many other people seem to love it. But up to that point, for me, it was a slog. A beautiful slog, but a slog nonetheless.

I originally played this game on console and eventually bought the steam version so i can use mods, just to explain why i only have 32 hours here.

The game itself is fine, though not as great as some say it to be. The fighting is fun. Nice regions, interesting story. The main problem i have with the game is the ridiculous amount of backtracking necessary. The regions are great but going through them 20 times is just too much. The large amounts of backtracking isn't too bad on a first playthrough but it makes replaying the game rather boring and tedious. There are only 1-2 fast travel points per region, some regions even don't have any at all.
Another problem is the grinding. The in-game currency "geo" is obtained by killing enemies, then collecting little silvery coins. But most enemies drop very little, especially in early game, forcing the player to grind so you can buy things. The worst case of this is an early game item that costs around 1200 geo, at a time where most enemies drop around 3 geo per kill. Whilst the currency is also obtainable by exploration which makes things a bit less terrible this still means that a lot of grinding is required in early game especially.
Besides these two points the game is nice. This is one of these cases where my recommendation is to try it and refund it if you don't like it or the beforementioned things make it unenjoyable for you.

I went on a bit of a Metroidvania kick in 2017- I played Symphony of the Knight, Super Metroid, Zero Mission, and Samus Returns, and enjoyed the lot. Without question, Hollow Knight blows them all out of the water. The world is immersive and shockingly expansive, the combat is finely tuned and fun, and the ambience and music are top-line. The tone rides the line between bleak and charming, and the characters you meet help stave off the sad tone of the world- for a time. The difficulty is nicely managed, giving you a nicely smooth curve with a couple real spikes of challenge. Even during those spikes, the game's perfect controls never feel like the scales are tipped too far against you. The game provides an enormous amount of content for $15, and is easily worth double or triple that price. It's like this game is always on sale, so... buy it.

This game has wonderful art, but...

1) The game is full with dead-ends, long walks and empty paths.
2) The "charm notches" and charm system overall actually flies in the face of everything that makes metroid-vania games what they are. It undermines the core reward behind exploration by limiting your capacity to actually use the tools that you unlock, so much so that I genuinely feel (having played and beaten metorid fusion, zero mission, AM2R, super metroid, Castlevania aria of sorrow, and symphony of the night, that this game is NOT a metroid-vania. It simply does NOT have the close knit interconnected world, the deep sense of progression and reward, or the brilliant level design that is a staple of the genre.
3) The game has a janky camera that does not properly show dangers such as spikes or enemies above or below, and the game does at best a terrible job of or at worst no job at all aof using the environment or other clues to actually warn the player about these obstacles.
4) The game is absurdly unfair. It's a shame I feel I have to say this, but I am no stranger to nor do I dislike a challenge, so I mean the word unfair when I say it. Just so this comment has some backing to it, I have played and beating dark souls 1, 2, 3, and bloodborne, as well as already mentioned, metroid fusion. So i am aware of what a challenge feels like - I want it and i look for it in games- but this game is genuinely unfair to the player in my opinion.

Ths game is largely improved by mods that disable the limitations on the charms you equip, and with such mods, the game does become a metroid-vania, which I apreciate.
Even so, the level and world design is a genuine slog to push through and, especially late game, become tiresome and boring.

I truly think the artists and musicians are absolute gems and deserve massive praise for their work here - this game's soundtrack and art are masterpieces. The game, however, is nowhere close.

Before you buy this game, there are a few very important things you should know:
Firstly, it's graphics intensive. Don't think you'll be able to play it on anything simply because it's a platformer. I have a integrated graphics chip on my computer, and there's a solid half second lag between the controls and the screen, which makes the game virtually unplayable. 
Secondly, some of the mechanics are extremely unfair. I won't go too far in case you want to enjoy this game yourself (and it is actually a wonderful game with all complaints aside, but that comes with some serious buts). Some examples include situations where it's impossible to reclaim your money without starting a boss fight, meaning you're almost doomed to lose it, or other places that are incredibly difficult to reach without dying. Another issue is that the animations on screen for taking, giving damage, and an assortment of other things can pile on to the point where it's almost impossible to avoid being hurt. Finally, I was somehow dumped into a somewhat late-game zone with no way out, meaning that I was entirely reliant a small amount of knowledge I already had about the game to circumnavigate some major areas of the map and get back to a place where I could play again. If you're willing to put up with the occasional bs (which I usually am), this is still a great game. That said, if you have integrated graphics, I strongly recommend against buying it, as it amplifies the level of occasional unfair incidents to the point where the game is more often unfun to play than fun.

A metroidvania-style game that looks fantastic, sounds great, has a really interesting aesthetic - but is ruined by some poor design choices, a barely comprehensible plot, and a frustrating degree of repetition.

Cons:

- Mapping is poorly implemented.
- Damage on contact with enemies is annoying.
- Bosses are bullet sponges.
- Requires far too much backtracking with a limited fast-travel system.

Hollow Knight, though bought for cheap, can't be described the same. It's the wonderfully fuzzy, warm experience that this game gives you that makes it worth purchasing, but there are a few major detractors for me personally, which many of the fanbase would consider heretical:

1. the Path of Pain, from the Grimm Troupe DLC
2. Godmaster DLC

Now there's a wierd consensus on the internet that if something is cheaply bought but expertly crafted, it's exempted from all criticism, so bear with me.

The Path of Pain and the Godmaster DLC are completely free, but they have major flaws in my eyes, and it comes down to what demographic in the playerbase Team Cherry wants to cater to more. The people who play for the great story and atmosphere, the rich art design for the characters and the environment, and the general sense of adventure that Metroidvanias excel at providing, versus the people who play to get the world record for best hitless speedrun of whatever.

Some might say that calling the Path of Pain unfair or flawed is the mark of someone who is salty that they haven't the skill the complete it, the in-community meme being "git-gud", but as someone who has completed it I can assure you that it's not only utterly not worth the effort, but the Path of Pain is in actuality the first indicator that Team Cherry was in favor of creating more content for the extremely "high-skilled" speedrun hitless players, over those who play for things such as story and art design and so on, which is extremely odd and ironic considering that the "reward" for the Path of Pain is simply a 3-second story cutscene and a Hunter's journal entry. By locking story elements behind absurd difficulty, a huge portion of the fanbase, the average players, is locked off from completely finishing the game unless they pour hours into practicing the platforming in Hollow Knight, which in my opinion needs extreme fine-tuning.

But it only gets worse with Godmaster.

See I haven't even played any Godmaster content, and I don't have to in order to tell you that it's for the hitless speedrunner Twitch-streaming crowd. A boss rush mode seems fine and dandy on paper, but locking the new true ending to the game behind what is to the average player an eternity of bossfights, back to back, is completely ridiculous. It's alienating the part of the playerbase that loves the lore, story, atmosphere, etc, behind an impossibly high skill ceiling; Only a select few have the time and patience to dedicate to this game's lacklustre platforming mechanics and incredibly tedious bossfights.

People have been asking for a way to repeat boss fights, because some are genuinely enjoyable, my favorites being the Mantis Lords, Soul Master, and Traitor Lord. The issue arises when a meta is born in a Metroidvania. Yes you read that correctly, there is a meta in Hollow Knight, in the choice of Charms one can equip, it is as follows:

1. Quick Slash
2. Mark of Pride
3. Fragile/Unbreakable Strength and/or Fragile/Unbreakable Heart

and for the Path of Pain specifically:
4. Hiveblood

And every other charm is relegated to the junk heap. This is an issue in an adventure game, where player freedom in how they want to approach the game is paramount. But while Godmaster does have mechanics that disable Charm effects, among other things, it is still indicative of how this portion of the playbase thinks and functions.

All in all, Hollow Knight is frustrating not because of the difficulty, but of how those like me, who enjoy taking their time with video games, to savor the experience and to wind down because our lives are a tumultuous wreathing pile of slag, are considered secondary on Team Cherry's priority list as opposed to the speedrunning hitless bossrush fans, which is why I have to give it a 7/10.

If you like adventure and exploration of a new world to a deliciously beautiful soundtrack, interactions with extremely unique characters and velvety rich atmospheres, or you just like taking your time with a game, then you will truly savour the first 40 hours of this game, and probably nothing beyond that unfortunately. 

If you like setting world records for fastest playthroughs, long and arduous boss rush modes, and aquisition of bragging rights, then you will absolutely love this game.

One of the most beautiful, intense, interesting, and fun games I've had the pleasure of experiencing. The combat is simple, but that basically means the combat fucking works. This game does not break, and even though there are times when it chooses to be motherfucking hard, it never feels unfair, or unwinnable. It boasts an incredible, fascinating world to explore and chart, extremely fun boss-battles, insane world-design and art-style, and an absolutely incredible sound-track. This is the only game I've ever felt the need to 100%, and it wasn't a slog to get there, it was actually tremendous fun and all the things you have to do to get to 100% completion are things that you end up wanting to do anyways. It can seem really daunting and overwhelming at first, so much so that I first played it for a couple days and abandoned it, but then a few months later I came back and I was completely sucked in to it, and I'm thankful I was. I still can't wrap my head around how such a small group of devs were so dedicated that they were able to produce this masterpiece, but why question a blessing? This game is easily worth 4 times more than they ask, please do yourself a favor and pick it up.

Hollow Knight is a difficult platformer.

Recommended to players that like the fragmented Downloadable Content style of patched design.
Not recommended to players that do not like the initial reception of losing geo and questioning why Confessor Jiji even exists.

The Good:

1. Music. Soothing and tranquil. Needed because of the difficulty.

2. Simplified introductory tutorials along a platformer path.

3. Easily customizable controls. And allowing for both a wireless Xbox controller and a trackball with multiple buttons to be played at the same time - ala Wiimote style with arms on the sides of a hip on a couch, but without the nunchuck cord.

4. Runs very well on a Surface Pro Core i5 processor and 8GB RAM fanless configuration with minimal framerate interruptions with timed button presses, and no video game streaming of Steam Link from a gaming PC or tablet streaming, which would add more visual delays to a demanding 2D platformer combat system.

5. Initial exploration and challenge, but that turns into frustration quickly because...

The Bad:

1. Only one recoverable corpse. 

Shade is an enemy type in Hollow Knight but it is also a player's corpse. While it is a good thing in design to not clutter up a save file with overflow data, that also means that any geo currency accumulated gets left behind a corpse and subsequent shades made overrides any previous shades made, and overrides large amounts of geo with what little a player collects along the way to recover their corpse. That then turns a nice atmospheric video game into a grind if a player still needs to buy needed items just to progress.

2. Inconsistent Save Spot Design. 

Because such a video game is pieced together Downloadable Content, later bosses such as Lost Kin show save spot considerations with a bench easily quickly placed with intentions of using that dash powerup ability to go back to a boss area and with the Colosseum of Fools a bench is placed immediately at such locations. Areas that show bad save spots are for a Galien boss fight and for the Broodling Mawlek. Galien's boss fight, if one re-travels through areas, has Stalking Devouts in very tight corridors to combat, that add to losing geo in large sums if a player has not learned how to deal with such difficulty.

Save benches are the only areas one can re-equip charms. That adds difficulty in itself to decide on traveling to a location with a compass charm that takes up valuable charm space for effective abilities. In a way, such design exercises memory, but that is a turnoff for those that are expecting maps to have built-in map marker compass indicators.

3. Maps are just homework templates.

Cornifer is a NPC that sells maps to a player to help exploration. However, fitting to the atmosphere, but not fitting to video gamers' expectations potentially, is the needed exploration by the player themselves to complete what little Cornifer actually maps out. For some, they may appreciate such world-built lore and it makes sense that when Cornifer is found, areas around him are only vaguely drawn. For some players, they would get frustrated from collecting geo to pay for map and a charm needed to show a player's location.

4. Useless Non-Playable-Characters. 

Confessor Jiji is unlocked once a player has found enough geo to unlock that town NPC. Such an NPC requires rancid eggs that are rare finds in Hollow Knight, and in hidden places. Making matters worse is that Confessor Jiji supposedly offers a good service to recover a player's shade to recover geo, but one has to traverse back up to town from a save bench from lengthy exploration, and possibly overriding a shade with large amounts of geo.

Even more tongue-in-cheek, later Downloadable Content areas have a well that blatantly mentions to a player to dump all of their currency into a well. That banker that keeps a player's geo safely stored is more out of the way travel.

Zote is also pretty useless, regarding in-game discovery a player will find along the way.

Typing of useless bosses that do not show abilities...

5. Boss tells and cues for attack anticipations are very very subtle.

The real problematic difficulty curve that creates frustrating time wasting gameplay are that some boss fight animations do not really show obvious tells or even distinctive tells of what to expect of reaction timing for platform combat. What prompted this review is the second fight for Lost Kin in the dreamworld region. Team Cherry obviously recognized that and intentionally made a save point restart right at the entrance of a made dreamworld area, but still has frustration and stressed thumbs platforming down to that boss fight area. This is with the small animation size of Lost Kin, compared to the Brooding Mawlek that at least show some pause cue indicators.

Some Fixes For The Bad?:

1. Multiple Shades With A Player's Kept Geo.

If players frequently make shades from platforming failure with deaths from terrain and or enemies then multiple shades are needed to be created, if Team Cherry insists on difficult challenges then creating shades as a result of death already blots out useless map features and pins, and more shades definitely would block out map views, increasing difficulty to navigate back to staged shade areas to clear up a player's map.

2. Dream Nail Abilities Clarified As A Teleportation Skill.

An ability a player would acquire from battling various warriors and finding another type of currency, essence, is that many essence later a skill of creating dreamworld access is awarded to a player. That skill does not mention very well on initial reception it is actually a portal skill, allowing a player to quickly go back to a place where a player made a dreamworld portal. 

That is within the lore of benches not placed just about everywhere, and allowing a player to easily fast-travel to retry bosses. However, that also means enough essence collected should not include from very out of reach boss placements, such as retries for fighting Galien. Creating dreamworld portals also uses up essence, and essence are not easily found while battling small enemies.

3. Save benches nearby boss areas.

With the lore of Hollow Knight, evident with the Hunter's journal, it is expected to be on guard and expect tough enemies to fight. Obvious save points of benches right before a boss defeats such lore with such notion, but it would alleviate frustrating play.

The Mantis Lords fight also faces lengthy navigation back to retry fighting those bosses, even with the first nail upgrade.


The Humor:

Hollow Knight is definitely what some developers think what an expanded A Bug's Life would entail, without a trite Ant and Grasshopper Aesop. Such a video game storytelling has a different aesop, somewhere, but that is up to players to discern context clues - that may not easily be translated if a player happens to be playing such a video game in another language.

Cornifer is a character that sells those meme templates of good face bad face images, but with no text filled to the side. And that in itself snarks at some digital markets.

In a way, Team Cherry also mocks video gaming industry.

Too Long, Only Looked At Emoji Thumbs Up Or Thumbs Down?

Recommended to those already well versed in video games.
Not Recommended to those new to video games, and should start with Nintendo's Kirby franchises video game series. Newcomers to video gamers should start with Steamworld Dig as another well-crafted platformer adventure, that also involves a well.

This review was typed without any prior research and reading reviews on Steam communities or any official video game review sites and unofficial video game reviews of other app stores. It seems others have not mentioned how useless some Non-Playable-Characters can be in such a video game, or the lack of sadomasochism tags that describes speedrunner achievements. Zote does have interesting things to mention once one recovers him from an arena event.
